Chapter 1
(rewrite summary - Alalu kill bunri becasue he covets the trees but this forms the firmanent destroying the trees and seperating the sun and moon from the earth)
Before the host of stars were born. When there was desolate silence; when silence first made sound. When there was. Then there was not.


From the whole and not whole, there came Abassil the emptiness. There was Zalisa, the filled.


Then, there was Umakhi and there was Eversor. 


Nako and Jaga were created. The moving and unmoving waters. They grew tall.


There was the most mighty of them. Bunri-Shita, the mighty dragon with two faces. With strength, they set themselves against him; there was conflict.


 Bunri-Shita stood tall like the mountain. His glare burned like fire. He burst forth like the wind.


The emptiness burst forth. That which was whole, was separated by Bushimo’s hand. The ether poured out. 


He trapped the moving water within the ether, and formed the unmoving water around it.


Before the earth took shape, Bunri gave form to the waters. The gods were called into being. Ages increased as they matured. Thus the Primevals were established. Alalu was foremost among them.


Tiamat, the bitter, Absu, the sweet; Their waters swirled about and mingled. They were married together. . 


The world was dark and formless as the waters gave birth to the rising and sinking mist. The world was dark and formless as Lahmu and Lahamu were born. Ages increased. Ansar and Kisar were created, surpassing them. 


Thus the Enmelam were established.


Years added to years.


Bunri spread his seed across the earth and three great trees grew; and, because of his cleverness, they were entrusted to Alalu to oversee. 


He fashioned a light like that of burning fire atop a tree; and so it was called Sohlas. He fashioned a light like that of glinting silver atop a tree; and so it was called Ar. 


The Great Lord was conceived. En, fashioned by the hand of Bunri. His strength was rival to Alalu. He was given seat at the feet of Bunri, who then fashioned Cho, who was paired with En..


Then came forth Anu, firstborn of Anzar, surpassing his forebearers


There was Nudimmud, born of En; whom his begetter, granted abounding in all wisdom. 


Creation matured.


Alalu brought offering of the fruit of the tree trees unto Bunri, as En shaped flockings for offering, and the fat thereof, for his lord. 


En was highly favoured for his sacrifice, and Alalu was wroth. His countenance fell as he called for his servant, Anzar, saying: 


“ Anzar, my cup bearer; who lifts my spirit
" What then shall we do?
“ The Lord has respect unto En and to his offering
“ But unto me and to my offering he has no respect
" What then shall we do?


Anzar answered, and gave counsel to Alalu:


“ Come, for En is strong, but thou shalt destroy him!”


Alalu hearkened unto him, and his countenance grew bright.


“ I shall take his place! I shall destroy his way, 
“ I will let there be lamentation!”


Alalu talked with En saying:
                
“ Come, I wish for you to illuminate the last Great Tree”


And when they reached the tree, Alalu rose up against En to slay him. Anzar, who lay in waiting, struck out with his cutter. The arms of Bunri which held the heavens together were severed from the blow as En was slain. 


The horizon formed as the heavens and the earth were split apart..


The blood poured from Bunri down into the waters forming the dark earth below. His body fell to the earth, and his skull formed the vault of heaven. The lights of Sol and Ar hung above, though the great trees were now gone.


The giants of heaven were all angry with each other and they split from each other. The Enshi went down to the earth. The Enmeshgal roamed the heavens. And the Entians went into the deep of the earth.


The Great Mountain Gods were then born.The wild bull, the Great mountain Father, Enlil was born to Anu and Antu. The mother goddess, the lady of the mountain, Ki was born to Alalu.


The holy settlements were marked out by Nudimmud. He burnt offering to Alalu. He built the first great city, Balla-chul. Its ramparts were of holy mesu-trees, its brickwork was oven fired. And there Alalu made his throne. 


Enki travelled eastward toward the Shining Plain, as Enlil was sent to the earth to oversee the work. 


Chapter 2


Enmesharra’s Reign; The Dialogue of Pessimism:
Darkness and Night came to the earth. 


Enmesharra gathered the heavenly mehs, and bestowed himself with their divine radiance. 


He fashioned the staff of kingship, and claimed Enlilship of the Gods. 


Praise was given. 


Enmasharra decreed:


“ Slave, listen to me!
“ Quickly!


“ Fetch me the chariot and hitch it up. 
“ I want to drive to the palace”


Papsukkal spoke from beside him:


“ Here I am, Master, here I am!


“ Drive, Master, drive! 
“ It will be to your advantage. 
“ When they see you, the king, 
“ They will give you honours”


“ O’ well, slave, I will not drive to the palace!”


“ Do not drive, Master, do not drive! 
“ When he will see you, 
“ As king,
“ You may be sent god knows where, 
“ You may take a route that you do not know, 
“ It will make you suffer agony day and night.” 


Enmasharra decreed:


“ Slave, listen to me!
“ Quickly! 


“ Fetch me water for my hands, 
“ I want to dine!”


Papsukkal gave reply:        


“ Dine, Master, dine! 
“ A good meal relaxes the mind!
“ The prepared bounty of the field shall be the meal of gods. 
“ To wash one's hands passes the time!”


“  O’ well, slave, if I must wait, I will not dine!”


“ Do not dine, Master, do not dine! 
“ To eat only when one is hungry, 
“ To drink only when one is thirsty is best!”


“ Slave, listen to me!
“ Quickly! 


“ Fetch me my chariot.
“ I am going to hunt!
“ Slave, listen to me!”


 Papsukkal gave reply:


“ Drive, Master, drive! 
“ Here I am!


“ A hunter gets his belly filled, 
“ The hunting dog will break the bones of the prey! 
“ The raven that scours the country can feed its nest, 
“ The fleeting onager finds rich pastures”


“ O’ well, slave, I will not hunt!”


“ Do not go, Master! 
“ Do not go, the hunter’s luck changes,
“ The hunting dog’s teeth will get broken! 
“ The raven that scours the country 
“ It has taken a hole in the wall as a home. 
“ The fleeting onager has deserted his stable”  


The Divine Enmesharra decreed:


“I want to set up a home, I want to have a son!
“ Slave, listen to me!”


“ Have them, Master, have them! 
“ The man who sets up a home leaves his father’s and makes his own “


“O’ well,slave, I will build no foundation. How could I set up a home?”


“ Do not set up a home; 
“ Otherwise you will break up your father's home”


Enmasharra decreed:


“ Quickly! 


“ Fetch the court, let my enemies strike me evilly,
“ I will speak against them”


“ Do not remain silent, Master, do not remain silent! 
“ If you do not open your mouth,  your opponent will have a free hand!
 
“ So speak, Master! 
“ Speak, let your enemies be laid bare”


“  O’ well, slave, then I shall not speak against my enemies”


“ Do not speak, Master! 
“ Do not speak, 


“  If you speak, your prosecutors will be savage to you.”


“ Slave, listen to me!
“ I want to lead a revolution!”


“ Lead, Master! 
“ Lead! 


“ If you do not lead a revolution, where will your clothes come from? 
“ And who will enable you to fill your belly?”


“ O’ well slave, I do not want to lead a revolution!”


“ Do not lead; 
“ Master, do not lead a revolution! 


“ The man who leads a revolution is either killed or flayed, 
“ Or has his eyes put out, or he is arrested and thrown to jail.”


“ Slave, listen to me!”


He gave reply:


“ Here I am, Master, here I am!“


Enmesharra continued:


“ I want to make love to a woman”


“ Make love,
“ Master, make love! 


“ The man who makes love to a woman forgets sorrow and fear!”


“ O’ well slave, I do not want to make love to a woman!”


“ Do not make love, 
“ Master, do not make love! 


“ A woman is a real pitfall, 
“ A hole, 
“ A ditch;
“ A woman is a sharp iron dagger that cuts a man's throat!”


“ Slave, listen to me!
 “ Quickly! 


“ Fetch water for the hands. 
“ I want them to sacrifice to me, their god”


“ Sacrifice, 
“ For the Master, sacrifice! 


“ The man who sacrifices to his god is satisfied at heart.
“ He accumulates benefit after benefit.”


“ O’ well slave, then I do not want the sacrifices of god.”


“ Do not take the sacrifice, 
“ Master, do not take the sacrifice!
 
“ They will teach their god to run after them like a dog.
“ Whether he asks of them rites 
“ Or, 


"” Do you not consult your god?"” 


“ Or anything else”


“ Slave, listen to me!
“ I want to invest in gold”


“ Invest, Master, Invest!
“ The man who invests keeps his capital, his interest is enormous.”


“ O’ well slave, I do not want to invest”


“ Do not invest, 
“ Master, do not invest! 
“ Making loans is as sweet as making love, 
“ But getting them back is like having children; 
“ They will take away your capital, cursing you without ceasing.
“ They will make you lose the interest on the capital.”  


“ Slave, listen to me!
“ I want to perform a public benefit for my people!”


“ So do it; Master, do it!


“ The man who performs a public benefit for his fellow. 
“ His actions are exposed to the Circle, the Light of Marduk.”


Enmesharra shuddered:


“ I do not want to perform a public benefit.”


“ Do not perform, Master, do not perform!
“ Go up the ancient tells and walk about; 
“ See the mixed skulls of plebeians and nobles. 


“ Which is the malefactor and which is the benefactor?”         


Enmesharra clutched at his heart, woe had gripped him. 


“ Slave, listen to me!”
                
Papsukkal made his voice heard:


“ Here I am, Master, here I am!”


Enmesharra pondered:


“ What then is good? 
“ To have my neck broken; and yours?


“ Or, to be thrown into the river? 
“ Is that good?


“ Who is so tall as to ascend to heaven? 
“ Who is so broad as to encompass the entire world?
“ O’ well, slave, I will kill you and send you first.“


Papsukkal nodded:


“ Yes, 
“ But, my Master would certainly not survive me, for no more than three days!”


Hymn to Anu:




He took his throne as the lord of the Great Heavens. Perfectly endowed with might, from the flashing sky above; son of Anshar, Father of the Gods. Perfectly endowed with vigour, of the dark earth below; son of Kishar, Mother of the Gods. 


Anu, succoured on stars! 


Holder of the tablets of lordship and divinity, the Tablets of Destinies. Perfectly endowed with great divine powers.


The great dragon, the encompassing hero, captured the glory of the light of the stars. Anu went forth. He went forth with Erra, whom not even the gods escaped his battle net. He went forth with the one engendered in wisdom, the Great Mountain, Sage of the Gods, Enlil.


Anu was king of the Heavens and Earth. 


 He established his rule, so that excellence flourish; the world be made abundant. The Holder of Divine Rites, Anu, took his throne. 


He approached the goddess Ki and set her on a throne. He exalted his father, and honoured him. 


The Lesser Gods he established. The Great Gods he established. The Great Anuna he, too, established. 


His most holy daughter, he determined for her a great destiny. Without Inanna, great An made no decisions, and Enlil determined no destinies. 


Who could oppose the mistress who raised her head and was supreme over the mountains? 


She stirred confusion and chaos against those who were disobedient with her game; to speed conflict and battle, untiring, strapping on her sandals. 


Anu, bestower of kingship and authority; upholder of life and order. 


Anu, Father of the Gods! 


King in Heaven!


Hymn to Nisaba:


Great Lady coloured like the stars of heaven. She was perfectly endowed, holding a lapis-lazuli tablet! Nisaba, great wild cow, born by Urac. Wild sheep, nourished on good milk; among holy alkaline plants. She opened the mouth and birthed the seven reeds! She was perfectly endowed with fifty great divine powers. My lady, the most powerful in E-kur!


As a dragon; emerging in glory at the festival, Aruru, mother goddess of the Land, emerged from the clay. Calming the land with cool water. Lavishing fine oil, of plenty, on the foreign lands. Engendered in wisdom by the Great Mountain Enlil! 


Good woman, Chief Scribe of An, Record-Keeper of Enlil, Wise Sage of the Gods!


In order to make barley and flax grow in the furrows, so that excellent corn could be admired; in order to provide for the seven great throne-daises, she made flax shoot forth. She made barley shoot forth at the harvest. She made them come forth at the great festival; the august festival of Enlil. 


In her great princely role she cleansed her body and put the holy priestly garment on her torso. She was draped in the Holy garment.


In order to establish bread offerings where none existed. In order to pour forth great libations of alcohol, so as to appease the god of grandeur; to appease Enlil, and to appease merciful Kusu and Ezina, she would appoint a great en priest. She would appoint a festival and a great en priest of the land.


Enki approached the maiden Nisaba in prayer. He organised pure food-offerings. He opened up Nisaba's house of learning, and placed the lapis-lazuli tablet on her knees. She consulted the holy tablet of the heavenly stars. In Aratta, he placed E-zagina at her disposal. 


He built up Erec in abundance, founded from little mud bricks. He who was granted the most complex wisdom!


In the Abzu, the great crown of Eridug; where sanctuaries are apportioned, where elevated, where the fates are apportioned. When Enki, the great princely farmer of the awe-inspiring temple, the carpenter of Eridug; the master of purification rites, the lord of the great en priest's precinct, occupied E-engur.


He built up the Abzu of Eridug. He took counsel in Hal-an-kug. He split, with an axe, the house of boxwood. 


The sage's hair hung loose, when he opened the house of learning. He stood in the street at the door of learning. 


He finished the great dining-hall of cedar.


He grasped the date-palm mace. He wore the priestly garment with that mace.Then, he uttered seven blessings to Nisaba, the supreme nursemaid:


" O’ Nisaba, good woman, fair woman, 
“ Woman born in the mountains,


“ Woman born by the mountains!


“ Nisaba, may you be the butter in the cattle-pen,
“ May you be the cream in the sheepfold,
“ May you be keeper of the seal in the treasury,
“ May you be a good steward in the palace,
“ May you be a heaper up, of grain, among the grain piles
“ And in the grain stores!"


All this, because the Prince Enki cherished Nisaba!


The Fertile Valley:
Tablet I:


There was a city, the city of Nibru. Dur-jicnimbar was the name in that time. Id-sala, its holy river. Kar-jectina is its quay. Kar-asar was its quay where boats make fast. Pu-lal is its fresh-water well.  Id-nunbir-tum is its branching canal. If one measures from there, its cultivated land is 50 sar each way. 


Enlil was one of its young men. Ninlil was one of its young women. Nun-bar-ce-gunu was one of its wise old women. Nisaba, she was faithfully sitting on the dias, admirable and full of charms. Then there came Haya, the noble son. 


Who, like him, can compare with An and Enlil? 


Haia, the Pure, put the holy semen into her womb. Nun-bar-ce-gunu faithfully gave birth to Sud. She brought her up in her holy place. Sud suckled her at her breasts full of good milk. 


The bosom of the young girl burgeoned, and she became full of flourishing beauty. In the courtyard of Nisaba, at the gate of the E-zagin, there she stood, the object of admiration, like a tall, beautifully shaped cow.


At that time Enlil had not yet been given a wife in the E-kur. Ninlil's name was not yet famous in the Ki-ur. After travelling through Sumer and to the ends of the universe, he sought rest. In his search throughout the Land, Enlil, the Great Mountain, stopped at Erec. As he looked around there, he found the woman of his choice. 


He approached her and, overflowing with joy, engaged her in conversation: 


" I will make you perfect in a queen's dress;
“ After standing in the street, you will be brought to my court. 


“ How impressed I am by your beauty, even if you are a shameless person!" 


In her youthful inexperience Sud answered Enlil:


" If I want, I will stand proudly at our gate,
“ Who dares to give me a bad reputation?
“ What are your intentions? Why have you come here?


“ Begone from my sight!" 


Others had already tried to deceive the young maiden before and in this, Enlil made her angry. Enlil answered Sud. He moved, standing closer to her: 


" Come, I want to speak to you! 
“ I will have a talk with you about your becoming my wife.
“ Kiss me, my lady of the most beautiful eyes; the matter rests in your hands."


But the words had barely left his mouth when, right in front of him, she went into the house. 


The maiden was then advised by her own mother. Sud was advised by Nun-bar-ce-gunu: 


" This river is holy, woman! 
“ The river is holy, don't bathe in it, Ninlil!
“ Don't walk along the bank of the Id-nunbir-tum,
“ His eye is bright;


“ The lord's eye is bright, he will look at you!


“ The Great Mountain, Enlil, he will see you, 
“ The shepherd who decides all destinies


“ His eye is bright!
“ He will look at you, and straight away he will want to have intercourse,
“ He will want to kiss!
“ He will be happy to pour lusty semen into the womb,
“ And then he will leave you to it!"


She advised her from the heart. She gave wisdom to her. The river was holy; the woman bathed in the holy river. 
                
Sud walked along the bank of the Id-nunbir-tum. The lord's eye was bright as he looked at her. 


The Great Mountain, Father Enlil,  looked at her. The shepherd who decides all destinies, his eye was bright. He looked at her and said to her: 


" I want to have sex with you!", 


But he could not make her let him. 


Enlil said to her:


" I want to kiss you!", 


But he could not make her let him.


She said to him:


" My vagina is small, it does not know pregnancy.
“ My lips are young, they do not know kissing.
“ If my mother learns of it, she will slap my hand,
“ If my father learns of it, he will lay hands on me.


“ But, right now, no one will stop me from telling this to my girl-friend!"


Enlil spoke to his minister Nuska: 


" Nuska, my minister!" 


Nuska came to his lord:


" At your service, what do you wish? 
" Master builder of the E-kur, I am at your service, my lord!" 


Enlil asked his vizier:


" Has anyone had intercourse with,
“ Or has anyone kissed, a maiden so beautiful, so radiant, 
“ Ninlil, so beautiful, so radiant?" 


The heart of the wise lord pounded, again he called for Nuska, who answered:


" What is your wish?" 


He gave the following instructions to him: 


" I want you to go back to Erec,
“ The city of Nisaba, the city whose foundations are august.
“ Do not delay!
“ Repeat to her what I am going to tell you: 


" I am a young man,
” I have sent this message to you because of my wish:
” I want to take your daughter as wife.
” Give me your consent.
” I will send you presents in my name, abundant, my marriage gifts.
“ I am Enlil, the descendant and offspring of Ancar, the noble,
“ The lord of heaven and earth.


“ The name of your daughter shall become Ninlil,
“ And all the foreign countries shall supplicate it.
” I will present her with the Ja-jic-cua as her storehouse.
” I will give her the Ki-ur to be her beloved private quarters.
” She shall sit with me in the E-kur, my august dais.
” She shall determine fates.
” She shall apportion the divine powers among the Anuna, the great gods.
” And as for you, I will place in your hands the lives of the black-headed people.”


" When you get there, let the woman I have chosen for her beauty answer her mother. 
” Do not go to her empty-handed,
” But take her some jewellery in your left hand.
“ Waste no time. 
“ Return with her answer quickly."


When Nuska, the head of the assembly, received Enlil's instructions, he wasted no time. Swiftly, he directed his steps to Erec. He entered E-zagin, the residence of Nanibgal and prostrated himself before her dais. He repeated the message of Enlil to her, and she asked him: 


" What?
“ What do you mean?”


And at that time, she knew what had been done. 


Then Nanibgal went on speaking flatteringly to the minister: 


" Adviser, fit for his king, ever observant!
“ Who, like you, could give counsel daily to the Great Mountain?


“ How could I contest the king's message which his slave has received?
“ If there is truth in what you have told me 
“ And may there be no falsehood 
“ Who could reject one who bestows such exceedingly great favours? 
“ This surely makes our mood and hearts happy.
“ Let us consider that amends have been made.
“ By bringing the marriage gifts and the presents
“ In his name, the insult is wiped away.


“ Tell him:. 


" You shall become my son-in-law; do as you wish!” 


“ Tell Enlil, the Great Mountain:


" Do as you wish!
“ Let his sister come from her side, 
“ And she shall accompany Sud from here, Aruru shall become Sud's sister-in-law
“ Let her be shown the household.”


“ Inform your lord thus in his august Ki-ur.
“ Repeat this to Enlil in the privacy of his holy bedchamber."


Tablet II:


Sud walked along the bank of the Id-nunbir-tum. 


His eye was bright. 


The lord's eye was bright. 


He looked at her. The Great Mountain, Father Enlil with his eyes bright, looked at her. The shepherd who decides all destinies, looked at her. 


At the instruction of Enlil, The minister brought his master across by boat. He brought him over with the rope of a small boat; bringing him over in a big boat. The lord floated downstream to Sud. 


He was actually to have intercourse with her. 


He was actually to kiss her! 


Father Enlil, floated downstream to the beautiful lady. 


He was actually to have intercourse with her. 


He was actually to kiss her!  


He grasped hold of her, whom he was seeking, so as to lie with her on a small bank. 


He actually had intercourse with her. 


He actually kissed her! 


At this one intercourse, at this one kissing he poured the seed of Suen-Acimbabbar into her womb.


Enlil was walking in the Ki-ur. As Enlil was going about in the Ki-ur, the fifty great gods and the seven gods who decide destinies had Enlil arrested in the Ki-ur. 


Enlil, the ritually impure, left the city. 


Nunamnir, the ritually impure, left the city. 


Enlil, ritually impure, left the city! 


Enlil, in accordance with what had been decided; Nunamnir, in accordance with what had been decided, went. Sud followed. 


Nunamnir went, the maiden chased him.


Enlil spoke to the man at the city gate: 


" City gatekeeper! 
“ Keeper of the barrier!
“ Porter! 
“ Keeper of the holy barrier! 
“ When your lady Ninlil comes,
“ If she asks after me, don't tell her where I am!" 


Sud addressed the city gatekeeper: 


" City gatekeeper!
“ Keeper of the barrier!
“ Porter!
“ Keeper of the holy barrier! 
“ When did your lord Enlil go by?"


She spoke to him, and Enlil answered as the city gatekeeper: 


" My lord has not talked with me at all,
“ O’ loveliest one!
“ Enlil has not talked with me at all, loveliest one." 


Sud gave reply:


" I will make clear my aim and explain my intent. 


“ You can fill my womb once it is empty 
“ Enlil, lord of all the lands, has had sex with me,
“ Just as Enlil is your lord, so am I your lady!"


The city gatekeeper answered:


" If you are my lady, let my hand touch your ..."


Sud stopped his words:


" The seed of your lord, the bright seed, is in my womb. 
“ The seed of Suen, the bright seed, is in my womb."


The city gatekeeper replied:


" My master's seed can go up to the heavens!
“ Let my seed go downwards!
“ Let my seed go downwards, instead of my master's seed!"


Enlil, as the city gatekeeper, got her to lie down in the chamber. 


He had intercourse with her there, he kissed her there. 


At this one intercourse, at this one kissing he poured the seed of Nergal-Meslamtaeda into her womb.


Enlil went. Sud followed. Nunamnir went, and the maiden chased him. 


Enlil approached the man of the Id-kura; River of the Underworld. The man-eating river: 


" My man of the Id-kura, the man-eating river!
“ When your lady Ninlil comes,
“ If she asks after me, don't you tell her where I am!"


Sud approached the man of the Id-kura, the man-eating river. 


" My man of the Id-kura, the man-eating river! 
“ When did your lord Enlil go by?", 


Enlil answered as the man of the Id-kura: 


" My lord has not talked with me at all,
“ O’ loveliest one!
“ Enlil has not talked with me at all " 


Sud gave reply:


" I will make clear my aim and explain my intent. 


“ You can fill my womb once it is empty 
“ Enlil, lord of all the lands, has had sex with me,
“ Just as Enlil is your lord, so am I your lady!"


The man of the Id-kura, the man-eating river spoke:


" If you are my lady, let my hand touch your ..." 


Sud stopped his words:


" The seed of your lord, the bright seed, is in my womb. 
“ The seed of Suen, the bright seed, is in my womb."


The man of the Id-kura, the man-eating river answered:


" My master's seed can go up to the heavens!
“ Let my seed go downwards!
“ Let my seed go downwards, instead of my master's seed!" 


Enlil, as the man of the Id-kura, got her to lie down in the chamber. 


He had intercourse with her there. 


He kissed her there. 


At this one intercourse; at this one kissing, he poured into her womb the seed of Ninazu, the king who stretches measuring lines over the fields.


Enlil went. Ninlil followed. 


Nunamnir went, the maiden chased him. 


Enlil approached SI.LU.IGI, the man of the ferryboat. 


" SI.LU.IGI, my man of the ferryboat! 
“ When your lady Ninlil comes,
“ If she asks after me, don't you tell her where I am!"


Sud approached the man of the ferryboat.


" Man of the ferryboat! When did your lord Enlil go by?", 


She said to him. Enlil answered as the man SI.LU.IGI:


" My lord has not talked with me at all, 
“ O’ loveliest one!
“ Enlil has not talked with me at all " 


Sud gave reply:


" I will make clear my aim and explain my intent. 


“ You can fill my womb once it is empty 
“ Enlil, lord of all the lands, has had sex with me,
“ Just as Enlil is your lord, so am I your lady!"


SI.LU.IGI, the man of the ferryboat answered:


" If you are my lady, let my hand touch your ..." 


Sud stopped his words:


" The seed of your lord, the bright seed, is in my womb. 
“ The seed of Suen, the bright seed, is in my womb."


SI.LU.IGI, the man of the ferryboat spoke:


" My master's seed can go up to the heavens!
“ Let my seed go downwards!
“ Let my seed go downwards, instead of my master's seed!" 


Enlil, as SI.LU.IGI, got her to lie down in the chamber. 


He had intercourse with her there. 


He kissed her there.


 At this one intercourse, at this one kissing he poured into her womb the seed of Enbilulu, the inspector of canals.


Tablet III:


Nanibgal called Nuska to her, and after a word of instruction, he wasted no time.


Nanibgal called Sud to her and gave her advice: 


" My little one, sleep indoors 
“ In your pure place,
“ The pleasant private quarters 
“ Do not take leave, the House of Nisaba's Wisdom. 
“ Behold, Nuska is knowing and wise.
“ Enter into his presence and pour him beer." 


According to the instructions of her mother, she washed his hands and placed in them a tankard. The minister opened his left hand and gave her the jewellery. He presented everything to her and set it before her. She received the gifts. 


He went. He directed his steps to Nibru. Nuska kissed the ground before Enlil. As the Great Lady had said, as she had instructed him, he repeated the words:


“ She said: 
" Adviser, fit for his king, ever observant!
“ Who, like you, could give counsel daily to the Great Mountain?
“ How could I contest the king's message which his slave has received?


“ If there is truth in what you have told me, and may there be no falsehood 
“ Who could reject one who bestows such exceedingly great favours? 
“ This surely makes our mood and hearts happy.
“ Let us consider that amends have been made.
“ By bringing the marriage gifts and the presents
“ In his name the insult is wiped away.
“ Tell him:. 


" You shall become my son-in-law; do as you wish! 
“ Tell Enlil, the Great Mountain, do as you wish! 
“ Let his sister come from her side, and she shall accompany Sud from here.
“ Aruru shall become Sud's sister-in-law:
“ Let her be shown the household."


This news made the Great Mountain feel good, and brought great rejoicing in Enlil's heart. He raised his head and rejoiced. 


Animals came running. 


Massive herds of four-legged animals that graze together in the desert. 


He caught birds, living in the mountains. He made wild bulls, red deer, elephants, fallow deer, gazelles, bears, wild sheep and rams, lynxes, foxes, wild cats, tigers, mountain sheep, water buffaloes, monkeys, and thick-horned fat cattle jostle together noisily. Cows and their calves, wild cattle with wide-spread horns, led by rope. Ewes and lambs, goats and kids, romping and fighting. Large kids with long beards, scratching with their hooves, and majestic sheep. They were despatched by Enlil toward Erec.


Large cheeses, mustard-flavoured cheeses, small cheeses, milk, cold hard-boiled eggs, butter, the sweetest dry honey and white honey. Bowls, and thick and large vessels were despatched by Enlil toward Erec.


Fruit, dates, figs, large pomegranates, jipar fruits, plums, halub nuts, almonds, acorns, Dilmun dates packed in baskets. Dark-coloured date spadices. Large pomegranate seeds squeezed out from their rinds. Big clusters of early grapes. Fruit trees. Trees from orchards. Trees grown in winter, and fruits from orchards were despatched by Enlil toward Erec.


Ores from Harali, the faraway land, filled storehouses. Copper, rock-crystal, gold, silver, tin, the yield of the uplands. Precious metals, heavy loads of them, were despatched by Enlil toward Erec. 


After the personal presents, the transported goods were sent, Ninmah and the Minister rejoiced. The dust from their march reached high into the sky like rain clouds. Enormous marriage gifts were being brought for Nanibgal to Erec; the city was getting full inside and out. E-zagin, it was to be replete. The rest piled up on the outlying roads reaching up into blue sky.

Nanibgal, the mother-in-law of Enlil, the woman who had been slandered, was treated kindly by Nuska. The woman of Ezina, was treated kindly by Nuska. 


But the lady disregarded the flatterer, and spoke to her daughter:


" May you be Enlil's favourite wife 
“ The wife of Enlil's heart,
“ And may he speak to you sweetly.
“ May he embrace you, the most beautiful of all, and tell you:


“" Beloved, open wide!"” 


“ May the two of you never lose the pleasure of excitement; make it last a long time. 
“ May it be that the pleasure of excitement will never be lost. 
“ You two will dwell on the hill, and have children afterwards!
“ When you enter the house to live there, may abundance precede you,
“ And may joy follow you.


“ May the people line up for you wherever you go,
“ And may all the people supplicate for you.
“ The fate I have determined for you should be fulfilled
“ It cannot be altered!


“ Go with head held high into the E-mah"


Then, Aruru grasped her hand and led her away into the Ec-mah. She brought her into the shining E-kur, the house of Enlil; and poured the best perfume over her face. 


In the sleeping quarters, in the flowered bed fragrant like a cedar forest, Enlil made love to his wife and took great pleasure in it. 


He sat her on his dais appropriate to the status of Enlil, and made the people pray to her. 


The lord whose statements are powerful also determined a fate for the Lady, the woman of his favour; he gave her the name Nintud, the Lady Who Gives Birth. He made beautiful En-Badtibira's countenance.


Enlil spoke: 


" From now on, a woman shall be the mistress;
“ A foreign woman shall be the mistress of the house.
“ May my beautiful wife,who was born by holy Nisaba,
“ Be Ezina, the growing grain, the life of Sumer.
“ When she appears in the furrows like a beautiful young girl,
“ May Ickur, the canal inspector, be her provider,
“ Supplying her with water from the ground.
“ The height of the year is marked with her new prime flax and your new prime grain;
“ Enlil and Ninlil procreate them as desired.”

A festival for the couple and the child Suen.

        The harvest crop raises its head high for the great festival of Enlil. 


The scribal art, the tablets decorated with writing, the stylus, the tablet board, the computing of accounts, adding and subtracting, the shining measuring rope, the builders hammer stone, the head of the surveyor's peg, the measuring rod, the marking of the boundaries, and the city were fittingly in his hands. The farmer entrusted cultivation into his hands. 


“ Woman, the proudest among the great princes,
“ Proud woman, surpassing the mountains, who always fulfils her desires. 
“ The goddess without name has a famous name. 


“ From now on, Sud is Ninlil 


“ Enlil is the king and Ninlil is the queen. 


“ From now on, Sud shall be known as Ninlil. 


“ You are lord! You are king! 
“ Enlil, you are lord! You are king! 


“ Nunamnir, you are lord! 
“ You are king! 
“ You are supreme lord, powerful lord! 


“ Lord who makes flax grow,
“ Lord who makes barley grow, you are lord of heaven,
“ Lord Plenty, Lord of the Earth! 


“ Lord of heaven, Enlil in heaven,
“ Enlil is king!
“ Lord whose utterances cannot be altered at all,
“ His primordial utterances will not be changed!


“ For the praise spoken for Ninlil the mother, praise be to the Great Mountain”


A holy song of praise of Enlil and Ninlil.


Enki and Ninḫursaĝa:


Tablet I:


Pure were the cities, and the ones to whom they were allotted. Pure was Dilmun land. Pure was Sumer. 


Virginal was Dilmun land. Pristine was Dilmun land.


The place where Enki had lain down with his spouse, that place was still virginal, that place was still pristine. The place where Enki had lain down with Ninsikila, that place was virginal, that place was pristine.


In Dilmun the raven was not yet cawing, the partridge not cackling. The lion did not slay, the wolf was not carrying off lambs. The dog had not been taught to make kids curl up. The pig had not learned of grain, or that it was to be eaten.


When a widow has spread malt on the roof, the birds did not yet eat that malt up there. The pigeon, then, did not tuck the head under its wing.


No eye-diseases said there: 


" I am the eye disease." 


No headache said there: 


" I am the headache." 


No old woman belonging to it said there: 


" I am an old woman." 


No old man belonging to it said there: 


" I am an old man." 


No maiden in her unwashed state walked about in the city. No man dredging a river said there: 


" It is getting dark." 


No herald made the rounds in his border district. No singer sang an elulam there. No wailings were wailed in the city's outskirts there.


Ninsikila said to her Father Enki: 


" You have given a city.
“ What does your giving avail me? 
“ You have given Dilmun 
“ And, what does your giving avail me?
" You have given Dilmun, a city that has no river quay.
" A city that has no fields, glebe or furrow
“ What does your giving avail me?"


Enki answered Ninsikila: 


" When Utu steps up into heaven, from Nanna's radiant high temple,
“ When the son steps into heaven, from his father’s radiant dwelling,
“ Fresh waters shall run out of the ground for you 
“ From the standing vessels on Ezen's shore, 
“ From the mouth of the waters running underground


" May the waters rise up from it into your great basins.
“ May your city drink water aplenty from them.
“ May Dilmun drink water aplenty from them.
“ May your pools of salt water become pools of fresh water.
“ May your city become an emporium on the quay for the land.


“ May Dilmun become an emporium on the quay for the land.
" May the land of Tukriš hand over to you gold from Ḫarali, lapis lazuli and silver 
“ May the land of Meluḫa load precious desirable cornelian,
“ Meš wood of Magan, and the best abba wood into large ships for you.


“ May the land of Marḫaši yield you precious stones, topazes.
“ May the land of Magan offer you strong, powerful copper, 
“ Dolerite, u stone and šumin stone 


“ May the Sea-land offer you its own ebony wood, worthy of a king. 
“ May the Tent-lands offer you fine multi-coloured wools. 
“ May the land of Elam hand over to you, choice wools, its tribute.
“ May the manor of Urim, the royal throne dais, the Shining City,
“ Load up into large ships sesame, august raiment, and fine cloth for you . 
“ May the wide sea yield you its wealth.
“ The city's dwellings are good dwellings.


“ Dilmun's dwellings are good dwellings.
“ Its grains are little grains, its dates are big dates, its harvests are triple,
“ Its wood is good wood”


At that moment, on that day, and under that sun, when Utu stepped up into heaven, from the standing vessels on Ezen's shore, from Nanna's radiant high temple, from the mouth of the waters running underground, fresh waters ran out of the ground for her.


The waters rose up from it into her great basins. Dilmun, her city, drank water aplenty from them. Her pools of salt water indeed became pools of fresh water. 


Her fields, glebe and furrows indeed produced grain for her. Dilmun,  her city, indeed became an emporium on the quay for the land. At that moment, on that day, and under that sun, so it indeed happened.


In Dilmun, she lay down for him. 


Her one month was one day. Her two months were two days, and three months were three days. Her four months were four days, and her five months were five days. Her six months were six days. Her seven months were seven days, and her eight months were eight days. Her nine months were nine days. 
 
In the month of womanhood, like a fine oil of abundance, Nintur, mother of the country, gave birth in the pristine city. 


Isimud, the god of two-faces, was born.


Tablet II:


All alone, Enki, the wise one, was digging his phallus into the dykes, plunging his digging stick into the reedbeds toward Nintur, the country's mother. She,the august one pulled his phallus aside and cried out:
 
" No man has taken me in the marsh."


Enki cried out: 


" By the life's breath of heaven I adjure you. 
“ Lie down for me in the marsh, lie down for me in the marsh, that would be joyous." 


Enki distributed his semen destined for Damgalnuna. He poured semen into Ninḫursaĝa's womb and she conceived the semen in the womb, the semen of Enki. 


Her one month was one day. Her two months were two days, and three months were three days. Her four months were four days, and her five months were five days. Her six months were six days. Her seven months were seven days, her eight months were eight days, and her nine months were nine days. 


In the month of womanhood, like fine oil, like oil of abundance, Nintur, mother of the country, gave birth to Ninnisig.


In turn Ninnisig went out to the riverbank. Enki was able to see up there from in the marsh, he was able to see up there. He said to his minister Isimud: 


" Is this nice youngster not to be kissed? 
“ Is this nice Ninnisig not to be kissed?" 


His minister Isimud answered him: 


" My master will sail, let me navigate. 
“ He will sail, let me navigate."


First he put his feet in the boat, next he put them on dry land. He clasped her to the bosom, kissed her, Enki poured semen into the womb and she conceived the semen in the womb, the semen of Enki. 


Her one month was one day, and her two months were two days. Her nine months were but nine days. And in the month of womanhood, like a fine oil of abundance, Ninnisig gave birth to Ninkura.


In turn Ninkura went out to the riverbank. Enki was able to see up there from in the marsh; he was able to see up there. He said to his minister Isimud: 


" Is this nice youngster not to be kissed? 
“ Is this nice Ninkura not to be kissed?" 


His minister Isimud answered him: 


" Kiss this nice youngster. Kiss this nice Ninkura.
“ My master will sail, let me navigate.
“ He will sail, let me navigate."


First he put his feet in the boat, next he put them on dry land. He clasped her to the bosom, kissed her, Enki poured semen into the womb and she conceived the semen in the womb, the semen of Enki. 


Her one month was one day, and her nine months were nine days. In the month of womanhood, like fine oil of abundance, Ninkura gave birth to Ninimma. 


She brought the child up and made her flourish. Ninimma in turn went out to the riverbank. Enki was towing his boat along and was able to see up there. He laid eyes on Ninimma on the riverbank and said to his minister Isimud: 


" Have I ever kissed one like this nice youngster?
“ Have I ever made love to one like nice Ninimma?" 


His minister Isimud answered him: 


" My master will sail, let me navigate."


First he put his feet in the boat, next he put them on dry land. He clasped her to the bosom, lying in her crotch, made love to the youngster and kissed her. Enki poured semen into Ninimma's womb and she conceived the semen in the womb, the semen of Enki.


To the woman, one month was but one day. Two months were but two days. Three months were but three days, and four months were but four days. At nine days, in the month of womanhood, like fine oil, like oil of abundance, Ninimma gave birth to Uttu, the exalted woman.


Nintur said to Uttu: 


" Let me advise you, and may you take heed of my advice. 
“ Let me speak words to you and may you heed my words.


“ From in the marsh one man is able to see up here,
“ From in the marsh Enki is able to see up here,
“ And he will set eyes on you."


Tablet III:


Enki was digging dykes. He was digging canals from the marsh. He laid eyes on Uttu, the exalted woman. Enki was able to see up there from in the marsh. 


He was able to see up there.


First he put his feet in the boat, next he put them on dry land. He directed his steps to the dwelling of Uttu and said:


" Have I ever kissed one like you, nice youngster?
“ Have I ever made love to one like you, nice Uttu ?" 


Uttu replied: 


" Bring cucumbers on their vine, 
“ Bring apples with their stems sticking out, 
“ Bring grapes in their clusters, 
“ And in the house you will indeed have hold of my halter, 
“ O’ Enki, you will indeed have hold of my halter."


When he was filling with water a second time, he filled the dykes with water, he filled the canals with water, he filled the fallows with water. The gardener in his joy rose from the dust and embraced him: 


" Who are you? 
“  Who are you, who cultivates the garden?"


Enki said to Abu the gardener:


“ I am brother of the Enlil, Sage of the Gods


“ From your fields I require cucumbers on their vines
“ From your fields I require apples with their stems sticking out
“ From your fields I require grapes in their clusters”


He brought him cucumbers on their vines. He brought him apples with their stems sticking out. He brought him grapes in their clusters. He filled his lap; Abu filled his lap with fruits.


He changed his face. Enki made his face attractive and took a staff in his hand. Enki came to a halt at Uttu's door, knocked at her house demanding: 


" Open up, open up." 


She asked: 


" Who are you?" 


He answered: 


" I am a gardener.
“ Let me give you cucumbers, apples, and grapes for your consent." 


Joyfully Uttu opened the house. Enki gave Uttu, the exalted woman, cucumbers on their vines. He gave her apples with their stems sticking out, and he gave her grapes in their clusters. He poured beer for her in the large ban measure.


Uttu, the exalted  woman, moved to the left for him. She waved the hands for him to enter.


 Enki aroused Uttu. He clasped her to the bosom. Lying in her crotch, he fondled her thighs, fondled her with the hand. 


He clasped her to the bosom, lying in her crotch, Enki made love to the youngster and kissed her. He poured semen into Uttu's womb and she conceived the semen in the womb, the semen of Enki.


Uttu, the beautiful woman, cried out: 


" Woe, my thighs." 


She cried out: 


" Woe, my body. 
“ Woe, my heart." 


Ninḫursaĝa heard the cries of Uttu, and went to see what ailed the youngster. She removed the semen from the thighs, and cast it to the ground.


From the semen of Enki, she grew the reed plant, she grew the sweet plant, she grew the u-jec plant, she grew the esparto grass, she grew the atutu plant, she grew the aštaltal plant, she grew the sajkal plant, she grew the amḫaru plant.


Enki was able to see up there from in the marsh, he was able to see up there, he was. He said to his minister Isimud: 


" I have not determined the destiny of these plants. 
“ What is this one? What is that one?"


His minister Isimud had the answer for him. 


" My master, the reed plant," 


He cut it off for him and Enki ate it. 


" My master, the the sweet plant," 


He pulled it up for him and Enki ate it. 


" My master, the u-jec plant," 


He cut it off for him and Enki ate it. 


" My master, the alfalfa grass," 


He pulled it up for him and Enki ate it.


" My master, the atutu plant," 


He cut it off for him and Enki ate it. 


" My master, the aštaltal plant," 


He pulled it up for him and Enki ate it. 


" My master, the sajkal plant," 


He cut it off for him and Enki ate it. 


" My master, the amḫaru plant," 


He pulled it up for him and Enki ate it. 


Enki determined the destiny of the plants, and had them know it in their hearts.


Tablet IIII:






Ninḫursaĝa cursed the name Enki: 


" Until his dying day, I will never look upon him with the life-giving eye."


At that time, Enki began to grow sick, and sat down in the dust. He sent a fox. And the fox carried word for Enki to his brother in Nibru. 


The Anuna sat down in the dust. But the fox was able to speak to Enlil: 


" If I bring Ninḫursaĝa to you, what will be my reward?" 


Enlil answered the fox: 


" If you bring Ninḫursaĝa to me, I shall erect two birch trees for you in my city and you will be renowned."


The fox first anointed his body, first shook out his fur, first put kohl on his eyes. He directed his step with Utu’s guidance and found Ninḫursaĝa sleep under a mighty cedar in the Garden of the Gods.


The fox said to Ninḫursaĝa: 


" I have been to Nibru, but Enlil would not help in this matter
“ I have been to Urim, but Nanna had his temple doors bolted shut
“ I have been to Larsam, but Utu was travelling by day 
“ I have been to Unug, but Inana was preparing to hunt
“ I am seeking refuge with one who is kind and gentle to assist me in this matter"


Ninḫursaĝa rose to her feet. She directed her steps toward the city. She followed the fox to Dilmun, where she found Enki. 


Knowing what she had done, her heart took pity.


Ninḫursaĝa hastened to the temple. The Anuna slipped off her garment, made the goddess resplendent with a staff in hand, determined its destiny and sent her away. 


Ninḫursaĝa made Enki sit by her vagina. She placed her hands on his hand and kissed Enki’s head on its outside.


Ninḫursaĝa asked: 


" My brother, what part of you hurts you?"


 " The ugu-dili hurts me." 
" The top of my head hurts me." 


She gave birth to Abuttu out of it. 


" My brother, what part of you hurts you?" 


" The siki hurt me." 
" The locks of my hair hurt me." 


She gave birth to Ninsikila out of it. 


" My brother, what part of you hurts you?" 


" My giri hurts me." 
" My nose hurts me." 


She gave birth to Ningiritud out of it. 


" My brother, what part of you hurts you?" 


" My ka hurts me." 
" My mouth hurts me." 


She gave birth to Ninkasi out of it.


" My brother, what part of you hurts you?" 


" My zi hurts me." 
" My throat hurts me." 


She gave birth to Nazi out of it. 


" My brother, what part of you hurts you?" 


" My a hurts me." 
" My arm hurts me." 


She gave birth to Azimua out of it. 


" My brother, what part of you hurts you?" 


" My ti hurt me." 
" My ribs hurt me." 


She gave birth to Ninti out of it. 


" My brother, what part of you hurts you?"
 
" My zag hurt me." 
" My sides hurt me." 


She gave birth to Ensag out of it.


She then said: 


" For the little ones to whom I have given birth may rewards not be lacking. 
“ Ab-u shall become king of the grasses, 
“ Ninsikila shall become lord of Magan, 
“ Ningiritud shall marry Ninazu, 
“ Ninkasi shall be what satisfies the heart, 
“ Nanshe shall marry Nindara, 
“ Azimua shall marry Ninĝišzida,
“ Ninti shall become the lady of the month,
“ And Ensag shall become lord of Dilmun."




Chapter 3 


Enlil in the E-kur:


Enlil's commands are by far the loftiest. His words are holy, his utterances are immutable. The fate he decides is everlasting. His glance makes the mountains anxious, his stare reaches into the interior of the mountains. 


The Holy Urac was favoured with beauty for Enlil. As he was greatly suited for the Abzu, the holy throne, the E-ngur, he refreshed himself in the deep underworld, the holy chamber. 


All the gods of the earth bowed down to father Enlil, who sat comfortably on the holy dais, the lofty dais of E-ngur. Nunamnir, whose lordship and princeship are most perfect, sat in his holy dais. 


The Anuna gods enter before him. 


They obey his instructions faithfully.


The mighty lord, the greatest in heaven and earth, the knowledgeable judge, the wise one of wide-ranging wisdom, took his seat in the Dur-an-ki. He made the Ki-ur, the great place, resplendent with majesty. He took up residence in Nibru, the lofty bond between heaven and earth. 


The front of the city is laden with terrible radiant fearsomeness. Its back is such that even the mightiest god does not dare to attack. Its interior is the blade of a sharp dagger. A blade of catastrophe. For the rebel lands, it is a snare, a trap, a net.


It cuts short the life of those who speak too mightily. It permits no evil word to be spoken in judgement. It forbids deception, inimical speech, hostility, impropriety, ill-treatment, wickedness, wrongdoing, looking askance, violence, slandering, arrogance, licentious speech, egotism and boasting. These are abominations not tolerated within the city.


The borders of Nibru form a great net, within which the hurin-eagle spreads wide its talons. Neither wicked nor evil man escape its grasp. In this city endowed with steadfastness, for which righteousness and justice have been made a lasting possession, and which is clothed in pure clothing on the quay, the younger brother honours the older brother and treats him with dignity. People pay attention to a father's word, and submit themselves to his protection. The child behaves humbly and modestly towards his mother and attains a ripe old age.


In the city, the holy settlement of Enlil, the beloved shrine of the Great Mountain Father; he has made the dais of abundance, the E-kur, shining temple, rise from the soil. 


He has made it grow on pure land as high as a towering mountain. 


Its prince, the Great Mountain, Father Enlil, has taken his seat. No god can cause harm to the temple's divine powers. Its holy hand-washing rites are everlasting like the earth. Its divine powers are the divine powers of the Abzu; none can look upon them.


Its interior is a wide sea which knows no horizon. In its shrine, glistening as a banner, the bonds and ancient divine powers are made perfect. Its words are prayers, its incantations are supplications. Its word is a favourable omen, its rites are most precious. 


Daily there is a great festival, and at the end of the day there is an abundant harvest. At the festivals, there is plenty of fat and cream; they are full of abundance. Its divine plans bring joy and rejoicing, its verdicts are great. The temple of Enlil is a mountain of abundance; to reach out, to look with greedy eyes, to seize are abominations in it.


The lagar priests of this temple whose lord has grown together with it are expert in blessing. Its gudu priests of the Abzu are suited for Enlil’s lustration rites. Its nuec priests are perfect in the holy prayers. Its great farmer is the good shepherd of the land, who was born vigorous on a propitious day. The farmer, suited for the broad fields, comes with rich offerings; he does not go into the shining E-kur.


When the holy settlements were marked out, Nibru was also built. The Ki-ur, the mountain, the pure place, was founded in the Duran-ki; in the middle of the four quarters of the earth. 


Its soil is the life of the land, and the life of all the foreign countries. Its brickwork is red gold, its foundation is lapis lazuli. It glistened on high, as Enlil had raised its glistening top in Sumer as if it were the horns of a wild bull. 


It makes all the foreign countries tremble with fear. 


At its great festivals, the people pass their time in abundance.


Enlil’s presence spread awesomeness over the E-kur. Its fearsomeness and radiance reached up to heaven. Its shadow stretched over all the foreign lands, and its crenellation reached up to the midst of heaven. All lords and sovereigns regularly supplied holy offerings there.


Enlil looked upon the shepherd favourably; elevating the one truly called in the land. Then, the foreign countries were in his hands; the foreign countries were at his feet. Even the most distant foreign countries would submit to him. 


He caused enormous incomes and heavy tributes, as if they were cool water, to reach the treasury. In the great courtyard, the shepard supplied offerings regularly. Into the E-kur, the shining temple, he brought prayer and supplication.


Enlil, faithful shepherd of the teeming multitudes, herdsman, leader of all living creatures, has manifested his rank of great prince, adorning himself with the holy crown. As the wind of the mountain occupied the dais, he spanned the sky as the rainbow. Like a floating cloud, he moved alone.


He alone is the prince of heaven, the dragon of the earth. The lofty god of the Anuna himself determines the fates. No god can look upon him. 


Enlil’s great minister and commander Nuska learned his command and his intentions from him, consulted with him, and then executed his far-reaching instructions on his behalf. He prayed to him with holy prayers and the wise son of Gibil was granted divine powers.


Without the Great Mountain Enlil, no city would be built, no settlement would be founded; no cow-pen would be built, no sheepfold would be established; no king would be elevated, no lord would be given birth; no high priest or priestess would perform extispicy; soldiers would have no generals or captains; no carp-filled waters would flood the rivers at their peak; 


The carp would not come straight up from the sea, they would not dart about. The sea would not produce all its heavy treasure, no freshwater fish would lay eggs in the reedbeds, no bird of the sky would build nests in the spacious land; 


In the sky the thick clouds would not open their mouths on the fields, dappled grain would not fill the arable lands, vegetation would not grow lushly on the plain; in the gardens, the spreading forests of the mountain would not yield fruits.


Without the Great Mountain, Enlil, Nintud would not kill. She would not strike dead. 


No cow would drop its calf in the cattle-pen, no ewe would bring forth its lamb in its sheepfold. The living creatures which multiply by themselves would not lie down in their fields. The four-legged animals would not propagate, they would not mate.


Enlil’s ingenuity would take one's breath away. By its nature it was like entangled threads which cannot be unravelled. Crossed threads which the eye cannot follow. His divinity was relied on. He was his own counsellor and adviser. 


He was a lord on his own. 


Who could comprehend his actions? 


No divine powers were as resplendent. No god could look him in the face.


Enlil, lord, god, and king, judged and made decisions about heaven and earth. His lofty word was as heavy as heaven, and there was no one who could lift it. The Anuna gods gleamed at his word. 


It was weighty in heaven, it was a great mountain, reaching up to the sky. It was a foundation on the earth, a foundation which could not be destroyed. When it related to the heavens, it brought abundance; and, abundance poured from the heavens. When it related to the earth, it brought prosperity; and, the earth produced prosperity. 


His word meant flax, his word meant grain. His word meant the early flooding, the life of the lands. It made the living creatures, the animals which copulate and breathe joyfully in the greenery. Enlil, the good shepherd, knew their ways. 


He ordered the sparkling stars.


Enlil married Ninlil, the holy consort, whose words were of the heart. Her of noble countenance in a holy ma-garment. Her of beautiful shape and limbs, the trustworthy lady, covered with allure. 


The lady who knows what is fitting for the E-kur. She whose words of advice are perfect, whose words bring comfort like fine oil for the heart. She who shares seat on the holy throne, the pure throne with Enlil. 


She took counsel and discussed matters with Enlil. They decided the fates together at the place facing the sunrise. Ninlil, the Lady of Heaven and Earth, the Lady of All the Lands, is honoured in the praise of the Great Mountain.


Prominent one whose words are well established, whose command and support are things which are immutable, whose utterances take precedence, whose plans are firm words, Great Mountain, Father Enlil, your praise is sublime.


Dilimbabbar’s Journey To Nibru:


The heroic Nanna fixed his mind on the city of his mother. Suen fixed his mind on the city of his father. Acimbabbar fixed his mind on the city of Enlil and Ninlil. 


The hero set off for his city, the shining city, the pure place. He set off for the city of kings, the City of Heaven. Its walls were very great, its canals were very great, its shrines were very great, and its temple was very great.


Suen said to himself:


" My Nibru, where black birch trees grow in a good place, 
“ My sanctuary Nibru,


“ Nibru's shrine is built in a good place. 
“ The sanctuary Nibru's name is a good name. 
“ My Nibru shrine is built on a good foundation. 


“ Before Dilmun existed, palm trees grew in my city. 
“ Before Dilmun existed, when the great mother Ninlil was clothed in fine linen."


Suen, then, set about constructing a barge and sent for reed matting. He despatched people to Tummal for the barge's reeds. Acimbabbar despatched people to the Abzu for the barge's pitch. Nanna despatched people to Duacaga for its rushes. 


Acimbabbar despatched people to the cypress forest for its strakes. Nanna-Suen despatched people to the forests of Kug-nuna for its ribbing. Acimbabbar despatched people to the Mountain of Fragrant Cedar for its beams.


Suen despatched people to the forests of Ebla for its planking. Nanna despatched people to the Fragrant Cedar Forest for its fir wood. Acimbabbar despatched people to the junipers of Langi.
 
Nanna-Suen despatched people to the mound.


When all the items for the barge were all brought forth from the lands, Gibil rejoiced at him. Utu rejoiced at him and affixed his splendour to the bow.


Nanna-Suen gathered bulls for the cow-pen of the house of Enlil. Acimbabbar collected fattened sheep for the house of Enlil. Nanna-Suen purified cows for the cow-pen for the house of Enlil. Acimbabbar fed meal to the goats for the house of Enlil. Nanna-Suen gathered porcupines for the house of Enlil.


Acimbabbar gathered long-tailed bush rats for the house of Enlil. Nanna-Suen collected  little kuda-birds for the house of Enlil. Acimbabbar brought small ubi-birds from the pond for the house of Enlil. Nanna-Suen brought small azagun-birds from the sea for the house of Enlil.


Acimbabbar gathered suhur carp for the house of Enlil. Nanna-Suen collected ectub-carp for the house of Enlil. Acimbabbar poured the oil of rushes onto the water for the house of Enlil. Nanna-Suen filled baskets with eggs for the house of Enlil. Acimbabbar caused old reed and fresh reed to thrive for the house of Enlil. 


Suen caused six hundred ewes to give birth to lambs for the house of Enlil, for he let loose their rams to be among them. Acimbabbar caused six hundred she-goats to give birth to kids for the house of Enlil, for he let loose their bucks to be among them. 


Nanna-Suen caused six hundred cows to give birth to calves for the house of Enlil, for he let loose their bulls to be among them, and they were distributed along the banks of the Id-surungal.


E-negir lay ahead of the offerings, Urim lay behind them. Ningirida brought out of the house, what should not come out of the house. 


" Welcome, welcome, welcome O’ boat, O’, boat of Suen!"


She laid out flour before the barge and spread bran. At her feet stood a covered bronze gakkul. She pulled out the boxwood bung for him, declaring:


" I shall rub precious oil on this peg. 
“ May ghee, syrup and wine be abundant in your midst, 
“ May the suhur carp and the ectub carp rejoice at the prow of your boat!" 


But the boat did not give her its cargo.


Suen said:


" I am going to Nibru!"


Larsa lay ahead of the offerings, E-negir lay behind them. The lovely Sherida brought out of the house, what should not come out of the house.


" Welcome, O’ boat of my father, welcome! welcome O’ boat!"


She laid out flour before the barge and spread bran.


Suen replied:        


" I am going to Nibru!"


Unug lay ahead of the offerings, Larsa lay behind them. Holy Inanna brought out of the house what should not come out of the house.


” O’ boat of Suen welcome! welcome O’ boat!"


At her feet stood a covered bronze gakkul. 


Suen declared:


" I am going to Nibru!"


Shuruppak lay ahead of the offerings, Unug lay behind them. Ninunuga brought out of the house, what should not come out of the house: 


" O’ boat of Suen welcome, welcome O’ boat!"


With her fingers she pulled out the boxwood bung for him.


Tummal lay ahead of the offerings, Shuruppak lay behind them. The fair Ninlil brought out of the house, what should not come out of the house: 


" Welcome, O’ boat of the princely son welcome!
" I shall rub precious oil on this peg. 
“ May ghee, syrup and wine be abundant in your midst, 
“ May the suhur carp and the ectub carp rejoice at the prow of your boat!" 


Nibru lay ahead of the offerings, Tummal lay behind them. At the Shining Quay, the White Quay, the quay of Enlil, Nanna-Suen-Dilimbabbar finally docked the boat. He stepped up to the cultic building of his father who begot him, and called out to the porter of his father’s house.


At the door Suen called:


" Open the house, Kalkal! 
“ Kalkal, doorkeeper, open the house! 


" I, Nanna-Suen, have gathered bulls for the cow-pen for the house of Enlil; 
“ I, Acimbabbar, have collected fattened sheep for the house of Enlil; 
“ I shall purify the cow-pen for the house of Enlil; porter, 
“ I shall feed meal to the goats for the house of Enlil
“ I have obtained porcupines for the house of Enlil


" I, Acimbabbar, have come
“ Open the house!


“  I have long-tailed bush-rats for the house of Enlil,
“ I, Nanna-Suen, have gathered little kuda birds for the house of Enlil;
“ I have brought small ubi birds from the pond for the house of Enlil;
“ I have brought small azagun birds from the pond for the house of Enlil; 
" I have gathered suhur carp for the house of Enlil


“ Porter, open the house.


“ I, Nanna-Suen, have collected ectub carp for the house of Enlil,
“ I shall pour the oil of rushes onto the water for the house of Enlil,        
” I have filled baskets with eggs for the house of Enlil,
“ I, Acimbabbar, have caused old reed and fresh reed to thrive for the house of Enlil;


“ Open the house;


" I have caused six hundred ewes to give birth to lambs for the house of Enlil, 
“ For I have caused their rams to be let loose among them, 
“ I have caused six hundred she-goats to give birth to kids for the house of Enlil, 
“ For I have caused their bucks to be let loose among them, 
“ I have caused six hundred cows to give birth to calves for the house of Enlil, 
“ For I have caused their bulls to be let loose among them, 
“ I have distributed them along the banks of the Id-surungal; 


“ Porter, open the house!
“ Kalkal, open the house! 


“ I will give you that which is in the prow of the boat as a first offering, 
“ And I will give you that which is in the stern of the boat as a last offering."


Kalkal took charge of the bolt-handle. Rejoicing, the porter Kalkal, the doorkeeper, opened the house. 


Nanna-Suen made the offerings. 


Enlil, rejoicing over the offerings, offered bread to Suen, his son.


Enlil rejoiced over Suen and spoke kindly: 


" Give sweet cakes to my little fellow, Nanna, who loves eating sweet cakes. 
“ Bring out from the E-kur the bread allotment and first quality bread for him. 
“ Pour out for him the finest beer, and my purest water
“ May the greatness of the towering tilimda-vessels,
“ Standing on the ground, provide pure water 
“ Order pure sweet cake, syrup, crescent cake and clear water for him."


Suen replied to his father who begot him:


" Father who begot me, 
“ I am indeed satisfied with what you have given me to eat. 


“ O’ Great Mountain, 
“ I am indeed satisfied with what you have given me to drink. 


“ Wherever you lift your eyes, there is kingship. 
“ O’ Enlil, your abundance is divine
“ I want to set off for Urim!


" Give to me, Enlil;


“ In the river give me the carp-flood,
“ In the fields give me speckled barley, 
“ In the marshes give me kuda and suhur carp, 
“ In the reedbeds give me old reed and fresh reed, 
“ In the forests give me the ibex and wild ram,
“ In the high plain, give me the macgurum tree, 
“ In the orchards give me syrup and wine,
“ In the palace give me long life,
“ I want to set off for Urim!” 


Enlil gave to him, and he set off for Urim.
Inanna and Dumuzi:


Tablet I:


The righteous man filled the bowls for the lord of the storehouse, the Great Mountain Enlil. The righteous man filled the bowls for the lady of the storehouse, great mother Ninlil. The righteous man filled the bowls for the youthful Utu, lord of the mountain. The righteous man filled the bowls for Cerida, youthful leader of battle. The righteous man filled the bowls for the Enki and Ninki deities, Enmul and Ninmul.


He, the shepherd, called out:


“ Unto distant days; indeed forever,
“ Stand by the righteous man who gives you bread, 
“ O’ exalted one, elalu!” 


“ Unto distant days, indeed forever,
“ Stand by the most righteous of men who give you bread
“ O’ lord of the storehouse, Great Mountain Enlil,
“ O’ lady of the storehouse, great mother Ninlil,
“ O’ youthful Utu, lord of the mountain,
“ O’ Cerida, youthful leader of battle,
“ O’ exalted ones, elalu!”


“ May the righteous man have a long life, indeed forever,
“ May the most righteous of men have a long life, indeed forever,
“ Stand by him, O’ exalted one, elalu!


“ Auamma, in the house, ulili, 
“ Alliliamma, In the house, ulili!” 


He poured out beer for him. He poured out liquor for him. The minister poured out liquor for the lord. 


Nuska poured out liquor for Enlil. He gave the beer to drink. He gave the liquor to refresh themselves. 


Nuska said to Enlil:


“ O’ lord, eat and drink!,
“ May it be acceptable to you!


“ O’ Enlil, eat and drink, 
“ May it please you!


“ As you ate, flax came into being, grain came into being.
“ As you drank, early floods came into being; early floods in the rivers.
“ As you ate, mottled grain came into being in the fields. 
“ Accept what the righteous man has brought to you,
“ Accept the flour that the most righteous man has brought to you,
“ He paid homage to you.
“ He says to you 


“" Eat!"”


“ And he says to you 


“" Drink!"”


The beer for the bur-gia-offerings had been filled to overflowing, among the offerings of the house; the place where the huge bowls stood under the heavens. Bread had been offered by pure hands, at the house where the righteous man offered prayers.


When the most righteous of men offered prayers, the god of the man offered prayers. Lord Enki offered prayers. The father offered prayers. There the righteous man filled the beer for the bur-gi-offerings to overflowing.


Nuska said to Enlil:


“ The righteous man, the most righteous of men; has filled them to overflowing,
“ The god of the man has offered prayers,
“ The beer for the bur-gia-offerings is overflowing.


“ O’ lord of the storehouse, Great Mountain Enlil,
“ He has filled them to overflowing


“ O’ lady of the storehouse, great mother Ninlil,
“ He has filled them to overflowing. 


“ The most righteous of men has filled the bowls with your beer.
“ This bronze vessel increased his long life.” 


When Nibru had been fully built, when his work had been completed; when the brickwork of this house had been laid, the living spouse entered. The seed of the house came forth. Utu came forth from the house.


Everyone set their gaze upon him, when Utu came forth. They gazed upon him, the wild bull of the E-babbar, the bearded one, son born to Ningal. They gazed upon him, as he emerged from below and gazed upwards.


There was Utu, great physician, father of the black-headed; wearing a lapis-lazuli beard in the E-babbar. 


Utu, great hero. 


The focus of the assembly, king, bison running over the mountains. 


Utu, bison running over the mountains!


A young wild cow, a young gazelle caught in a trap. Utu, the son born within the city; born to Ningal in the E-nun-ana, a bull, a cedar fed with water thriving among cypresses, possessing holy light, and patient-hearted. Playful, radiating light, he was iridescent radiance.


Utu came forth, and the heavens trembled before him. The earth shook before him. He left the palace, and travelled. 


The heavens rejoiced. 


The bolt of heaven opened for him. The stars, in his presence, were awe-struck. His mother rejoiced in the streets, as she spread her protection towards Utu. 


He has raised his head over the mountains, as their king.


Utu placed the diadem on his head to decree judgments for all countries. The lord, the son of Nanna and Ningal, rose to render decisions for all countries. The lord who is highly skilled at verdicts, the son of Enlil, was highly knowledgeable and majestic. 


The son of Ningal, held the 50 divine powers in his hand and thundered over the mountains like a storm. He lifted his head over the land, he lifted his gaze over the mountains, and crossed all the shining mountains like an eagle..


Nuska spoke to the youthful Utu: 


“ Hero of Justice, calf of the Wild Cow,
“ Calf of the righteous son


“ Utu, royal brother of Inana, 
“ He who brings thirst to streets and paths


“ Youthful Utu, he of the tavern has provided beer for you. 


“ When you gaze upon the bulls in the cattle-pen
“ Let bulls fill the cattle-pen


“ When you gaze upon the sheep in the fold
“ Let sheep fill the fold


“ When you have gazed upon the man, most righteous; 
“ Then, you will rest on your travels.
“ From the grain, the priests will gather the desirable malt for you
“ From the field, the priests will gather small birds for you


“ May the lord eat this produce
“ May the youthful hero, Utu, drink


“ For, whoever has eaten good bread, has also drunk good beer; 


“ May youthful Utu provide food to eat, for those who venture forth single-handed,
“ May he give beer to drink, for those who venture forth from a man's house
“  As for the orphans, Utu, as for the widows you succour them as their mother
“ And you are their father”


* A cir-namcub to Utu
Tablet II:
Inana, holy daughter of Suen, carried the splendour of beauty and captured the eyes of many suitors.The shepard Dumuzi, son of Ea was among her many suitors. The time had come for Inanna to be paired, though she had yet to decide who to marry, preparations were already underway for the ceremony. 


Utu walked with his sister in the fields of flax. Utu spoke gently to his sister, he spoke tenderly to holy Inana: 


" Young lady, the flax in the garden beds is full of loveliness,
“ Like the barley in the furrows, it is overflowing with loveliness and delight.


“ Inana, you took a fancy to a grand length of linen.
“ I will dig up the plants for you and give them to you.
 
“ My sister, I will bring you flax from the garden beds.


Inana spoke to the god Utu, saying:


“ My brother, when you have brought me flax from the garden beds,
“ Who will ret it for me? 
“ Who will ret that flax for me?”


And, Utu answered:


" My sister, I will bring it to you already retted”


" Who will spin it for me?”


“ Inana, I will bring it to you already spun”


“ Who will twine that flax for me?”


" My sister, I will bring it to you already twined”


“ Who will warp it for me?“


“ Inana, I will bring it to you already warped”


“ Who will weave that flax for me?”


" My sister, I will bring it to you already woven”


“ Who will bleach it for me? 
“ Who will bleach that linen for me?”


“ Inana, I will bring it to you already bleached"


Inana asked Utu:


“ Brother, after you’ve brought my bridal sheet to me,
“ Who will go to bed with me? 
“ Utu, who will lie down on it with me?
“ Who will lie down on that linen with me?"


Utu answered Inanna, saying:


“ There shall lie down with you, your bridegroom, 


“ Ama-ušumgal-ana shall lie down with you, 
“ The companion of Enlil shall lie down with you,
“ The issue of a noble womb shall lie down with you,
“ The offspring of a ruler shall lie down with you.


“ Sister, he was born from a fertile womb, 
“ He was conceived on the sacred marriage throne,
“ Dumuzi the Shepherd will go to bed with you, 
“ He will be your bridegroom.
“ He will go to bed with you.”


Inana spoke:


“ No, brother, the farmer, he is the man of my heart, 
“ The man of my heart works the hoe.
“ He gathers the grain into great heaps,
“ And he brings the grain regularly into my storehouses.”


Utu spoke:


“ Why are you unwilling? 
“ Sister, marry the shepherd. His cream is good; his milk is good. 
“ Whatever he touches shines brightly.


“ Inana, marry Dumuzi.
“ You, who wear the cuba jewels,
“ Why are you unwilling? 


“ Dumuzi will share his rich cream with you. 
“ He will eat his good butter with you. 


“ You, the king’s protector, 
“ Why are you unwilling?”


Inana spoke:


“ The shepherd, 
“ I will not marry the shepherd! 
“ His clothes are coarse; his wool is rough. 
“ He shall not make me carry his garments of new wool.


“ I will marry the farmer. 
“ The farmer grows colourful, lovely, flax for my clothes. 
“ The farmer grows barley for my table.”


Utu was unwilling to quarrel with his sister. He told Dumuzi of this news. Unhappy with Inana’s reply, Dumuzi went to confront the young goddess.


He spoke to Inana:


“ Why do you speak about the farmer
“ Enkimdu, the man of the dykes and canals?
“ In what, is that farmer superior to me? 


“ Let him give me his black garment, and I will give the farmer my black ewe for it
“ Let him give me his white garment, and I will give the farmer my white ewe for it.
“ Let him pour me his best beer, and I will pour the farmer my yellow milk for it.
“ Let him pour me his fine beer, and I will pour the farmer my curdled milk for it.
“ Let him pour me his brewed beer, and I will pour the farmer my whipped milk for it.
" Let him give me his best filtered beer, and I will give the farmer my curds.
“ Let him give me his little beans, and I will give the farmer my small cheeses for them.  
“ Let him give me his large beans, and I will give the farmer my big cheeses for them.


“ After letting him eat and letting him drink, I will even leave extra butter for him,
“ And, I will leave extra milk for him. 


“ In what, is the farmer superior to me?”


The shepherd left Inana to decide who would be her bridegroom. 


Now, the shepherd pastured the sheep on the riverbank. He was cheerful, at the edge of the riverbank; as she watched on. Enkimdu, the farmer, came to the shepherd Dumizi, there on the river bank. He traveled from the plains after a quarrel had been provoked between them.


Enkimdu spoke to Dumuzi:


" Why should I compete against you? 
“ Why am I set against you, shepherd?
“ Let your sheep eat the grass of the riverbank, 
“ Let your sheep graze on my stubble. 
“ Let them eat grain in the shimmering fields of Unug, 
“ Let your kids and lambs drink water from my Surungal canal. 


Dumuzi gave his reply:


“ When I am married, farmer, you are going to be counted as my friend. 
“ Farmer Enkimdu, you are going to be counted as my friend."
                        
The two came into agreement.


Enkimdu spoke:


" When you marry, I will bring you wheat, and I will bring you beans; 
“ I will bring you two-row barley from the threshing-floor. 
“ And you, maiden, I will bring you whatever you please, maiden Inanna!
“ Bread and barley or beer and beans."


With that, the dispute between the farmer and the shepherd was quashed. Inana met her betrothed, but again there was quarrel.


Inana made her voice heard. To Dumuzi she addressed her words:
