“ Shepherd, without my mother, Ningal, 
“ Without my grandmother, Ningikuga,
“ Without my father, Nanna, 
“ Without my brother, Utu,
” You’d be chasing me along the dark paths of the steppe”


He stopped her words. Dumuzi spoke:


“ Inanna, do not start a quarrel.
“ I am just as good as Utu,
“ Ninegala, let us discuss it together, 
“ My father, Ea, is as good as your father, Suen
“ My grandfather, Enki, is as good as your grandfather, Enlil
“ My mother, Sirtur, is as good as your mother, Ningal
“ My sister, Geshtinanna, is as good as yours
“ Queen of the palace, let us talk it over


“ I am as good as Utu,
“ Ninegala, let us sit and speak together.”


-


The words they spoke were words towards desire; provoking a quarrel was the desire of the heart.


Tablet III:
Ningal addressed Inana:


“ Lady, you who wander among sweet-voiced cows
“ And gentle-voiced calves in the cattle-pen;
“ Young woman, no sooner will you arrive here than the churn should sound,
“ May the churn of your spouse sound, 
“ Inana, may the churn of Dumuzid sound.
“ The rocking of the churn will sing for you, Inana, thus making you joyous
“ Ninegala, the good shepherd, the man of sweet songs, will loudly sing songs for you; 
“ Inana, lady with the sweetest songs, may he make your heart joyous!
“ For the king you have chosen with your heart, for Dumuzid, the son of Enlil,
“ May the cattle-pen produce butter and milk,
“ May the sheepfold produce abundance,
“ May the days of the true shepherd be numerous! 


“ Ninegala, your wedding ushers are lords,
“  Inana, your ushers are lords! 
“ The fowler of the fields is first, 
“ And the fisherman from the depths of the reed-beds is second.


Inana spoke:


" I will send a messenger to the shepherd:
“ Let him treat me to the best butter and the best milk! 
“ I will send a messenger to my farmer, 
“ Let him treat me to beer and wine. 
“ I  will send a messenger to the fowler, whose bird-nets are spread out,
“ Let him treat me to fine birds.
“ I, Inana, will send a messenger to the fisherman, too, 
“ Whose nets are set up in the reed-beds, let him treat me to fat carp."


The messenger went, and spoke the words of Inana, his queen: 


“ While I, the lady Inana, was passing the day yesterday, dancing, 
“ While I was singing songs all day until evening, he met me,
“ The lord, the friend of An, met me;
“ The lord took me in his hands, Ucumgal-ana embraced me about my neck:


“ Friend of Enlil, let me go, so that I can go to my house! 
“ What lie can I offer to my mother? What lie can I offer to my mother Ningal?
“ And you said:


“" Let me teach you!
“” Inana, let me teach you the lies of women: 
“" My girlfriend was dancing with me in the square. 
“” She ran around playfully with me, banging the drum. 
“” She sang her sweet songs for me.
“” I passed the day there with her in pleasure and delight." 
“” Offer this as a lie to your own mother. 


“” As for us, let me make love with you by moonlight,
“” Let me loosen your combs on the holy and luxuriant couch. 
“” May you pass a sweet day there with me in voluptuous pleasure."”


“ My own mother Ningal, gave birth to me for your sake.
“ My beloved of heart, may Ucumgal-ana come to me , and I will rejoice over him.
“ May Dumuzid come to me, and I will rejoice over him.
“ Let us embrace, my bridegroom,
“ Come, let us rejoice in play!
“ Let us embrace, my Ama-ucum,
“ Friend of An, lord, my heart's desire, cheering the mood, gladdening the heart:
“ May you be our shining god!


" You found favour with Enlil, and my father, Suen, chose you in his heart. 
“ As do I, myself, chose you in my heart;
“ He has placed on your head for me the good headdress and the holy crown.
“ You shall stand in service before the great gods,
“ The Anuna gods who shine forth radiantly. 
“ Let us embrace, my bridegroom, let us lie on my flowered bed.”


The ushers brought the gifts. The fowler brought choice birds, and the fisherman brought fat carp. The shepherd went to the royal house with butter in his hands, Dumuzid went to the royal house with milk on his shoulders. Before the door, he called out: 


“ Open the house, my lady!
“ Open the house!”


Inana ran to Ningal, the mother who bore her, who counselled her daughter, saying: 


“ My child, the young man will be your father. 
“ My daughter, the young man will be your mother. 
“ He will treat you like a father. 
“ He will care for you like a mother. 


Before the door, Dumuzid called out: 


“ Open the house, my lady!
“ Open the house!”


Inana, at her mother’s command, bathed and anointed herself with scented oil. She covered her body with the royal white robe. She readied her dowry. She arranged her precious lapis beads around her neck. She took her seal in her hand.


Utu came into the presence of Inana and asked: 


" My sister, what have you been doing in the house? 


She replied: 


" I was bathing,
“ I was washing myself with water from the holy kettle,
“ I was rubbing myself with the soap from the white stone bowl.
“ I was anointing myself with good oil from the stone bowl,
“ And dressing myself in the formal dress proper to Inana.
“ That is how I was busying myself in the house.
" I have put lots of kohl on my eyes,
“ I have arranged the lapis beads on the nape of my neck.
“ And I have put a golden bracelet on my wrist.
“ I have washed my dangling hair,
“ And I have tested my weapons that make his reign propitious.
“ I have straightened my tousled head of hair, I have combed out my untied locks,
“ And let them fall down the back of my neck.”


Utu, then, said:


" Sister, I will bring you whatever you desire.
“ I will bring the loving heart of your heart.
“ Your goddess has given you good looks.
“ Sister, shining bright, you are the honey of your own mother.
“ My sister, to whom I shall bring five things,
“ My sister, to whom I shall bring ten things,
“ Sister, she has perfected your appearance for you; 
“ Shining bright, she has really made it a delight."


Inana replied:


" Let him bring pats of butter and cream,
" Let him bring, let him bring me lambs like ewes
“ Let him bring, let him bring me kids like goats.
" Let the lambs be as comely as ewes,
" Let the kids be as fine as goats!" 


Utu cried aloud:


"My sister, I will bring them with me to the house!"


Inana went, saying to herself:


" When my brother enters from the palace, the singers shall rejoice
“ And I shall pour wine into his mouth. 
“That should gladden and please his heart.
“ See now, my breasts stand out; 
“ See now, hair has grown on my genitals, 
“ Signifying my progress to the embrace of a man.
“ Let us be very glad and dance,
“ O’ Bau, let us be very glad about my genitals,
“ Later on it will delight him!”


Tablet IIII:


“ There he is, standing at our mother's gate, while I am rushing around in excitement. 
“ Oh that someone would tell my mother! 
“ May our neighbour come to sprinkle water on the floor,
“ The fragrance of her dwelling is pleasant, and her words are delightful.


“ My lord is perfect for the holy embrace. Ama-ucumgal-ana, the son-in-law of Suen,
“ Lord Dumuzid, is perfect for the holy embrace, son-in-law of Suen,
“ My lord: how pleasing is your lavishness, 
“ And how sweet-tasting are your herbs and plants from the plain!
“ O’ Ama-ucumgal-ana”


Utu, The burgeoning one, walked with his own mother.  Dumuzi, with kindly eyes, took counsel with the father, as he waited expectantly.


“  You are our brother, you are our brother. 
“ You are our brother in charge of the palace gate,
“ You are our captain of the barge, 
“ You are our commander of the chariot, the servant of the hunting chariot;
“ You are our city father and judge, 
“ The son-in-law of five things, the son-in-law of ten things. 
“ Brother, you are the son-in-law of our father, you are our son-in-law supreme;
“ Our mother speaks favourably with you.


Inana approached her bride groom:


“ Your coming here is life indeed, your entering the house is abundance; 
“ Lying at your side is my utmost joy.
“ My sweet, let us delight ourselves on the bed.


Dumuzid spoke, as his countenance brightened:


“ Dearest, my darling, 
“ My honey-mouthed of her mother,
“ The gazing of your eyes is pleasant to me, come my beloved sister. 
“ The speaking of your mouth is pleasant to me, my honey-mouthed of her mother.
“ The kissing of your lips is pleasant to me, come my beloved sister.
“ The beer of your barley is good, 
“ The ale of your beer-bread is good; 
“ Come my beloved sister, in the house, your luxuriance is brilliant.” 


Inana replied:


" For as long as you live, as long as you live, you shall take an oath for me,
“ Brother of the countryside, you shall take an oath for me. 
“ You shall take an oath for me that you will not touch another.
“ You shall take an oath for me that you will not rest your head on anyone else.
“ My one who wears the princely nijlam-garment, my beloved, man of my heart!
“ I shall impose an oath on you, my brother of the beautiful eyes. 
“ You are to place your right hand on my genitals while your left hand rests on my head, 
“ Bringing your mouth close to my mouth, and taking my lips in your mouth:
“ Thus you shall take an oath for me. 
“ This is the oath of women, my brother of the beautiful eyes.”


 Inana opened the door for him. Inside the house she shone before him, like the light of the moon. Dumuzi looked at her joyously. He pressed his neck close against hers and kissed her.


-
 The marriage of Dumuzi and Inana was nigh. 
The singer wove into song what I tell you,
 Let it flow from ear to mouth, 
 Let it pass from old to young:
-


Inana said, softly:


” My vulva, the horn, the boat of heaven, is full of eagerness
“ Like the young moon, my untilled land lies fallow.
“ As for me, Inana,
“ Who will plow my vulva? Who will plow my high field?
“ Who will plow my wet ground?
“ As for me, the young woman, who will plow my vulva? 
“ Who will station the ox there?“


Dumuzi replied:


“ Great lady, the king will plow your vulva. 
“ I, Dumuzi the King, will plow your vulva.”


Inana gave command:


“ Then plow my vulva, man of my heart! 
“ Plow my vulva!”


At the king’s lap stood the rising cedar. Plants grew high by their side. Grains grew high by their side. Gardens flourished luxuriantly.


Dumuzi declared:


“ May my sheep eat my stubble which is growing in the fields,
“ My plants, my camel-thorn. 
“ May my sheep eat my winnowed barley. 
“ May my sheep eat my life of the Land which is growing in the fields, 
“ May my sheep eat my support of orphans and sustenance of widows,
“ My plants, my cakir-plants.
“ May my sheep eat my vine of clay balls which is growing in the fields,
“ My plants, my colocynth.
“ May my sheep eat my beer wort mixed with honey,
“ My plants, my marsh reeds.
“ May my sheep eat, and my calves going together with their bulls,
“ My plants, my reed shoots. 
“ May my sheep eat my blossoming garden of apple trees,
“ My plants, my rising reeds.
“ May my sheep eat my aromatic plants of the fields, dripping with syrup,
“ My plants, my licorice. May my sheep eat as my waterskin hangs from the saddle,
“ My plants, my harub-pods.
“ May my sheep eat my grass spread out like a mantle,
“ My plants, my meadow grass.
“ May my sheep eat my native-born of flourishing Sumer,
“ My plants, my kali-plants.
“ May my sheep eat my unkempt long hair, 
“ My plants, my esparto-grass. 
“ May my sheep eat my plants that ignore winter and summer,
“ My plants, my date palm saplings. 
“ May my sheep eat my beautiful plants with a head of fine hairs,
“ My plants, my lilangi-plants.
“ May my sheep eat my stubble growing in the desert, 
“ My plants, my apple grass.


The wild bull stood in prayer before her. He is gentle towards holy Inana. Embracing Ninegal, he treats her gently. Dumuzid stood in prayer before her.


Inana sang:


“ He has sprouted; he has burgeoned; 
“ He is lettuce planted by the water. 
“ He is the one my womb loves best.
“ My well-stocked garden of the plain, 
“ My barley growing high in its furrow,
“ My apple tree which bears fruit up to its crown, 
“ He is lettuce planted by the water.
“ My honey-man, my honey-man sweetens me always. 
“ My lord, the honey-man of the gods, He is the one my womb loves best. 
“ His hand is honey, his foot is honey, He sweetens me always.
“ My eager impetuous caresser of the navel, 
“ My caresser of the soft thighs, 
“ He is the one my womb loves best,
“ He is watered lettuce .”


Dumuzi sang:


“ O’ Lady, your breast is your field. 
“ Your broad field pours out grain.
“ Water flows from on high for your servant. 
“ Bread flows from on high for your servant. 
“ Pour it out for me, 
“ Inana, I will drink all you offer.”


 Inana sang back:


“ Make your milk sweet and thick, 
“ My bridegroom,
“ My shepherd, I will drink your fresh milk. 
“ Wild bull, Dumuzi,
“ Make your milk sweet and thick. 
“ Let the milk of the goat flow in my sheepfold. 
“ Fill my holy churn with honey cheese.
“ Lord Dumuzi, I will drink your fresh milk.
“ My husband, I will guard my sheepfold for you. 
“ I will watch over your house of life, the storehouse, 
“ The shining quivering place which delights Sumer
“ The house which decides the fate of the land,
“ The house which gives the breath of life to the people. 
“ I, the queen of the palace, will watch over your house.”


Dumuzi spoke:


“ Inana, I would go with you to my garden. 
“ I would go with you to my orchard. 
“ I would go with you to my apple tree. 
“ There I would plant the sweet, honey-covered seed.”


And they went.


Inanna thought to herself:


“ He brought me into his garden. 
“ My brother, Dumuzi, brought me into his garden. 
“ I strolled with him among the standing trees,
“ I stood with him among the fallen trees, 
“ By an apple tree I knelt as is proper.
“ I knelt before my brother, coming in song, 
“ Who rose to me out of the poplar leaves,
“ Who came to me in the midday heat.


“ Before my lord Dumuzi, 
“ I poured out plants from my womb. 
“ I placed plants before him, 
“ I placed grain before him,
“ I poured out grain from my womb.”


Tablet V:


Inana spoke to Utu:


" My brother, awe-inspiring lord, let me ride with you to the mountains,
“ Lord of heaven, awe-inspiring lord, let me ride with you to the mountains; 
“ To the mountains of herbs, to the mountains of cedars,
“ To the mountains of cypresses, to the mountains of silver,
“ To the mountains of lapis lazuli,
“ To the mountains where the gakkul plants grow,
“ To the distant source of the rolling rivers, to the mountains.


" My brother, come, let me travel with you
“ My brother, the midst of the sea 
“ Let my eyes behold it
" I am unfamiliar with womanly matters, 
“ Unfamiliar with sexual intercourse!
“ I am unfamiliar with womanly matters, 
“ Unfamiliar with kissing!


" Whatever exists in the mountains, let us eat that.
“ Whatever exists in the hills, let us eat that.
“ In the mountains of herbs, the mountains of cedars, and the mountains of cypresses,


" After the herbs have been eaten, after the cedars have been eaten,
“ Put your hand in my hand and then escort me to my house.
“ Escort me to my house, in Zabalam.
“ Escort me to my mother, Ningal.
“ Escort me to my mother-in-law, Ninsumun.
“ Escort me to my sister-in-law, to Jectin-ana."
Tablet VI:
Inana, called for the bed that rejoiced the heart. She called for the bed that sweetens the loins. She called for the bed of kingship; she called for the bed of queenship. 


Inana called for the bed, and spread the bridal sheet. 


Then, she called to her bridegroom: 


“ The bed is waiting.”


He put his hand in her hand. He put his hand to her heart. 


Sweet is the sleep of hand-to-hand. Sweeter still the sleep of heart-to-heart.


Inana spoke:


“ I bathed for the wild bull, 
“ I bathed for the Shepherd Dumuzi, 
“ I perfumed my sides with ointment, 
“ I coated my mouth with sweet-smelling amber, 
“ I painted my eyes with kohl.


“ My loins were shaped with his fair hands, 
“ The shepherd, Dumuzi, filled my lap with cream and milk, 
“ He stroked my pubic hair, 
“ He watered my womb. 
" He laid his hands on my holy vulva, 
“ He smoothed my black boat with cream, 
“ He quickened my narrow boat with milk, 
“ He caressed me on the bed.


“ Now, I will caress my high priest, 
“ I will caress the faithful shepherd, Dumuzi, 
“ I will caress his loins, the shepherdship of the land,
“ I will decree a sweet fate for him.”


The Queen of Heaven, the Heroic Woman; greater than her mother, who was presented the meh by Enki decreed the fate. Inana, the first daughter of the Moon, decreed the fate of Dumuzi: 


“ In battle I am your leader, 
“ In combat I am your armour-bearer, 
“ In the assembly I am your advocate, 
“ On the campaign I am your inspiration. 
“ You, the chosen shepherd of the holy shrine, 
“ You, the king, the faithful provider of Uruk, 
“ The light of An’s great shrine, 
“ In all ways you are fit to hold your head high on the lofty dais, 
“ To sit on the lapis lazuli throne, and cover your head with the holy crown, 
“ You are fit to wear long clothes on your body,
“ To bind yourself with the garments of kingship, 
“ You are fit to carry the mace and sword, to guide straight the long bow and arrow, 
“ To fasten the throw-stick and sling at your side, 
“ You are fit to race on the road with the holy sceptre in your hand,
“ And the holy sandals on your feet, 
“ To prance on the holy breast like a lapis lazuli calf.


“ You, the sprinter, the chosen shepherd, 
“ In all ways you are fit.
“ May your heart enjoy the long days.
“ That which An has determined for you, may it not be altered. 
“ That which Enlil has granted, may it not be changed. 
“ You are the favourite of Ningal
“ And I, Inana, hold you dear.”


Ninshubar, the faithful servant of the holy shrine, led Dumuzi to the sweet thighs of Inana and spoke:


“ My queen, here is the choice of your heart, 
“ The king, your beloved bridegroom. 
“ May he spend long days in the sweetness of your holy loins.
“ Give him a favourable and glorious reign. 
“ Grant him the king’s throne, firm in its foundations. 
“ Grant him the shepherd’s staff of judgement. 
“ Grant him the enduring crown with the radiant and noble diadem.
“ From where the sun rises to where the sun sets,
“ From north to south,
“ From the upper sea to the lower sea,
“ From the land of the huluppu-tree to the land of the cedar,
“ Let his shepherd's staff protect all of the land.


“ As the farmer, let him make the fields fertile,
“ As the shepherd, let him make the sheepfolds multiply,
“ Under his reign let there be vegetation,
“ Under his reign let there be rich grain.


“ In the marshland may the fish and birds chatter,
“ In the canebrake may the young and old reeds grow high,
“ In the steppe may the deer and wild goats multiply,
“ In the orchards may there be honey and wine,
“ In the grasslands may the lettuce and cress grow high,
“ In the palace may there be long life.


“ May there be floodwater in the Tigris and Euphrates,
“ May the plants grow high on their banks and fill the meadows,
“ May the Lady of Vegetation pile the grain in heaps and mounds.
“ O’ my Queen of Heaven and Earth,
“ Queen of all the universe,
“ May he enjoy long days in the sweetness of your holy loins.”
        
The king went with lifted head to the holy loins. He went with lifted head to the loins of Inana. He went to the queen with lifted head. He opened wide his arms to the holy priestess of heaven.


Then, Inana addressed him:


“ My beloved, the delight of my eyes, met me. 
“ We rejoiced together. 
“ You took your pleasure of me.
“ You brought me into your house.
“ And laid me down on the fragrant honey-bed. 


“ My sweet love, lying by my heart, 
“ Tongue-playing, one by one, 
“ My fair Dumuzi, you did so fifty times.
“ Now, my sweet love is sated.
“ And he says: 


“” Set me free, my sister, set me free.
“” You will be a little daughter to my father. 
““ Come, my beloved sister, I would go to the palace.
“” Set me free…'”


“ My blossom-bearer, your allure was sweet. 
“ My blossom-bearer in the apple orchard,  Dumuzi-abzu, your allure was sweet.
“ My fearless one, 
“ My holy statue outfitted with sword and lapis lazuli diadem, 
“ How sweet was your allure…” 


Exaltation of Enki:


Divine goddess endowed with lofty power. Anu, Father of the Gods, took her as a wife. Ki; great Mother of the Gods and Earth. In those days, they called on her as Urash, the mother with multitudes of divine powers. Cherished by Anu, who takes heed of her word. She spoke in favour of Enki, and Anu took heed. 


No deity excelled like the lord and lady; no deity excelled like Enlil or Ninlil. Powerful princes, they were brilliantly revealed deities. Powerful princes; they were lords who can decide destinies. 


Enlil called for Inana, and when she went, no one could keep pace with her. The great gods were filled with fear at her cry. Her holy utterances were as grand as those of An, and as weighty as those of Enlil. Inana, supreme with multifarious divine powers, surpassing the other divine ladies, went to Enki at Father Enlil’s command:


" Great An, your commands are great indeed: who can revoke them? 
“ Father Enlil, no one knows how to dissipate the great destinies that you determine. 
“ Both of you, bestow permanently the role of Lord of the Earth,
“ To the youth who knows, the builder of living and nonliving things.
“ Founder of dwellings!


“ He has brought with him from the holy womb
“ A knowledge of how to build homes and cities, and how to strengthen the land.
“ He knows how to direct all things: 
“ Let him give great commands.
“ May the good crown be your glory. Inspiring fear and trembling, O’ lion of kingship,
“ May you wear the royal robe,
“ May you establish the divine powers of the Abzu, the waters of the deep!” 
“ Now, Great An has made you forever pre-eminent,
“ Even to the limit of the mountains!
“ To prolong your days, 
“ In E-kur, Enlil has commanded the holy life-giving embrace upon you.
“ You shall not cease to sit on the holy dais
“ May the foreign lands rejoice at you.”


The Anuna gods came to him, the lord, who among the gods makes the clever decisions. They made him most prominent among them. Enki, who, holding a staff in his hand, determined their destinies, as one who, possessing all the divine powers, is alone surpassing. 


Enki’s utterances were given power to make justice flourish, to strengthen the divine powers of heaven and earth. He, who could examine the ordinances and carry them out correctly, was proclaimed as their great prince.


Enki rose to the most supreme among the gods. The highest ranking among the Anuna gods. His divine powers and wisdom, surpassing theirs, were specially favoured. 


Enki, with his precious and ingenious divine powers, was to be the guardian of their true divine powers. His prominence imposed itself on them like awesome stillness; covering them like a storm, taking their breath away, and wrapping them like a swaddle. His loftiness was inaccessible; like a remote and far-away mountain.


 He gave advice; he set his mind to work, the skillful labour. He opened up the house of instructions and surpassing wisdom. Everything created was good and full of great awesomeness. Dimkurkurra, mound of creation, where the primeval lords dwell, was made a place clad in fearsome radiance.

 Father Enlil and the great mother, Ninlil, bestowed on him the task of founding the marshes of Eridug, the great arable lands and the fields of Kenigr, and the fruit orchards that bear pure goodness of holy food and drink for the Anuna gods.


The Anuna, the great gods, said, "Let it be so!", to the destiny determined by great An and by Nunamnir. 


Inana, the great daughter of Suen, gathered together all the divine powers and placed them in his hand. 


And because Enlil favoured the farmer, Enki brought him forth in tribute. Because Enlil favoured the son of Enki, Imutud was brought forward, and given name. Nunamnir and Nimdummud determined his destiny.


Enki spoke:


“ Like the warrior Erra, radiant and strong, 
“ May my avenging son, lord Ninurta, be a furious storm against the enemy,
“ May he be your helper on the battlefield, and may you put your trust in him.


Enlil replied:


“ May he subdue the enemy land,
“ And may he spread barley out in heaps for me. 
“The holy purification rites of E-kur shall be perfected, and served daily.
“ May your offerings on the august table in my great dining hall be everlasting.
“ May you lift your head and raise your neck to the heavens.
“ Life is yours, irrevocably."


Enki, again, addressed the young one, Ninurta:


" Youth with beautiful and well-formed limbs,
“ You shall stand tall, radiantly and proudly lifting your head,
“ Full of charm and beauty, fitted for lordship, worthy of the holy dais, 


“ Ninurta, I have decided to give you your precious divine powers.
“ I shall call to you, when you lift your faithful gaze
“ You are the one whom I shall call by name.


“ Great An has made you forever pre-eminent,
“ To prolong your days, O’ youth,
“ In E-kur Enlil has commanded my holy life-giving embrace.
“ You shall not cease to sit on the august dais which I care for. 


“ May the lands rejoice at you, my Ninurta,
“ As at Nanna when admired by the land,
“ As at Utu when he appears in the holy heavens.


“ Fearsomely you were made, and the heavens shall know your praise!


“ Ninurta, warrior, 
“ You shall gather their authorities,
“ You shall have seat in their offices 
“ And you shall have kingship of lords entrusted in your hand. 


“ Your anger shall be as a flood, 
“ You will become great warrior of the gods,
“ You are and shall be exalted, lord Ninurta!


“ Your face is the sun, your locks Nisaba,
“ And your two eyes, lord, are Enlil and Ninlil,
“ Your eyeballs are Gula and Belet-ili,
“ And your eyelids, are the twins Sin and Nergal,
“ Your eyebrows are brilliance of the sun, which travels, 


“ Your mouth’s shape is Ištar of the stars, which travel.
“ Anu and Antu are your lips, your speech is mighty.
“ Your tongue is Pabilsag, who is exalted on high.
“ The heaven of your mouth, lord, is circumference of heaven and earth,
“ Dwelling place of the great gods. 


“ Your teeth are the Seven, the slayers of evil,
“ O’ lord, your cheeks are the rising of brilliant stars,
“ Your ears are Ea and Damkina, your father and mother, sages of wisdom


“ Your head is Adad, who makes heaven and earth billow like a kiln,
“ Your brow is Shala, beloved spouse, who makes Adad’s heart feel happy.


“ Your neck is Marduk, judge of heaven and netherworld, deluge upon the earth,
“ Your throat is Zarpanitu, creatress of people.
“ Your chest is Shullat, who examines the broad earth,
“ Your shoulders are Hanish, who establishes plenty, and rains down abundance.
“ Your right side is Uta-ulu, your left side is Ninpanigarra,
“ The fingers of your fists are the fire of Gibil,
“ Your nails are the bright star Nibru,


“  Dagan, shall affix the winds to your feet, 
“ Your counsel is Zababa, mighty and fierce!”


 He gave the youth a mace and jewel. Inana and Ninurta then went, joyfully from Enki's presence, to take their seats in the palace; the dwelling-place of sweet honey.




Enki's journey to Nibru:




In those remote days, when the fates were determined; in a year when An brought about abundance, people broke through the earth like herbs and plants. The Lord of the Abzu, King Enki, the lord who determines the fates built up his temple entirely from silver and lapis lazuli. Its silver and lapis lazuli were the shining daylight. 


He brought joy into the shrine of the Abzu.


An artfully made bright crenellation rising out from the Abzu was erected for Lord Nudimmud. He built the temple from precious metal. He decorated it with lapis lazuli, and covered it abundantly with gold. 


In Eridug, he built the house on the bank. Its brickwork made utterances and gave advice. Its eaves roared like a bull; the temple of Enki bellowed. During the night the temple praised its lord and offered its best for him.


Before Lord Enki, Isimud the Minister praised the temple. He went to the temple and spoke to it. He went to the brick building and addressed it: 


" Temple, built from precious metal and lapis lazuli; 
“ Temple, whose foundation pegs are driven into the Abzu; 
“ Temple, which has been cared for by the prince in the Abzu
“ Like the Tigris and the Euphrates, it is mighty and awe-inspiring
“ Joy has been brought into Enki's Abzu.


" Your lock has no rival. Your bolt is a fearsome lion.
“ Your roof beams are the bull of heaven, an artfully made bright headgear. 
“ Your reed-mats are like lapis lazuli, decorating the roof-beams.
“ Your vault is a wild bull raising its horns. 
“ Your door is a lion who seizes a man. 
“ Your staircase, a lion coming down on a man.


" Abzu, pure place, which fulfils its purpose,
“ E-engura, your lord has directed his steps towards you
“ Enki, Lord of the Abzu, has embellished your foundation pegs with cornelian.
“ He has adorned you with silver and lapis lazuli.
“ The temple of Enki is provisioned with holy wax;
“ It is a bull obedient to its master, roaring by itself and giving advice at the same time.  
“ Enki has surrounded E-engura, with a holy reed fence,
“ In your midst a lofty throne is erected,
“ Your door-jamb is the holy locking bar of heaven.


" Abzu, pure place, where the fates are determined, 
“ The Lord of Wisdom, Lord Enki, the lord who determines the fates,
“ Nudimmud, the Lord of Eridug, lets nobody look into its midst.
“ Your abgal-priests let their hair down their backs.


" Enki's beloved Eridug, E-engura whose inside is full of abundance
“ Abzu, life of the land, beloved of Enki,
“ Temple built on the edge, befitting the artful divine powers
” Eridug, your shadow extends over the midst of the rising sea
“ Without a rival; mighty awe-inspiring river which terrifies the land


“ E-engura, high citadel standing firm on the earth
“ Temple at the edge of the Engur,
“ Lion in the midst of the Abzu;
“ Lofty temple of Enki, which bestows wisdom on the land;
“ Your cry is like that of a mighty rising river, reaching King Enki.


" He made the lyre, the aljar-instrument,
“ The balaj-drum of your sur-priests with the drumsticks 
“ He made the plectrum, the harhar, the sabitum, and the miritum-instruments
“They offer their best for his holy temple;


“ The instruments resounded by themselves with a sweet sound.
“ The holy aljar-instrument of Enki played for him on his own,
“ Tigi-drums resounded and seven singers sang
" What Enki says is irrefutable; his command is well established." 


This is what Isimud spoke to the brick building. He praised the E-engura duly with sweet songs as it had been built; as Enki had raised Eridug up. An artfully built mountain which floats on the water. His shrine spread out into the reed-beds. 


At night, birds brood in its green orchards laden with fruit. The suhur-carp play among the honey-herbs, and the ectu- carp dart among the small gizi-reeds. When Enki rises, the fishes rise before him like waves. The Abzu stood as a marvel, as he brought joy into the Engur.


Like the sea, he is awe-inspiring; like a mighty river, he instils fear. The Euphrates rises before him, as it does before the fierce south wind. His punting pole is Nirah. His punting pole is Imdudu. His oars are the small reeds. When Enki embarks, the year will be full of abundance. 


The ship departs of its own accord, by itself, with tow rope held. As he leaves the temple of Eridug, the river gurgles to its lord. Its sound is a calf's mooing, the mooing of a good cow.


Enki had oxen slaughtered. He had sheep offered there, lavishly. Where there were no ala-drums, he installed some in their places. Where there were no bronze ub-drums, he despatched some to their places.


He directed his steps to Nibru and entered the giguna, the shrine of Nibru. Enki reached for the beer, he reached for the liquor. He had liquor poured into big bronze containers, and had emmer-wheat beer pressed out. In kukuru-containers, which make the beer good, he mixed beer-mash. By adding date-syrup to its taste, he made it strong. He poured into cups, its bran-mash.


In the shrine of Nibru, Enki provided a meal for Enlil, his father. He seated An at the head of the table and seated Enlil next to An. He seated Nintud in the place of honour and seated the Anuna gods at the adjacent places. 


All of them were drinking and enjoying beer and liquor. They filled the bronze aga-vessels to the brim and started a competition, drinking from the bronze vessels of Urac. They made the tilimda-vessels shine like holy barges. After beer and liquor had been libated and enjoyed, Enlil was happy in Nibru.


Enlil addressed the Anuna gods: 


" Great gods who are standing here!
“ Anuna, who have lined up in the place of assembly,
“ My son, king Enki, has built up the temple!


“ He has made Eridug rise up
“ It has come out from the ground like a mountain!


“ He has built it in a pleasant place,
“ Eridug, the pure place, where no one is to enter 
“ A temple built with silver and decorated with lapis lazuli,
“ A house which tunes the seven tigi-drums properly, and provides incantations;
“ Where holy songs make all of the house a lovely place 
“ The shrine of the Abzu, the good destiny of Enki,
“ Befitting the elaborate divine powers; the temple of Eridug has been built with silver
“ For all this, father Enki be praised!”


Enki and the World Order:


The gathered Annuna made their words to Enki:


“ Lord who walks nobly on heaven and earth, self-reliant,
“ Father Enki, engendered and begotten by a wild bull,
“ Prized by Enlil, the Great Kur, and loved by Holy An,
“ King, who turned out the mes-tree in the Abzu,
“ Raised it up over all the lands,
“ Great Dragon,who planted it in Eridu
“ Its shade spread over heaven and earth
“ As a grove of fruit trees stretching over the land


“ Enki, lord of the prosperity the Anunna-gods possess
“ Nudimmud, the mighty one of the Ekur,
“ Strong one of the Anunna, whose noble house is set up in the Abzu
“ The mast of heaven and earth.
“ Enki, who, lifting but a single eye, convulses the Kur,


“ Where the bison is born, the stag is born,
“ Where the wild sheep is born, in the germinated meadows,
“ In the pits in the heart of the mountain, in the verdant fields,
“ And in the place where no one dares to enter,
“ There you have fixed your eyes like a halhal-reed.


“ Father Enki, come close to the seeded land,
“ Let it bear healthy seeds.
“ Nudimmud, come close to the pregnant ewe,
“ Let it give birth to a healthy lamb.
“ Come close to the inseminated cow,
“ Let it give birth to a healthy calf.
“ Come close to the pregnant goat,
“ Let it give birth to a healthy kid.
“ Once you come close to the cultivated field, the germinated fields,
“ The heaps and piles stack high with grain in the high steppe.”


Enki, then, addressed the assembly:


“ My father, ruler above and below, made my features blaze.
“ My great brother, ruler of all the lands, gathered all the meh together,
“ And placed them in my hands.


“ From the Ekur, House of Enlil, I passed on the arts and crafts to my Abzu, Eridu
“ I am the true offspring, sprung from the wild ox
“ I am the leading son of En.
“ I am the great storm the breaks over the great below
“ I am the great lord over the land, I am the first among the rulers,
“ I am the father of all the lands, I am the big brother of the gods,
“ I am the seal-keeper above and below, I am cunning and wise in the lands.


“ I am the one who directs justice alongside An,
“ I am the one who, having gazed upon the Kur, decrees the fates alongside Enlil:
“ He has placed in my hands the decreeing of fates, at the place where the sun rises.
“ I am the leader of the Anunna-gods.
“ I am the one born a leading son of An.”


After the lord had proclaimed his loftiness, the Anunna-gods stood up in prayer and supplication:


“ Lord who stands watch over the arts and crafts,
“ Expert at decisions, 
“ Adored one, 
“ O’ Enki, praise.”


Enki set his spell. He eyed over the council of the Annuna-gods. He eyed Enlil. Enki, king of the Abzu, again invoked his own praise as he looked toward An:


“ I am lord, the one whose word endures
“ I am eternal,
“ I built my house, the shrine, in a pure place, gave it a good name,
“ I built my Abzu, the shrine, in Eridu and decreed for it a good fate,


“ The shade of my house stretches over the marsh
“ There, the goatfish wave their beards
“ Among the honey-plants, the gud-fish wave their tails for me
“ Among the small gizi-reeds, the flock of birds keep chirping in their nests,
“ Sacred songs and spells filled my Abzu


“ I, the Lord, will go,
“ I am Enki, and I will draw near to my land
“ Let the lands Magan and Dilmun set eyes upon me
“ Let Dilmun boats be loaded with wood.
“ Let the Magan boats be filled sky high.
“ Let the magilum-boats of Meluhha transport gold and silver,
“ Let them take it to Nippur for Enlil, king of the lands.


“ To him who has no city, to him who has no house, the Martu
“  I furnish cattle as a gift.”


The great prince had drawn near to his land, the Anunna-gods spoke with affection:


“ Lord who rides the great meh, the pure meh,
“ Lord who stands watch over the great meh, the myriad meh,
“ The one who is foremost everywhere, above and below,
“ At Eridu, the pure place, the most precious place
“ Where the noble meh have been taken in 
“ O’ Enki, praise!”


For the great prince who was passing through all his land, all the rulers, all the incantation-priests of Eridu, all the linen-wearers of Sumer, carried out the lustration rites of the Abzu. They stood watch over the holy places, the precious places for father Enki, and cleansed the great house of the prince. Its stations were named, the noble shrine purified.


Tall juniper, the pure plant, was carried into the midst of the Abzu. The holy chorus was organized at the noble water-courses of Father Enki. With skill, the staircase of Eridu, at the good quay, was built. The Ibex of the Abzu was moored at the good quay; the noble quay. The holy usga-shrine was set-up, and the priests gave voice in prayer after prayer to him there.


Prosperity prevailed above and below. 


Proudly, Enki stepped forth to the land, and decreed its fate:


“ Shinar, great kur,
“ Kengir, land of what is above and below,
“ Robed in enduring light, and settling the meh upon the people,
“ From sunrise to sunset, your meh are lofty meh; untouchable!
“ Your heart is a maze,
“ Inscrutable is your life-giving womb,
“ The place where the gods give birth
“ Like heaven, it cannot be touched
“ It gives birth to kings who fasten the lasting diadem.
“ It gives birth to the high priests who put crown to head.
“ Your lord, the honoured lord, sits with King An on the dais of An”


The words pleased the heart of An.


“ The Anunna-gods, the great gods, have taken up dwelling in your midst,
“ They consume their food in your giguna, among your rare trees.
“ House Sumer, have your many stalls built,
“ Have your cows multiply, have your many sheepfolds set up,
“ Have your sheep be many, have your giguna reach the sky,
“ Have your temples reach the sky and have your lasting shrine lift its hand to heaven.”


He crossed to the shrine; he crossed to Ur.


Enki, king of the Abzu, decreed its fate:


“ City with everything proper to it, water-washed,
“ A firm-standing bull, a dais of plenty in the kur, knees wide open,
“ Lofty as a mountain, hasur-grove, wide of shade
“ Counting on its own strength,
“ You can well direct the meh that has been perfected for you.
“ Enlil, the Great Kur, has pronounced your lofty name above and below.
“ City whose fate has been decreed by Enki, Shrine Ur, you can rise high to heaven.”


 He crossed to the kur-Meluhha. Enki, the king of the Abzu, decreed its fate:


“ Black kur, your trees will be large trees, they will be mes-groves of the kur:
“ Their thrones will be set in royal palaces.
“ Your reeds will be large reeds, 
“ Heroes shall work them as weapons in the battlefields
“ As the reeds of kur, your bulls will be large bulls
“ Their roar will be the roar of the bulls of the kur.
“ The great meh of the gods will be perfected for you.
“ All the dar-birds of the kur wear carnelian beards; your birds will be haia-birds;
“ Their cries will fill the royal palaces
“ Your silver will be gold, your copper will be bronze-tin.
“ Kur, everything you have will increase, your people will multiply,
“ Your male will go after his fellow male like a bull.


Enki cleansed and purified the kur-Dilmun, and set Ninsikilla in charge of it. Lagoons were allotted to the princely shrine. Dilmun ate its fish. Palm trees he allotted to its fertile field. Dilmun ate their dates.


Enki looked toward Elam and Marhasi, the peoples who were all devouring. Given martial power by Enlil, Enki attacked their houses, attacked their walls, plundered their silver, lapis lazuli, and storehouses. He raised the lands of Elam over the Euphrates; he brought the goods to Nippur for Enlil, King of All the Lands.


To him who had no city, who had no house, the Martu, Enki furnished cattle as a gift.


Once he had turned his eye away from that spot, he stood up full of lust like an attacking bull. He lifted his penis, and filled the swift river with flowing semen. Like a big wild bull he thrilled the heart of the Tigris, and stood by as it gave birth.


The water brought was flowing water, its wine was sweet. The grain it grew, gunu-grain,
the people ate it. The Ekur, the house of Enlil, he packed with goods; and, with Enki, Enlil rejoiced. 


Nippur exulted.


The lord fastened on the diadem of the en, put on the enduring tiara of the king. He trod the ground from south to north. On his left side, prosperity sprang out of the earth for him. 


Enki, King of the Abzu, decreed the fate of the river.


Enbilulu, the inspector of canals, inspector of waterways, the one who holds a scepter in his right hand. Who, to make the two rivers eat together, speaks words with an exulting mouth. Who carried away prosperity from the palace like fat. Enki placed in charge of them.
        
He called the marshland, and stocked it with carp. He called the canebrake, and stocked it with full-grown reeds and green reeds. 


Enki, King of the Abzu, decreed the fate of the marshland.


Kullulu, the son of Tiamat, half breed loved by the fish. The one from whose net no fish escapes; from whose trap, no life escapes. From whose snare, no bird escapes. Enki placed in charge of them.


The Anunna, for Enki, erected a shrine. A holy shrine. Its interior like a maze; a twisted thread. A thing unknown to man. A shrine whose lower station is the roving iku-constellation, the four cornered field. 


A holy shrine whose upper station moves toward the chariot-constellation. Its awesomeness a turbulent flood-wave. Its melam a terrifying splendour. They set up a lofty dais in the Sea-House, the E-enigura. The Anunna-gods, the Great Gods, dared not go near it. 


Enki turned to the temple on the sea, and made his voice heard.


Innin, who induces copulation. The one who sets sail to the east, born in the holy shrine. Great flood-wave of the sea, turbulent flood-wave. The inundation of the sea, who springs forth out of the sea-foam. Innin of Sirara, mother Nanse, the sea in all its breadth, Enki placed in charge of it.


He called the waters above the rain, fixed them there as floating clouds, and stretched to the horizon, their breath of life. The hillocks turned to fields where emmer grew.


Enki, King of the Abzu, decreed the fate of the Sumer sky.


Great son of An, the canal-inspector of heaven and earth, the one who rides the great storm, who charges with lightning who, with the holy bolt, blocks up the inside of heaven; Ishkur, the man of abundance, Enki placed in charge of it.


Enki trained the plow, the yoke, and furnished them with the team of oxen. He opened the mouth of the holy furrow. Made grow the grain, in the seeded field.


Enki, King of the Abzu, decreed the fate of the Sumer fields.


Enkimdu, the man of ditch and dike, the lord who fastened on the diadem, the ornament of the high steppe, the tool expert formerly of Martu, farmer of Enlil, Enki placed in charge of them.


Enki called on the cultivated field. Stocked it with gunu-barley. Stocked it with chick-peas, with lentils, and vetch. He heaped up the estub-barley, the gunu-barley, the innuha-barley in piles. He multiplied the heaps and piles of grain; with Enlil calling upon the winds, he spread the seed through the land.


Enki, King of the Abzu, decreed the fate of the Sumer grain.


Innin, good lady, whose face drips honey, the one whose head and body are dappled, she who breeds copulation, vigour of the land, the life of the black heads, Goddess Asnan, the good bread, bread of all the earth, Enki placed in charge of them.


The Great Prince placed a string on the pickaxe, guided the brickmold. He made it penetrate mother-earth as if it were precious oil.


Enki, King of the Abzu, decreed the fate of the pickaxe, the noble hoe, and the masonry.


Kulla, mighty brickman of the land, shaped by the hands of Ea, builder of Lord Enlil, whose firmly set brickmold is a guide that sets the river straight, the one whose pronged pickaxe is a corpse-devouring snake that shapes the mighty zalag, Enki placed in charge of them.


He fixed the cords, straightened the footers, and erected a house at the side of the assembly. He guided the lustrations. The Great Prince set down the footers, and fitted the brickwork upon them.


Enki, King of the Abzu, decreed the fate of construction.


Musdamma, great builder of Enlil, the one whose footers, once laid down, do not sag. Whose lasting house once built does not collapse. Whose vault reaches to mid-sky like a rainbow, Enki placed in charge of them.


He turned to his left, and gave the lord of the lofty steppe a holy crown to wear. To the high steppe he tied a lapis lazuli beard. He fastened on it, a lapis diadem. 


The good earth he lavished with teeming vegetation. He multiplied the herd of the high steppe, placed them where they are supposed to be. He multiplied the rams and the wild rams in the pastures; he made them breed.


Enki, King of the Abzu, decreed the fate of steppes, and the mountain lowlands.


Sumugan, king of the foothills, great lion, the hero who is the crown of the high steppe, the powerful, lofty, hand of Enlil, Enki placed in charge of them.


He built stalls, directed cleaning of them, and raised the sheepfolds. He stocked them with the best fat and milk. He filled the dining halls of the gods with luxury. Then in the verdant steppe, he dispensed the flock.


Enki, King of the Abzu, decreed the fate of the herd, the milk and the herdsmen.


The spouse of Holy Inanna, the Innin, Queen of All the Great Meh. The king, unfailing provider of Eanna, friend of An, who fosters copulation in the boulevards of Kullab. Dumuzi, the usumgal of heaven, Enki placed in charge of them.


He fixed the borders, marked them off. Enki, for the Anunna-gods, erected the kiurua and situated them in cities. He disposed agricultural land and fields alongside them.


Enki, King of the Abzu, decreed the fate of the heavens and earth:


The valiant Utu, the firm-standing bull, who proudly emblazons his power. The hero, the bull who bursts out of the hasur-forest, who roars like a lion. Father of the great city, the place where the sun rises. The great herald of holy An, the judge, the one who makes decisions for the gods. Utu, son of Ningal, who has tied a beard of lapis lazuli, and blazes out in holy heaven out of the horizon, Enki placed in charge of the universe in its entirety.


He picked out the tow from the fibres, and adapted it for rags. He guided the approach, and wove the mug-cloth. Enki perfected the woman's art. For Enki the people clothed themselves in the suluhu-garment. 


Enki, King of the Abzu, decreed the fate of the weave.


Uttu, the conscientious one, the unfailing woman of silence. The one who is the dignity of the palace, the decorum of the king, Enki placed in charge of them.


Then, alone lacking any functions, the great woman of heaven, the young Inanna, who had been left without a single post, entered his house, wept, and muttered a complaint:


“ Of the Anunna, the Great Gods, and their fate
“ Enlil fixed for certain in your hand


“ Me, the loftiest woman, why did you treat in a different way?
“ For I, the Holy Inanna, where are my functions?
        
“ Aruru, sister of Enlil, you made Nintu, queen of birth-giving,
“ She got the holy brick of birth-giving for her nin-ship,
“ And carried off her umbilical-cord lancet, the imman-stone, and leeks,
“ She got the silagarra-vessel of greenish lapis lazuli,
“ And carried off her holy consecrated ala-vessel,
“ She is now the midwife of the land,
“ The birth of kings, the birth of en's, has been put in her hands!


“ My noble sister, the Holy Ninisinna, got the suba-jewel,
“ She is now the lover of An, inciting the heart's desires.
                        
“ Of my noble sister, the holy Ninmug
“ The golden chisel, the silver hammer, the large flint knife,
“ Her antasurra were carried off,
“ She is now the metal and wood-worker of the land.
“ Fastening the lasting diadem on the one born king,
“ Placing the crown on the head of the one born en,
“ You have put this in her hands.
        
“ My noble sister, the Holy Nidaba, got the measuring rod,
“ And tied about her arm the lapis measuring line,
“ Proclaiming all the great meh, she fixes the borders, marks off the boundaries,
“ And is now the scribe of the land,
“ Feeding the gods has been put in her hand.
        
“ Nanse, the noble nin, at whose feet the holy u-bird stands,
“ She is now the customs inspector of the sea.
“ Good fish and tasty birds, she grants her father Enlil in Nippur.


“ Me, the greatest woman, why did you treat in a different way?
“ I, the Holy Inanna, where are my functions?”


Enki answered his daughter, the Holy Inanna:


“ What did I keep from you?
“ Innin, what did I keep from you?
“ What more could we add to you?                        


“ You proclaim the voice of women
“ The diadem has been adorned for you, as holy garment.        


“ You put on the garment, the strength of the young man,
“ You introduced the words of the young man, spoken words.


“ I put women's speech in your mouth
“ I placed in your hands the spindle and the hairpin. 
“ You were allowed to settle with the crook, the staff, the wand of shepherdship.


“ Young Inanna, what did I keep from you? What more could we add to you?                


“ You interpret the oracular omens of battlefields and combatants,
“ You are no raven, but recite the ill-omened words in their midst,
“ You have twisted the straight thread.
“ Young Inanna, you have straightened the twisted thread.


“ You put on a garment there, you dressed yourself in linen there,
“ I wove the mug-cloth there, I threaded the spindle there,
“ And you spun with it!
“ In your honor, I bestowed you the dyed multi-colored woven thread.


“ Yet Inanna, you have heaped up heads like dust,
“ You have cast heads about like seeds.
“ Inanna, you have destroyed what cannot be destroyed;
“ You have conceived the inconceivable.
“ You have removed the cloth from the sem of lamentations.
“ Young Inanna, you have turned the tigi and the adab back to their houses
“ You who do not weary the eye of your suitors.


“ Young Inanna, you who know nothing of fastening the ropes of distant wells
“ Do not set up your meh against me!


“ The lapis-lazuli headdress of kur is your prerogative, 
“ Ninship is your prerogative, 
“ Sumer is your prerogative, 
“ My command is your prerogative."
Enki and The Goddess of the Mountain:
 In those days, in the days when heaven and earth were created, in those years, when the fates were determined, when the Anuna-gods were born, when the goddesses were taken in marriage, when the gods and goddesses were distributed in heaven and earth, when the goddesses laid with the gods, became pregnant and gave birth, when the gods were obliged to grow their food, and work for their meals, the senior gods oversaw the work, while the minor gods were bearing the toil. 


The gods were digging the canals over the lands and piling up the silt in Harali. The gods, dredging the clay, began complaining about this life.


Enki lay on his bed, not waking up from his sleep, in the deep Engur, in the flowing water, the place the inside of which no other god knows. The gods cried out to Namma, the primeval mother who gave birth to the senior gods. 


They lamented that Enki was the cause of their suffering. The unseen goddess, the hidden one, took the tears of the gods to the one who lay sleeping. She went to the one who did not wake up from his bed.


She spoke to her son: 


" Are you really lying there asleep, and dreaming, 
“ Are you not awake? 
“ The gods, your creatures, are smashing their hands to the earth.
 
“ My son, wake up from your bed! 


“ Please, apply the skill deriving from your wisdom and create a substitute for the gods,
“ So that they can be freed from their toil."


 At the word of his mother Namma, Enki rose up from his bed. Upon waking he found himself in Halan-kug, his room for pondering. He slapped his thigh in annoyance, the wise and intelligent one, the prudent one, master of skills, the fashioner of the design of everything, who brought to life goddesses of birth giving. 


Enki reached out his arm over them and turned his attention to them. After Enki had pondered the matter, he said to his mother Namma: 


“ My mother, the creature you planned will really come into existence.
“ Impose on him the work of carrying baskets. 
“ You should knead clay from the top of the Abzu; 
“ The birth-goddesses will nip off the clay and you shall bring the form into existence. 
“ Let Ninmah act as your assistant; let Ninimma, Cu-zi-ana, Ninmada, Ninbarag, 
“ Ninmug, Ninti and Ninguna stand by as you give birth. 
“ My mother, after you have decreed his fate, 
“ Let Ninmah impose on him the work of carrying baskets."


Enki set a feast for his mother Namma and for Ninmah, and for all the princely birth-goddesses. They ate delicate reeds and bread. An, Enlil, and the lord Nudimmud roasted holy sheep.


The senior gods praised him: 


" O’ Lord of Wide Understanding, who is as wise as you?
“ Enki, the great lord, who can equal your actions?
“ Like a corporeal father, you are the one who has the meh of deciding destinies,
“ In fact, you are the meh."
 
Enki and Ninmah drank beer, and their hearts became elated. Ninmah said to Enki: 


" Man's body can be either good or bad
“ And whether I make a fate good or bad depends on my will."


 Enki answered Ninmah:


" I will counterbalance whatever fate, good or bad, that you happen to decide." 


Ninmah took clay from the top of the Abzu in her hand. She fashioned from it, first, a man who could not bend his outstretched weak hands. Enki looked at the man who could not bend his outstretched weak hands, and decreed his fate. He appointed him as a servant of the king.


Second, she fashioned one who turned back the light, a man with constantly opened eyes. Enki looked at the man with constantly opened eyes and decreed his fate, allotting to it the musical arts, making him as the chief singer in the king's presence.


Third, she fashioned one with both feet broken. He was one with paralysed feet. Enki looked at the one with paralysed feet and appointed, for him, the work of metal and the silversmith.


Fourth, she fashioned one who could not hold back his urine. Enki looked at the one who could not hold back his urine and bathed him in enchanted water. He drove out Namtar, the demon, from his body.


Fifth, she fashioned a woman who could not give birth. Enki looked at the woman who could not give birth, and decreed her fate. He made her belong to the queen's household, as a weaver. 


Sixth, she fashioned one with neither penis nor vagina on its body. Enki looked at the one with neither penis nor vagina on its body, he gave it the name Nibru-eunuch, and decreed ,as its fate, to stand before the king.


 Ninmah threw the pinched-off clay from her hand on the ground and a great silence fell. 


The great Lord Enki, said to Ninmah: 


" I have decreed the fates of your creatures and given them their daily bread.
“ Come, now I will fashion somebody for you
“ And you must decree the fate of the newborn one."


Enki devised a shape with a head; two unformed eyes and mouth in its middle. He said to Ninmah:


" Pour ejaculated semen into a woman's womb, 
“ And the woman will give birth to the semen of her womb." 


Ninmah stood by for the newborn. 


The woman brought forth the child in the midst of the gods in that place. A name was given in return. 


This was Umul.


Its head was afflicted. Its place of breath was afflicted. Its eyes were afflicted. Its neck was afflicted. 


It could hardly breathe. Its ribs were shaky. Its lungs were afflicted. Its heart was afflicted. Its bowels, too, were afflicted. 


With its hand and its lolling head, it could not not put bread into its mouth. Its spine and head were dislocated. The weak hips and the shaky feet could not carry it to the field.


The noble Lord Enki fashioned it in this way.


Enki said to Ninmah: 


" For your creatures I have decreed a fate,
“ I have given them their daily bread. 
“ Now, you should decree a fate for my creature,
“ Give him its daily bread as well." 


Ninmah looked at Umul and turned to him. She went nearer to Umul, and asked him questions, but he could not speak. She offered him bread to eat, but he could not reach out for it. He could not lie on its back. He could not sit on its bottom. 
 
Ninmah answered Enki: 


" The man you have fashioned is neither alive nor dead.
“ He cannot support himself."


Enki answered Ninmah:                


" I decreed a fate for the first man with the weak hands, 
“ I gave him bread. 


“ I decreed a fate for the man who turned back the light, 
“ I gave him bread. 


“ I decreed a fate for the man with broken, paralysed feet, 
“ I gave him bread. 


“ I decreed a fate for the man who could not hold back his urine, 
“ I gave him bread. 


“ I decreed a fate for the woman who could not give birth, 
“ I gave her bread.


“ I decreed the fate for the one with neither penis nor vagina on its body,
“ I gave it bread. 


“ My sister, can nothing be done for this lowly one?" 


 Ninmah answered Enki:


" You entered my city, and gave me command. 
“ I have made man as you requested, yet you make mockery of me.


“ Look! 


“ You do not dwell in heaven, you do not dwell on earth, 
“ You do not come out to look at the land!


“ Where my house is built, your words cannot be heard.
“ Where my city is built, I myself am silenced.
“ My city is ruined, my house is destroyed, 
“ My child has been taken captive.


“ I am a fugitive who has had to leave the E-kur, 
“ And even I myself could not escape from your hand."


Ninmah could not rival the great Lord Enki. She grew frustrated with the lord's challenge. 


Enki replied to Ninmah:


" Who could change the words that left your mouth? 
“ Remove Umul from your lap. 
“ Ninmah, may your work be holy, 


“ You fashioned for me what is imperfect; who can oppose this? 


“ The man whom I shaped will know no other after you 


“ Let him dwell with me, let him pray to you! 


“ Today let my penis be praised,
“ And may your wisdom be confirmed! 
“ May the Enkum and Ninkum , proclaim your glory,
“ My sister, the heroic strength of Hursag,
“ The song of your name, be praised, 
“ The writing of your deed engraved.


“ The gods, who heard, supplicate you
“ Let Umul build my house."


Enki rose up from his bed. Upon waking he found himself in the deep Engur, and slapped his thigh in annoyance. The wise and intelligent one, the prudent one, master of skills, the fashioner of the design of everything, brought to life mankind. 


The debate between Sheep and Grain:


When, upon the hill of heaven and earth, An spawned the Anuna-gods. He neither spawned nor created grain with them. In the land, he neither fashioned the yarn of Uttu nor pegged out the loom for Uttu.


With no sheep appearing, there were no numerous lambs; and, with no goats, there were no numerous kids. The sheep did not give birth to her twin lambs. The goat did not give birth to her triplet kids. The Anuna, the Great Gods, did not even know the names Ezina-Kusu or Sheep.


There was no muc grain of thirty days. There was no muc grain of forty days. There was no muc grain of fifty days. There was no small grain, grain from the mountains, or grain from the holy habitations. 


There was no cloth to wear, as Uttu had not been born. No royal turban was worn; for lord Nijir-si, the precious lord, had not been born. Cakkan had not gone out into the barren lands. 


The people of those days did not know about eating bread. They did not know about wearing clothes. They went about with naked limbs in the land. Like sheep, they ate grass with their mouths. They drank water from the ditches.


After that time, at the place of the gods' formation, in their own home, on the Holy Mound, the gods created sheep and grain. Having gathered them in the divine banqueting chamber, the Anuna-gods of the Holy Mound partook of the bounty of sheep and grain but were not sated. The Anuna-gods of the Holy Mound partook of the sweet milk of their holy sheepfold but were not sated. 


Enlil lifted his gaze around as a stag lifts its horns when climbing the terraced mountain hills. He looked southwards and saw the wide sea. He looked northwards and saw the mountain of aromatic cedars. 


Enlil piled up the barley. He gave it to the mountain. He piled up the bounty of the land, and gave the innuha barley to the mountain. He closed off access to the wide-open hill, bolted shut its lock, which heaven and earth shut fast, its bolt, which barred entry.


Then Ninazu saw, and said to his brother Ninmada: 


" Let us go to the mountain, to the mountain where barley and flax grow; 
“ There past the rolling river, where the water wells up from the earth. 
“ Let us fetch the barley down from its mountain, 
“ Let us introduce the innuha barley into Sumer. 
“ Let us make barley known in Sumer, which knows no barley."


Ninmada, the worshipper of An, replied to him: 


" Since our father has not given the command, 
“ Since Enlil has not given the command, 
“ How can we go there to the mountain? 
“ How can we bring down the barley from its mountain? 
“ How can we introduce the innuha grain into Sumer? 
“ How can we make barley known in Sumer, which knows no barley?


Then Ninazu said to his brother: 


"Come, let us go to Utu of heaven, to the hero, the son of Ningal, 
“ Who lies there and sleeps a sound sleep." 


Ninazu raised his hands towards Utu of the seventy doors , and the brothers directed their steps toward the Holy Mound from the land below. They talked with Utu at the table, and in turn Utu went and spoke to Enki.


For their own well-being in the holy sheepfold, they gave sheep and grain to mankind as sustenance.


At that time Enki spoke to Enlil:


" Father Enlil, sheep and grain have been created on the Holy Mound,
“ Now, let us send them down from the Holy Mound." 


Enki and Enlil, having spoken their holy word, sent sheep and grain down from the Holy Mound.


Sheep being fenced in by her sheepfold, was given grass and herbs generously. For Grain there was made a field; and she was given the plough, yoke, and team. Sheep, standing in her sheepfold, was given a shepherd of the sheepfolds who brimmed with charm. Grain standing in her furrow was a beautiful girl, radiating charm. Lifting her raised head up from the field she was suffused with the bounty of heaven. 


Sheep and Grain had a radiant appearance.


They brought wealth to the assembly. They brought sustenance to the land. They fulfilled the ordinances of the gods. They filled the store-rooms of the land with stock, the barns of the land were heavy with them. 


When they entered the homes of the poor, who crouch in the dust, they brought wealth. Both of them, wherever they directed their steps, added to the riches of the household with their weight. Where they stood, they were satisfying; where they settled, they were seemly. They gladdened the heart of An and the heart of Enlil.


They drank sweet wine, and enjoyed sweet beer. Then, a quarrel started, concerning the arable fields. Sheep and Grain began to quarrel. They began a debate in the dining hall.


Grain called out to Sheep:


" Sister, I am your better;
“ I take precedence over you.
“ I am the glory of the lights of the land.
“ I grant my power to the sajursaj
“ He fills the palace with awe, and people spread his fame to the borders of the land.


“ I am the gift of the Anuna-gods, and am central to all princes.
“ After I have conferred my power on the warrior,
“ He goes to war and knows no fear,
“ He knows no faltering
“ And he goes to the battlefield, as if to the playing field.


" I foster neighbourliness and friendliness
“ I sort out quarrels started between neighbours
“ When I come upon a captive youth and give him his destiny,
“ He forgets his despondent heart and I release his fetters and shackles.


“ I am Ezina-Kusu; I am Enlil's daughter
“ In sheep shacks and milking pens scattered on the high plain,
“ What can you put against me?
“ Answer me what you can reply!"


Thereupon, Sheep answered Grain: 


" My sister, whatever are you saying?
“ An, king of the gods, made me descend from the holy place,
“ My most precious place. 
“ All the yarns of Uttu, the splendour of kingship, belong to me.


“ Cakkan, King of the Mountain, embosses the king's emblems, 
“ And he puts his implements in order;
“ He twists a giant rope against the great peaks of the rebel land,
“ He girds the sling, the quiver and the longbows.


" The watch over the elite troops is mine,
“ Sustenance of the workers in the field is mine,
“ The waterskin of cool water and the sandals are mine.
“ Sweet oil, the fragrance of the gods, mixed oil, pressed oil,
“ Aromatic oil, and cedar oil for offerings are mine.
" In the gown, my cloth of white wool, the king rejoices on his throne.
“ My body glistens on the flesh of the great gods.


“ After the purification priests, the incantation priests,
“ And the bathed priests have dressed themselves in me, for my holy lustration,
“ I walk with them to my holy meal.


“ But, your harrow, your ploughshare, your binding and strap,
“These are tools that can be utterly destroyed
“ What can you put against me?
“ Answer me what you can reply!"


Again, Grain addressed Sheep:


" When the beer dough has been carefully prepared in the oven,
“ And the mash tended in the oven, Ninkasi mixes them for me.
“ All while your big billy-goats and rams are despatched for my banquets
“ And on their thick legs, they are made to stand separate from my produce


" Your shepherd on the high plain eyes my produce enviously;
“ When I am standing in the furrow in the field,
“ My farmer chases away your herdsman with his cudgel.
“ Even when they look out for you, from the open country to the hidden places,
“ Your fears are not removed from you.
“ Fanged snakes and bandits, the creatures of the desert,
“ Want your life on the high plain.


" Every night your count is made and your tally-stick put into the ground,
“ Your herdsman can tell people how many ewes there are,
“ And how many young lambs, 
“ How many goats and how many young kids.


“ When gentle winds blow through the city and strong winds scatter,
“ They build a milking pen for you;
“ But when gentle winds blow through the city and strong winds scatter,
“ I stand up as an equal to Ickur.


“ I am Grain,
“ I am born for the warrior;
“ I do not give up!


“ The churn, the vat on legs, the adornments of shepherding,
“ These make up your properties.
“ What can you put against me?
“ Answer me what you can reply!"


And again, Sheep answered Grain:


" You, like Holy Inana of heaven, love horses.


“ When a banished enemy, a slave from the mountains
“ Or a labourer with a poor wife and small children comes,
“ You’re bound with his rope of one cubit, and taken to the threshing-floor
“ Or you are taken away from the threshing-floor,
“ And his cudgel pounds your face,
“ He pounds your mouth, as a pestle grounds your ears. 


“ You stand tall in fields and you are thrown around by the south wind,
“ And the north wind.


“ The mortar, as if you were pumice, makes your body into flour.


" When you fill the trough
“ The baker's assistant mixes you and throws you on the floor,
“ And the baker's girl flattens you out broadly.
“ You are put into the oven and you are taken out of the oven.


“ When you are put on the table, I am before you,
“ You are behind me.


“ Grain, heed yourself!
“ You too, just like me, are meant to be eaten.


“ At the inspection of your essence, why should it be I who comes second?
“ Is the miller not evil?
“ What can you put against me?
“ Answer me what you can reply!"


Then, Grain was hurt in her pride, and hastened for the verdict. Grain answered Sheep: 


" As for you, 
“ Ickur is your master, Cakkan your herdsman, and the dry land your bed,
“ Like fire beaten down in houses and in fields,
“ Like small flying birds chased from the door of a house,
“ You are turned into the lame and the weak of the land.
“ Should I really bow my neck before you?


“ You are distributed into various measuring-containers
“ When your innards are taken away by the people in the market-place,
“ And when your neck is wrapped with your very own loincloth,
“ One man says to another: 


“" Fill the measuring-container with grain for my ewe!."”


Then Enki spoke to Enlil:


" Father Enlil,
“ Sheep and Grain should be sisters, they should stand together
“ Of their threefold metal, their glory shall not cease


“ But of the two, Grain shall be the greater.
“ Let Sheep fall on her knees before Grain,
“ Let her kiss the feet of grain;
“ From sunrise till sunset, may the name of Grain be praised.


“ People should submit to the yoke of Grain.
“ Whoever has silver, whoever has jewels, whoever has cattle,
“ Whoever has sheep shall take a seat at the gate of whoever has grain,
“ And he shall pass his time there."


Dispute spoken between Sheep and Grain: Sheep was left behind and Grain came forward.


Chapter 4
Dream of Dumuzi:


Dumuzid's heart was full of tears as he went out into the countryside. He carried with him his shepherd's stick on his shoulder, sobbing. He could feel his death upon him, though he did not know why. 


He cried for his mother. He cried for his sister. He cried to be remembered. He cried for his life.


In ancient times, the shepherd lay down to dream. 


He woke up; it was a dream! 


He shivered; it was sleep! 


He rubbed his eyes. 


He was terrified.


Dumuzi’s mouth gave cry. He made hurried calls for his sister, proficient scribe, verse in tablet, expert singer of songs, diviner of dreams, Geshtinanna.


He told her of his dream, how the rushes kept growing, attempting to devour him. A single reed shook at him. A single branch of a twin reed was being torn away from him. Trees grew tall over him, casting their words against him in judgement. 


Water was poured over his holy coals. The cover of his holy churn was being removed. His holy drinking cup was torn down from the peg where it hung. His shepherd's stick was stolen. 


An owl  took a lamb from the sheep house, a falcon caught a sparrow on the reed fence. His male goats were dragging their dark beards in the dust for him. His rams were scratching the earth with their thick legs for him. 


The churns lay on their side. No milk was poured. The drinking cups were lying on their side. Dumuzi then knew he was dead, haunting sheepfold. 


Geshtinanna silenced Dumuzi, having heard enough. She told him the meaning of his dream:


“ The single reed shaking its head at you, it is your mother who bore you
“ Shaking her head for you. 


“ The twin reeds, of which one was being separated from you,
“ It is you and I; I will be separated from you. 


“ The tall trees in the forest rising up together over you
“ They are the evil men catching you within the walls. 


“ That water was poured over your holy coals 
“ Means the sheepfold will become a house of silence. 


“ That the cover of your holy churn was being removed for you
“ Means the evil man will bring it inside in his hands. 


“ Your holy drinking cup being torn down from the peg where it hung
“ It is you falling off the lap of the mother who bore you. 


“ That your shepherd's stick disappeared from you means the demons will smash it. 


“ The owl taking a lamb from the sheep house
“ It is the evil man who will destroy the sheep house. 


“ The falcon catching a sparrow on the reed fence
“ It is the big demon coming down from the sheep house. 


“ That the churns were lying on their sides, no milk was poured, 
“ The drinking cups were lying on their sides, 
” That Dumuzi was dead, and the sheepfold haunted, 
“ It means your hands will be bound in handcuffs, 
“ Your arms will be bound in fetters. 


“ That your male goats were dragging their dark beards in the dust for you 
“ Means that my hair will whirl around in the air like a hurricane for you. 


“ That your rams were scratching the earth with their thick legs for you 
“ Means that I shall lacerate my cheeks with my fingernails for you
“ As if with a boxwood needle”


Hardly before she finished, Dumuzi gave her command. She was sent up onto a mound to search for ones that sought him. He gave instructions to her that she should go up to the mound and mark herself like the dead. Her heart, liver, clothes, and crotch were to be lacerated.


The evil demons, hated by men, were to arrive by a river barge. They held in their hands the wood to bind the hands, and they were to be identified from the wood to bind the neck, no man knew how to undo it.


Geshtinanna went up onto the mound and looked around. She craned her neck. Her girlfriend, Geshtindudu, advised her that the men had already made their way into the land. She agreed to alert Geshtinanna of the men she sought, and Geshtinanna sent Dumuzi away to hide in the grass.


Dumuzi hid, leaving his sister, telling her that if the men come to the fields, he will hide in the ditches of Arali.


Geshtinanna spoke. To him she promised:


" If I reveal your whereabouts to them, 
“ May your dog devour me! 
“ The black dog, your shepherd dog,
“  The noble dog, your lordly dog, 


“ May your dog devour me!"


        Dumuzi came across his friend on the way to the field. Dumuzi attempted to hurry, but his friend would not let him pass. Dumuzi knew the voice of his brother but could not make out his face. 


With little time Dumuzi spoke:


" My friend, I will duck down my head in the grass. 
“ Don't reveal my whereabouts to them,
“ I will drop down into the ditches of Arali! 


“ Don't reveal my whereabouts to them!" 


Dumuzi’s friend addressed him:


" If I reveal your whereabouts to them, 
“ May your dog devour me! 
“ The black dog, your shepherd dog,
“  The noble dog, your lordly dog, 


“ May your dog devour me!"


Those who came for the king were a motley crew. They knew not food, nor drink. They were those that ate no sprinkled flour, who drank no poured water, who would accept no pleasant gifts. They were those who do not enjoy a wife's embrace, who never kissed dear little children. They were men who never chew sharp tasting garlic, who eat no fish, and who eat no leeks. 


There were two men of Adab who came for the king. They were thistles in dried-up waters, they were thorns in stinking waters .There were two men of Akshak who came for the king, with nets carried on their shoulders. 


Then, there were two men of Unug who came for the king, with head-smashing clubs tied to their waists. There were two men of Urim who came for the king, with shining clothes on the quayside. There were two men of Nibru who came for the king. 


Crying, man ran after man.


They caught Geshtinanna at the sheepfold and cow-pen. 


They offered a river of water, but she would not accept it. They offered her a field of grain, but she would not accept it. The little demon spoke to the big demon, the wise demon, the lively demon, and the big demon who was between them. 


Wise like the Anunnaki, they spoke:


" Who since the most ancient times has ever known
“ A sister to reveal a brother's whereabouts? 


“Come, let us go to his friend!" 


Then, they offered his friend a river of water, and he accepted it. They offered him a field of grain, and he accepted it. 


The friend spoke with them. Dumuzi was betrayed:


" My friend ducked down his head in the grass, but I don't know his whereabouts." 


They looked for Dumuzi's head in the grass, but they couldn't find him. The friend led them to the short grass, when they could not find him. They looked for him in the short grass, but they could not find him.


They caught Dumuzi in the ditches of Arali. He began to weep and was tear-stricken.


Dumuzi’s mouth gave cry:


" In the city my sister saved my life, yet my friend caused my death. 


“ If a sister leaves a child in the street, someone should kiss it. 
“ But if a friend leaves a child in the street, no one should kiss it."


The men surrounded him and drained the standing waters. They twisted a cord for him, they knotted a net for him. They wove a reed hawser for him, they cut sticks for him. The one in front of him threw missiles at him, the one behind him beat his back, leaving cuts of one cubit. His hands were bound in handcuffs, his arms were bound in fetters. 


The lad raised his hands heavenward to Utu:


" Utu, you are my brother-in-law, I am your sister's husband,
