It is that son who is hostile toward his father. He grew up and reached adulthood. He no longer looks at his father. 


It is he whom his father's gods have made accursed.


Tablet IIII: 


Leave that tale, and I will tell you another tale. Hear my instructions.


A dog ran off with a kugulla-bread from in front of an oven. He pulled it out of the oven and dropped it in oil. In oil he dropped it, sat down, and he began to eat it. 


This is not a dog, but a human being!


It is he whom his lord makes the lord of an administrative unit. He took increased tribute, behind the back of that city. He became very discontented. 


He no longer looks after the city. The citizens inform on him before his lord. He began to pour out, before his lord, those items of tribute, that which he was continually swallowing. 


Tablet V:


Leave that story, and I will tell you another tale. I will tell you an instructive example. 


A builder built a tower for glory. He dug its foundation trenches deep down to the Sun-Goddess of the Netherworld. He brought its battlements up, near to the sky. 


The foolish wall began to curse the one who built it:


" If only the arm of him who built me would be broken,
“ If only his right arm muscles would be paralyzed!" 


The builder heard and became sad at heart. The builder said to himself: 


" Why is the wall which I built cursing me?" 


Then the builder uttered a curse on the tower:


" Let Tessub strike the tower, 
“ Let him expose its foundation stones upon it,
“ Let its battlements fall down into the ditch,
“ Let its brickwork fall down into the river." 


This is not a tower, but a human being! 


It is that son, who is hostile towards his father. He grew up and reached maturity. He no longer regards his father. 


Therefore, his father's gods have made him accursed.


Tablet VI:


Now, leave that tale, and I will tell you a final story. 


Wood laid upon a saw; they have been split up. A donkey carried it away. A stacker makes a wood pile next to a canal. Its bottom reaches to the netherworld. Its top reaches up to heaven. 


The foolish wood cursed the man who stacked it:


" If only the arm of him who built me would be broken,
“ If only his right arm muscles would be paralyzed!"


The stacker heard the word, and it became unpleasant in his heart. The stacker said to himself: 


" Why is this wood which I stacked cursing me?


The stacker uttered a curse on the wood: 


" Let Tessub strike it, the wood,
“ May it fall into the irrigation ditch.
“ Let its bark be scattered in the water." 


This isn't a wood pile. 


It is a human being!


It is he, that apprentice, who belittles his master. He grew up and reached a mature age. He belittles his master, but he will die in a ditch!  


Like a dog, he will die under a chair. 


The Warden - Hymn to Nergal:


The hero, majestic awe-inspiring son of Enlil, battered like a storm and roared against the rebel lands. The storm went forth, immense at his front. At his rear was a surging flood.


 The lord inspired awe, in heaven and on earth. He, who is resplendent in the land. Engendered for kingship by his father, his awesomeness weighed upon the black-headed. The Anuna, the Great Gods, cowered together before his awesomeness and radiance.


After his father begat the hero, Father Enlil, bestowed on him the Mountain of the Earth and all of the people. He definitively handed the deciding of destinies to the hero, Nergal, the king!


Lord Meclamta-Eda inspired terrifying awe. His magnificent strength did not release the hostile land. His luxuriant horn resounded in the land. 


He struck the rebel lands with weapons!


The king, sired by Enlil, great dragon, hero, flood of battle; he was placed on the dias. He was positioned in his heroic strength.


Raising his head, he was authoritative with a ringing voice. Knowing the divine powers of the gods, as Judge of the Land, he travelled through heaven and organised everything.


When angered, he took command of the storm which flattens the hostile land. He poured dust over its evil. He poured it over, for as long as it disobeyed. The great hero of the lands spoke justice.


He went forth, where no foot had previously been placed. Pouring out, like a vessel with seven spouts, the black-headed gathered together at the front. He outpaced the pigeons; with his stride, he outpaced the black and white locusts.


He stood in the hushed streets, with terrifying awesomeness covering the city. The majestic, and just, crown radiated awesomeness. The wind was a south-wind that none could withstand. 


At the place of the queen, the most precious place, he exercised the role of supreme deity. Directing a noble gaze, he exercised kingship in the land.


For his father, he had led the people from afar. Nergal had brought them to the realm of the netherworld, the land where the sun sets.


Erra welcomed him as king: 


" They have come, you surpass An. Perform the stewardship for An, the King. 
“ In accordance with destiny, you determine fates with him, Nergal." 


Nincubur, the minister of the great place, the underworld, greeted Nergal: 


" You are the lord who has made the bandits come forth from the mountains.
“ As with Enlil, the Great Mountain, no part of a foreign land escapes your grasp.


“ Hero,
“ For Enlil, you piled up Enlil's enemies in a single day.
“ Hating evil, Nergal, as fire you rise up in the lands where the sun rises." 


The Anuna gods stepped forward: 


" You are Nergal!
“ Lord, who imposes silence, son of Enlil
“ Who in his heroism, like a flood, demands respect! 
“ May the people of the land spread on your pure table, everything you need. 
“ Nergal, may you stretch forth your arm over the land


“ Nergal, lord, you are the enlil of this land 
“ Like Alla, you have no rival;
“ Like Ictaran, it has been given to you to reach correct judgments.


“ Enlil, Shepherd of the Black-Headed, has been given you as your name
“ Lord Who Leads Away the Enemies of Enlil, 
“ Avenger of his Father, Strong Hero


“ The gods have determined your fate, they have given this to you;


“ On earth you are king, in heaven you are king.


“ Because of your noise, no god opposes you 
“ Just young man, who brandishes the plague,
“ Provider for the place, Lagac 
“ Meclamta-eda, may people praise you in song!


“ Lord of the Just Word, Lord of Abundance, hero!.
“ At your name, may people obey.
“ As you rise up in the frightening shrine, with your kingship,
“ May you inspire terrifying fear!”


The hero, with magnificent strength, piled up the rebel lands in heaps. 


Nergal! 


His name is praised in song.


The lady, An's daughter; the lady who loved her city, Bau. She concerned herself with him, in Irikug, her city of ladyship. She was made his appointed sukkal famous for a reign of distant days.


Praise; Nergal, Son of Enlil, Lugal-Era 




Atrahasis:
Tablet I: Council:


He was furious. Enlil was furious with the Igigi:


“ We, the great Anunnaki, all of us agreed together on a plan
“ Anu and Adad were to guard above
“ I was to guard the earth
“ Where Enki went,
“ He was to undo the chain and set us free.
“ He was to release abundance for the people,
“ He was to exercise control,
“ He was to hold the balance.”
 
Enlil made his voice heard and spoke to the vizier Nusku:


“ Have the fifty lahmu-heroes fetched for me! 
“ Have them brought into my presence!”
 
The fifty lahmu-heroes were fetched for him. 


The warrior Enlil addressed the assembly:


“ The noise of mankind has become too much,
“ Sleep cannot overtake me because of their racket.
“ Command that Anu and Adad keep the air above earth locked,
“ Sin and Nergal keep the middle earth locked. 
“ As for the bolt that bars the sea, Ea with his lahmu-creatures shall keep it locked”


Anu and Adad kept the air above earth locked. Sin and Nergal kept the middle, the earth, locked. The bolt that bars the sea, Ea with his lahmu-creatures, kept it locked. 


Ziusudra wept daily. He would carry a massakku-offering along the riverside pasture; although the irrigation-water was silent. Half-way through the night he offered a sacrifice. 


Sleep began to overtake him. He addressed the irrigation-water: 


“ May the irrigation-water take it, may the river carry it,
“ May the gift be placed in front of Ea, my lord.
“ May Ea see it and think of me, so I may see a dream in the night.” 


When he had sent the message by water, he sat facing the river, and wept. The man wept facing the river, as his plea went down to the Apsu. Then Ea heard his voice. He summoned his lahmu-creatures and addressed them. 


The bolt that barred the seas was undone. Adad made his rain pour down.


Then, the god grew anxious as he sat. 


In the assembly, worry gnawed at him. Enki grew anxious, in the assembly of the gods. 


They were shouting. They were furious with each other, Enki and Enlil:


“ We, the great Anunna, all of us agreed together on a plan.
“ Anu and Adad were to guard above,
“ I was to guard the earth.
“ Where you went, you were to undo the chain and set us free.
“ You were to release abundance for the people.
“ You were to exercise control, and to hold the balance.


“ It would seem, 
“ You imposed your loads on man,
“ You bestowed noise on mankind,
“ You slaughtered a god together with his intelligence,
“ So, you must go and create a flood.


“ It is indeed your power that shall be used against your people!
“ You agreed to the wrong plan, a bad plan!
“ Have it reversed!”


“ Let us make far-sighted Enki swear, here and now, an oath.”


Enki made his voice heard and spoke to his brother gods:


“ Why should you make me swear an oath? 
“ Why should I use my power against my people?
“ The flood that you mention to me.
“ What is it? I don't even know!


“ Could I give birth to a flood?


“ That is Ellil's kind of work!
“ Let him choose 
“ Let Shullat and Hanish march ahead
“ Let Errakal pull out the mooring poles
“ Let Ninurta march, let him make the weirs overflow.”


Enki’s pleas were ignored. The gods were divided. Enlil cried for his weapons and the assembly cowered. 


They cast off Enki’s reasoning. They did not listen. The gods gave explicit command. Enlil performed a bad deed to the people.


Tablet II: The Great Deluge:


Ziusudra made his voice heard and spoke to his master:


“ Indicate to me the meaning of the dream,
“ Let me find out its portent.”


Enki made his voice heard and spoke to his servant:


“ You say,


 “" I should find out in bed””  


“ Make sure you attend to the message I shall tell you.


“ Wall, listen constantly to me!
“ Reed hut, make sure you attend to all my words!:


“ Dismantle the house, build a boat, 
“ Reject possessions, and save living things.


“ The boat that you build 
“ Roof it like the Abzu.
“ Seal it so that the sun may not see inside
“ Make upper decks and make lower decks
“ The tackle must be very strong
“ The bitumen, also strong, to give strength.


“ I shall make rain fall on you here,
“ Gather a wealth of birds, a hamper of fish.
“ At the stated time of which I will inform thee, 
“ Enter the ship and close the door.
“ Aboard her bring thy grain, thy possessions, thy goods,
“ Thy wife, thy family, thy relations, and the craftsmen. 


“ Beasts of the field, creatures of the field, as many as eat herbs, 
“ I will send to thee, and they shall guard thy door." 


Ziusudra opened his mouth to speak, saying to Enki, his lord:


" I have never built a ship.
“ Nor have I laid eyes on one as such described,
“ Draw a design of it on the ground; 
“ That seeing the design, I may build the ship.
 
Enki drew the design on the ground saying:


 " Here is what thou hast commanded." 


He opened the sand clock and filled it. He said the sand needed for the flood was seven nights worth. 


Ziusudra received the message. He gathered the elders and the seniors at his door. Ziusudra made his voice heard. He addressed the elders: 


“ My god is out of favour with your god!


“ Enki and Ellil  have become angry with each other.
“ They have driven me out of my house! 
“ Since I always stand in awe of Enki,
“ He has told me of this matter.


“ I can no longer stay in the house of my father,
“ I cannot set my foot on Ellil's territory again. 
“ I must go down to the Apsu and stay with my god,
“ This is what he told me.”


The elders gathered the people of the city, and provided wisdom for the assembly. 


The carpenter brought his axe. The reed worker bright his stone. A child brought bitumen. The poor man fetched what was needed. Everything there was prepared perfectly. Everything there was waiting. 


Pure and fat ones, he selected and put on board. The birds that take to the sky, the cattle of Shakkan, wild beasts of the steppes, he put them on board.


He invited his people to join him. 


The people arrived to a feast. Ziusudra, put his family on board. They were eating and drinking, but in and out he went. He could not stay still, or rest on his haunches. His heart was breaking. He was vomiting bile.


The face of the weather changed. 


Adad bellowed from the clouds. The time was nigh. 


When Ziusudra heard this noise, bitumen was brought and he sealed the door. Adad’s bellowing grew louder as the door closed. The winds were raging as he went up. He cut through the rope to release the boat.  


Anzu was tearing at the sky with his talons. Quingu struck out at the land. He broke the gate of the Abzu. The flood roared forth. The kasusu-weapon went against the people like an army. 


No one could see anyone else. No one could see their own hands. They could not be recognized in the catastrophe.


The flood roared like a bull. Like a wild ass screaming, the winds howled. 


The darkness was total. There was no sun. The torrent was white, like a stampede of sheep. The calamity of the flood poured out.


The noise was thunderous. The clamour went up to heaven. 


An went berserk. The gods cried, his sons cried before him. 


As for Nintu, the Great Mistress, her lips became encrusted with rime. The Great Gods, the Anunna, stayed parched and famished.


The goddess watched and wept. She spoke, Midwife of the Gods, wise Mami made words: 


“ Let daylight return!


“ However could I, in the assembly of the gods,
“ Have ordered such destruction with them?


“ Enlil was strong enough to give such a wicked order,
“ Like Tiruru, he ought to have cancelled that wicked order! 


“ I heard their cry leveled at me,
“ Against myself, against my person. 
“ Beyond my control my offspring have become like white sheep.
“ As for me, how am I to live in a house of bereavement?


“ My noise has turned to silence.
“ Could I go away, up to the sky and live, as in a cloister? 


“ What was Anu's intention as decision-maker?
“ It was his command that the gods his sons obeyed,
“ He who did not deliberate, but sent the flood,
“ He who gathered the people to catastrophe”


Nintu was now wailing:


“ Would a true father have given birth to the rolling sea? 
“ So that they could clog the river like dragonflies? 


“ They are washed up, like a raft on a bank,


“ They are washed up, like a raft on a bank in open country! 
“ I have seen, and wept over them!
“ Shall I ever finish weeping for them?”


She wept. 


She gave vent to her feelings. Nintu wept and fuelled her passions. 


The gods wept with her for the country. She was sated with grief, but she longed for beer in vain. 


Where she sat weeping, there the great gods sat too. Like sheep, they could only fill their windpipes with bleating. Thirsty as they were, their lips discharged only the rime of famine. For seven days and seven nights The torrent, storm and flood came on.


Six days and six nights the flood winds blew. The south-storm swept the land . When the seventh day arrived, The flood carrying south wind subsided in its battle; which it had fought like an army. 


The sea grew silent. The tempest was still; the flood ceased . 


The weather calmed; stillness had set in. All of mankind had returned to clay, save for the occupants of the boat. The landscape was as level as a flat roof. 


The hatch opened, and light fell on his face; tears ran down his cheeks.


There, on Mount Nisir, the ship came to a halt . Mount Nisir held the ship fast, allowing no motion. One day passed.


When the seventh day arrived, a dove was set free and sent forth. The dove went forth, but came back. Since no resting-place for it was visible, she turned round. 


Then, a swallow was sent forth and set free. The swallow went forth, but came back.


Then, a raven. A raven was set free. A raven was sent forth. The raven went forth and saw the waters had diminished. He ate, circled, cawed, and turned not round. 


To all the four winds, Ziusudra offered a sacrifice . He poured out a libation on the top of the mountain . Seven bowls and seven cult-vessels he set up. Upon their pot-stands was heaped cane, cedarwood, and myrtle. 


The gods smelled the savour. The gods smelt the sweet fragrance. They gathered like flies over the offering. Nintu got up and blamed them all:


“ Whatever came over Anu, who makes the decisions? 
“ Did Enlil dare to come for the smoke offering? 
“ The two who did not deliberate, but sent the flood gathered the people to catastrophe,
“ You agreed to the destruction.


“ Now, their bright faces are dark forever!”


 She then went up to the big flies which Anu had made, and declared before the gods: 


“ His grief is mine. My destiny goes with his.
“ He must deliver me from evil, and appease me!


“ Let me go out in the morning,
“ Let these flies be the lapis lazuli of my necklace, 
“ By which I may remember it daily forever.” 


The warrior Enlil spotted the boat. He became furious with the Igigi. 


“ We, the great Anunnaki, all of us, agreed together on an oath,
“ No form of life should have escaped!
“ How did any man survive the catastrophe?” 


Anu made his voice heard and spoke to the warrior Enlil:


“ Who but Enki would do this?
“ He made sure that the reed hut disclosed the order.”


 Enki made his voice heard and spoke to the great gods:


“ I did it, in defiance of you!
“ I made sure life was preserved.


“ Henceforth, exact your punishment from the sinner.
“ And whoever contradicts your order 
“ Be lenient, lest he be cut off, be patient, lest he be dislodged.


“ Instead of thy bringing on the deluge,
“ Would that a lion had risen up to diminish mankind.
“ Instead of thy bringing on the deluge, 
“ Would that a wolf had risen up to diminish mankind.
“ Instead of thy bringing on the deluge,
“ Would that a famine had risen up to lay low mankind.
“ Instead of thy bringing on the deluge,
“ Would that pestilence had risen up to smite down mankind. 
“ I have given vent to my feelings!


“ Lo’, It was not I who disclosed the secret of the great gods .
“ I let him see a dream,and he perceived the secret of the gods .
“ Now then take counsel in regard to him!” 


Enlil made his voice heard and spoke to far-sighted Enki:


“ Come, summon Nintu the womb-goddess,
“ Confer with each other in the assembly. 


Enki made his voice heard and spoke to the womb-goddess Nintu,


“ You are the womb-goddess who decrees destinies.
“ Decree good fate to the people.
“ Let one-third of them be born healthy
“ Let another third of them be  born sickly.


“ In addition, let there be one-third among the people,
“ Of the woman, who yet does gives birth, not give birth successfully;


“ Let there be the pasittu-demon among the people,
“ To snatch the baby from its mother's lap. 


“ Establish ugbabtu, entu, egisitu-women,
“ They shall be taboo, and thus control childbirth.”


Enlil went aboard the ship. Holding Ziusudra by the hand, he went aboard. Enlil took his wife aboard and made her kneel by Ziusudra’s side. Standing between them, he touched their foreheads to bless them:


“ Hitherto, Ziusudra had been but human.
“ Henceforth, he and his wife shall be like unto us gods.
“ Atrahasis shall reside far away, at the mouth of the rivers.”


 Thus they took him to the land of blessed splendour, at the mouth of rivers, Dilmun.








Nergal and Ereshkigal
Tablet I: Feast


Anu made his voice heard and spoke, he addressed his words to Kakka,


" Kakka, I shall send you to Kurnugi, you must speak thus to Ereshkigal,
“ Say these words: 


“ It is impossible for you to come up in your year,
” You cannot come up to see us and it is impossible for us to go down
” In our months, we cannot go down to see you


” Let your messenger come, take from the table, 
“ Let him accept a present for you
” I shall give something to him to present to you.'


Kakka went down the long stairway of heaven. When he reached the gate of Ereshkigal, he said:


" Gatekeeper, open the gate to me!"
        
The gatekeeper greeted the messenger:


" Kakka, come in, and may the gate bless you."


He let the god Kakka in through the first gate. He let the god Kakka in through the second gate. He let the god Kakka in through the third gate. 


He let the god Kakka in through the fourth gate. He let the god Kakka in through the fifth gate. He let the god Kakka in through the sixth gate; and he let the god Kakka in through the seventh gate.


He entered there. Kakka entered into her spacious courtyard. He knelt down and kissed the ground in front of her. Then, he straightened up, he stood and addressed her:


" Anu, your father, sent me, I am to say:


““ It is impossible for you to go up, in your year you cannot go up to see us,
“” And it is impossible for us to go down, in our month, we cannot go down to see you


“” Let your messenger come, take from the table, 
“” Let him accept a present for you
“” I shall give something to him to present to you,"'


Ereshkigal made her voice heard and spake her words to Kakka:


" O’ messenger of Anu, our father, you who have come to us,
“ May peace be with Anu, Ellil, and Ea, the Great Gods
“ May peace be with Nammu and Nanshe, the Pure Goddesses
“ May peace be with the Husband of the Lady of Heaven
“ May peace be with Ninurta, champion in the land"


Kakka made his voice heard and spake, he addressed his words to Ereshkigal,


" Peace is indeed with Anu, Ellil, and Ea, the great gods.
“ Peace is indeed with Nammu and Nanshe the pure.
“ Peace is indeed with the husband of the Lady of Heaven.
“ Peace is indeed with Ninurta, champion in the land."


Kakka made his voice heard and spake, he addressed his words to Ereshkigal,


" As all may be well with you."


Ereshkigal made her voice heard. She addressed her words to her vizier Namtar:


" O’ Namtar my vizier, I shall send you to the Heaven of Our Father Anu.


“ Namtar, go up the long stairway of heaven,
“ Take from the table and accept a present for me.


“ Whatever Anu gives to you, you must present to me."


Then, from the courtyard of Ereshkigal, the two viziers set out for the realm of heaven.


Ea addressed Nergal:


" Can you not see it, a path laid before you?
“ The gods are all kneeling together before him, 
“ The great gods, the lords of destiny honor him,
“ For it is he who controls the rites, 


“ He, who controls the rites of death!


“ The god who dwells within Irkalla, why do you not kneel before him?


“ I keep winking at you, but you pretend no to realize,
“ And now, all the gods look your way 


“Namtar grows angry!”


Nergal spoke, in return:


“ What concern is that to me, why should I do him obeisance?


“ I shall go, I will rise to my feet,
“ I will take up the rite you spoke of
“ I will bring them up!"


When Ea heard this he rejoiced to himself.


Then Ea made his voice heard and spoke. He addressed his words to Nergal:


" My son, you shall go on the journey you want to make,
“ Be off,


“ Grasp your sword in hand. and go down to the forest of the mesu-trees
“ Cut down mesu-trees, tiaru-trees, and juniper-trees,
“ Break off kanaktu-trees and simberru-trees."


When Nergal heard this, he took an axe up in his hand; and, he drew the sword from his belt. He went down to the forest of mesu-trees. As ordered, he cut down mesu-trees, tiaru-trees, and junipers. He went down the forest and broke off kanaktu-trees and simberru-trees.


Then, he made a throne; working before Far-Sighted Ea. He painted it with alabaster paste as a substitute for silver. Painted it with yellow paste and red paste as a substitute for gold. Painted it with blue glaze as a substitute for lapis lazuli. The work was finished, the chair complete.


Then, Ea called out and laid down instructions for him:


" My son, about the journey which you want to make
“ From the moment you arrive, follow whatever instructions I give you!
“ From the moment they bring a chair to you,
“ Do not go to it, do not sit upon it, the throne!


“ When the baker brings you bread, do not go to it, do not eat the bread.
“ When the butcher brings you meat, do not go to it, do not eat the meat.
“ When the brewer brings you beer, do not go to it, do not drink the beer.
“ When they bring you a foot bath, do not go to it, do not wash your feet.
“ When Ereshkigal has been to the bath and dressed herself in a fine robe,
“ If she allows you to glimpse her body, you must not do that which men and women do."


Tablet II: Irkalla


Nergal set his face toward Kurnugi, to the dark house, dwelling of Erkalla's god. He set his face to the house which those who enter cannot leave. On the road, where travelling is one way only. He set his face to the house, where those who enter are deprived of light.


Dust is their food. Clay their bread. They are clothed, like birds, with feathers. They see no light. They dwell in darkness. They moan like doves.


The gatekeeper opened his mouth and addressed his words to Nergal:


" I must take back a report about the god standing at the door."


The gatekeeper entered and addressed his words to Ereshkigal:


" My lady, a guest has come to see us.”


Namtar spoke up promptly.


“ I will go, I will identify him."


Ereshkigal made her voice heard and spoke to Namtar:


" Why trouble yourself with the matter?"


Namtar gave answer:


" Oh, my lady, let me identify him, let me meet him at the outer gate,
“ Let me bring back, to my lord, a description of him."


Namtar went and looked at Erra, standing in the shadow of the door. Namtar's face went livid, as a cut tamarisk. His lips grew dark, as the rim of a kuninu-vessel. Namtar went and addressed his lady:


" My lady, when you sent me to your father, when I entered the courtyard of Anu,
“ All the gods were kneeling, humbled before him,
“ All the gods of the land were kneeling humbled before him.
“ Then, a god rose to their feet in my presence,
“ And now they have come down to Kurnugi."


Ereshkigal addressed her words to Namtar:


" My dear Namtar, you should not seek enlil-power,
“ Nor should you desire to do heroic deeds.
“ Why, come up and sit on the throne of the royal dais?


“ You, perform the judgments of the broad earth?


“ Should I go up to the heaven of Anu my father?
“ Should I eat the bread of the Anunnaki?
“ Should I drink the water of the Anunnaki?
“ Go and bring the god into my presence!"


Namtar went and let in the god, Erra.


He let Nergal in through the first, the Gate of Nedu. He let Nergal in through the second, the Gate of Enkishar. He let Nergal in through the third, the Gate of Endashurimma. He let Nergal in through the fourth, the Gate of Enuralla. He let Nergal in through the fifth, the Gate of Endukuga. He let Nergal in through the sixth, the Gate of Endushuba. He let Nergal in through the seventh, the Gate of En-nugigi.


He came into the broad courtyard. He knelt down, kissed the ground in front of her. He straightened up, stood and addressed her:


" Anu, your father, sent me to see you, saying: 


“” Sit down on this throne, judge the cases of the great gods,
“” The great gods who live within Erkalla.'"


As soon as they brought him to a throne, he did not go to it; he did not sit on it. When the baker brought him bread, he did not go to it; he did not eat the bread. When the butcher brought him meat, he did not go to it; he did not eat the meat. When the brewer brought him beer, he did not go to it; he did not drink the beer. When they brought him a footbath, he did not go to it; and did not wash his feet.


Then, dressed in a fine robe, she went to the bath. She allowed him to catch a glimpse of her body. He resisted his heart's desire to do what men and women do.


Nergal sat upon his chair. He took up the rites of the dead, the revered glory of Namtar. 


When the baker brought him bread, he did not eat the bread, for bread was his offering. When the butcher brought him meat, he did not eat the meat, for the offering of meat was made. When the brewer brought him beer, he did not drink the beer, for they poured beer for him daily. When they brought him a footbath, he did not wash his feet, for the priest kept his image clean.


Then, she went to the bath. Dressed in a fine robe, she allowed him to catch a glimpse of her body. He gave in to his heart's desire to do what men and women do. 


The two embraced each other and went passionately to bed.


They lay there, Queen Ereshkigal and Erra, for a first day and a second day. They lay there, Queen Ereshkigal and Erra, for a third day and a fourth day. They lay there, Queen Ereshkigal and Erra, for a fifth day and a sixth day. 


When the seventh day arrived, Nergal, without word, took his leave. He readied his mouth to speak, but he spoke only to himself:


" Let me go, and my sister, I shall return; do not make the foundations tremble.
“ Let me go now, and I will return to Kurnugi later."


Her mouth turned dark with rage. Her eyes ran with tears. Nergal went and made his voice heard and spoke. He addressed his speech to the gatekeeper:


" Ereshkigal your lady sent me, saying: 


“” I am sending you to the Heaven of Anu, Our Father””


“ So let me be allowed out!
“ I must deliver a message!"


Nergal came up along the long stairway of heaven. When he arrived at the gate of Anu, Ellil, and Ea, they, Anu, Ellil, and Ea saw him and said:


" The Son of Ishtar has come back to us,
“ Ereshkigal will search for him and drag him back.


“ Ea, his father, must sprinkle him with spring water, 
“ And bareheaded, blinking and cringing
“ Let him sit in the assembly of the gods."


Nergal cried to Ea:


“ Ereshkigal went to the bath, I glimpsed her body and she called out to me.”


" The chair?“


Ea asked him, but the throne remained in the underworld.


“ Sprinkle the room with the water of heaven
“ Sprinkle the room with the water of middle heaven
“ Sprinkle the room with the water of the earth
“ The bring the throne of the two daughters of Lamashtu and Enmesharra,
“ Sprinkle with the waters of purity
“ The messenger of Anu, Our Father, who came to see us, shall be arriving,
“ To eat our bread and drink our water."


Ea commanded and thus it was so.


Tablet III: Return


Namtar made his voice heard and spoke. He addressed his words to Ereshkigal, his lady:


" The messenger of Anu, Our Father, who came to see us,
“ Before daylight, he disappeared!"


Ereshkigal cried aloud, grievously. She fell from the throne to the ground. Then, she straightened up from the ground. Her tears flowed down her cheeks:


" Erra, the lover of my delight,
“ I did not have enough delight with him before he left.


“ Erra, the love of my delight.
“ I did not have enough delight with him before he left."


Namtar made his voice heard. He addressed his words to Ereshkigal:


" Send me to Anu, your father, and let me arrest the god.
“ Let me take him to you, that he may kiss you again!"


Ereshkigal addressed her words to Namtar her vizier:


" Go, Namtar, you must speak to Anu, Ellil, and Ea!
“ Set your face towards the gate of Anu, Ellil, and Ea, to say: 


” Ever since I was a child and a daughter,
” I have not known the playing of other girls,
” I have not known the romping of children.


” That god whom you sent to me, and who has impregnated me
” Let him sleep with me again!
” Send that god to us, and let him spend the night with me as my lover.


” I am unclean, 
” And I am not pure enough to perform the judging of the great gods,
” The great gods, who dwell within Erkalla.


” If you do not send that god to me, according to the rites of Erkalla and the Great Earth,
” I shall raise up the dead, and they will eat the living.
” I shall make the dead outnumber the living!”


Namtar came up the long stairway of heaven. When he arrived at the gate of Anu, Ellil, and Ea; they Anu, Ellil, and Ea saw him and said,


" What have you come for, Namtar?"


" Your daughter sent me to say:


“” Ever since I was a child and a daughter,
“” I have not known the playing of other girls,
“” I have not known the romping of children.


“” That         god whom you sent to me, and who has impregnated me,
“” Let him sleep with me again!
“” Send that god to us, and let him spend the night with me as my lover.


“” I am unclean, and I am not pure enough to perform the judging of the great gods,
“” The great gods, who dwell within Erkalla.


“” If you do not send that god to me according to the rites of Erkalla and the Great Earth,
“” I shall raise up the dead, and they will eat the living.
”” I shall make the dead outnumber the living!'"


Ea made his voice heard and addressed his words to Namtar,


" Enter, Namtar, enter the court of Anu,
“ Search out your wrongdoer and bring him!"


When he entered the court of Anu, all the gods were kneeling humbly before him. 


He went straight up to one, but did not recognize that god. He went straight up to a second and a third, but did not recognize that god either.


Namtar returned, and addressed his words to his lady,


" My lady, about your sending me up to the Heaven of Anu, your father,
“ My lady, there was only one god 
“ Who sat bareheaded, blinking and cringing at the assembly"


Ereshkigal cried out:


" Go, seize that god and bring him to me!


“ Ea, his father sprinkled him with spring water,
“ And he is sitting in the assembly of all the gods bareheaded, blinking, and cringing."


Namtar came up the long stairway of heaven. When he reached the gate of Anu, Ellil, and Ea;  Anu, Ellil, Ea saw him and said:


" What have you come for, Namtar?"
" Your daughter sent me, to say: 


“” Seize that god and bring him to me.'"


Then entered Namtar; into the courtyard of Anu. He searched out the wrongdoer to take him.


He went straight up to one god, but did not recognize him. He went straight up to a second and third, but did not recognize him either. Then he made his voice heard and spoke. He addressed his words to Ea:


“ The one I seek, I do not find”


Ea proclaimed aloud:


" Let Namtar, the messenger who has come to us,
“ Drink our water, wash, and anoint himself."


Namtar refused. He addressed his words to Ea:


“ Bring me the one I seek and I shall be off.”


Again Ea replied:


" Let Namtar, the messenger who has come to us,
“ Drink our water, wash, and anoint himself."
“ And I shall send him.


" He is not to strip off his garments, 
“ Erra, will go to your queen
“ I shall send him"


Namtar made his voice heard. He addressed his words to Erra:


" Erra, shall go then, all the rites of the great underworld shall be his


“ When you go from the assembly
“ You shall carry the throne, you shall carry the staff and the sceptre,
“ You shall carry the diadem, and you shall carry the rites .


“ The gatekeeper! Do not grapple with him lest he bind your chest."


Erra took to heart the speech of Namtar. He took an axe up in his hand. He drew the sword from his belt. He oiled his strap and slung his bow. 


Nergal went down the long stairway of heaven. When he arrived at the gate of Ereshkigal he said:


" Gatekeeper, open the gate!"


He struck down Nedu, the doorman of the first gate, and did not let him grapple with him. He struck down the second doorman, and did not let him grapple with him. He struck down the third doorman, and did not let him grapple with him. He struck down the fourth doorman, and did not let him grapple with him. 


He struck down the fifth doorman, and did not let him grapple with him. He struck down the sixth doorman, and did not let him grapple with him. He struck down the seventh doorman. He did not let him grapple with him.


He entered Ereshkigal’s wide courtyard. He went up to her and laughed. He seized her by her hairdo, and pulled her from the throne. He seized her by her tresses and pinned her to the ground.


The two embraced each other, and went passionately to bed.


They lay there, Queen Ereshkigal and Erra, for a first day and a second day. They lay there, Queen Ereshkigal and Erra, for a third day. They lay there, Queen Ereshkigal and Erra, for a fourth day. They lay there, Queen Ereshkigal and Erra, for a fifth day. They lay there, Queen Ereshkigal and Erra, for a sixth day.


When the seventh day arrived, Anu made his voice heard and spoke. He addressed his words to Kakka, his vizier,


" Kakka, I shall send you to Kurnugi,
“ To the home of Ereshkigal, who dwells within Erkalla, to say:


" That god, whom I sent you, he cannot remain there forever.
” Those above rites of the dead, those below rites of the dead,
” Those lustrous rites Erra now holds.


” He must come up to render judgement,
“ He must go below to render judgement.


” Let him rise and dwell in his heavenly abode for half the year,
” And with his mistress below he shall dwell for the other.


” And so it shall be his destiny!”


Ninurta and The Turtle:


He was angry. An was upset. He called for the king, the hero, Ninurta. 


An paced his quarters. When the young hero, Lord Ninurta, arrived, An made his voice heard. The Anzu address his words to Ninurta:


“ At his command your weapon struck me evilly.
“ As I let the meh go out of my hand,
“ These meh returned to the Abzu.


“ As I let the divine plan go out of my hand,
“ This divine plan returned to the Abzu.


“ The Tablet of Destinies returned to the Abzu.
“ I was stripped of the meh.


Then, An spoke to the warrior, saying:


“ Be off, go now, 
“ Return the meh.


“ For, I need send no other.
“ You, my most mighty son, wreck his dwelling.
“ Let terror thunder above him,
“ Lock him there with those Primeval Gods!


“ Whenceforth, let your name be greater than his 
“ Let you truly be counted as my equal!”


These words pleased Ninurta, and he began to  rejoice within his heart. He took up his bow. Girded on his strap, oiled with the finest of oil. He took up his mace in his right hand, and summoned that fearful, shrieking, wind.


The bird, Anzu, took the Hero Ninurta by his hand and drew near with him to Enki's dwelling, the Abzu.The bird, Anzu, returned Uta-ulu to the Abzu. 


The lord was delighted with the hero, Father Enki was delighted with the Hero Ninurta.


The Lord Nudimmud honoured him duly: 


“ Hero, no god, among your brother gods, could have acted so. 
“ As for the bird which your mighty weapon captured, 
“ From now to eternity you will keep your foot placed on its neck. 


“ May the great gods give your heroic strength its due. 
“ May your father, Enlil, do whatever you command. 
“ May Ninmena not fashion for you, your equal. 
“ May no one be as awesome as you 
“ And no god extend an upraised hand before you. 


“ Monthly may your house regularly receive tributes in the shrine, in the Abzu. 
“ May An proclaim your name in the seat of honour.”


The hero secretly was not happy with these promises. Where he stood, he darkened and yellowed like a flood-storm. He contemplated great deeds and inwardly he was rebellious. 


He uttered a word which no god had spoken. 


The Hero Ninurta set his sights on the whole world.
 
The great lord, Enki, intuitively grasped the substance of the plan. In the shrine, in the Abzu, he stirred up a dark flood-storm. By the house the minister Isimud opposed the hero. The Hero Ninurta refused to come out and raised his hand against the minister Isimud, striking him evilly.


Enki fashioned a turtle from the clay of the Abzu, and stationed the turtle at an opening, at the gate of the palace shrine. Enki talked to him near the place of the ambush, and brought him to where the turtle was. The turtle was able to grab Ninurta‘s tendon from behind. 


Enki, as if perplexed, said: 


“ What is this?”' 


He had the turtle scrape the ground with its claws, and it dug an evil pit. The hero fell into it with the turtle. Trapped, the turtle kept on gnawing his feet, and digging with its claws .


The great, Lord Enki, said to him:


“ You who set your mind to kill me,
“ You think it is I who makes big claims 


“ I cut down forests, I raise mountains. 


“ You who set your sights on me like this 
“ What has your position seized for you, 
“ How shall you destroy the one who fights back? 


“ Where has your strength fled? 


“ Where is your heroism? 


“ In the great mountains you caused destruction,
“ But how will you get out now?”


Ninmena learned of this situation. She ripped the clothes on her body and she wailed. Quickly, she went to him. She set her face to the Abzu; she went to confront Enki:
 
“ You, my plant-eater Enki, who shall I send to you?
“ Men will shake their heads in fear at your corpse.


“ Who shall I send to you?


“ That name is not Enki.
“ That name is Ugugu-That-Does-Not-Pour.
“ You who are death which has no mercy,


“ Who shall I send to you?


“ You who are so wise
“ Yet act like you know nothing!


“ Who shall I send you to?


“ If not for your plans, would none of this have happened?
“ Let him be free, let there be another punishment
“ A righteous one!”


Chapter 7


Remains of Kengir:
The Instructions of Shuruppag:
Tablet I:


In those days, those far remote days. In those nights, those faraway nights. In those years, those far remote years. At that time the wise one, who knew how to speak in elaborate words, lived in the land. 


Curuppag, the wise one, who knew how to speak with elaborate words, gave instructions to his son. Curuppag, the son of Ubarat-utu, gave instructions to his son Zi-ud-sura:


“ My son, let me give you instructions, you should pay attention!
“ Zi-ud-sura, let me speak a word to you
“ Do not neglect my instructions, do not transgress the words I speak.
“ The instructions of an old man are precious; you should comply with them.


“ The need for food makes some people ascend the mountains;
“ It also brings traitors and foreigners,
“ Since the need for food brings down other people from the mountains.”


“ A small city provides its king with a calf, a huge city digs foundations,
“ A good city is well equipped, and the married man is well equipped;
“ The unmarried, makes his bed in a haystack.”


“ The poor man inflicts all kinds of illnesses on the rich man.”


“ He who wishes to destroy a house will go ahead and destroy the house;
“ He who wishes to raise up will go ahead and raise up.”


“ You should not speak arrogantly to your mother, that causes hatred for you.
“ You should not question the words of your mother and your personal god.
“ The mother, like Utu, gives birth to the man; the father, like a god, makes him bright.
“ The father is like a god, his words are reliable.
“ The instructions of the father should be complied with.”


“ The elder brother is indeed like a father; the elder sister is indeed like a mother.
“ Listen therefore to your elder brother,
“ And you should be obedient to your elder sister, as if she were your mother.”


“ The negligent one ruins his family.”


“ A woman with her own property ruins the house.”


“ Property is something to be expanded; 
“ But, nothing can equal my little ones.”


“ Without suburbs a city has no centre.”


“ You should not worry unduly about what leaves the house.”


“ A female burglar, she flies into the houses like a fly.
“ A she-donkey brays on the street, a sow suckles its child on the street.
“ A woman who pricked herself begins to cry,
“ And she holds the spindle which pricked her in her hand.
“ She enters every house; she peers into all streets, wailing and screaming 
“ She keeps saying: 


“" Get out!"” 


“ She looks around from all parapets, she pants where there is a quarrel.”

“ You should not make a young man, best man.
“ You should not be the young man, yourself.
“ You should not play around with a married young woman,
“ The slander could be serious.
“ My son, you should not sit alone in a chamber with a married woman.”


“ One appoints a reliable woman for a good household.”


“ You should not abduct a wife; you should not make her cry.
“ The place where the wife is abducted to, will be cursed.
“ She will make you run in circles, saying:


“" Oh, my foot, oh, my neck!"”.




“ You should not choose a wife during a festival.
“ Her inside is illusory; her outside is illusory.
“ The silver on her is borrowed; the lapis lazuli on her is borrowed. 
“ The jewellery on her is borrowed; the dress on her is borrowed.
“ The linen garment on her is borrowed.
“ With nothing, see if she is comparable.”


“ You tell your son to come to your home;
“ You tell your daughter to go to her women's quarters.”


“ A loving heart maintains a family; a hateful heart destroys a family.”


“ A weak wife is always seized by fate.
“ And he who works with leather will eventually work with his own leather.”


“ As for you,
“ You should not work using only your eyes;
“ You will not multiply your possessions using only your mouth.”


“ Heaven is far, and earth is most precious,
“ But it is with heaven that you multiply your goods,
“ All foreign lands breathe under it.”


“ You should not locate a field on a road; 
“ You should not plough a field at a road or a path; 
“ You should not make a well in your field,
“ People will cause damage on it for you.”


“ You should not place your house next to a public square
“ There is always a crowd there.”


“ My son, a field situated at the bottom of the embankments,
“ Be it wet or dry, is nevertheless a source of income.”


“ At harvest time, at the most priceless time,
“ Collect like a slave girl, eat like a queen.
“ My son, to collect like a slave girl, to eat like a queen,
“ This is how it should be.”


“ If you hire a worker, he will share the bread bag with you;
“ He eats with you from the same bag, and finishes up the bag with you.
“ Then he will quit working with you, saying:


“" I have to live on something"”,


“ He will serve at the palace.”




Curuppag gave these instructions to his son. Curuppag, the son of Ubara-Tutu, gave these instructions to his son Zi-ud-sura.


Tablet II:


A second time, Curuppag gave instructions to his son. Curuppag, the son of Ubara-Tutu, gave instructions to his son Zi-ud-sura:


“ My son, let me give you instructions; you should pay attention!
“ Zi-ud-sura, let me speak a word to you
“ Do not neglect my instructions! Do not transgress the words I speak!
“ The instructions of an old man are precious; you should comply with them.”


“ You should not draw up water which you cannot reach,
“ That which is dredged up from the bottom, it will make you weak.”


“ You should not scatter your sheep into unknown pastures.”


“ You should not hire someone's ox for an uncertain journey.
“ A safe route means a safe journey.”


“ You should not buy an onager, it lasts only until the end of the day.”


“ You should not buy a donkey which brays; it will split your skull,
“ And make your midriff unpleasant”.


“ You should not buy a donkey at the time of harvest.”
“ A donkey which eats at the harvest will quarrel with another donkey.”

“ You should not buy a wild bull, you should not buy a vicious bull; 
“ It will gore a hole in the cattle-pen.”


“ A vicious donkey hangs its neck;
“ However, a vicious man, my son, cuts his own neck”


“” Having reached the field of manhood,
“ You should not jump forward with your hand outstretched.”


“ The warrior is unique, he alone is the equal of many;
“ Utu is unique, he alone is the equal of many.
“ With your life you should always be on the side of the warrior;
“ With your life you should always be on the side of Utu.”


“ The beer-drinking mouth should be mildly tempered.
“ My little one. The beer-drinking mouth should not be overfilled.
“ Your own man will not repay it for you. 
“ The reed-beds are thin, they can hide not the slander.
“ The palace is like a mighty river, its middle is goring bulls;
“ What flows in is never enough to fill it, and what flows out can never be stopped.”


“ You should not pass judgement when you drink beer.”


“ A drunkard will drown the harvest.”


“ You should not boast in beer halls, or breweries, like a deceitful man
“ Then your words will be trusted”


“ You, yourself should not curse strongly, it rebounds on you.”


“ You should not speak improperly, later it will lay a trap for you.”


“ My words of prayer bring abundance, prayer is cool water that cools the heart.
“ Only insults and stupid speaking receive the attention of the land.”


“ The artistic mouth recites words; the harsh mouth brings litigation documents;
“ The sweet mouth gathers sweet herbs.”


“ The garrulous fills his bread bag; the haughty one brings an empty bag,
“ And can fill his empty mouth only with boasting.”


“ The liar, shouting, tears up his garments.
“ Insults bring advice to the wicked; 
“ To speak arrogantly is like an abscess:
“ Like a herb that makes the stomach sick, it bitters the tongue.”


“ One who insults can hurt only the skin. Greedy eyes, however, can kill.”


“ You should not steal anything; you should not take what is not for yourself.
“ You should not break into a house; you should not wish for the money chest.
“ A thief is a lion,
“ But after he has been caught, he will be a slave
“. My son, you should not commit robbery; you should not cut yourself with an axe.”


“ Nothing at all is to be valued, but life should be sweet.
“ You should not serve things, things should serve you. 
“ My son, you should not count the pieces of grain, its heaps are numerous."


“ You should not eat stolen food with anyone.
“ You should not sink your hand into ill-gotten blood.
“ After you have apportioned the bones, you will be made to restore the ox,
“ You will be made to restore the sheep.”


“ When it is about someone's else bread, it is easy to say:


“" I will give it to you."”,


“ But the time of actual giving can be as far away as the sky.
“ If you go after the man who said:


“" I will give it to you”",


“ He will say: 


“" I cannot give it to you, the bread has just been finished up."”


“ You should not drive away a debtor, he will be hostile towards you.
“ You should not drive away a powerful or strong man;
“ You should not destroy the outer wall.
“ You should not drive away a young man;
“ You should not make him turn against the city.”


Curuppag gave these instructions to his son. Curuppag, the son of Ubara-Tutu, gave these instructions to his son Zi-ud-sura.


Tablet III:


A third time, Curuppag gave instructions to his son. Curuppag, the son of Ubara-Tutu gave instructions to his son Zi-ud-sura: 


“ My son, let me give you instructions: you should pay attention!
“ Zi-ud-sura, let me speak a word to you
“ Do not neglect my instructions, do not transgress the words I speak!
“ The instructions of an old man are precious, you should comply with them”


“ My son, you should not use violence;
“ You should not commit rape on someone's daughter;
“ The courtyard will learn of it.”


“ You should not abuse a ewe,
“ Otherwise you will give birth to a daughter.
“ You should not throw a lump of earth into the money chest;
“ Otherwise you will give birth to a son.
“ You should not beat a farmer's son:
“ He has constructed your embankments and ditches.”


“ You should not kill a child, 
“ He is a child born by the god’s decree.
“ You should not kill a child nor should you bind him.
“ The wet-nurses in the women's quarters determine the fate of their lord.”


“Yet,  fate is a wet bank; it can make one slip.”


“ You should not vouch for someone: that man will have a hold on you;
“ And you yourself, should not let somebody vouch for you.
“ That man will despise you.
“ You should not make an inspection of a man‘s heart,
“ The  judge will give it back to you.”


“ You should not provide a stranger with food;
“ You should not wipe out another’s quarrel.”


“ You should not loiter about where there is a quarrel;
“ You should not let the quarrel make you a witness.
“ You should not let yourself be in a quarrel.
“ You should not cause a quarrel;
“ You should flee to the gate of the palace, stand aside from the quarrel.
“ You should not take up a quarrel, but another road.


“ You should not pick a quarrel; you should not disgrace yourself.
“ You should not speak lies; and to add further, you should not boast.
“ Then your words will be trusted.”


“ You should not deliberate for too long; you cannot bear their glances.”


“ The eyes of the slanderer always move around as shiftily as a spindle.
“ You should never remain in his presence.
“ His intentions should not be allowed to have an effect on you.”


“ You should not establish a home with an arrogant man,
“ He will make your life like that of a slave girl.
“ You will not be able to travel through any human dwelling without being shouted at:


 “" There you go! There you go!"”


“ You should not undo the binding of the garden's reed fence; 


“" Restore it! Restore it!"” 


“ They will say to you.”


“ You should not buy a prostitute: she is a mouth that bites.
“ You should not buy a house-born slave, he is a herb that makes the stomach sick.
“ You should not buy a free man, he will always lean against the wall.
“ You should not buy a palace slave girl, she will always be the bottom of the barrel.
“ You should, rather, bring down a foreign slave from the mountains.
“ Or you should bring somebody from a place where he is an alien,
“ My son, then, he will pour water for you.
“ Where the sun rises, he will walk before you.
“ He does not belong to any family, so he does not want to go to his family.
“ He does not belong to any city, so he does not want to go to his city.
“ He cannot knock at the door of his kindred, he will not quarrel with you,
“ He will not be presumptuous with you.”


“ When you bring a slave girl from the hills, she brings both good and evil with her.
“ The good is in the hands; the evil is in the heart.
“ The heart does not let go of the good; but the heart cannot let go of the evil either.
“ As if it were a watery place, the heart does not abandon the good.
“ Evil is a store-room that cannot be filled.
        “ May the boat with the evil sink in the river! May its waterskin split in the desert!”


“ You should not have sex with your slave girl,
“ She will chew you up, she will curse you!”


“ The fool loses something. When sleeping, the fool loses something.


“" Do not tie me up!”"  He pleads. 


“" Let me live!”" 


“ He pleads, but the strong one can escape from anyone's hand.”


“ The imprudent decrees fates;
“ The shameless one piles up things in another's lap, saying:


“" I am such that I deserve admiration."”


“ My son, you should not travel alone eastwards.
“ Your acquaintance should not be acquainted with one whom you quarrel.
“ It is a name placed on another one; you should not pile up a mountain on another one.”


“ The palace is like a mighty river: its middle is goring bulls;
“ What flows in is never enough to fill it, and what flows out can never be stopped.
“  By grasping the neck of a huge ox, you can cross the river.
“ By moving along the river at the side of the mighty men of your city,
“ My son, you will certainly ascend the crossing.
“ To have authority, to have possessions and to be steadfast are princely divine powers.
“ You should submit to the respected; you should be humble before the powerful.
“ My son, you will then survive against the wicked.”


“ You should not travel during the nigh,: it can hide both good and evil.”


“ It is inconceivable that something is lost forever.”


“ The land of Dilmun is far,
“ To get lost is bad for a dog,
“ But terrible for a man, on the unfamiliar way, at the edge of the mountains,”


“ The gods of the mountains are man-eaters; they do not build houses there as men do,
“ They do not build cities there as men do, they do not live there as we do.
“ For the shepherd there, he stopped searching, he stopped bringing back the sheep.
“ For the farmer there, he stopped ploughing the field.
“ There is only death there”


This gift of words was something which soothed the mind. When it entered the palace; it soothed the mind. The gift of many words like a shower of stars. These are the instructions given by Curuppag, the son of Ubara-Tutu.


Praise be to the lady who completed the great tablets, the maiden Nisaba, that Curuppag, the son of Ubara-Tutu gave his instructions.


The Farmer's Instructions:


Ud-ul-uru, the old man cultivator, gave advice to his son:


“ When you have to prepare a field,
“ Inspect the levees, canals and mounds that have to be opened.
“ When you let the flood water into the field, this water should not rise too high in it.


“ At the time that the field emerges from the water,
“ Watch its area with standing water; it should be fenced.
“ Do not let cattle herds trample there.


“ After you cut the weeds and establish the limits of the field,
“ Level it repeatedly with a thin hoe weighing two-thirds of a mina.
“ Let a flat hoe erase the oxen tracks, let the field be swept clean.
“ A maul should flatten the furrow bottoms of the area,
“ A hoe should go round the four edges of the field, until the field is dry.
“ It should be smoothed out.


“ Your implements should be ready.
“ The parts of your yoke should be assembled,
“ Your new whip should hang from a nail,
“ The bindings of the handle of your old whip should be repaired by artisans.
“ The adze, drill, and saw, your tools and your strength, should be in good order.
“ Let braided thongs, straps, leather wrappings and whips should be attached securely.
“ Let your sowing basket be checked, and its sides made strong.
“ What you need for the field should be at hand; inspect your work carefully.


“ The plough oxen will have back-up oxen, the attachments of ox to ox should be loose.
“ Each plough will have a back-up plough,
“ The assigned task for one plough is 180 iku, approx. 65 ha. 
“ But if you build the implement at 144 iku, 
“ The work will be pleasantly performed for you. 
“ 180 sila of grain will be spent on each 18 iku area.


“ After working one plough's area with a bardil plough,
“ And after working the bardil plough's area with a tugsig plough,
“ Till it with the tuggur plough; harrow once, twice, then three times.


“ When you flatten the stubborn spots with a heavy maul,
“ The handle of your maul should be securely attached,
“ Otherwise it will not perform as needed.


“ When your field work becomes excessive, you should not neglect your work;
“ No one should have to tell anyone else: 


“" Do your field work!"”


“ When the constellations in the sky are right,
“ Do not be reluctant to take the oxen force to the field many times.
“ The hoe should work everything.


“ When you have to work the field with the seeder-plough,
“ Your plough should be properly adjusted.
“ Put a leather sealing on the kacu of your plough,
“ Provide your beam with narrow pegs,
“ Your boards should be spread, make your furrows.


“ Make eight furrows per ninda of width, (approx. 6 m.)
“ The barley will lodge in more closely spaced furrows.
“ When you have to work the field with the seeder-plough,
“ Keep your eye on the man who drops the seed.
“ The grain should fall two fingers deep,
“ You should put one gij of seed per ninda.


“ If the barley seed is not being inserted into the hollow of the furrow,
“ Change the wedge of your ploughshare.
“ If the bindings become loose, tighten them.


“ Where you have made vertical furrows, make slanted furrows,
“ And where you have made slanted furrows, make vertical furrows.
“ Straight furrows will give you edges that are wide enough and nice.
“ Your crooked furrows should be straightened out.
“ Make the furrows clear, plough your portion of field.
“ The clods should be picked out.
“ The furrows should be made wide where the soil is open,
“ And the furrows should be narrower where the soil is clogged,
“ It is good for the seedlings.


“ After the seedlings break open the ground, perform the rites against mice,
“ Turn away the beaks of small birds.


“ When the plants overflow the narrow bottoms of the furrows,
“ Water them with the water of the first seed. 
“ When the plants resemble a braided reed mat, water them.
“ Water the plants when they are heading.


“ When the plants are fully leafed out, do not water them, 
“ Or they will become infected by leaf rust.
“ When the barley is right for husking, water it,
“ It will provide a yield increase of one sila per ban.
        
“ When you have to reap the barley, do not let the plants become overripe.
“ Harvest at the right time.


“ One man is to cut the barley, and one to tie the sheaves;
“ And one before him should apportion the sheaves,
“ Three men should harvest for you.


“ Do not let those who gather the barley, bruise the grain.
“ They should not scatter the grain when it is in the stacks.


“ Your daily work starts at daybreak.
“ Gather your force of helpers and grain gatherers in sufficient number,
“ And lay down the sheaves. 
“ Your work should be carefully done.
“ Although they have been having stale, coarse, flour,
“ Do not let anyone thresh for your new bread.
“ Let the sheaves have a rest.
“ The rites for the sheaves should be performed daily.
“ When you transport your barley, your barley carriers should handle small amounts.


“ Mark the limits of a vacant lot of yours, establish properly your access paths to it.
“ Your wagons should be in working order; feed the wagon's oxen.
” Your implements should be in good order.


“ Let your prepared threshing floor rest for a few days;
“ When you open the threshing floor, smooth its surface.
“ When you thresh, the teeth of your threshing sledge,
“ And its leather straps,
“ Should be secured with bitumen.
“ When you make the oxen trample the grain, your threshers should be strong.
“ When your grain is spread on the ground, perform the rites of the grain not yet clean.


“ When you winnow,
“ Put an intelligent person as your second winnower.
“ Two people should work at moving the grain around.


“ When the grain is clean, lay it under the measuring stick.
“ Perform the rites in the evening and at night; release the grain at midday.”


The instructions of the god, Ninurta, son of Enlil, the faithful farmer of Enlil.
Advice to a Prince:
  
If a king does not heed justice, his people will be thrown into chaos and his land will be devastated. If he does not heed the justice of his land, Ea, King of Destinies, will alter his destiny and he will not cease from hostilely pursuing him.


If he does not heed his nobles, his life will be cut short. If he does not heed his adviser, his land will rebel against him. 


If he heeds a rogue, the status quo in his land will change. If he heeds a trick of Ea, the Great Gods in unison, and in their just ways, will not cease from prosecuting him.


If he improperly convicts a citizen of Sippar, but acquits a foreigner, Shamash, Judge of Heaven and Earth, will set up a foreign justice in his land. The princes and judges will not heed justice. 


If citizens of Nippur are brought to him for judgement, but he accepts a present and improperly convicts them, Enlil, Lord of the Lands, will bring a foreign army against him to slaughter his army. Prince and chief officers will roam his streets like fighting-cocks.


If he takes silver from the citizens of Babylon and adds it to his own coffers, or if he hears a lawsuit involving men of Babylon, but treats it frivolously, Marduk, Lord of Heaven and earth, will set his foes upon him. He will give his property and wealth to his enemy.


If he imposes a fine on the citizens of Nippur, Sippar or Babylon, or if he puts them in prison, the city where the fine was imposed will be completely overturned. A foreign enemy will make his way into the prison in which they were put.


If he mobilized the whole of Sippar, Nippur and Babylon, and imposed forced labour on the people; exacting from them a corvée at the herald´s proclamation, Marduk, the Sage of the Gods, the Prince, the Counsellor, will turn his land over to his enemy. The troops of his land will do forced labour for his enemy. For Anu, Enlil and Ea, the Great Gods, who dwell in heaven and earth, in their assembly affirmed the freedom of those people from such obligations. 


If he gives the fodder of the citizens of Sippar, Nippur and Babylon to his own steeds, the steeds who eat the fodder will be led away to the enemy´s yoke. Men will be mobilized with the king's men, when the national army is conscripted. Mighty Erra, who goes before his army, will shatter his front line and go at this enemy's side. 


If he looses the yokes of their oxen, and puts them into other fields; or gives them to a foreigner, the fields of their dwelling will be devastated by the deluge of Addu. If he seizes their stock of sheep, Addu, Canal Supervisor of Heaven and Earth, will extirpate his pasturing animals by hinge. He will amass offerings for Shamash.


If the adviser or chief officer of the king's presence, denounces them and so obtains bribes from them, at the command of Ea, King of the Apzu, the adviser chief officer will die by the sword. Their place will be covered over as a run, the wind will carry away their remains. Their achievements will be given over to the storm wind.


If he declares their treaties void, or alters their inscribed treaty stele, if he sends them on a campaign or press-gangs them into hard labour, Nabu, scribe of Esagila, who organizes the whole of heaven and earth, who directs everything, who ordains kingship, will declare the treaties of his land void, and will decree hostility.


If either a shepherd or a temple overseer, or a chief officer of the king, who serves as a temple overseer of Sippar, Nippur or Babylon, imposes forced labour on the people in connection with the temples of the Great Gods, the Great Gods will quit their dwelling, in their fury, and will not enter their shrines. 


Establishment of Agade:
Ningishzida


The hero, lord of field and meadow, lion of the distant mountains, he who was chosen to become king, any youth who had a personal god was at his disposal. Ninjiczida issued his commands. 


He brought together giants, snakes, and dragons. Ningishzida, great wild bull, who, in the murderous battle, was a flood that overtakes the earth.


Ninjirida gave birth from her luxurious body, to he whom is beloved by his mother. He drank the good milk at her holy breast, and sucked in lion's spittle, he, who grew up in the Abzu. 


He was the mighty august icib-priest, who held the holy ecda-vessels. He was the checker of tablets, who secured justice. Now king, the wild bull with tall limbs was director of speech aright. 


He, who hated wickedness, Ninjiczida was mighty power; one whom none dared stop when he spread confusion.


The troops were constantly at service of Mighty Ninjiczida. The shepherd understood how to keep a check on the black-headed. The sheep and lambs came to seek him out. He understood how to wield the sceptre over the goats and kids, into the distant future. 


Ninjiczida, understood how to wield the sceptre, into the distant future.


The Wise King Enki, entrusted him from birth with words of prayer, he let Ninjishzida hold life and creation. The prince endowed with attractiveness, Ninjiczida, took seat on the throne-dais in an elevated location. Lord, god, youth, right arm clothed in holy vestment, with the shining sceptre grasped hand.


The gudu priests performed a shir-namcub song to him: 


" O' king, honeyed mouth of the gods!
“  Hero!
“ You bring calming of the heart to the one who calls out supplicating words. 
“ You fall upon the many foreign lands and you burn them like fire.
“ The king, who is the lord of broad understanding has determined a good destiny
“ For you, on your elevated throne-dais.” 


The god who loves justice had spoken these favourable words: 


" Foremost one, leader of the assembly, Glory of Ninazu, 
“ King endowed with awesomeness; sun of the masses, 
“ Advance in front of them!  


“ Who can rival you in the highest heaven? 
“ What can equal you? 
“ Praise be to Enki. 
“ Ninjiczida, son of Ninazu.
“ Praise be to Father Enki.”


The hero, after surveying the battle, went up to the high mountains. 


Balbale to Ningishzida-


The lord with holy dignity, was imbued with great savage awesomeness. The king, Lord Ninjiczida, hero, the falcon preying on the gods was dignified, with sparkling eyes. He was fully equipped with arrows and quiver. 


His mouth was that of a pure magician. He was a snake with a great tongue, Lord Ninjiczida, the magician, the poisonous snake, the impetuous leopard. The murderous howling muchuc, the dragon snarling in the lagoon, raged as a storm, reaching all people.


The lofty-headed prince rested in the midst of the mountains. 


The hero went about enemy lands, smashing heads! 


He fell upon the river as a flood-wave; he rose in the fields as a devastating flood. In his own heart he carried the magilum boat; he carried the high flood of battle which could chop down lords. 


Linen-clad, when he abandoned the High-Throne, the black-headed people cried: 


“ Who can find out your intentions? 


“ Your holy word is known to them that know it, 
“ But is unknown to them that do not know it.
“ Indeed your great word comes to the earth, as a great muchuc.”


Ningishzida was as a dead man.


Ningishzida's Journey-


The Boatman sung:


" Arise and get on board. Arise, we are about to sail.
 “ Arise and get on board!"


Woe! There was weeping, for the bright daylight, as the barge was steered away.


Ningishzida addressed the Boatman:


" I am a young man, a lugal! 
“ Let me not be covered against my wishes ,
“ Let me not be covered by a cabin, as if with a blanket."


She was stretching out a hand to the barge, to the young man being steered away, to the young man Damu, to Ictaran of the bright visage, to the sukkal Alla, master of the battle-net, being taken away on the barge. Stretching out a hand to Lugal-cud-e, to Ninjiczida being taken away on the barge, his younger sister was crying in lament to him in the boat's cabin.


His older sister removed the cover from the cabin at the boat's stern:
 
" Let me sail away with you, 
“ Let me sail away with you, my brother.
“ My brother, let me sail on your splendid barge with you.” 


She was crying a lament to him at the boat's bow:
 
" Brother, let me sail away with you, let me weep .
“ Let me pray for you in your boat's stern, brother.
 " The gudu-priest sits in the cabin at your boat's stern." 


There was an evil demon who was in their midst; he called: 


" Lugal-ki-suna!”


The evil demon sneered: 


“ Lugal-ki-bura, look at your sister!" 


Having looked at his sister, Ningishzida said to her: 


" He sails with me,
“ Why should you sail, when he sails with me?


“ Lady, the demon sails with me, why should you sail to the underworld? 
“ The thresher sails with me, the man who has bound my hands sails with me. 
“ The man who has tied my arms sails with me. 


" The river of the nether world produces no water, no water is drunk from it.
“ The fields of the nether world produce no grain, no flour is eaten from it. 
“ The sheep of the nether world produce no wool, no cloth is woven from it. 
“ As for me, even if my mother digs, as if for a canal, 
“ I shall not be able to drink the water meant for me. 
“ The waters of springtime will not be poured for me, as they are for the tamarisks.
“ I shall not sit in the shade intended for me.
“ The dates I should bear, like a date palm, they will not reveal their beauty to me. 
“ I am a field threshed by my demon.
“ You would scream at it, as he puts manacles on my hands.
“ You would scream at it, as he  puts a stock on my neck. 
“ Amashilama, my dear sister, you would scream at it."


Amashilama said to Ninjiczida: 


" The ill-intentioned demon!


“ My brother, your demon may accept something. 
“ For him let me pour the wine from my hand.
” For him let me hang from my hips the dainty lapis lazuli beads.
“ You are a beloved son, my brother, how they treat you, 
“ O’ how haughtily they treat you!


" I am hungry, but the bread has slipped away from me!
" I am thirsty, but the water has slipped away from me!"


The evil demon who was in their midst, the clever demon, that great demon called out to the man at the boat's bow and to the man at the boat's stern:
 
" Don't let the mooring stake be pulled out, 
“ So that she may come on board to her brother.


“ This lady may come on board the barge."


When Ama-cilama had gone on board the barge, a cry approached the heavens, a cry approached the earth. That great demon set up an enveloping cry before him on the river: 


" Urim, at my cry to the heavens, lock your houses, 
“ Lock your houses, city! 
“ Lock your houses! 
“ Shrine Urim, lock your houses! 
“ City, lock your houses! 
“ Against your lord who has left the Jipar; 
“ City, lock your houses!"


The sacrifice of his sister freed Lord Ningishzida from his shackles. Returned to him a holy sceptre, a holy robe of office,a holy crown,a lapis-lazuli sceptre.


Enki came to the empty river, the rejoicing river, the wailing river: 


" Demon, you shall not draw near to this house, 
“ They shall go to the place of Ereshkigal. 


“ The mother will come, out of her love,


“ As for you, you may be a great demon, 
“ Raise not, your hand against the nether world's office of throne-bearer.
“ My king will no longer shed tears in his eyes, the drum will direct his joy in tears. 


“ Come!  
“ May the fowler utter a lament for you in his well-stocked house, 
“ Lord, may he utter a lament for you;


“ How he has been humiliated! 


“ May the young fisherman utter a lament for you in his well-stocked house. 
“ How he has been humiliated! 
“ May the mother of the dead gudu-priest utter a lament for you in her empty Jipar, 
“ May she utter a lament for you. 
“ May the mother, high-priestess, utter a lament for you who have left the Jipar 
“ For you, now dead, used to be in your Jipar, 
“ Lord, may she utter a lament for you. 
“ How he has been humiliated!”


Enki commanded:


" My king, bathe, with water, your head that has rolled in the dust. 
“ Dress, in sandals, your feet defiled from the defiled place." 


The king bathed with water, his head that had rolled in the dust. Covered in sandals, his feet defiled from the defiled place. 


The Great Demon sung praise to Ningishzida:
                        
" I will not draw near to this house!
" In this far away place, may your throne give support to you.


“ Sit down! 
“ May your bed give rest to you;


“ Lie down!”
 
He ate food in his mouth, he drank choice wine. 


Chapter 8 - Aysam
The Aratta Saga
Enmerkar and En-suhgir-ana: 
Tablet I:
 
Brickwork rose out from the pristine mountain on the edge of the glistening plain. Kulaba was the city which reached from the Bond of Heaven and of Earth. The city, Unug, was the city whose fame like the rainbow reached up to the sky, as a multicolour sheen, as the new moon standing in the heavens.


Built in magnificence with all the great powers; the lustrous mount was founded on a favourable day. Like moonlight coming up over the land, like bright sunlight radiating over the land, it was the rear cow and bull cow coming forth in abundance. 


All this is Uruk, the glory of which reached the highland. Its radiance like genuine refined silver. It covered Aratta like a garment; it spread over it like linen.


At that time the day was lord, the night was sovereign, and Utu was king.


The name of the lord of Aratta's minister was, Ansiga-ria. The name of the minister of Enmerkar, the Lord of Kulaba, was Namena-tuma. Namena-tuma was minister of he with the knowledge of a lord, he with the title of prince.


Namena-tuma was minister of he with the beauty of a lord, he with the goodness of a prince, he with the splendour of a lord, he with the blood of a prince. 


The man born to be a god, 


The man manifest as a god, 


The lord of Unug,


The lord of Kulaba.


En-suhgir-ana, the Lord of Aratta, was to make a contest with Uruk. He said first to his messenger, concerning the matter:


" Let him submit to me, let him bear my yoke. 
“ If he submits to me, and indeed truly submits to me, 
“ Then as for him, he may dwell with Inanna in the E-Jipar. 
“ But I, I will dwell with Inana in the E-zagin of Aratta. 
“ He may lie with her on the splendid bed, 
“ But I lie in sweet slumber with her on the adorned bed,.
“ He may see dreams of Inanna at night,
“ But I converse with Inana awake!


“ He may feed the geese with barley,
 “ But I, 
“ I will definitely not feed the geese with barley. 
“ I will gather the geese's eggs in a basket and stomp their goslings. 
“ The small ones into my pot, the large ones into my kettle, 
“ And the rulers of the land who submitted will consume, together with me, 
“ That what remains from the geese." 


This is what he said to Enmerkar.


The messenger ran like a wild ram; he took flight like a falcon.
