He left in the morning and returned already at dusk. Like small birds at dawn, he crossed over the open country. Like small birds at midnight, he hid himself in the interior of the mountains. Like a throwing stick, he stood at the side. 


Like a perfect donkey of Shakkan, he ran over mountains. He dashed like a large, powerful donkey. Like a slim donkey, eager to run, he rushed forth. 


A lion in the field at dawn, he let out roars. Like a wolf which has seized a lamb, he darted about. The small places he had reached, he filled with dread for him. The large places he has reached, he crossed their boundary.


He entered the presence of the lord in his holy Jipar, his most holy place. He entered the presence of Enmerkar: 


" My king has sent me to you!  
“ The lord of Aratta, En-suhgir-ana, has sent me to you.
" This is what my king said:”


“" Let you submit to me, let you bear my yoke. 
““ If you submit to me, and indeed truly submit to me, 
““ Then as for you, you may dwell with Inanna in the E-Jipar.
“” But I, I will dwell with Inana in the E-zagin of Aratta.
“” You may lie with her on the splendid bed. 
“” But, I lie in sweet slumber with her on the adorned bed. 
“” You may see dreams of Inanna at night,
“” But I converse with Inana awake!


“” You may feed the geese with barley,
“” But I,
“” I will definitely not feed the geese with barley. 
“” I will gather the geese's eggs in a basket and stomp their goslings. 
“” The small ones into my pot, the large ones into my kettle, 
“” And the rulers of the land who submitted will consume, together with me, 
“” That what remains from the geese."” 




The Lord of Unug, he was their holy sceptre. He was their rudder. He was the neck-stock which clamps down upon them, the brickwork to the place of great foundation. He was their falcon which flies in the sky. He was their bird-net. 


He held the brickwork of the Great Temple of Aratta. He was unaware of such works in Aratta, such great works. Enmerkar thought to bring artisans from the city.


He patted it like a lump of clay, he examined it like a clay-tablet:


" He may dwell with Inana in the E-Zagin of Aratta, 
“ But I dwell with her always, as her earthly companion.
“ He may lie with her in sweet slumber on the adorned bed,
“ But I lie on Inana's splendid bed strewn with pure plants.
“ Its back is an ug-lion, its front is a pirij-lion. 
“ The ug-lion chases the pirij-lion, the pirij-lion chases the ug-lion.
“ The day does not dawn, the night does not pass. 
“ I accompany Inanna for a journey of fifteen leagues,
“ And even Utu the Sun-God cannot see my holy crown.


“ She enters my holy Jipar.
“ Enlil has given me the true crown and sceptre. 
“ Ninurta, the son of Enlil, held me on his lap as the frame held the water-skin.
“ Aruru, the sister of Enlil, extended her right breast to me, then her left breast. 
“ When I go up to the great shrine, the mistress screeches like an Anzud chick, 
“ And other times when I go, she, like a duckling, shrieks. 


“ She dwells in the city of her birth.
“ No city was made to be so well-built as the city of Unug, 
“ It is Unug where Inanna dwells. 
“ Why would she regard Aratta, what does it have to do with this? 
“ It is the brick-built Kulaba where she lives, my mount of lustrous meh, 
“ What should I do about this? 


“ For five or ten years she would definitely not go to Aratta. 
“ Since the Great Holy Lady of the E-ana took counsel with me, 
“ Counsel also about whether to go to Aratta, 
“ Since she told me about this matter, I know now that she will not go to Aratta.


“ He who has nothing shall not feed the geese with barley.
“ I,
“ I will feed the geese with barley! 
“ I will gather the geese's eggs in a basket and gather their goslings.
“ The small ones into my pot, The old ones into my kettle. 
“ The rulers of the land who submitted will consume, together with me, 
“ What remains from the geese."


The messenger of Enmerkar reached En-suhgir-ana in his holy Jipar, his most holy place. 


En-suhgir-ana asked for instruction., He searched for an answer. He summoned the icib-priests, the lumah-priests, the gudu-priests, and girsiga-attendants who dwell in the Jipar. He took counsel with them:


" What shall I say to him? What shall I say to the Lord of Unug, the Lord of Kulaba? 


“ His bull stood up to fight my bull, and the bull of Unug has defeated it.
“ His man has been struggling with my man, and the man of Unug has defeated him.
“ His warrior has been struggling with my warrior 
“ And the warrior of Unug has crushed him."


The convened assembly answered him straightforwardly:
 
" It was you who first sent a boastful message to Unug for Enmerkar. 
“ If you cannot hold back Enmerkar, you have to hold back yourself!


“ Calm down, your heart will prompt you to achieve nothing, as far as can be known." 


En-suhgir-ana roared:


" If my city becomes a ruin mound, then I will be a potsherd of it,
“ But I will never submit to the Lord of Unug, the Lord of Kulaba."
Tablet II:


Ur-jirnuna was a sorcerer whose skill was that of a man of Hamazu. Ur-jirnuna, came over to Aratta after Hamazu had been destroyed. He practised sorcery in the inner chamber at the E-Jipar. 


Ur-jirnuna said to Minister Ansiga-ria:


" My lord, why is it that the great fathers of the city,
“ The founders in earlier times, do not speak; they do not give advice? “


His voice rose:


“ I will make Unug dig canals, I will make Unug submit to the shrine of Aratta.
“ After the word of Unug is captured,
“ I will make the territories, from below to above,
“ From the sea to the cedar mountain, 
“ From above to the mountain of the aromatic cedars,
“ Submit to my great army,


“ Let Unug bring its own goods by boat,
“ Let it tie up boats as a transport flotilla towards the E-Zagin of Aratta."


 The minister En-suhgir-ana rose up in his city. This made the lord extremely happy. 


He gave five minas of gold to him, and five minas of silver. He promised him that he would be allotted fine food to eat; promised him that he would be allotted fine drink to quaff: 


" When their men are taken captive, your life will know happiness.
“ Gold will flow in your hand, you will have enduring prosperity.", 


He promised.


Ur-jirnuna the Sorcerer, farmer of the best seeds, directed his steps towards Eresh, the city of Nisaba. He reached the animal pen, the house where the cows live. 


The cow trembled with fear at him in the animal pen. He made the cow speak so that it conversed with him as if it were a human: 


" Cow, who will eat your butter? Who will drink your milk?" 


The cow answered:


" My butter will be eaten by Nisaba, my milk will be drunk by Nisaba
“ My cheese, skilfully produced bright crown, was made fitting for the great dining hall, 
“ The dining hall of Nisaba. 


“ Until my butter is delivered from the holy animal pen,
“ Until my milk is delivered from the holy byre,
“ The steadfast wild cow Nisaba, the first-born of Enlil,
“ She will not impose any levy on the people." 


A spell was cast:


" Cow, your butter to your shining horn; your milk to your back." 


So the cow's butter was sent to fill its shining horn; its milk was swollen in its back.


Ur-jirnuna reached the holy byre of Nisaba. The goat trembled with fear at him in the byre. He made the goat speak so that it conversed with him as if it were a human being:


" Goat, who will eat your butter? Who will drink your milk?" 


" My butter will be eaten by Nisaba, my milk will be drunk by Nisaba.” 


A spell, a curse:


" Goat, your butter to your shining horn; your milk to your back." 


So the goat's butter bloated its shining horn; its milk was made to depart to its back.


On that day the animal pen and the byre were turned into a house of silence. 


A disaster! 


There was no milk in the udder of the cow. The day darkened for the young calf, who was hungry and wept bitterly. 


There was no milk in the udder of the goat. The day darkened for the kid. The goat and its kid lay starving. 


Its life waning, the cow spoke bitterly to its calf; the goat shouted to its kid. The holy churn was empty. Nisaba was hungry, as the byre and pen lay starving.


On that day the animal pen and the byre were turned into a house of silence; the cow-herder dropped his staff from his hand. The shepherd hung the crook at his side. They wept bitterly. 


The shepherd boy did not enter the byre and animal pen. He took another way. The milk carrier did not sing loudly, but took another road. 


The cow-herder and shepherd of Nisaba, sons born of the same mother, were brought up in the animal pen and byre. The name of the first one was Mac-gula, the name of the second was Ur-Edina. 


At the great gate, facing sunrise, at the place of marvel in the land, both of them crouched in the debris and appealed to Utu for help:


" The sorcerer from Aratta entered the animal pen; he made the milk scarce, 
“ The young calves could not get any. 


“ In the animal pen and the byre he caused distress; he made the butter and milk scarce
“ He threw its body into confusion! 
“ The land was dealt a disaster!"


She turned toward Erec. A woman approached the place Ur-jirnuna caused damage. She came to the weeping brothers at the Euphrates, the River of the Gods. 


She made her way to the city whose destiny was decreed by An and Enlil, Wise Woman Sajburu, with golden walking sceptre in hand; the sceptre of Utu, she carried for him.


The Wise Woman Sajburu approached Ur-jirnuna.


Both of them threw fish spawn into the river. The sorcerer made a giant carp arise from the water. Wise Woman Sajburu, however, made an eagle fly out from the water. The eagle seized the giant carp from the waves and fled up to the mountains.


A second time. They threw fish spawn into the river. The sorcerer made a ewe and its lamb come out from the water. Wise Woman Sajburu, however, made a wolf raise from the water. The wolf seized the ewe and its lamb from the bank and dragged it to the wide desert.


A third time. They threw fish spawn into the river. The sorcerer made a cow and its calf arise from the water. Wise Woman Sajburu, however, made a lion come out. The lion seized the cow and its calf and took them to the reedbeds.


They threw fish spawn into the river. The sorcerer made an ibex and a wild sheep come out from the water. Wise Woman Sajburu, however, made a mountain leopard arise from the water. The leopard seized the ibex and the wild sheep and dragged them to the mountains.
 
The sorcerer made a gazelle and kid come out from the water. Wise Woman Sajburu, however, made a tiger and a pirij-lion come out from the water. The tiger and the pirij -lion seized the gazelle and kid and took them to the forest. 


The face of the sorcerer darkened, his mind became confused. Wise Woman Sajburu said to him:


" Sorcerer, you do have magical powers, but where is your sense?
“ How on earth could you think of going to do sorcery at Eresh?
“ It is the city of Nisaba, a city whose destiny was decreed by An and Enlil,
“ The primeval city, the beloved city of Ninlil"


The sorcerer answered her: 


" I went there without knowing all about this, I acknowledge your superiority
“ Please do not be bitter." 


He pleaded, he prayed to her:


" Set me free, my sister, set me free, let me go in peace to my city.
“ Let me return safely to Aratta, the Mount of the Lustrous Meh.
“ I will make known!
“ I shall declare your greatness in all the lands.
“ I will sing your praise in Aratta, the Mount of the Lustrous Meh."


Wise Woman Sajburu answered to him:


" You have caused distress in the animal pen and the byre;
“ You have made the butter and milk scarce.
“ You have removed the lunch table, the morning, and evening table.
“ You have cut off butter and milk from the evening meal of the great dining hall,
“ You have caused distress amongst the land.


“ Your sin that butter and milk run dry, cannot be forgiven.
“ Nanna, the king of the byre, lover of milk, has established that it was a capital offense 
“ And I,
“ I am not pardoning your life."


Wise Woman Sajburu gave her decision about the sorcerer in the assembly. She threw her prisoner from the bank of the Euphrates. She seized from him his life-force and then returned to her city, Eresh.


Having heard this matter, En-suhgir-ana sent a man to Enmerkar: 


" You are the beloved, Lord of Inanna, you alone are exalted. 
“ Inanna has truly chosen you for her holy lap, you are her beloved.


“ From the west to the east, you are the great lord,
“ I am only second to you, from the moment of conception,
“ I was not your equal, you are the older brother.
“ I cannot match you ever."


In the contest Enmerkar proved superior to En-suhgir-ana. 


Nisaba, be praised!


Lugalbanda in the mountain cave:
Tablet I:


When, in ancient days, heaven was separated from earth, that which was fitting was fashioned. When after the ancient harvests grew, barley was eaten. When boundaries were laid out; borders were fixed. 


When boundary-stones were placed, they were inscribed with names. When dykes and canals were dug, they were purified. When deep wells were dug, they bore straight down. 


When the bed of the Euphrates, the plenteous river of Unug, was opened up, Holy An abandoned the E-ana. When the offices of en-priest and king were famously exercised at Unug, the sceptre and staff of Kulaba were held high in battle, Inanna's game. 


The black-headed were blessed with long life, and were in their settled ways and in their cities. They presented the mountain goats with pounding hooves, and the mountain stags beautiful with their antlers to Enmerkar, Son of Utu.


Now at that time, the king set his mace towards the city. Enmerkar, the son of Utu, prepared an offensive expedition against Aratta, the Mountain of the Holy Divine Powers. He was going to set off to destroy the rebel land.


The lord began a mobilization of his city. The herald made the horn signal sound in all the lands. Now levied, Unug took to the field with the wise king. Indeed, levied Kulaba followed Enmerkar.


Unug's levy was a flood. Kulaba's levy was a clouded sky. As they covered the ground like heavy fogs, the dense dust whirled up by them and reached up to heaven. As if to rooks on the best seed, rising up, he called to the people. Each one gave his fellow the sign.


Their king went at their head. He went, at the front of the army. Enmerkar went at their head, to go at the lead of the army. Behind him, the army stood like fields of gu-nida and emmer-grain, growing abundantly. 


When the righteous one who takes counsel with Enlil, took away the whole of Kulaba, like sheep they bent over at the slope of the mountains. Like wild bulls at the edge of the hills, they ran forward. He sought a guide at his side, one that recognised the way. 


He sought passage to Aratta.


Five days passed. 


On the sixth day they bathed. Then, on the seventh day they entered the mountains. When they had crossed over on the paths, like an enormous flood billowing upstream into a lagoon, their ruler, Enmerkar, riding on a storm, stepped down from heaven to the great earth. Utu's son, the good bright metal, stepped down to the great earth. 


His head shone with brilliance. The barbed arrows flash past him like lightning. The visage of the bronze pointed axe, his emblem, shines for him. The pointed axe protrudes for him prominently, like a dog eating a corpse.


At that time there were seven young ones; the young ones, born in Kulaba. The goddess Urash had borne these seven. The wild cow had nourished them with milk. They were heroes, living in Sumer; they were princely in their prime. 


They had been brought up eating at the god An's table. These seven were the overseers for those that were subordinate to overseers. They were the captains for those that were subordinate to captains. They were the generals for those that were subordinate to generals.


They were overseers of 300 men. 300 men each. They were captains of 600 men. 600 men each. They were generals of 7 car of soldiers. 25,200 soldiers each. They stood at the service of the lord as his elite troops.


Lugalbanda, the eighth of them, bathed and was washed in pure water. In awed silence he went forward. Forward he marched with the troops. When they had covered half the way, a sickness befell him there.


Head sickness befell him. He jerked like a snake dragged by its head with a reed. His mouth bit the dust, like a gazelle caught in a snare. 


No longer could his hands return the hand grip. No longer could he lift his feet high. 


Neither king nor contingents could help him. In the great mountains, crowded together like a dust cloud over the ground, they said: 


" Let them bring him to Unug"


But they did not know how they could bring him. 


" Let them bring him to Kulaba." 


But they did not know how they could bring him. 


As his teeth chattered in the cold places of the mountains, they brought him to a warm place there. A wooden storehouse, they made him. They made him an arbour, like a bird's nest. 


Dates, figs, and various sorts of cheese were brought for him. They put sweetmeats suitable for the sick to eat in the baskets of dates. They made him a home. 


They set out, for him, the various fats of the cow-pen. They set out the sheepfold's fresh cheese. They set out oil with cold eggs, cold hard-boiled eggs, as if laying a table for the holy place, the valued place.They set out a table, as if for a funerary offering. 


Directly in front of the table, they arranged for him beer for drinking, mixed with date syrup, and rolls in baskets with butter. Provisions were poured into leather buckets. Provisions were all put into leather bags. His brothers and friends, like a boat unloading from the harvest-place, placed stores by his head in the mountain cave. 


They poured water in their leather waterskins. Dark beer, alcoholic drink, light emmer beer, wine for drinking which is pleasant to the taste, they distributed by his head in the mountain cave. 


They prepared for him incense resin, fragrant resin, aromatic resin, ligidba resin and first-class resin on pot-stands in the deep hole. They suspended them by his head in the mountain cave. They pushed into place by his head, his axe, whose metal was tin, imported from the Zubi mountains. 


They wrapped up by his chest, his dagger of iron, imported from the Gig-mountains. His eyes were irrigation ditches, because they were flooding with water. Holy Lugalbanda kept open and directed towards them, the outer door of his lips. Overflowing, like Holy Utu, he did not speak to his brothers.


When they lifted his neck, there was no breath there any longer. His brothers, and his friends took counsel with one another:


" If our brother rises, like Utu, from bed, 
“Then the god who has smitten him will step aside, 
“ And, when he eats this food, when he drinks this drink, it will make his feet stable.
“ May he bring him over the high places of the mountains to the brick-built Kulaba,
“ But, if Utu calls our brother to the holy place, the valued place,
“ The health of his limbs will leave him;
“ Then, it will be up to us, when we come back from Aratta,
“ To bring our brother's body to the brick-built Kulaba."


Like the dispersed holy cows of Nanna, as with a breeding bull when in his old age, they have left him behind in the cattle pen. His brothers and friends abandoned Holy Lugalbanda in the mountain cave, with repeated tears and moaning. With tears, with lamentation, with grief and weeping, Lugalbanda's older brothers set off into the mountains.


Tablet II:


Two days passed, during which Lugalbanda remained ill. To these two days, half a day was added. Utu turned his glance towards his home, as the animals lifted their heads toward their lairs. At the day's end, in the evening cool, his body was as if anointed with oil.


But he was not yet free of his sickness.


When he lifted his eyes to heaven, to Utu, he wept to him as if to his own father. In the mountain cave he raised his fair hands to him:


" Utu, I greet you! 
“ Let me be ill no longer!
“ Hero, Ningal's son, I greet you! 
“ Let me be ill no longer!


“ Utu, you have let me come up into the mountains in the company of my brothers.
“ In the mountain cave, the most dreadful spot on earth, let me be ill no longer! 
“ Here where there is no mother, there is no father, and there is no acquaintance,
“ There is no one whom I value.
“ My mother is not here to say:


“" Alas, my child!"”


“ My brother is not here to say
        
“" Alas, my brother!"” 


“ My mother's neighbour, who enters our house, is not here to weep over me.


“ If the male and female protective deities were standing by,
“ The deity of neighbourliness would say:


“" A man should not perish!"”


“ A lost dog is bad; but, a lost man is terrible,
“ On the unknown way at the edge of the mountains. 


“ Utu!
“ Is a lost man, a man in an even more terrible situation?
“ Don't make me flow away like water in a violent death! 
“ Don't make me eat saltpetre as if it were barley! 
“ Don't make me fall like a throwstick somewhere in the desert unknown to me!
“ Afflicted with a name which excites my brothers' scorn, let me be ill no longer!
“ Afflicted with the derision of my comrades, let me be ill no longer! 
“ Let me not come to an end in the mountains like a weakling!"


Utu accepted his tears, and he sent down his divine encouragement to him in the mountain cave.


Inanna, the evening star, who makes wealth for the poor, whose game is sweet, the prostitute who goes out to the inn, who makes the bedchamber delightful, who is food to the poor man, Inanna, the evening star, the daughter of Suen, arose before him, like a bull in the land. Her brilliance, like that of Holy Cara. Her stellar brightness illuminated for him, the mountain cave. 


When he lifted his eyes upwards to Inanna, he wept as if before his own father. In the mountain cave he raised to her, his fair hands:


" Inanna, if only this were my home, if only this were my city! 
“ If only this were Kulaba, the city in which my mother bore me was near,
“ Even if it were to me, as the wasteland to a snake.
“ If it were to me, as a crack in the ground to a scorpion.
“ My mighty people, praise,
“ My great ladies, praise,
“ Praise to E-ana!
        
“ May my limbs not perish in the mountains of the cypresses!"


Inanna accepted his tears. With the power of life, she let him go to sleep, just like the sleeping Utu. Inanna enveloped him with heart's joy, as if with a woollen garment. Then, just as swiftly as she came, she went to the brick-built Kulaba.


The bull that eats up the black soup, the Astral Holy Bull-Calf, came to watch over him. He shone in the heavens like the morning star. He spread bright light in the night.


The glorious lord whom the crown befits, Suen, the beloved son of Enlil, who is greeted as the new moon, Father Nanna, gave the direction for the rising Utu. The lord reached the zenith splendidly, his brilliance, like lapis lazuli. His starry radiance illuminated for him, the mountain cave. 


When Lugalbanda raised his eyes to heaven, to Suen, he wept to him as if to his own father. In the mountain cave he raised to him his fair hands:


" King whom one cannot reach in the distant sky,
“ Suen, whom one cannot reach in the distant sky,
“ King Suen who loves justice, who hates evil!
“ Justice brings joy, justly, to your heart.


“ A poplar, a great staff, forms a sceptre for you,
“ You who loosen the bonds of justice, and who does not loosen the bonds of evil.
“ If you encounter evil before you, it is dragged away behind you
“ When your heart becomes angry,
“ You spit your venom at evil, like a snake which drools poison!"


Suen accepted his tears and gave him life. He conferred on his feet the power to stand, as the bright bull rose up from the horizon. The bull rested among the cypresses, as a shield standing on the ground, watched by the assembly. Like a shield coming out from the treasury; watched by the young men, the Youth Utu extended his holy, shining, rays down from heaven. 


Before Holy Lugalbanda, his brilliance illuminated the mountain cave for him. He bestowed it on Holy Lugalbanda in the mountain cave. 


His good, protective, god hovered ahead of him. His good, protective, goddess walked behind him. The god which had smitten him stepped aside, and went out from him. When he raised his eyes heavenward to Utu, he wept to him as to his own father. In the mountain cave he raised to him his fair hands:


" Utu, shepherd of the land, father of the black-headed,
“ When you go to sleep, the people go to sleep with you;
“ Youth Utu, when you rise, the people rise with you.
“ Utu, without you no net is stretched out for a bird, no slave is taken away captive.
“ To him who walks alone, you are his brotherly companion.


“ Utu, you are the third of them who travel in pairs.
“ You are the blinkers for him who wears the neck-ring.
“ Like a holy zulumhi-garment, your sunshine clothes the poor man,
“  Your sunshine clothes the scoundrel, as well as him who has no clothes;
“ As a garment of white wool, it covers the bodies, even of debt slaves.
“ Like rich old men, the old women praise your sunshine sweetly,
“ Until their oldest days, your sunshine is as mighty as oil; great wild bulls run forward,
        " Hero, son of Ningal, praise to you.
“ Praise to you is so very sweet, it reaches up to heaven.
“ Hero, son of Ningal, they laud you, as you deserve."


Tablet III:


Holy Lugalbanda came out from the mountain cave. Then, the righteous one who takes counsel with Enlil, caused life-saving plants to be born. The rolling rivers, mothers of the hills, brought life-saving water. Lugalbanda bit on the life-saving plants, he sipped from the life-saving water.


After biting on the life-saving plants, and after sipping from the life-saving water, there he, on his own, set a trap in the ground. From that spot he sped away like a horse of the mountains. Like a lone wild ass of Cakkan he darted over the mountains. Like a large powerful donkey he raced. Like a slim donkey, eager to run, he bounded along.


That night, in the evening, he set off, hurrying through the mountains, a wasteland in the moonlight. He was alone and, even to his sharp eyes, there was not a single person to be seen. 


With the provisions stocked in leather pails, provisions put in leather bags, his brothers and his friends had been able to bake bread on the ground, with some cold water.


Holy Lugalbanda had carried the things from the mountain cave. He set them beside the embers. He filled a bucket full with water. In front of him he split what he had placed. He took hold of the fuel stones, repeatedly striking them together. 


He laid the glowing coals on the open ground. The fine flintstone caused a spark. Its fire shone out for him over the wasteland, like the sun. Not knowing how to bake bread or a cake, not knowing an oven, with just seven coals he baked giziecta-dough. 


While the bread was baking by itself, he pulled up culhi-reeds of the mountains. Roots and all, he stripped their branches. He packed up all the cakes as a day's ration. Not knowing how to bake bread or a cake, not knowing an oven, with just seven coals he had baked giziecta-dough.
 
He garnished it with sweet date syrup.


A brown wild bull went. A fine-looking wild bull went, as a wild bull tossing its horns. A wild bull in hunger was resting, and seeking with its voice the brown wild bulls of the hills. In the pure place, it was chewing aromatic cimgig as if it were barley. It was grinding up the wood of the cypress as if it were esparto-grass. It was sniffing with its nose at the foliage of the cenu-shrub as if it were grass. It was drinking the water of the rolling rivers. It was belching from ilinnuc, the pure plant of the mountains. 


While the brown wild bulls of the mountains were browsing about among the plants, Lugalbanda captured one in his ambush. He uprooted a juniper tree of the mountains and stripped its branches. With a knife, Holy Lugalbanda trimmed its roots, which were like the long rushes of the field. He tethered the brown wild bull of the mountains, to it with a halter.


A brown goat and a nanny-goat, flea-bitten goats, lousy goats, goats covered in sores in the pure place, were chewing aromatic cimgig as if it were barley. They were grinding up the wood of the cypress as if it were esparto-grass. They were sniffing with their noses at the foliage of the cenu-shrub as if it were grass. They were drinking the water of the rolling rivers. They were belching from ilinnuc, the pure plant of the mountains. 


While the brown goats and the nanny-goats were browsing about among the plants, Lugalbanda captured these two in his ambush. He uprooted a juniper tree of the mountains and stripped its branches. With a knife, Holy Lugalbanda cut off its roots, which were like the long rushes of the field. With chains, he fettered the brown goat and the nanny goat; both the goats, he piled up.


He was alone and, even to his sharp eyes, there was not a single person to be seen. Sleep overcame the king, Lugalbanda. Sleep, the country of oppression, like a towering flood overcame him. Sleep fell upon him like a hand demolishing a brick wall, like a hand raised high, or a foot raised high.


Sleep, covering like syrup, that which is in front of it; overflowing like syrup onto that which is in front of it. It knows no overseer. Knows no captain. Yet, it was overpowering for the hero. By means of Ninkasi's wooden cask, the help of beer, sleep finally overcame Lugalbanda. 


He laid down ilinnuc, pure herb of the mountains, as a couch. He spread out a zulumhi-garment. He unfolded there, a white linen sheet. There being no washing-room for bathing, he made do with that place. 


The king lay down not to sleep, but he lay down to dream. Not turning back at the door of the dream, not turning back at the door-pivot. 


To the liar, it talks in lies. To the truthful, it speaks truth. It can make one man happy, it can make another man sing, but it is the closed tablet-basket of the gods. It is the beautiful bedchamber of Ninlil. It is the counsellor of Inanna. The multiplier of mankind. The voice of one not alive, Zangara, the God of Dreams. 


Himself like a bull, he bellowed at Lugalbanda. Like the calf of a cow, he lowed:


" Who will slaughter a brown wild bull for me? Who will make its fat melt for me?
“ He shall take my axe whose metal is tin; he shall wield my dagger which is of iron.
“ Like an athlete I shall let him bring away the brown wild bull,
“ The wild bull of the mountains.


“ I shall let him, like a wrestler, make it submit; its strength will leave it.
“ When he offers it before the rising sun, let him heap up like barley-corns
“ The heads of the brown goat and the nanny goat, both the goats.
“ When he has poured out their blood in the pit,
“ Let their smell waft out in the desert,
“ So that the alert snakes of the mountains will sniff it."


Lugalbanda awoke. It was a dream. He shivered. It was sleep. He rubbed his eyes. He was overawed. 


He took his axe whose metal was tin, he wielded his dagger which was of iron. Like an athlete he brought away the brown wild bull, the wild bull of the mountains.


Like a wrestler, he made it submit. Its strength left it. He offered it before the rising sun. He heaped up, like barleycorns, the heads of the brown goat and the nanny goat, both of the goats. He poured out their blood in the pit so that their smell wafted out in the desert. The alert snakes of the mountains sniffed it.


As the sun was rising, Lugalbanda, invoking the name of Enlil, made An, Enlil, Enki, and Ninhursaja sit down to a banquet at the pit, at the place in the mountains which he had prepared. The banquet was set, the libations were poured. Dark beer, alcoholic drink, light emmer-beer, wine for drinking, which is pleasant to the taste. 


Over the plain he poured cool water as a libation. He put the knife to the flesh of the brown goats, and he roasted the dark livers there. 


He let their smoke rise there, like incense put on the fire. As if Dumuzid had brought in the good savours of the cattle pen, so did An, Enlil, Enki, and Ninhursaja consumed the best part of the food prepared by Lugalbanda. Like the shining place of pure strength, he set on top of the altar of Utu and the altar of Suen, their offering. He decorated the two altars with the lapis lazuli, the holy stone of Inanna. 


Suen granted Lugalbanda a weapon, and he bathed the A-an-kar. When he had bathed the weapon, he set out all the cakes properly.


Tablet IIII:


Disease, the demons, they make sickness. Of Enki, Father of the Gods, they are fearful. They do not eat. Like a string of figs dripping with lusciousness, they hang their arms. They are gazelles of Suen running in flight. They are the fine smooth cloths of Ninlil.


They are the helpers of Ickur; they pile up flax, they pile up barley. They are wild animals on the rampage. They descend like a storm on a rebel land hated by Suen; indeed they descend like a storm.


They lie up, during all the long day, and during the short night they enter into houses. During the long day, and during the short night they lie in beds, yet, not sleeping. They give shouts and cries, at dead of night they sing out, terribly. In the breeze they take flight, like the swallows of Utu. 


They enter into house after house. 


They peer into street after street. They are talkers. They are repliers to talkers. Seeking words with a mother. Replying to a great lady, they nestle at the bedside. 


They smite without reason. When the black-heads are stolen, they leave grief at the doors and tables of humans. They change destinies. They tie the door-pivots together. 


The hero who grants justice, Utu. The Heroic Youth, Utu of the good word, recites the incantation, the good word of the Youth Utu. He recites that which the Anuna, the Great Gods, do not know, from that time before the wise elders of the city gathered those who would heed their word. He recites the incantation; the good word of the Youth Utu, which the Anuna, the Great Gods, do not know.


They are driven there. They are able to enter the presence of Utu, the bearded son of Ningal. They give to Suen, cries for mercy. They confirm with their power, the fate of the foreign lands. At the dead of night they know the black wild boar. 


At midday, to Utu, a man can recite his incantation. At midday, to Utu, a man can file suit against the demon.


May his judgement be pure!



Lugalbanda and the Anzud-
Tablet I:


Lugalbanda lay idle in the mountains, in faraway places. He had ventured into the Zabu Mountains. No mother was with him to offer advice. No father was with him to talk to him. No one was with him whom he knows, whom he values. No confidant was there to talk to him.


In his heart he spoke to himself:


" I shall treat the bird as befits him, I shall treat Anzud as befits him.
“ I shall greet his wife affectionately; 
“ I shall seat Anzud's wife and Anzud's child at a banquet.
“ An, will fetch Ninguenaka for me from her mountain home,
“ The expert woman, who redounds to her mother's credit.
“ Ninkasi the expert, who redounds to her mother's credit.


“ Her fermenting-vat is of green lapis lazuli,
“ Her beer cask of refined silver and of gold.
“ As cupbearer she mixes the beer.
“ If she sits by the beer, there is gladness; if she stands by the beer, there is joy.
“ Ninkasi, never wearying as she walks back and forth, with the keg at her side,
“ With the keg on her hips, may she make my beer-serving perfect.


“ When the bird, Anzud, has drunk the beer and is happy,
“ He can help me find the place to which the troops of Unug are going,
“ Anzud can put me on the track of my brothers."


Now the splendid eagle-tree of Enki, on the summit of Inanna's mountain of multi-coloured cornelian, stood fast on the earth like a tower. All shaggy like an aru, with its shade, it covered the highest eminences of the mountains like a cloak. It was spread out over them, like a tunic.


Its roots rested, like sajkal-snakes, in Utu's river of the seven mouths. Nearby, in the mountains where no cypresses grew, where no snake slithered, where no scorpion scurried, in the midst of the mountains, the buru-az-bird had put its nest. It laid its eggs therein. 


Nearby the bird, Anzud, set its nest, and settled therein its young. It was made with wood from the juniper and the box trees. The bird had made the bright twigs into a bower. At daybreak the bird stretched himself; when at sunrise Anzud cried out. 


At his cry the ground would quake in the Lulubi mountains. He had a shark's teeth and an eagle's claws. In terror of him wild bulls would run away into the foothills, stags into their mountains.


Lugalbanda achieved mighty exploits, for he was wise. In preparation of the sweet celestial cakes he added carefulness to carefulness. He kneaded the dough with honey, and added more honey to it. 


He set them before the young nestling, the anzud-chick. He gave the baby salted meat to eat. He fed it sheep's fat. He popped the cakes into its beak, then settled the anzud-chick in its nest.


He painted its eyes with kohl. Dabbed white cedar scent onto its head. Put up a twisted roll of salted meat. He withdrew from the Anzud's nest, and awaited him, in the mountains where no cypresses grow. 


At that time the bird, Anzud, was herding together wild bulls of the mountains. He held a live bull in his talons. He carried a dead bull across his shoulders. He poured forth his bile like ten gur of water.


 Anzud flew around once, and called back to his nest; but, his fledgling did not answer him. When the bird called a second time to his nest, his fledgling did not answer. Before, if the bird called back to his nest, his fledgling would answer; but now, when the bird called back to his nest, his fledgling did not answer him from its nest.


The bird uttered a cry of grief that reached up to heaven, and his wife cried out:


" Woe!"


Her cry reached the Abzu. This cry of " Woe!".
 
And his wife, with this cry of grief, made the Anuna gods of the mountains actually crawl into crevices like ants. 


Anzud, the bird, said to his wife:


" Foreboding weighs upon my nest, as over the great cattle-pen of Nanna.
“ Terror lies upon it, as when wild bulls start butting each other.
“ Who has taken my child from its nest? 
“ Who has taken the Anzud from its nest?"
Tablet II:


It seemed to Anzud, when it came near its nest, that it had been made like a god's dwelling-place. It was brilliantly festooned. His chick was settled therein. 


Its eyes were painted with kohl. Sprigs of white cedar were fixed on its head. A twisted piece of salt meat was hung up high. The bird was exultant. Anzud was exultant: 


" I am the prince who decides the destiny of rolling rivers.
“ I keep on the straight and narrow path of the righteous who follow Enlil's counsel.
“ My father Enlil brought me here, he let me bar the entrance to the mountains, 
“ As if with a great door.
“ If I fix a fate, who shall alter it? If I but say the word, who shall change it?
“ Whoever has done this to my nest,
“ If you are a god, I will speak with you, indeed I will befriend you.
“ If you are a man, I will fix your fate.
“ I shall not let you have any opponents in the mountains.
“ You shall be hero, Fortified By Anzud."


Lugalbanda, partly from fright, partly from delight, flattered the bird, Anzud: 


" Bird with sparkling eyes, born in this district.
“ Anzud!


“ With sparkling eyes, you frolic as you bathe in a pool.
“ Your grandfather, the prince of all patrimonies,
“ He placed heaven in your hand, set earth at your feet.
“ Your wingspan, extended, is like a birdnet stretched out across the sky!
“ Here, on the ground your talons are like a trap laid for the wild bulls,
“ Your talons are like a trap laid for the wild cows of the mountains!


“ Your spine is as straight as a scribe's.
“ Your breast, as you fly, is like Nirah parting the waters.
“ And as for your back, you are a verdant palm garden,
“ Breathtaking to look upon!”


“ Yesterday I escaped safely to you,
“ Since then I have entrusted myself to your protection.
” Your wife shall be my mother, you shall be my father,
“  I shall treat your little ones as my brothers.


“ Since yesterday I have been waiting for you,
“ In the mountains where no cypresses grow.
“ Let your wife stand beside you to greet me.
“ I offer my greeting and leave you to decide my destiny."


The bird presented himself before him. Anzud rejoiced over him. He said to Lugalbanda the Pure:


" Come now, my Lugalbanda, 
“ Go! 
“ Like a boat full of precious metals,
“ Like a grain barge, like a boat going to deliver apples,
“ Like a boat piled up high with a cargo of cucumbers, casting a shade,
“ Like a boat loaded lavishly at the place of harvest,
“ Go back to brick-built Kulaba with head held high!" 


Lugalbanda, who loved the seeds, would not accept this.


" Like Shara, Inanna's beloved son,
“ Shoot forth with your barbed arrows, like a sunbeam,
“ Shoot forth with reed-arrows like moonlight!
“ May the barbed arrows be a horned viper to those they hit!
“ Like a fish killed with the cleaver, may they be magic-cut!
“ May you bundle them up like logs hewn with the axe!"


Lugalbanda, who loved the seeds, would not accept this.


" May Ninurta, Enlil's son, set the helmet, “Lion of Battle”, on your head.
“ May the breastplate, that in the great mountains, does not permit retreat,
“ Be laid on your breast.
“ May you wield the battle-net against the enemy!”


Lugalbanda, who loved the seeds, would not accept this.


" The plenty of Dumuzi's holy butter churn, whose fat is the fat of all the world,
“ Shall be granted to you.
“ Its milk is the milk of all the world. 
“ It shall be granted to you!"


Lugalbanda, who loved the seeds, would not accept this. 


As a kib-bird, a fresh-water kib, flew along a lagoon, he answered him in words. The bird listened to him. Anzud said to Lugalbanda the Pure:


" Now look, my Lugalbanda, just think again.
“ It's like this:
“ A wilful plough-ox should be put back in the track,
“ A baulking ass should be made to take the straight path.


“ Still, I shall grant you what you put to me.
“ I shall assign you a destiny according to your wishes."


Lugalbanda the Pure answered him:


" Let the power of running be in my thighs; let me never grow tired!
“ Let there be strength in my arms,
“ Let me stretch my arms wide, let my arms never become weak!
“ Moving like the sunlight, like Inanna, like the seven storms of Ickur,
“ Let me leap like a flame, blaze like lightning!
“ Let me go wherever I look, to set foot wherever I cast my glance,
“ Reach wherever my heart desires.
“ And let me loosen my shoes in whatever place my heart has named to me!
“ When Utu lets me reach Kulaba my city,
“ Let him who curses me have no joy thereof;
“ Let him who wishes to strive with me never say:


" Just let him come!”


“ I shall have the woodcarvers fashion statues of you,
“ And you will be breathtaking to look upon.
“ Your name will be made famous thereby, in Sumer,
“ And will redound to the credit of the temples of the great gods."


So, Anzud said to Lugalbanda the Pure:


" The power of running be in your thighs,
“ Never grow tired!


“ Strength be in your arms,
“ Stretch your arms wide, may your arms never become weak!


“ Moving like the sun, like Inanna, like the seven storms of Ickur,
“ Leap like a flame, blaze like lightning!


“ Go wherever you look to,
“ Set foot wherever you cast your glance,
“ Reach wherever your heart desires,
“ Loosen your shoes in whatever place your heart has named to you!


“ When Utu lets you reach Kulaba, your city,
“ He who curses you shall have no joy thereof;
“ He who wishes to strive with you shall never say: 


" Just let him come!"


“ When you have had the woodcarvers fashion statues of me,
“ I shall be breathtaking to look upon.
“ My name will be made famous thereby, in Sumer,
“ And will redound to the credit of the temples of the great gods.
“ May the heavens shake for you!"


He took in his hand such of his provisions, as he had not eaten, and his weapons, one by one. Anzud flew on high, Lugalbanda walked on the ground. The bird, looking from above, spied the troops. 


Lugalbanda, looking from below, spied the dust that the troops had stirred up. 


The bird said to Lugalbanda:


" Come now, my Lugalbanda, I shall give you some advice:
“ May my advice be heeded, I shall say words to you: bear them in mind.
“ What I have told you, the fate I have fixed for you,
“ Do not tell it to your comrades, do not explain it to your brothers.
“ Fair fortune may conceal foul,
“  It is indeed so.
“ Leave me to my nest, you keep to your troops."


The bird hurried to its nest. Lugalbanda set out for the place where his brothers were.
Tablet III:


Like a pelican emerging from the sacred reed-bed, like lahama-deities going up from the Abzu, like one who is stepping from heaven to earth, Lugalbanda stepped into the midst of his brothers' picked troops. His brothers chattered away. The troops chattered away. 


His brothers, and his friends wearied him with questions:


" Come now, my Lugalbanda, here you are again!


“ The troops had abandoned you, as one killed in battle.
“ Certainly, you were not eating the good fat of the herd.
“ Certainly, you were not eating the sheepfold's fresh cheese.


“ How is it that you have come back from the great mountains,
“ Where no one goes alone, whence no one returns to mankind?"


Again his brothers, his friends, wearied him with questions:


" The banks of the mountain rivers, mothers of plenty, are widely separated.
“ How did you cross their waters as if you were drinking them?"


Lugalbanda the Pure replied to them: 


" The banks of the mountain rivers, mothers of plenty, are widely separated.
“ With my legs I stepped over them; I drank them like water from a waterskin.
“ Then, I snarled like a wolf and I grazed the water-meadows.
“ I pecked at the ground like a wild pigeon, I ate the mountain acorns." 


Lugalbanda's brothers and friends considered the words that he had said to them. Exactly as if they were small birds flocking together all day long, they embraced him and kissed him. As if he were a gamgam-chick sitting in its nest, they fed him and gave him drink. They drove away sickness from Lugalbanda the Pure.


Then, the men of Unug followed them as one man. They wound their way through the hills like a snake over a grain-pile. When the city was only a double-hour distance, the armies of Unug and Kulaba encamped by the posts and ditches that surrounded Aratta. 


From the city it rained down javelins as if from the clouds. Slingstones, numerous as the raindrops falling in a whole year, whizzed down loudly from Aratta's walls. The days passed, the months became long, the year turned full circle. 


A yellow harvest grew beneath the sky. They looked askance at the fields. Unease came over them. Slingstones, numerous as the raindrops falling in a whole year, landed on the road. They were hemmed in by the barrier of mountain thorn-bushes, thronged with dragons.


No one knew how to go back to the city. No one was rushing to go back to Kulaba. In their midst, Enmerkar, son of Utu, was afraid. He was troubled. He was disturbed by this upset. 


He sought someone whom he could send back to the city. He sought someone whom he could send back to Kulaba, but no one said to him:


" I will go to the city; I will go to Kulaba"


He went out to the foreign host. No one said to him:


" I will go to the city; I will go to Kulaba"


He stood before the elite troops. No one said to him:


" I will go to the city; I will go to Kulaba".


 A second time, he went out to the foreign host.


No one said to him: 


" I will go to the city; I will go to Kulaba."




He stepped out before the elite troops. Lugalbanda alone arose from the people, and said to him:


" My king, I will go to the city, but no one shall go with me.
“ I will go alone to Kulaba;
“ No one shall go with me."


The king affirmed:


 " If you go to the city, no one shall go with you.
“ You shall go alone to Kulaba, no one shall go with you."
 
The king swore by Heaven and by Earth:


" Swear that you will not let go from your hands, the great emblems of Kulaba."


Lugalbanda swore by Heaven and Earth.


After he had stood before the summoned assembly, within the palace that rests on earth; like a great mountain, Enmerkar, the son of Utu, berated Inanna:


" Once upon a time, my princely sister, Inanna the Pure, 
“ Summoned me in her holy heart.
“ From the bright mountains she had me enter brick-built Kulaba.
“ Where there was a marsh then, in Unug, it was full of water.
“ Where there was any dry land, Euphrates poplars grew there.
“ Where there were reed-thickets, old reeds and young reeds grew there.
“ Divine Enki, who is king in Eridu, tore up for me, the old reeds,
“ He drained off the water completely.


“ For fifty years, I built; for fifty years, I gave judgments. 
“ Then the Martu peoples, who know no agriculture, arose in all Sumer and Akkad,
“ But the wall of Unug extended out across the desert like a bird net.


“ Yet now, here in this place, my attractiveness to her has dwindled.
“ My troops are bound to me as a cow is bound to its calf;
“ But like a son, who hating his mother, leaves his city,
“ My princely sister, Inanna the Pure, has run away from me, back to brick-built Kulaba.


“ If she loves her city and hates me, why does she bind the city to me?
“ If she hates the city and yet loves me, why does she bind me to the city?
“ If the mistress removes herself from me, to her holy chamber,
“ If she abandons me like an Anzud chick,
“ Then may she at least bring me home to brick-built Kulaba.
“ On that day, my spear shall be laid aside; on that day she may shatter my shield.
“ Speak thus to my princely sister, Inanna the Pure."


Thereupon, Lugalbanda the Pure came forth from the palace. Although his brothers and his comrades barked at him, as at a foreign dog trying to join a pack of dogs: 


" Send someone else to Unug for the lord."


He stepped proudly forward like a foreign wild ass, trying to join a herd of wild asses: 


" For Enmerkar, son of Utu, I shall go alone to Kulaba.
“ No one shall go with me."


How he spoke to them! 


" Why will you go alone and keep company with no one on the journey? 
“ If our beneficent spirit does not stand by you there,
“ If our good protective deity does not go with you there,
“ You will never again stand with us where we stand,
“ You will never again dwell with us where we dwell,
“ You will never again set your feet on the ground where our feet are.


“ You will not come back from the great mountains,
“ Where no one goes alone, 
“ Whence no one returns to mankind!" 


He replied:


" Time is passing,


“ I know! 


“ None of you are going with me over the great earth." 


Tablet IIII:


The hearts of his brothers beat loudly. The hearts of his comrades sank. Lugalbanda took in his hand, that of his provisions, as he had not eaten. He gathered each of his weapons one by one. 


From the foot of the mountains, through the high mountains, and into the flat land, from the edge of Ancan to the top of Ancan, he crossed five, six, then seven mountains. By midnight, but before they had brought the offering-table to Inanna the Pure, he set foot joyfully in brick-built Kulaba. His lady, Inanna the Pure, sat there on her cushion. 


He bowed and prostrated himself on the ground. With joyful eyes, Inanna looked upon Lugalbanda the Pure, as she would look at the shepherd Ama-ucumgal-ana. In a joyful voice, Inanna spoke to Lugalbanda, as she would speak to her son, Lord Cara: 


" Come now, my Lugalbanda, why do you bring news from the city?
“ How have you come here, alone from Aratta?"


Lugalbanda the Pure answered her:


" What Enmerkar, son of Utu, quoth and what he says,
“ What your brother quoth and what he says, is:


“" Once upon a time, my princely sister, Inanna the Pure, 
““ Summoned me in her holy heart from the bright mountains.
“” She had me enter brick-built Kulaba, where there was a marsh.
“” Then in Unug, it was full of water. 
“” Where there was any dry land, Euphrates poplars grew there.
“” Where there were reed-thickets, old reeds and young reeds grew there.
“” Divine Enki, who is king in Eridu, tore up for me, the old reeds,
“” He drained off the water completely.


“” For fifty years, I built; for fifty years, I gave judgments. 
“” Then the Martu peoples, who know no agriculture, arose in all Sumer and Akkad;
“” But the wall of Unug extended out across the desert like a bird net.


“” Yet now, here in this place, my attractiveness to her has dwindled.
“” My troops are bound to me as a cow is bound to its calf;
““ But like a son, who hating his mother, leaves his city,
““ My princely sister, Inanna the Pure, has run away from me back to brick-built Kulaba.


““ If she loves her city and hates me, why does she bind the city to me?
““ If she hates the city and yet loves me, why does she bind me to the city?
““ If the mistress removes herself from me, to her holy chamber,
““ If she abandons me like an Anzud-chick,
““ Then, may she at least bring me home to brick-built Kulaba.
““ On that day my spear shall be laid aside, 
““ On that day she may shatter my shield””


Inanna the Pure uttered this response: 


" Now, at the end, on the banks,
“ In the water-meadows of a clear river, of a river of clear water,
“ Of the river which is Inanna's gleaming waterskin,
“ The suhurmac-fish eats the honey-herb; the kijtur-fish eats the mountain acorns;
“ The fish, which is a god of the suhurmac-fish, plays happily there and darts about,
“ With his scaly tail he touches the old reeds in that holy place.


“ The tamarisks of the place, as many as there are, drink water from that pool.
“ It stands alone, one tamarisk stands alone at the side.
“ When Enmerkar, son of Utu, has cut that tamarisk and has fashioned it into a bucket,
“ He must tear up the old reeds in that holy place roots and all,
“ And collect them in his hands.
“ When he has chased out from it the fish, which is a god of the suhurmac-fish,
“ Caught that fish; cooked it, and garnished it,
“ When he brought it as a sacrifice to the A-an-kara weapon, Inanna's battle-strength,
“ Then his troops will have success for him; then he will have brought to an end
“ That which, in the subterranean waters, provides the life-strength of Aratta.


" If he carries off from the city its worked metal and smiths,
“ If he carries off its worked stones and its stonemasons,
“ If he renews the city and settles it, all the moulds of Aratta will be his."


Now, Aratta's battlements are of green lapis lazuli. Its walls and its towering brickwork are bright red, their brick clay is made of tinstone dug out in the mountains where the cypress grows. 


Praise be to Lugalbanda the Pure.


Enmerkar and the Lord of Aratta-
Tablet I:


City of the majestic bull, bearing vigour and great awesome splendour, Kulaba, Jipar, breast of the storm, where destiny is determined, Unug, great mountain in the midst of heaven, 


There, the evening meal, of the great abode of An, was set. 


In those days of yore, when the destinies were determined, the great princes allowed Unug-Kulaba's E-ana, to lift its head high. Plenty, carp floods, and the rain which brings forth dappled barley were increased in Unug-Kulaba. 


Before the land of Dilmun yet existed, the E-ana of Unug-Kulaba was well founded, and the Holy Jipar of Inanna, in brick-built Kulaba, shone forth like the silver in the lode. The E-ana, colourfully adorned, and Jipar, the holy place, was decorated with flawless lapis lazuli. Its interior beautifully formed like a white mes-tree bearing fruit. 


The Lord of Aratta placed on his head the golden crown for Inanna; but, he did not please her like the Lord of Kulaba. Aratta did not build, for Holy Inanna, a shrine like the E-ana, the Jipar, the holy place, unlike brick-built Kulaba.


At that time, the lord, chosen in her holy heart, chosen by Inanna, was he from the bright mountain, Enmerkar, the son of Utu. Utu made a plea to his sister, the lady who grants desires, Holy Inanna:


" My sister, let Aratta fashion gold and silver skilfully on my behalf for Uruk. 
“ Let them cut the flawless lapis lazuli from the blocks, 
“ Let them polish the translucence of the flawless lapis lazuli,
“ Let them build a holy mountain in Uruk. 


“ Let Aratta build a temple brought down from heaven,
“ Your place of worship, the Shrine E-ana;
“ Let Aratta skillfully fashion the interior of the holy Jipar, your abode.
“ May I, the radiant youth, be embraced there by you. 
“ Let Aratta submit beneath the yoke for Uruk, on my behalf.
“ Let the people of Aratta bring down for me their mountain stones, 
“ Let them build the great shrine for me, and erect the great abode for me.


“ Let them make the great abode, the abode of the gods, famous for me, 
“ Let the people make my meh prosper in Kulaba, 
“ Let them make the Abzu grow for me like a holy mountain.
“ Make Eridu gleam for me like the mountain range,
“ Cause the Abzu shrine to shine forth for me, like the silver in the lode.


“ When in the Abzu I utter praise; when I bring the meh from Eridug,
“ When, in lordship, I am adorned with the crown, like a purified shrine.
“ When I place on my head the holy crown in Uruk-Kulaba,
“ May the radiance of the great shrine bring me into the Jipar,
“ And may the splendour of the Jipar bring me into the great shrine.
“ May the people marvel admiringly, 


“ And may I, Utu, witness it in joy."


Thereupon, the splendour of Holy An, the Lady of the Mountains, the wise, the goddess whose kohl is for Ama-ucumgal-ana, Inanna, the Lady of All the Lands, called to Enmerkar, the son of Utu:


" Come, Enmerkar!
“ I shall offer you advice: let my counsel be heeded. 
“ I shall speak words to you; let them be heard.


Enemerkar stood before Inanna as king, and she gave word:


“ Choose from the troops as a messenger,
“ One who is eloquent of speech and endowed with endurance.”


The king replied:


“ Where and to whom shall he carry the important message of wise Inanna?”


Inanna continued:


“ Let him bring it up into the Zubi Mountains,
“ Let him descend with it from the Zubi Mountains.
“ Let Susin, and the land of Ancan, humbly salute Inanna like tiny mice.
“ In the great mountain ranges, let the teeming multitudes grovel in the dust for her.
“ Aratta shall submit beneath the yoke to Uruk.


“ The people of Aratta shall bring down the mountain stones from their mountains,
“ And shall build the great shrine for you, and erect the great abode for you.
“ You will cause the great abode, the abode of the gods, to shine forth for you. 
“ You will make your meh flourish in Kulaba,
“ You will make the Abzu grow like a holy mountain,
“ You will make Eridu shining for you like the mountain range,
“ You will cause the Abzu shrine to shine forth for you like the glitter in the lode.


“ When in the Abzu utter praise,
“ When you bring the meh from Eridu, 
“ When, in lordship, you are adorned with the crown, like a purified shrine,
“ When you place on your head the holy crown in Uruk-Kulaba, 
“ Then may the radiance of the great shrine bring you into the Jipar.
“ And may the splendour of the Jipar bring you into the great shrine.
“ May the people marvel admiringly, 
“ May Utu witness it in joy.


“ Because Utu shall carry justice daily, 
“ When in the evening cool he will take his rest in the place of Dumuzid;
“ Where the ewes, kids and lambs are numerous,
“ The people of Aratta shall run around for you,
“ Like the mountain sheep in the akalag-fields,the fields of Dumuzid! 


“ Rise like the sun over my holy breast!
“ You are the jewel of my throat!
“ Praise be to you, Enmerkar, son of Utu!"


The lord gave heed to the words of Holy Inanna, and chose from the troops, a messenger. One who was eloquent of speech and endowed with endurance was his messenger. 


Where and to whom will he carry the important message of wise Inanna?


Enmerkar gave his order:


" You shall bring it up into the Zubi Mountains, 
“ You shall descend with it from the Zubi Mountains.
“ Let Susin, and the land of Ancan, humbly salute Inanna, like tiny mice.
“ In the great mountain ranges, let the teeming multitudes grovel in the dust for her.
“ Messenger, speak to the lord of Aratta and say to him:


" Lest I make the people fly off from that city like a wild dove from its tree, 
” Lest I make them fly around like a bird over its well-founded nest,
” Lest I requite them as if at a current market rate,
” Lest I make it gather dust like an utterly destroyed city,
” Lest like a settlement cursed by Enki and utterly destroyed, I too utterly destroy Aratta,
” Lest like the devastation which swept destructively, 
” And in whose wake Inanna arose, shrieked, and yelled aloud:


“” I too wreak sweeping devastation there!””


” Let Aratta pack nuggets of gold in leather sacks,
” Place alongside it the kumea ore.
” Package up precious metals, and load the packs on the donkeys of the mountains.
” And then may the junior enlil of Sumer have them build for me,
“ The lord whom Nudimmud has chosen in his sacred heart, 
” A mountain of a shining meh.
” Have them make it luxuriant for me, like a boxwood tree,
“ Have them make its shining horns colourful for me 
“ As when Utu comes forth from his chamber.
“ Have them make its doorposts gleam brightly for me.”


" Chant to him the holy song, the incantation, sung in its chambers,
“ The incantation of Nudimmud”


The king and messenger sung together:
        
 " On that day, when there is no snake, when there is no scorpion,
“ When there is no hyena, when there is no lion,
“ When there is neither dog nor wolf,
“ When there is thus neither fear nor trembling, and man has no rival,
“ At such a time, may the lands of Cubur and Hamazi, the many-tongued,
“ Sumer, the great mountain of the meh of magnificence,
“ Akkad, the land possessing all that is befitting,
“ And the Martu land, resting in security,
“ The whole universe, the well-guarded people,
“ May they all address Enlil together in a single language.


“ For at that time, for the ambitious lords, for the ambitious princes, 
“ For the ambitious kings,
“ Enki, the lord of abundance and of steadfast decisions,
“ The wise and knowing lord of the land,
“ The expert of the gods, chosen for wisdom, the Lord of Eridu,
“ Shall change the speech in their mouths, as many as he had placed there.
“ And so the speech of mankind will truly be one."


The lord added further instructions for the messenger going to the mountains, to Aratta:


" Messenger, by night, drive on like the south wind, by day, be up like the dew!"


The messenger gave heed to the words of his king. He journeyed by the starry night, and by day he traveled with Utu of Heaven. He carried the important message of Inanna with its stinging tone.


He brought it up into the Zubi Mountains. He descended with it from the Zubi Mountains. Susin, and the land of Anshan, humbly saluted Inanna, like tiny mice. In the great mountain ranges, the teeming multitudes grovelled in the dust for her.


 He traversed five mountains, six mountains, seven mountains. He lifted his eyes as he approached Aratta.


Tablet II:


He stepped joyfully into the courtyard of Aratta. He made known the authority of his king. Openly, he spoke out the words in his heart. The messenger transmitted the message to the lord of Aratta:


" Your father, my master, has sent me to you.
“ The Lord of Unug, the Lord of Kulaba, has sent me to you
“ My king who from his birth has been fitted for lordship, his crown made for only him,


“ The Lord of Unug,
“ The sajkal snake living in Sumer, who pulverizes the head of mountains like flour,
“ The stag of the tall mountains, endowed with princely antlers,
“ Wild cow, kid pawing the the holy soapwort with its hoof,
“ He whom the good cow had given birth to in the heart of the mountains,
“ Enmerkar, the son of Utu, has sent me to you." 


En-suhgir-ana, the lord of Aratta, furrowed his brow:


 " What is it to me your master has spoken? What is it to me he has said?"


The messenger spoke aloud:


" This is what my master has spoken:


“" Lest I make the people fly off from that city like a wild dove from its tree, 
““ Lest I make them fly around like a bird over its well-founded nest,
““ Lest I requite them as if at a current market rate,
““ Lest I make it gather dust like an utterly destroyed city,
““ Like a settlement cursed by Enki, utterly destroyed, I too utterly destroy Aratta, 
“" Lest like the devastation which swept destructively, and in whose wake Inanna arose,
“" She shrieked, and yelled aloud:


“”” I too wreak a sweeping devastation there”””


“" Let Aratta pack nuggets of gold in leather sacks, place alongside it the kumea ore.
“" Package up precious metals, and load the packs on the donkeys of the mountains.
“" Then, may the junior enlil of Sumer have them build for me,
“" The lord whom Nudimmud has chosen in his sacred heart,
“" A mountain of shining meh.
“" Have them make it luxuriant for me like a boxwood tree,
“" Have them make its shining horns colourful for me
“” As when Utu comes forth from his chamber, 
“" Have them make its doorposts gleam brightly for me.”


He made the chant to him. The holy song. The incantation song in his chambers rang.
 The Incantation of Nudimmud filled the air:


 " On that day when there is no snake, when there is no scorpion,
“ When there is no hyena, when there is no lion,
“ When there is neither dog nor wolf,
“ When there is thus neither fear nor trembling, and man has no rival,
“ At such a time, may the lands of Cubur and Hamazi, the many-tongued,
“ Sumer, the great mountain of the me of magnificence,
“ Akkad, the land possessing all that is befitting,
“ And the Martu land, resting in security,
“ The whole universe, the well-guarded people,
“ May they all address Enlil together in a single language.


“ For at that time, for the ambitious lords, for the ambitious princes,
“ For the ambitious kings,
“ Enki, the lord of abundance and of steadfast decisions,
“ The wise and knowing lord of the land,
“ The expert of the gods, chosen for wisdom, the Lord of Eridu,
“ Shall change the speech in their mouths, as many as he had placed there.
“ And so the speech of mankind will truly be one."


The messenger briefly paused:


" Say whatever you will say to me,
“ I shall announce that message in the shrine E-ana 
“ I shall come as glad tidings to the scion of him with the glistening beard,
“ Whom his stalwart cow gave birth to in the mountain of the shining meh,
“ Who was reared on the soil of Aratta,
“ Who was given suck, at the udder of the good cow,
“ Who is suited for office in Kulaba, the mountain of great meh,
“ To Enmerkar, the son of Utu.
“ I shall repeat it in his Jipar, fruitful as a flourishing mes-tree,
“ To my king, the Lord of Kulaba."


When he had spoken thus to him, the Lord of Aratta replied: 


" Messenger, speak to your king, the Lord of Kulaba, and say to him:


“ It is I, the lord suited to purification,
” I, whom bears the huge heavenly neck-stock,
” The Queen of Heaven and Earth, the goddess of the numerous meh,
” Holy Inanna, has brought to Aratta, the mountain of the shining meh,
“ I, whom she has let bar the entrance of the mountains as if with a great door.
” How then shall Aratta submit to Unug? 


“ Submission to Unug is out of the question!”


“ Say this to him."


The Lord of Aratta gave pause:


" The Great Queen of Heaven, who rides upon the awesome meh,
“ Dwelling on the peaks of the bright mountains,
“ Adorning the dais of the bright mountains
“ My lord and master, who is her servant,
“ Has had them install her as the divine queen of E-ana.
“ Aratta shall bow, O’ lord, in absolute submission!
“ She has spoken to him thus, in brick-built Kulaba."


Thereupon, the lord became depressed and deeply troubled. He had no answer and took time searching for it. He stared at his own feet, trying to find an answer. 


He gave a cry. He bellowed the answer to the message like a bull:


" Messenger! Speak to your king, the Lord of Kulaba, and say to him: 


“ This great mountain range is a mes-tree grown high to the sky;
” Its roots form a net, and its branches are a snare.
” It may be a sparrow but it has the talons of an anzud bird or of an eagle.


“ The barrier of Inanna is perfectly made and is impenetrable.
“ Those eagle talons make the blood of the enemy run from the bright mountain.
” Although in Aratta there is weeping in the streets,
” Water libations are offered and flour is sprinkled on the mountain,
” Sacrifices and prayers are offered in obeisance.
“ With fewer than five or ten men,
“ How can a mobilized Unug proceed against the Zubi mountains?”


A laughed parted from the Lord of Aratta’s lips as he spoke:


“ Your king is heading in all haste against my military might,
“ But I am equally eager for a contest.


“ He who ignores a rival, does not get to eat everything up,
“ Like the bull which ignores the bull at its side.
“ But he who acknowledges a contest can be the outright winner,
“ Like the bull which acknowledges the bull at its side.


“ Or does he reject me in this contest?”


The Lord of Aratta calmed his thoughts:


“ Again, I have words to say to you, messenger
“ I have an artful proposal to make to you messenger,
“ May it get across to your king; repeat this to your master, to the Lord of Kulaba:


“ Lion lying on its paws in E-ana, bull bellowing within his Jipar,
” Fruitful as a flourishing mes-tree.
“ The mountain range is a warrior, soaring high,
“ Like Utu going to his abode at twilight, like one from whose face drips blood;
“ Or like Nanna, who is majestic in the high heavens,
” Like him whose countenance shines with radiance,
“ Who is fruitful and tall, who is like the woods in the mountains.


" Now if Enmerkar just makes straight for the country of Aratta,
” For the benevolent protective spirit of the mountain of holy powers, for Aratta,
“ Which is like a bright crown of heaven, then I shall make my pre-eminence clear,
“ And he need not pour barley into sacks, nor have it carted,
” Nor have that barley carried into the settlements,
” Nor place collectors over the labourers."


He glared:


" But if he were actually to have barley poured into carrying-nets,
” And to have it loaded on the packasses, 
“ At whose sides reserve donkeys have been placed, 
” And were to have it heaped up in a pile in the courtyard of Aratta,
” Were he really to heap it up in such a manner, the luxuriance of the grain pile,
” And were Inanna, who is the illuminator of the lands,
” The ornament of the settlements, who adorns the seven walls,
” The heroic lady, fit for battle, as the heroine of the battleground,
” Who makes the troops dance the Dance of Inanna
” Were she actually to cast off Aratta as if to a carrion pursuing dog,
“ Then in that case I should submit to him.
” He would indeed have made me know his pre-eminence;
” Like my city, I, in my smallness, would submit to him.” 


" So say to him."


After he had spoken thus to him, the Lord of Aratta made the messenger repeat the message he himself had just said. The messenger turned on his thigh like a wild cow; like a sand fly he went on his way in the morning calm.


He set foot joyfully in the brick-built Kulaba. The messenger rushed to the great courtyard, the courtyard of the throne room. He repeated its word, perfect to his master, the lord of Kulaba. He even bellowed at him like a bull, and Enmerkar listened to him like an ox driver.


The king had him sit nobly at his right side. As he turned his left side to him, he said:


" Does Aratta really understand the implications of his own stratagem?"


After day had broken and Utu had risen, the Sun-God of the land lifted his head high. The king convened with the Tigris, and with the Euphrates. Large vessels were placed in the open air, and he stood small vessels beside them, like lambs lying on the grass. Then the king, Enmerkar, placed wide apart the ecda-vessels, which were of gold.


 Thereupon, the tablet of clay, the pointed stylus of the assembly, the golden statue fashioned on a propitious day, beautiful Nanibgal, grown with a fair luxuriance, Nisaba, the lady of broad wisdom, opened for him her holy house of wisdom. 


He entered the palace of heaven, and became attentive. Then the lord opened his mighty storehouse, and firmly set his great lidga-measure on the ground. The king removed his old barley from the other barley.


He soaked the green malt all through with water. The ecda-vessels’ lip was covered with the hirin-plant. He narrowed the meshes of the carrying nets. He measured out in full the barley for the granary, accounting for the teeth of locusts. 


He had it loaded on the packasses at whose sides, reserve donkeys were placed. The king, the lord of broad wisdom, the Lord of Uruk, dispatched them directly to Aratta. He made the people go to Aratta on their own, like ants out of crevices. Again the lord added instructions for the messenger going to the mountains, to Aratta:


" Messenger, speak to the lord of Aratta and say to him:


“ The base of my sceptre is the divine power of magnificence.
” Its crown provides a protective shade over Kulaba;
” Under its spreading branches Holy Inanna refreshes herself in the shrine E-ana.
” Let him snap off a splinter from it and hold it in his hand;
” Let him hold it in his hand like a string of cornelian beads,
” A string of lapis lazuli beads;
“ Let the lord of Aratta bring that before me.” 


“ So say to him."


After he had thus spoken to him, the messenger went on his way to Aratta. His feet raised the dust of the road, and made the little pebbles of the hills thud. Like a dragon prowling the desert, he was unopposed. 
Tablet III:


After the messenger reached Aratta, the people stepped forward to admire the packasses. In the courtyard of Aratta, the messenger measured out, in full, the barley for the granary, adding for the teeth of locusts. As if from the rains of heaven and the sunshine, Aratta was filled with abundance.


As if the gods had returned to their seats, Aratta's hunger was sated. The people of Aratta covered their fields with the water-soaked green malt. Afterwards, couriers and catam officials gave praise to their lord, the Lord of Aratta.


The citizens of Aratta were mindful. He revealed the matter to them, attentively in Aratta. From the messenger to his hand, the sceptre, to the hand of the Lord of Unug:


" As for us, in the direst hunger, in our most dire famine,
“ Let us prostrate ourselves before the Lord of Kulaba!"


The eloquent elders wrung their hands in despair. They leaned against the wall. Indeed, they were even placing their treasuries at the disposal of the lord. His sceptre topped the mound of treasure, in the palace courtyard. Openly the messenger spoke out the words in his heart:


" Your father, my master, sent me to you. Enmerkar, the son of Utu, sent me to you."


" What is it to me that your master has spoken?  What is it to me that he has said?" 


" This is what my master has spoken, this is what he has said:


“" The base of my sceptre is the divine power of magnificence.
“” Its crown provides a protective shade over Kulaba;
“” Under its spreading branches Holy Inanna refreshes herself in the shrine E-ana.
“” Let him snap off a splinter from it and hold that in his hand.
“” Let him hold it in his hand like a string of cornelian beads,
“” A string of lapis lazuli beads;
“” Let the lord of Aratta bring that before me.”"


After he had spoken thus to him, they went inside the sanctuary. The Lord of Aratta laid himself down in a fast. Day broke. He discussed the matter at length. He spoke unspeakable words. He circulated with this matter as if it were barely eaten by a donkey.


And what did one speak to another?.


" Messenger, speak to your king, the Lord of Kulaba, and say to him:


“ Let him contemplate and put in his hand a sceptre that is not of wood,
“ Nor designated as wood ildag-wood, nor cim-gig wood, cedar wood, or cypress wood.
” Not hacur-cypress, palm-wood, hardwood, nor zabalum-wood.
” Nor poplar as in a chariot; not reedwork as in whip handles;
” not gold, nor copper, not genuine kumea-metal
” nor silver, not cornelian, nor lapis lazuli.


” Let him snap off a splinter from that and hold it in his hand;
” Let him hold it in his hand like a string of cornelian beads,
” A string of lapis lazuli beads, let the Lord of Kulaba bring that before me.”
        
" So say to him."


After he had spoken to him thus, the messenger went off like a young donkey, braying as it is cut off from the chariot tongue. He trotted like an onager running on dry land. He filled his mouth with wind. He ran in one trek like a long-wooled sheep, butting other sheep in its fury.


He set foot joyfully in the brick-built Kulaba. He transmitted the message word for word to his master, the Lord of Kulaba. Enki gave Enmerkar wisdom, and the lord gave instructions to his chief steward.


In his house, the king received an offering of bitumen. He wrapped it up like a baby’s swaddle, and inspected it. He pounded it with a pestle like herbs. He poured it like oil on the gasab-reed. From the sunlight it emerged into the shade, and from the shade it emerged into the sunlight.


After five years, nay, ten years had passed, he split the gasab-reed with an axe. The lord looked at it; pleased, he poured on it, fine oil, fine oil of the bright mountains. The lord placed the sceptre in the hands of the messenger going to the mountains. 


The messenger, whose journeying to Aratta was like a pelican over the hills, like a fly over the ground, like a fly who darted through the mountains as swiftly as carp swim, he reached Aratta. He setfoot joyfully in the courtyard of Aratta, and put the sceptre in the hand of the Lord of Aratta.
