The old women who survived those days, the old men who survived those days and the chief lamentation singer, who survived those years, set up seven ba-laj-drums. As if they stood at the horizon, together with ub, meze, lilis, and bronze cem-drums, made them resound to Enlil, like Ickur, for seven days and seven nights. 


The old women did not restrain the cry: 


"Alas for my city!" 


The old men did not restrain the cry: 


"Alas for its people!". 


The lamentation singer did not restrain the cry: 


"Alas for the E-kur!". 


Its young women did not restrain from tearing their hair. Its young men did not restrain from sharpening their knives. Their laments were as if Enlil's ancestors were performing a lament, as if in the awe-inspiring Holy Mound, by the holy knees of Enlil. Because of this, Enlil entered his holy bedchamber and lay down fasting.
Tablet IIII:


Suen, Enki, Inanna, Ninurta, Ickur, Utu, Nuska, and Nisaba, the Great Gods; they sprinkled Enlil's heart with cool water and prayed to him: 


" Enlil, may the city that destroyed your city, be treated as your city has been treated,
“ May the one that defiled your giguna, be treated as Nibru!


“ In this city, may heads fill the wells! 


“ May no one find his acquaintances there,
“ May brother not recognize brother,
“ May its young woman be cruelly killed in her woman's domain,
“ May its old man cry in distress for his slain wife!


“ May its pigeons moan on their window ledges, 
“ May its small birds be smitten in their nooks,
“ May it live in constant anxiety like a timid pigeon!"


Suen, Enki, Inana, Ninurta, Ickur, Utu, Nuska and Nisaba, all the gods whosoever, they turned their attention to the city, and cursed Agade severely: 


" City, you pounced on E-kur: it is as if you had pounced on Enlil.
“ Agade, you pounced on E-kur, it is as if you had pounced on Enlil.
“ May your holy walls, to their highest point, resound with mourning.
“ May your giguna be reduced to a pile of dust!


“ May your pilasters with the standing lahama-deities fall to the ground.
“ Like tall young men drunk on wine, may your clay be returned to its abzu.
“ May it be clay cursed by Enki! 
“ May your grain be returned to its furrow,
“ May it be grain cursed by Ezinu! 
“ May your timber be returned to its forest,
“ May it be timber cursed by Ninilduma! 


“ May the cattle slaughterer slaughter his wife,
“ May the sheep butcher, butcher his child! 


“ May water wash away your pauper as he is looking for your scattered treasures,
“ May your prostitute hang herself at the entrance to her brothel,
“ May your pregnant hierodules and cult prostitutes abort their children!


“ May your gold be bought for the price of silver,
“ May your silver be bought for the price of pyrite,
“ May your copper be bought for the price of lead!


" Agade, may your strong man be deprived of his strength,
“ So that he will be unable to lift his sack of provisions and wealth.
“ He will not have the joy of controlling your superior asses;
“ May he lie idle all day, may this make the city die of hunger! 


“ May your citizens, who used to eat fine food, lie hungry in the grass and herbs,
“ May your poor man eat the coating on his roof,
“ May he chew the leather hinges on the main door of his father's house.
“ May depression descend upon your palace built for joy, that joyous palace, 
“ May the evils of the desert, the silent place, howl continuously!
" May foxes that frequent ruin mounds brush with their tails,
“ Your fattening-pens, that were established for purification ceremonies! 


“ May the u-ku-ku, the bird of depression,
“ Make its nest in your gateways established for the land,
“ In your city that could not sleep because of the tigi-drums, that could not rest from joy, 
“ May the bulls of Nanna that fill the pens, bellow like those who wander in the desert, 
“ The silent place! 


“ May the grass grow long on your canal-bank tow-paths, 
“ May the grass of mourning grow on your highways laid for waggons! 
“ Moreover, may ibex, wild rams, and alert snakes of the mountains
“ Allow no one to pass on your tow-paths built up with canal sediment!
“ In your plains where fine grass grows, may the reed of lamentation grow!
“ Agade! May brackish water flow!
“ May brackish water flow in the river, where fresh water flowed for you! 
“ If someone decides:


“" I will dwell in this city!"”, 


“ May he not enjoy the pleasures of a dwelling place!
“ If someone decides:


“" I will rest in Agade!"”, 


“ May he not enjoy the pleasures of a resting place!"


Before Utu, on that very day, so it was! 


On its canal bank tow-paths, the grass grew long. On its highways, laid for wagons, the grass of mourning grew. Moreover, on its tow-paths built up with canal sediment; ibex, wild rams, and alert snakes of the mountains allowed no one to pass. 


On its plains, where fine grass grew, now the reeds of lamentation grew. Agade's flowing fresh water flowed as brackish water. When someone decided, " I will dwell in that city!", he could not enjoy the pleasures of a dwelling place. When someone decided, "I will rest in Agade!", he could not enjoy the pleasures of a resting place.


Inanna be praised for the destruction of Agade.


The Feast of the Goddess Allani-


When Tessub went, he set out to go to the palace of Allani. A chair was set up for him. When Tessub, the king, came in from outside, he was sat down high on a throne whose surface measured one IKU of field measure. He raised his feet on a stool whose surface measured seven tawalla. 


Tessub and Suwaliyat were down in the Dark Netherworld. Allani girded herself for work. She went back and forth in front of Tessub. Allani made a fine feast at the Bolts of the Netherworld. 


She slaughtered 10,000 cattle before Great Tessub. She slaughtered 30,000 fat-tailed sheep. But there was no counting the goat kids, lambs and adult goats, for so many were slaughtered. 


The bakers made their wares ready, and the cupbearers came in. The cooks took up the brisket portions. They brought them in with bowls as mortars. The meal time arrived, and Tessub, the king, sat down to eat. Allani seated the Primeval Deities on Tessub's right.


Allani stepped up in front of Tessub as a cupbearer. The fingers of her hands were long. Her four fingers were placed under the animal-shaped vessel. Flavorfulness was in the vessel from which she gave her guests drink.
 
Tessub and Suwaliyat ate and drank their fill. Tessub then stood up, and clapped his hands.
 
His order was sent up. 


Debts of Ebla:


There is no one who spoke against him among the elders. There is no one who spoke against him who could present an effective argument against him. If ever there was, in the city of Ebla, a powerful orator whose words no one could counter, Zazalla was that powerful orator. No one could equal his speech in the place of assembly. 


Zazalla began to say to Mēgi: 


" Why do you speak of such compliance, Ο’ Mēgi, star of Ebla?
“ Tessub is oppressed by debt, so he asks for debt-remission?


“ If Tessub is ever in debt for silver,
“ Then everyone will give one shekel of silver to Tessub. 
“ Everyone will give him a half shekel of gold,
“ But of silver we will each give one shekel to him.


“ If Tessub is ever hungry,
“ Then each of us will give to the god one parisu of barley.
“ Each one will heap up for him one half parisu of wheat,
“ But of barley each one will heap up for him one parisu.


“ If Tessub is ever naked, 
“ Each of us will clothe him with a fine garment,  
“ But, if Tessub is ever dried out from the heat of the day,
“ Each of us will give him a little flask of fine oil. 
“ If he is suffering from the cold of night,
“ We will provide fuel for him,
 “ The Storm-God, we will bring him back from dire need!
“ So, we will rescue him, Tessub the debtor! 
“ Who then will oppress him?


“ But, we will make no release
“ And there will be rejoicing in your soul, Ο’ Mēgi.


“ Well, on the one hand, if we were to make the release,
“ There would be no rejoicing in your soul, Mëgi.
“ Secondly, there would be rejoicing in the soul of Purra of the Ikinkalis 
“ Who are to be given back.
“ If we were to release them,
“ Who would be given to us, to marry?


“ And on the other hand,
“ They are our cupbearers; they bring us food..
“ They are our cooks, and they wash up for us 
“ The woollen threads they spin are strong as the thick hair of a cow.
“ But if you, Mēgi, wish a debt remission,
“ You must release your own slave man and slave woman. 
“ Give away your own son,
“ Send back your wife to her father's house!
“ Remain with us, Mēgi, in the throne city, Ebla.”


Now when Mëgi heard the word, he began to weep. Mēgi wept and prostrated himself at the feet of Tessub. 


Lying prostrate, Mēgi spoke his words to Tessub: 


" Hear me, Ο’ Tessub, great King of Kumme!
" I will keep on sending the parìššān, but my city will not give it!
“ Zazalla, the son of Pazzanikarri, will not allow a debt remission."


So, Mēgi cleared his city, Ebla, from its debts. For the sake of the city he remitted all debts owed to him as the ruler. 


        Still, Zazalla and the elders of Ebla would make no release of their slaves. 


So, Mēgi released his own slave men and slave women. He gave away his own son. He sent back his wife to her father's house.


There, he remained, Mēgi in the throne city, Ebla.


Megi stood before the assembly and he began to say:


“ Release the citizens of Ikinkal. 
“ Release him, Purra, the one to be given back.
“ He, who will give something to eat to nine kings!


“ In Ikinkal, the city of the throne, Purra was giving something to eat to three kings.
“ In Ebla, the city of the throne, Purra was giving something to eat to six kings.
“ But now Tessub stands up before me, Mēgi, who are the tenth king, and says: 


“" If you make a debt remission in Ebla, the city of the throne, 
““ If you make a debt remission, I will exalt your weapons. 
““ Your weapons will begin to conquer your enemies.
““ Your ploughed land will prosper in glory. 


““ But if you do not make a debt remission for Ebla, the city of the throne,
““ In the space of seven days I will come upon you, upon your bodies. 
““ I will destroy the city of Ebla, the city of the throne. 
““ I will make it like that which never existed. 
““ I will break the surrounding wall of Ebla's lower city like a cup.
““ I will trample down the surrounding wall of the upper city, like a clay pit. 
““ In the midst of the market square I will  trample the rabble 


““ I will break Ebla's walls, like a cup, and by myself, I will carry off its riches. 
““ I will curse the hearth of the upper wall.
““ And I will bring it down to the wall of the lower city.
““ I will move the hearth of the lower wall down to the river, 
““ And I will pour out the hearth of the upper wall down on the lower wall.” 


The Marriage of Martu-


When the city of Inab already existed, but the city of Kiritab did not yet exist, when the holy crown already existed, but the holy tiara did not yet exist, when the holy herb already existed, but the holy cedar did not yet exist, when holy salt already existed, but holy alkali did not yet exist, when intercourse and kissing already existed, when giving birth in the fields already existed, I was the grandfather of the holy cedar. 


I was the ancestor of the mes-tree. I was the mother and father of the white cedar. I was the relative of the hacur-cedar.


At that time there was a princely land among the cities. Inab was this princely land among the cities. The ruler of Inab was Tigi-cem-ala. Now, he had a wife whose name was Cage-gur, Desired-by-the-Heart, and a child, who sang, and her name was Šuʾilia.


The people living around the city hung up nets. The people chased and killed the gazelles, as one kills humans. 


One day, as the evening came, they had reached the place of rations. They established the rations before the God Amardu. 


The ration of a married man was established as double. The ration of a man with a child was established as triple. The ration of a single man was established as single; but the ration of Martu, though being single, was also established as double.


Martu went home to his own mother, and spoke to her: 


" In my city I am among my friends and they all have already married wives.
“ Unlike my friends in my city I am single, 
“ I am single and I have no children.
“ Yet, the imposed share exceeds that of my friends;
“ I receive half of theirs."


One day, as the evening came, and they had reached, again, the place of rations. They established the rations before the god Kusdununna. 


The ration of a married man was established as double. The ration of a man with a child was established as triple. The ration of a single man was established as single; but the ration of Martu, though being single, was also established as double.


Martu went home to his own mother, and spoke to her: 


" My mother, find me a wife to marry and I will bring you my ration." 


His own mother replied to Martu: 


" Su-henuna, my son, I will give you advice; 
“ Let it be heeded, I shall say a word to you.
“ Marry a wife of your choice,
“ Marry a wife of your heart's desire, give me thus a companion.
“ Find me a slave-girl!
“ Having built the houses of your people living around the city, and irrigated gardens,
“ You will dig the wells of your mates.


“ Martu, shall be great among his mates"


At that time a festival was announced in the city of Inab. Martu spoke, saying: 


" Come, friends, let us go, 
“ Let us go there,
“ Let us visit the ale-houses of Inab,


“ Let us go there." 


The God Numucda participated in the festival. His beloved daughter Adjar-Kidug participated in the festival. His wife Namrat, the lovely woman, participated in the festival. In the city, bronze cem-drums were rumbling, and the seven ala-drums resounded.


Strong men, girdled champions, entered the wrestling house to compete with each other for Numucda in the temple of Inab. There were many coming to Inab, the city where the festival was taking place, to marvel at this.


Because Numushda was holy, Martu also strode around the great courtyard. He too came to compete in wrestling at the gate of Inab. They kept looking for strong fighters for him. They kept offering him strong fighters. 


As Martu strode around in the great courtyard, he hit the fighters with a destructive force one by one. In the battle he caused them to be bandaged. In the great courtyard of Inab, he lifted the bodies of the dead.


Rejoicing over Martu, Numucda offered him silver, but he would not accept it. He offered jewels, but he would not accept them. He tried a second time, then a third time. Martu spoke, saying: 


" Where does your silver lead?.
“ Where do your jewels lead?
“ I, Martu, would rather marry your daughter,
“ I would rather marry your daughter Adjar-kidug."


Numucda replied:


" You shall have that when, with calves as a marriage gift,
“ Milch cows shall feed the calves.
“ In the byre, the calf and the cow shall lie down.
“ Milch cows shall live in the cow-fold, suckling calves shall stay at their right side.
“ You must give your word thus and only thus,
“ And then I will give you my daughter Adjar-kidug."


" You shall have a wife, with lambs as a marriage gift. 
“ Milch ewes shall feed the lambs.
“ In their sheep-fold the lamb and the ewe shall lie down.
“ Milch ewes shall live in the sheep-fold, and suckling lambs shall stay at their left side.
“ You must give your word thus and only thus,
“ And then I will give you my daughter Adjar-kidug."


" You shall have the wife you seek, with kids as a marriage gift. 
“ Milch goats shall feed the kids.
“ In their stall the kid and the goat shall lie down.
“ The goats shall live in the stall, and suckling kids shall stay tucked under the mother
“ You must give your word thus and only thus,
“ And then I will give you my daughter Adjar-kidug."


He rejoiced with a great shout. He shouted like a howling wind at the quay of Inab. He gratified the elders of Inab with golden torcs. He gratified the old women of Inab with golden shawl-šakkukum. He gratified the men and women of Inab with golden belts. He gratified the slaves of Inab with silver jugs; and gratified them also with coloured cloths. 


The days multiplied. No decision had yet been made. 


Adjar-Kidug's girlfriend spoke to her, saying: 


" Now listen,
“ Their hands are destructive 
“ And their features are those of monkeys; 
“ He is one who eats what Nanna forbids and does not show reverence.
“ They never stop roaming about the earth,


“ They are an abomination to the gods’ dwellings,
“ Their ideas are confused; they cause only disturbance.


“  He is clothed in sack-leather of beasts, lives in a tent, exposed to wind and rain,
“ And he cannot properly recite prayers.
“ He lives in the mountains and ignores the places of gods,
“ He digs up truffles in the foothills,
“ He does not know how to bend the knee, and eats raw flesh.
“ He has no house during his life,
“ And when he dies he will not be carried to a burial-place.


“ My girlfriend, why would you marry Martu?" 


Adjar-kidug replied to her girlfriend:


" I will marry Martu!"


Inab, ulum, alam!


Now, Numucda called on Martu. Because Ebla made no release, because there would be no manumission; Numucda sent for Martu.  His shout was like the blasting wind, as Martu cried battle. 


 The city of the throne was besieged. There, reason became confounded. The surrounding wall of Ebla's lower city was broken like a cup. The surrounding wall of the upper city lay trampled, like a clay pit. The city’s young men were slaughtered in the midst of the market square. The rabble was trampled 


Ebla's walls were broken like a cup. Its riches were carried off. The hearth of the upper wall. Martu cursed; and it was brought down to the wall of the lower city. The hearth of the lower wall was thrown down to the river; and the hearth of the upper wall poured down on the lower wall. 


Silence befell the city, as it was made like that which never existed. 


Utu-Hejal:


Enlil, the king of all the lands, entrusted Utu-Hejal, the mighty man, the King of Unug, the king of the four quarters; the king whose orders could not be countermanded.


Enlil entrusted Utu-hejal with wiping out the name of Gutium, the fanged snake of the mountains. They, who acted with violence against the gods, carried off the kingship of Sumer to foreign lands. The Gutians who filled Sumer with wickedness, took away spouses from the married, and took away children from parents. 


They made wickedness and violence normal in the land.


 Utu-Hejal went to his lady, Inanna, and prayed to her: 


" My lady, lioness in the battle, who butts the foreign lands,
“ Enlil has entrusted me with bringing back the kingship to Sumer.
“ May you be my help!"


The enemy troops established themselves everywhere. Tirigan, the King of Gutium, blocked the mouths of the channels. Nobody came out of his city to face him; he already occupied both parts of the Tigris. 


In the south, in Sumer, he blocked the water from the fields. In the uplands he closed off the roads. Because of him the grass grew high on the highways of the land.


The king, endowed with power by Enlil, chosen by Inanna with her holy heart, Utu-hejal the Mighty Man, came out from Unug to face him, and set up camp at the temple of Ickur. He addressed a speech to the citizens of his city: 


" Enlil has given Gutium to me.
“ My lady, Inanna, will be my help! 
“ Dumuzid-ama-ucumgal-ana has declared: 


“" It is a matter for me!"” 


“ And assigned Gilgamec, the son of Nin-sun to me as a constable!" 


The citizens of Unug and Kulaba rejoiced and followed him with one accord. He lined up his elite troops.


After departing from the temple of Ickur, on the fourth day, he set up camp in Najsu on the Surungal canal. On the fifth day he set up camp at the shrine at Ili-tappê. He captured Ur-Ninazu and Nabi-Enlil, generals of Tirigan sent as envoys to Sumer, and put them in handcuffs.


After departing from the shrine at Ili-tappê, on the sixth day he set up camp at Karkara. He went to Ickur and prayed to him: 


" O’, Ickur, Enlil has provided me with weapons, may you be my help!" 


In the middle of that night, from camp he departed; and above Adab, he went to the rising Utu and prayed to him: 


" O’, Utu, Enlil has given Gutium to me, may you be my help!" 


He laid a trap there behind the Gutian. Utu-Hejal, the mighty man, defeated their generals.


Then, Tirigan, the King of Gutium, ran away alone on foot. He thought himself safe in Dabrum, where he fled to save his life. Since the people of Dabrum knew that Utu-Hejal was a king endowed with power by Enlil, they did not let Tirigan go. An envoy of Utu-Hejal arrested Tirigan together with his wife and children in Dabrum. 


He put handcuffs and a blindfold on him. Before Utu, Utu-Hejal made him lie at his feet and placed his foot on his neck. He made Gutium, the fanged snake of the mountains, drink again from the crevices. He reduced the Gutian storehouses, he took back the gods and he carried them by boat. 


He brought back the kingship of Sumer.




Chapter 12
The Song of Emergence-
Tablet I:


A song of Kumarbi, Father of the Gods.
 
The primeval deities, who are in the dark earth, let those important primeval deities listen! 
Let Nara, Napšara, Minki, and Ammunki listen! Let Ammezzadu and Nammezzadu, father and mother of the great gods, listen! Let Anu and Apandu, father and mother of Išḫara, listen! Let Enlil and Ninlil, who are weighty and eternal deities, be contented and quiet. 


Listen!


Long ago, in primeval years Alalu was king in heaven. Alalu was sitting on the throne, and weighty Anu, the foremost of the gods, was standing before him. He was bowing down at Alalu's feet, and was placing in his hand the drinking cups.


For a mere nine years, Alalu was king in heaven. In the ninth year Anu gave battle against Alalu, and defeated him. Alalu fled before him and went down to the dark earth. Anu took his seat on his throne. 


Anu was sitting on his throne. The weighty Kumarbi was giving him drink. Kumarbi was bowing down at his feet and placing drinking cups in his hand.


For a mere nine years, Anu remained king in heaven. In the ninth year Anu gave battle against Kumarbi. Kumarbi gave battle against Anu. 


Anu could no longer withstand Kumarbi's eyes. 


Anu wriggled loose from Kumarbi's hands and fled. He set out for the sky. Kumarbi rushed after him, and seized him by the feet. He seized him by the legs, and dragged him down from the sky.


Kumarbi was snapping his jaws, like a dog. He snapped at Anu’s buttocks, but Kumarbi bit Anu's loins. His manhood united with Kumarbi's insides like bronze. When Kumarbi had swallowed the manhood of Anu, he rejoiced and laughed out loud. Anu turned around and spoke to him: 


" Are you rejoicing within yourself because you have swallowed my manhood?
" Stop rejoicing within yourself! For I have placed inside you, a burden. 
“ First, I have impregnated you with the Noble Storm-God, Tessub.
" Second, I have impregnated you with the irresistible Aranzah River. 
“ Third, I have impregnated you with the Noble Tašmišu 


“ I have placed three inside you as burdens. 
“ In the future you will end up striking the boulders of Mount Tassa with your head!"


When Anu had finished speaking, he went up to the sky and hid himself. Kumarbi, the wise king, spat from his mouth. He spat from his mouth spittle and semen mixed together. Mount Kanzura became pregnant with the terrible Tašmišu from that which Kumarbi had spat up.


Kumarbi, upset, went to the city of Nippur. He was sat down on a lordly throne. Kumarbi didn’t move. The seventh month arrived, and inside of him the mighty deities stirred.


In pain, Kumarbi cursed. From his pain, he doubled over. Kumbari pleaded:
 
“ Leave Kumarbi!
“ From his vigorous lions come forth, or from the body, the swelling, come out! 
“ Or come out from the good-place!” 


The gods, A.GILIM, watching over Kumarbi within the Kubala palace interior, began to speak words to Ea: 


“ May you be living Ο’ Lord of the Source of Wisdom!
“ Where are the children to come out? “


They prepared Kumarbi for the birth of Mount Kanzura; they brought him to her. They brought Kumarbi to Mount Kanzura. The birth-deities caused Mt. Kanzura to give birth, and she heaved forth.


The birth-deities caused her to give birth like a woman in childbed, and the hero came out. Tašmišu came out through the good place. The Heroic Šuwaliyat emerged. Anu rejoíced, as he beheld his son.


He slapped his thigh and heartily laughed: 


“ This will be you Kumbari, this will happen to you!


“ You, frowning thing, god whom I once feared. 
“ You will be mother of what seeds I gave into you,
“ The ones to which I gave everything!


“ When they come, they will birth from him like another woman. 
“ Come out in just the same way! 


“ Come out by the mouth! 
“ From his body come out! 
“ If you wish, come out by the good place!” 


After Mount Kanzura had given birth to Tašmišu, Ea and the Kotharat took the catatonic Kumbari into the Absu-Eridu. Ea began to speak words to Kumarbi in the interior palace. The god within Kumbari called out to Ea.


“ If I come out to you, from his lion it will snap me off like a reed. 
“ If I come out to you from his anus, that too will defile me. 
“ It will defile me on the ear, it will befuddle my senses 
“ If I come out through the good place, 
“ A midwife will seize upon my head, and strike me.”


Thereupon Ea seized Kumbari. He split his head, like a stone, and he, the Storm-God, left Kumarbi. Šassūrātu, the Fate Goddesses, closed up his good place, like they would mend a torn garment or the second place, the place of birth where women babies came out. They, the midwives, brought him to birth like a woman of the bed. 


The divine KA.ZAL, the valiant king, came up out of his skull. As he went, KA.ZAL took his stand before Ea and bowed. 


Kumarbi fell down from his throne. 
Tablet II:


Kumarbi’s face changed hue as he looked for NAM.HE. He was incensed, and began to speak to Ea: 


" Give me my child, that I may eat him up. 
“ He who oppressed me, like a woman! 
“ He who came to me, like a woman!


“ I will eat up Tessub.
“ I will smash him like a brittle reed." 


Ea gave over Ka.Zal, he intentionally gathered him at Mount Tassu. 


Kumarbi gleamed at the notion of revenge. He burned bright on the top of the mountain as the Sun-God of the Sky saw him. 


Kumarbi began to eat. 


Basalt injured Kumarbi's mouth and teeth! 


When it had shattered his teeth, Kumarbi began to weep and roar. Tessub watched and spoke his words: 


" Who was I afraid of?"


 Kumarbi sobbed like a woman as Tessub began to speak to him: 


“ Let them call it the lord’s stone, let it be placed in the shrine!” 


He threw thebasalt into the mountain , saying:


" In the future let them call you Lord!
“ Let the rich men, the valiant lords, slaughter for you cattle and sheep! 
“ Let the poor men make their sacrifice to you with meal!"


And it was so.


The rich men began to slaughter with cattle and rams. The poor men began to sacrifice with meal. The gods began to perform their works. They closed up his skull as they would mend a torn garment. 


The Storm-God left Kumarbi. 
Tablet III:


Now, because  Kumarbi birthed from the mouth, no one would serve him as king. He was unable to muster a force to take his throne back. 


Kumarbi spoke somberley. A thought occurred to him. The gods controlled the lands above and below, so Kumbari appealed to Primeval Gods. 


There he plotted, in council.


“ Hear me, 
“ We will destroy the Great God, Anu! 


“ Furthermore we will destroy, also, Tašmišu, the hero in their midst.
“ Hear me, in the midst of the Great Gods, we will destroy NAM.HE 
“ Like a stone, we will crush them.” 


When Kumarbi, spoke what words he spoke, the Primeval-Gods replied:


“ What will you destroy, Kumarbi?


“ Does he sit on the throne, Kumarbi?


“ Who will destroy Tessub for us? 
“ And when he, the destroyer, comes to maturity, they will make someone else king.
“ It is he who will, indeed, leave us slain.


“ Abandon him!


“ O’ King Ea, Lord of the Source of Wisdom. 
“ Make my brother king, place Kumarbi on the throne
“ And heed my words, 


“ The storm devours” 


When Tessub heard these words, he became sad. Tessub said to his bull, Seri:


" Who can come against me in battle? 
“ Who can defeat me now?
“ Even Kumarbi cannot rise against me!
“ Even Ea, the chosen son, and the Sun-God honor me.


“ I drove Kumarbi from his throne at the time of my birth; I cursed him, eternally.
“ I cursed Ea for my humiliation.
“ I cursed the War-God too, and brought him to the town Banapi.
“ So who now can do battle any more against me?"


The bull, Seri, replied to Tessub:


" My lord! Why are you cursing them, why are you cursing the gods? 
“ My lord, why are you cursing them?
“ Why are you also cursing Ea?


“ Ea will hear you, his domain is as big as the land.
“ Powerful, you may be, but one day consequence will come. 
“ You will not be able to lift your neck.
“ When it is in the stock, you will not be able to lift your neck 


“ Wise is he, Lord Ea.”


 Tauri heard the word of Tessub and with speed went to inform Ea. When Ea heard the words, he became sad in his heart. He began to speak words back to the God Tauri:


" Do not speak curses to me!
“ He who curses me does so, at great risk to himself.


“ You who repeat to me those curses; you are yourself cursing me!
“ Heed my words:


“ Under the pot a fire is placed, and that pot will boil over."


The Slaying of Labbu-


The cities became exhausted, the people. The people were diminished. Because of their lamentation Enlil could not sleep. Because of their groaning sleep did not overcome him. 


" Who will give birth to the serpent? 
“ Let the Sea give birth to the serpent!" 


Enlil in heaven designed strength. Its length was 50 leagues, its breadth one league. Its mouth was 6 cubits, its tongue 12 cubits. Its ear-flaps were 12 cubits. For 60 cubits it rose into the sky alongside the birds. For nine cubits it drew along its belly in the water. 


It raised its tail. It slammed it to the earth and shook the land. All the gods of heaven cried out. In heaven the gods bowed down before Enki, as Sin's face was obscured with his cloak. 


" Who will go and kill Labbu, who will save the broad land.
“ Slay the serpent and will exercise kingship
“ Save the broad land and exercise kingship" 


There was not one who came forward. Samas was thrown in the water, Enki opened his mouth to speak, addressing a word to Isimud: 


" Bring me Aruru, let Aruru give a hero" 


She came:


" Aruru, listen to me, Aruru, heed my word! Among the 66 sons of your brothers 
“ To whom did you give strength? To whom did you grant a full measure of might? " 


Aruru gave answer:


" To Nergal I gave strength; to Nergal I granted a full measure of might" 


Enki gave shout:


" Bring me Nergal, Nergal-Meslamtaeda; of Nergal, I will speak the utterance of kingship" 


" Nergal, listen to me, Nergal , heed my word!  Keep listening to the utterance of my lips
“ A great serpent has been created in the sea; its length is 60 leagues, 
“ Its head is 30 leagues high, its eyelids extend for half a league! 
“ For 20 leagues, its tail keeps moving! 


“ It has devoured fish, the creatures of the sea, 
“ It has devoured birds, the creatures of the sky; 
“ It has devoured wild asses, the creatures of the steppe, 
“ It has devoured humans, the peoples of the earth
" Go, Tispak, kill Labbu, 
“ Go, Nergal! 


“ Kill the serpent with haste, go, quickly!" 


Aruru opened her mouth to speak, addressing a word to Nergal, 


"  Father Enki has bestowed you with exalted might; a hatchet of 30 minas, a dirk of nine 
“ He, who receives the abundance of the seas; may he protect you!" 


Then, he proceeded on his way.


Tispak replied:


" You have sent me, Lord of the Abzu, but I do not know the weakness of Labbu.”


Enki opened his mouth and spoke to Tispak 


" Send a cloud upon him, raise a storm, 
“ Hold in front of you the seal around your neck,
“ Shoot, and kill Labbu." 


He sent a cloud upon him. He raised a storm. He held in front of him the seal around his neck. He shot, and killed Labbu. 


For three years, three months, one day and night, the blood of Labbu flowed.


The Song of Hedammu-
Tablet I:


Tašmišu was separated from his brother. He saw his chance to strike down the Storm-God. Kumarbi began to speak words to his mind:


" If I, Kumarbi, were to rise, 
“ If in the place of assembly, I spoke heatedly,
“ If he perhaps struck me; Ea, our king of wisdom, would slay me.


“ If humankind perhaps suffered, being foremost of them,
“ Ea also, would surely suffer." 


Kumarbi, again, began to speak before his mind, 


" Me, Kumarbi, has suffered him.
“ It was me, that Ammezzadu, held to her breast. 
" And, among the gods, I, Kumarbi, should have rule.
“ If for Tarhun, then a rival I shall fashion.
“ For Tarhun, the heroic bull, he shall be great, he shall eat greatly.” 


Kumarbi summoned Mukishanu and began to speak:


“ Hear my words my vizier, Mukisanu!
“ The words which I speak to thee, to my words, hold thine ear inclined!
“ To me, the Sea God, you shall bring.


“ Make your way away, under the river and earth. 
“ Let Kusuh, Istanu, and the Gods of the Dark Earth not see you.
“ And thus, tell him:


“ Go up to Kumarbi under river and earth.” 


Mukishanu heard the words. He promptly stood up and made his way away, under the rivers and earth. Kusuh, Istanu, and the gods of the earth did not see him go down to the sea.


Mukishanu began to speak the words of Kumarbi to the Sea:


" Come, the Father of the Gods, Kumarbi, calls you,
“ And for which matter he calls you, it is an urgent matter
“ Come promptly!


“ Come away under river and earth.
“ Let not Kusuh, Istanu, and the Gods of the Dark Earth see you."


When the Great-Sea heard the words, he stood up promptly. He went away down by the path of earth and of river, completing it in one go. He came up to Kumarbi from below the pillar of the earth. A throne for the Sea, they set for him to sit. 


The Great-Sea sat on his throne. A laid table for him to eat was set. Sweet wine to drink, the cup-bearer gave him. Kumarbi, the Father of the Gods, and Kiaže, the Great-Sea, sat. They ate and drank their fill, while Kumarbi spoke to the Sea-God of his plans and kingship.


The Sea-God heard, and his mind rejoiced. He propped his foot on a stool and they put a rhyton in the sea god's hand. The great Sea-God began to reply to Kumarbi: 


" Our matter is settled Kumarbi, Father of the Gods.
“ Come to my house in seven days,
" I will give you Sertapsuruhi, my daughter,
“ Whose length is nine furlong and whose width is eight furlong.


“ You will drink of Sertapsuruhi, like sweet cream." 


When Kumarbi heard this, his mind within him rejoiced. 


Night fell. 


They brought the great Sea-God out of Kumarbi's house accompanied by the music of bronze arkammi and galgalturi-instruments. They escorted him to his house. Once there he sat down on a good chair made of wood. 


The Sea-God waited seven days for Kumarbi.


Kumarbi wed the sea goddess; to Kumarbi she made obeisance. Back to Kummi, the city, and then to Duddulla, the city, she went. But it was in front of the sea, where they raised Hedammu. They placed him in oil, and anointed him; they then placed him in water. 


Like an apple tree they protected him from the cold. They gave him two thousand cows and horses to eat. The goats and sheep which they gave him, there was no counting at all. 


He ate cows and horses by the thousand. He raised up horrible fish, lizards, and frogs from the sea. He put them into a storage place, like in a pit. In the water he dragged them, and an IKU's worth of fish. 


The dogs of the river were within, and by the thousand he ate them. Not even chewing, but like smooth honey, he swallowed them down. Like a hound he lapped the bowl he finished eating. 


He never tired. He never filled. 


The Sea-Goddess sent a messenger to Kumarbi. She went to him, and came to him from under the pillar of the dark earth. She spoke to Kumarbi the words of the sea; likewise, Kumarbi remembered his pregnant wife.


When Kumarbi heard the words of the sea, he shouted seven times for the messenger. A wreath of gold was gifted, and Kumarbi sent the messenger back downwards secretly.


Kumarbi, then, went down to the sea. They gave him food to eat and wine to drink. The Sea-Goddess lifted the boy, placing him on the knees of Kumarbi. He held him forth, amusing the boy and decided his name.


The Sea-Goddess came back and forth from sea to land. She would gather food for the snake. Her motherly duty, she did. She feared leaving the sea serpent to himself. 


For him, back and forth she moved through heaven and earth. Back and forth she went searching for aid. 


She went unto the temple. Kumarbi began to speak words to his vizier:


" Mukishanu, my vizier, go in the temple of Nippur
“ Drive away the snakes, and drive them away from the city
“ From the walls drive them away, from the pegs and from the courtyard gate,
“ Drive them away from the doors,
“ And which windows are facing Kummi, let them be closed, 
“ Which doors are facing Nineveh, let them be shut off with a cloth of Byblos,
“ Let Anzili of Nineveh not hear this matter,
“ And inside of the house prepare a lot to eat and to drink.
“ Call the Great-Sea and I will give the boy into the care of a nurse." 


Mukishanu heard the words, and promptly stood up. He traveled through the Nether-Gods lands. He entered the temple of Nippur, holding a bronze knife. 


He did drive away the snakes from the foundations. He opened the gate. He drove away the snakes from the pegs. From the ceiling, he drove them away.


Which windows that were facing Kummi were closed up. Which doors and windows were facing Nineveh, were shut off with a cloth of Byblos. Inside of the house he prepared a lot to eat and to drink. He filled vessels with wine-beer. The laid tables were piled with bread.


 Mukishanu carried out the words of Kumarbi, and he brought back word:


" Where you sent me, I have carried out everything, in that very spot,
“ And also inside of the house, I have prepared things to eat and drink,


“ As the will of my lord had commanded."


Kumarbi began to speak words to Mukishanu:


" As a god does, we shall hold a one time festival
“ For the birth of the child, the rival for Tarhun!”


Tablet II:


The river began rising in front of the Mount of Gods. 


She ran. 


Anzili ran to Tarhun. 


She crossed the distance to Storm-god.


When he saw the Anzili coming, Tasmisu began to speak:


" Why does she come, the Queen of Nineveh, the sky's queen? 
“ The matter about which she comes, that matter must be grave,
“ It is not to be cast aside!


“ Strong it is, the struggle, strong it is, the battle!
“ Heaven's uproar it is, the land's hunger and death it is!"  


The Storm-God  began to speak to Tasmisu:


“ Sauska, Queen of Nineveh, comes. 
“ Let them set up a chair for her to sit in.
“ Let them spread a table for her to eat at.”
 
While they were thus speaking, Sauska reached them. They set up a chair for her to sit in. She did not sit down in it. They decked a table for her to eat at, but she did not reach out to it. They gave her a cup; and the Queen of Nineveh did not put her lip to it.


Tessub began to speak: 


" Why do you not eat or drink, my lady? 
“ Is it because you don't know it, namely, food?
“ Is it because you don't know drinking? 


Sauska spoke:


“ My lord, why are you eating? Why are you drinking?
“ Do you not know?
“ Against the gods, the Sea-God has been incited.
“ Have you not heard, O’ He in Sky and Earth?
“ Shall I describe the usurper in the sea?" 


She described Hedammu, and Sauska wailed for him, her brother. 


Tessub heard, and became sad. His tears flow forth like streams. Like canals, they flowed from the house. 


The serpent, like a millstone ground dust in the lands. The rival, Hedammu, reduced men to dust.


Kumarbi turned his eyes to the sea, as he began to speak his words:


"  Go To him! 
“ Because of hunger, Hedammu arrives.


“ To him! 


“ You, I must ask!
“ But if, to him, you cannot go, send a messenger to feed him. 
“ He eats a cow, and horses; he eats frogs, fish, and lizards by the thousand.”


Away the messenger went to him, the Lord of the Sea. He heard the word. The child of great power was sent down into the dark earth. The words of Kumarbi they followed, with great regard to the matter of the watch. The Sea-God was simply silent. 


Kumarbi, then, went down to the sea. Before him, the Sea-God did not speak. 


Kumarbi went up from the sea, and placed his feet up on a stool. Tarhun, heroic king of Kummi, went before him, and began to speak:


" Kumarbi, Father of the gods, where were you, and what have you done?


“ Who is he, this enemy?" 


Kumarbi began to speak his response to Tarhun:


" From Mt. Hurshana, O’ Lord Tarhun, I have raised a snake,


“ It will devour!”


Ea, King of Wisdom, spoke among the gods: 


" Why are you destroying mankind? 
“ They will not give sacrifices to the gods; they will not burn cedar as incense to you.


“ If you destroy mankind, they will no longer worship the gods.
“ No one will offer bread or libations to you any longer.


“ Even Tessub, Kumm        iya's heroic king, will himself grasp the plow.
“ Even Sauska and Hebat will themselves grind at the millstones." 


Ea, King of Wisdom, said to Kumarbi:


" Why are you, Kumarbi, seeking to harm mankind?
“ Does not the mortal take a grain heap 
“ And do they not promptly offer it to you, Kumarbi?.


“ Does he make offering to you alone Kumarbi, Father of the Gods?


“ Joyfully in the midst of the temple,
“ Do they not also offer to Tessub, the Canal Inspector of Mankind?
“ And don't they call me, Ea, by name as king? 
“ But you, Kumarbi, are putting wisdom behind the death of all the gods!


“ You will dry up the blood and tears of mankind Kumarbi, Father of the Gods!" 


Kumarbi began to speak words to his own mind:


" Why would you boil at me, Kumarbi, like a slave, in the place of assembly?
“ Why would you strike me, Ea, O’ Great King of Wisdom? 
“ Why would you stand up against me? Why would you defend mankind? 
“  Why is Ea, foremost among the gods?"


Kumarbi spoke words before his mind, again:


" I, Kumarbi, son of Alalu, I of the god Ammezzadu.
“ In the midst of the gods,
“ I, Kumarbi, raised up Hedammu like a rival, as a usurper against Tessub.


“ The heroic end to Tessub!”


Kumarbi summoned Mukishanu and began to speak:


“ Hear my words, my vizier, Mukisanu 
“ The words which I speak to thee, hold thine ear inclined!
“ To me, the Sea-God you shall bring.


“ Make your way away, under the river and earth. 
“ Let the sun god, the moon god, and the gods of the dark earth not see you.
“ And thus, tell him:


“ Go up to Kumarbi under river and earth.” 


Mukishanu heard the words. He promptly stood up. He made his way away, under the river and earth. The Sun God, the Moon God, and the Gods of the Dark Earth did not see him. He went down to the sea.


Mukishanu began to speak the words of Kumarbi to the Sea:


" Come, the Father of the Gods, Kumarbi, calls you,
“ And for which matter he calls you, it is an urgent matter, come promptly,


“ Come away under river and earth.
“ Let not Kusuh, Istanu, and the Gods of the Dark Earth see you."


Again, Kumarbi spoke words to his vizier:


" Mukisanu, my vizier, listen carefully to the words I speak to you!


“ Bolt the door, throw the latch! 
“ Let not the breeze, like an aroma, nor light, like a drop of water
“ Let them not enter!
 “ Lest poor men shall hound us, like hungry wolves " 


Mukisanu heard the words, and quickly arose. He began to throw the latch and bolted the door. In hand, he held a bronze knife . 


A blow clattered! 


A strike fell against the serpent! 


The lightning flashed and the thunder roared!


Mukisanu cried: 


“ Tessub and Sauska have not yet gone away with the water!
“ We have not yet come out of peril, and our knees tremble beneath us.
“ Our head spins like a potter's wheel,
“ Our little family suffers, like lost travellers in the vast desert! 


Tablet III:


Tessub spoke to Sauska: 


“ Now we will engage in conflict, and Hedammu will rise. 
“ If I kill Hedammu, it will restore my glory.
“ But if Hedammu devours all, then it is my fault”


When Tessub finished speaking, he went away. Sauska went to the bath house. The Queen of Nineveh went and washed herself. She bathed; she anointed herself with fine perfumed oil. She adorned herself. The qualities which arouse love ran after her like puppies.


Sauska spoke to Ninatta and Kulitta, and said:


" Take an arkammi-instrument, take a galgalturi-instrument.
“ At the sea on the right play the arkammi, on the left play the galgalturi
“ Sing to him songs of kingship,
“ Perhaps Hedammu will hear our message?
“ Now, let us see how he responds."


Ninatta and Kulitta went to the sea. On the right, the arkammi played, on the left, the galgalturi. The singers sang the Song of Kingship. The Heavens and Earth echoed back. Sauska sprang towards Hedammu. She leapt in the deep waters. 


When Hedammu saw Sauska, he grew curious. He wondered, and raised his head from the watery deep. He spied Sauska. 


Sauska held up her naked members before the sea, and Hedammu began to speak words to her:


" What deity are you that you do not know?
“ You leapt, and into the sea you came" 


Sauska bellowed to him, as to a bull:


“ It is you, who doesn't know?’


Sertapsuruhi, Daughter of the Sea, came to Hedammu. She began to speak to Hedammu: 


" Heroic Tessub, he fills the sky in anger.
“ Your mother will protect you dear, valiant usurper!"


Hedammu turned back his eyes, and began to say to Sauska:
 
" You are unlike any other woman, so I will eat you up.
“ The clouds are angry, but they will bow to me!" 
 
Again, Hedammu spoke words to Sauska:


" What kind of woman are you?" 


Sauska replied to Hedammu: 


" I am an angry woman.
“ The mountains spread out their greenery for me, like woven cloth."


Sauska then spoke flattery to Hedammu. She praised him with words. They began to intoxicate him. Hedammu said to Sauska: 


" What kind of woman are you? 
“ That I may know your name! 


“ I am….." 


The words trailed off.


In the sky, clouds filled from conjured waters Tessub made. When Sauska, Queen of Nineveh, had approved the water, she filled a love potion. Sahis and parnulli wood brewed strong waters and smelled. The love potion, the sahis and parnulli wood in the waters, grew fragrant.


Now, when Hedammu had tasted the aroma, the beer, sweet sleep overcame him. Sleep seized the mind of the valiant Hedammu. He was dozing like an ox or ass. He recognized nothing. He was eating his frogs and snails, as he was seized in dreaming.


Sauska said to Hedammu:


" Come up again, come from the strong waters.
“ Come through the midst of the Earth." 


Hedammu’s length was 90,000 miles . He leveled a deep place from the earth. Sauska held out her naked members toward Hedammu. Hedammu saw the beautiful goddess. His penis sprang forth. 


His penis impregnates cities! 


He impregnated 130 cities. Lo’, he filled 70 more cities with his belly’s seed, when his ejaculation came to an end. There were heaped up piles of heads.


Sauska, Queen of Nineveh, came down to him. Hedammu was struck at the pedestal. . The second he was seized in sleep, she came down to Hedammu. Sauska, Queen of Nineveh, walked before him. 


Sauska came down to him, and after her, Hedammu, like a river, poured out semen. It blanketed it on the earth like frightful floods. The valiant Hedammu fell down from his throne, from the sea. He came out onto the dry land.


Hedammu began to speak to Anzili:


" Young woman, if to you are meant love,
“ Then  come give love.
“ Come in the strong waters .... beautiful Anzili!” 


She went. Sauska went away from the sea. The fragrance trailed behind her like a cloak. The Serpent still chased her. Away from the sea he chased her. 


Away from his mother they went.


The Illuyanka Tales-
Tablet I:


This is the text of the Purulli Festival for the children of the Storm-God of Heaven. Written according to Kella, the gudu-priest of the Storm-God of Nerik; they speak thus:


" Let the land prosper and thrive, and let the land be protected
“ And when it prospers and thrives, they shall perform the Purulli Festival.” 


When the Storm-God and the Serpent fought each other in Kiskilussa, the serpent defeated the storm god. Then, the Storm-God invoked all the gods:


" Come together,


“ To me!" 


Inara prepared a feast. 


In the vessels, she prepared abundant refreshment. She prepared everything on a grand scale. Storage vessels full of wine. Storage vessels of marnuwan-beer and walhi-drink. 


Inara then went to the town of Ziggaratta and found a mortal named Hupasiya. 


Inara spoke as follows to Hupasiya:


" I am about to do such-and-such a thing and you will join with me" 


Hupasiya replied as follows to Inara: 


" If I may sleep with you, then I will come and I shall perform your heart's desire." 


So he slept with her.


Inara led Hupasiya off. She concealed him. 


Inara dressed herself up and called the Serpent up from its hole, saying: 


" I'm preparing a feast.
“ Come; eat and drink!" 


The Serpent and his offspring came up. 


Joyfully, they ate and drank. They drank up every vessel, so that they became drunk. They did not want to go back down into their hole again. 


Hupasiya came and tied up the serpent’s offspring with a rope. The Storm-God came and killed the serpents and the gods that were with them. 


Then Inara built a house on a rock outcropping in the town of Tarukka, north of Hattusa. She settled Hupasiya there, in the house. Inara repeatedly instructed him:


" When I go out into the open country, do not look out the window!
“ If you look out, you will see your wife and children." 


Twenty days had passed and he looked out the window; there, he saw his wife and children. 


When Inara returned from the open country, he began to weep; saying: 


" Let me go back home!"


Inara said to Hupasiya: 


“ Then away! You go, by means of an offence to Daughter of the Storm-God”
 
The meadow flooded. She wept; and he was gone.


Then, Inara went to the town of Kiskilussa. 


How did the hand of the king establish her house and the river of the watery abyss? 


Because we celebrate the first Purulli Festival, the hand of the king establishes the River of the Watery Abyss of Inara!


The divine mountain Zaliyanu, first in rank of all the gods. When he has granted rain in Nerik, the staff bearer brings thick bread from Nerik. 


He asked Zaliyanu for rain, so it was brought to him. The thick bread grows there, in the meadow. 
Tablet II:


This is the text of the Purulli Festival for the children of the Storm-God of Heaven. That which Kella, the gudu-priest, has said:


When the Serpent defeated the Storm-God, he took his heart and eyes. The Storm-God feared him. 


He took as his wife, the daughter of a poor man and sired a son. 


When the son grew up, he took the Daughter of the Serpent as his wife. The Storm-God repeatedly instructed him: 


" When you go to live in the house of your wife,
“ Demand from them my heart and eyes as a bride-price." 


So, when he went, he demanded from them the heart; and they gave it to him. After that, he demanded from them the eyes. They gave those too, to him. He brought them to his father, the Storm-God, who took back his heart and his eyes. 


When he was sound in body as before; again, he went to the sea to do battle. When he gave him battle, he at last began to defeat the Serpent. 


The Storm God's son, who was with the Serpent, called up to his father in the sky: 


" Include me with them; and have no pity on me." 


So, the Storm-God killed both the Serpent and his own son. 


Then, the Storm-God wept. 


Thus says Kella, the gudu-priest of the Storm-God of Nerik.




The Disappearance of Telipinu-
Tablet I:


Telipinu became enraged. He shouted: 


" Let there be no intimidating language!" 


Then he drew on the right shoe on his left foot, and the left shoe on his right foot.


Mist seized the windows. Smoke seized the house. In the fireplace the logs were stifled. 


At the altars, the gods were stifled. In the sheep pen, the sheep were stifled. In the cattle barn, the cattle were stifled. The mother sheep rejected her lamb. The cow rejected her calf. 


Telipinu went away, and removed grain, animal fecundity, luxuriance and growth, abundance to the steppe, and to the meadow. He went into the moor, and blended with it. Over him, the halenzu-plant grew. 


Barley and wheat no longer ripened. Cattle, sheep, and humans no longer became pregnant. Even those that were pregnant could not give birth. The mountains and the trees dried up. The shoots did not come forth.


The pastures and the springs dried up. Famine broke out in the land. Humans and gods were dying of hunger. 


The Great Sun-God made a feast and invited the thousand gods. 


They ate but were not sated. They could not be sated! They drank but couldn't quench their thirst.


The Storm-God remembered his son, Telipinu:


" My son, Telipinu, is not there.
“ He became enraged,
“ He removed everything good, so that famine broke out in the land." 


The great gods and lesser gods began to search for Telipinu, but they did not find him. The Sun-God sent the swift eagle: 


" Go search the high mountains, search the deep valleys, search the blue deep." 


The eagle went. It searched the springs. It searched the rivers, but it didn't find him. He brought back a report to the Sun-God:


" I couldn't find Telipinu, the Noble God."


The Storm-God said to Hannahanna:


" How shall we act? 
“ We are going to die of hunger." 


Hannahanna said to the Storm-God: 


" Do something, Storm-God! 
“ Go search for Telipinu yourself." 


The Storm-God began to search for Telipinu. In his city, the Storm-God grasped the city gate. He couldn’t manage to open it; instead, the Storm-God broke his hammer and his peg. He wrapped himself up in his garment, and sat down. 


Hannahanna sent a bee: 


" You go search for the son, Telipinu. 
“ When you find him, sting his hands and feet and make him stand up.
“ Then take wax and wipe him off, purify him, make him holy again
“ Then, conduct him back here to me." 


The Storm-God said to Hannahanna:


" Since the great and small gods have been searching for him, they haven't found him, 
“ So will this bee find him, how? 
“ His wings are small, and he too, himself is small; furthermore, it is all by itself!" 


Hannahanna replied to the Storm-God: 


" Desist! It will go find him." 


Hannahanna said to bee, once more:


" You, Ο’ bee, go now; look for Telipinu. 
“ When you find him, sting him on his hands and feet to make him stand up,
“ Take wax and wipe off his eyes and his hands, purify him and bring him back to me."


The bee went, and it began to search for Telipinu.


The bee searched the high mountains. It searched the deep valleys. It searched the blue deep. 


The honey was exhausted in its interior. The wind was exhausted under its wings. 


There, it found him in a meadow, in the town of Lihzina! 


It found him in a forest. 


It stung him on his hands and feet, so that he got up. 


Telipinu stood and said: 


" I was both angry and sleeping!
“ Why did you arouse me when I was sleeping?
“ Why did you make me talk when I was sulking?"


Telipinu became even more angry. 


He dried the spring. He drew the rivers and brooks. He hurled the stones and made them leap and flee. He heaped up the riverbanks. He knocked down cities. He knocked down houses. 


He destroyed people. He destroyed cattle and sheep. The gods feared him:


" Telipinu has become angry, now how shall we act? 
“ How shall we act?" 


Telipinu shouted:


" Summon the mortal, a human being, and let him be the sacrifice on Mount Ammuna
“ Let the eagle move him, let him bring him.
“ Let the eagle approach and move him with a wing. 
“ Let the mortal come, make him arise!"
. 
The eagle brought him. The eagle moved him with its wing. They feared him and shouted.


The bee went to Telipinu, and it applied the wax. He, the bee, spoke:


” Here lies wax for you.
“ Drive Telipinu's anger, wrath, sin, and sullenness out from your presence. 
“ Here lies wheat for you,
“ Just as this wheat is pure, 
“ Let Telipinu's heart and soul become pure again in the same way.” 


The gods accompanied the bee in trying to appease Telipinu:


“ Here lies malt and beer bread for you. 
“ Just as malt and beer bread blend in essence,
“ So that their soul and heart become one, 
“ So may your soul be united in the same way with the words of these mortals
“ May the sweet odour invoke you, Telipinu.


“ Frustrated, may you be relaxed. 
“ They get an angry person drunk with beer of malt, and his anger vanishes from him.
“ They get a timid man drunk and his timidity vanishes from him. 
“ Let the malt and beer bread get you drunk, Telipinu, in the same way, 
“ Let wrath and anger, sin, and sullenness vanish from you. 
“ As the fire is extinguished, so may Telipinu's evil anger, wrath, sin, and sullenness 
“ Be extinguished in the same way. 


“ O’ Telipinu, let go of anger, let go of wrath, let go of sin and sullenness. 
“ As the water in drain pipes doesn't flow backward,
“ So may the evil anger, wrath, sin, and sullenness of Telipinu likewise not come back. 
“ May the evil anger, wrath, sin, and sullenness go away.
“ But may it not go into the fruitful field, the forest, or the garden.
“ May it go on the road to the dark earth.


“ Down in the dark earth stand iron vats, and their lids are of lead. 
“ Whatever goes into them doesn't come up again; it perishes therein.
“ So may Telipinu's evil anger, wrath, sullenness, and sin go into them 
“ And not come up again, but perish therein. 


“ Here lies galaktar, may your soul, Ο’ Telipinu, be made tranquil.
“ Here lies parhuenas-fruit, may its essence implore you. 
“ Here lies samama-nuts, let them be manifested. 
“ Here lie figs, and just as a fig is sweet, so let your soul, Telipinu,
“ Become sweet in the same way. 
“ Just as an olive holds its oil in its heart, just as a grape holds its wine in its heart, 
“ So you, Telipinu, must hold goodness in your soul and heart in the same way. 


“ Here lies resinous wood. Let it anoint your soul, Telipinu.
“ Just as honey is sweet, and as ghee is mild, 
“ So let the soul of Telipinu become sweet in the same way,
“ And let it become mild in the same way. 


“ Telipinu eat fine things, drink fine things.
“ May your path, Ο’ Telipinu, be sprinkled with fine oil.
“ Then set out upon it 
“ May your bedding be fragrant sahis and happuri-yasas boughs. 
“ Then sleep upon it.
“ As fragrant reed is pleasant,
“ May you be pleasant also to the king and queen and to the land of Hatti.”
Tablet II:


They could not stop him. They sat down under the tree, the tree he previously, too, had sat under. 


Telipinu came in anger. He thundered together with lightning. Below he struck the dark earth. Kamrusepa saw him and came down, herself, with the eagle's wing. 


She stopped the anger. 


She stopped the wrath. 


She stopped sin. 


She stopped sullenness. 


Kamrusepa said to the Gods:


" Go, ‘Ο gods. Now tend the Sun-God's sheep for Hapantali,
“ And cut out twelve rams, so that I may treat Telipinu's karas-grains. 


“ I have taken for myself a basket with a thousand small holes.
“ And upon it I have poured karas-grains, the Rams of Kamrusepa. 
“ I have made a burning back and forth over Telipinu, on one side and on the other. 


“ I have taken from Telipinu,


“ From his body, his evil I have taken;
“ I have taken his sin; I have taken his anger; 
“ I have taken his wrath; I have taken his pique; I have taken his sullenness.
“ Telipinu is angry, his soul and essence were stifled like burning brushwood.
“ Just as they burned these sticks of brushwood,
“ May the anger, wrath, sin, and sullenness of Telipinu likewise burn up. 
“ And just as malt is sterile, so that they don't carry it to the field and use it as seed, 
“ As they don't make it into bread and deposit it in the seal house, 
“ So may the anger, wrath, sin, and sullenness of Telipinu likewise become sterile. 


“ Telipinu is angry. His soul and heart are a burning fire.
“ And just as this fire is extinguished, so may his anger, wrath, and sullenness 
“ Likewise be extinguished
“ Telipinu, let anger go, let wrath go, let sullenness go.
“ And just as the water in a drain pipe doesn't flow backward,
“ So may the anger, wrath, and sullenness of Telipinu likewise not come back. 


“ The gods are sitting in the place of convocation under the hawthorn tree.
“ And under the hawthorn tree, long the days are;
“ All the gods are sitting: 
“ Papaya, Istustaya, the Fate Goddesses, the Mother Goddesses,
“ The Grain Goddess Miyatanzipa, the tutelary deity, and Hapantali
“ O’ Telipinu, 
“ I have treated the gods under the hawthorn for many long years
“ I have purified them. 


“ I have taken evil from Telipinu's body. I have taken his anger. I have taken his wrath. 
“ I have taken his sin. I have taken sullenness. I have taken the evil tongue.
“ I have taken the evil path from him.”


Kamrusepa directed the evils from Telipinu into the hawthorn tree and addressed it:


“ The ox passes under you, and you pull its lock of hair. 
“ The sheep passes under you, and you pull its tuft of wool.
“ Pull the anger, wrath, sin, and sullenness from Telipinu too. 


“ The storm god comes, full of anger, and the man of the storm god stops him.
“ The bowl comes, and the wooden spoon stops it.
“ In addition, may my mortal words likewise stop Telipinu's anger, wrath, and sullenness. 


“ May Telipinu's anger, wrath, sin, and sullenness depart.
“ May the house release it.
“ May the middle door release it, may the window release it, may the hinge release it.
“ May the middle courtyard release it, may the city gate release it.
“ May the gate complex release it and may the king's road release it.
“ May it not go into the fruitful field, garden, or forest.
“ May it go the route of the Sun-Goddess of the Dark Earth.”


The gatekeeper opened the seven doors. He drew back the seven bars. Down in the Dark-Earth stood bronze vats. Their lids were of lead. Their latches of iron. That which goes into them doesn't come up again; it perishes therein.


“ So may they seize Telipinu's anger, wrath, sin, and sullenness,
“ And may they not come back here.”


Telipinu came back home to his house and took account of his land. The mist released the windows. The smoke released the house. The altars were in harmony again with the gods. The fireplace released the log. 


In the sheepfold he released the sheep. In the cattle barn he released the cattle. Then, the mother looked after her child. The sheep looked after her lamb. The cow looked after her calf.
Telipinu, too, looked after the king and queen and took account of them in respect to life, vigour, and longevity. Telipinu took account of the king. 


Before Telipinu there stood an eyan-tree. From the eyan was suspended a hunting bag, made from the skin of a sheep. In the bag there was sheep fat. In it lay symbols of animal fecundity and wine. In it lay symbols of cattle and sheep. In it lay longevity and progeny. 


In it lay the Gentle Message of the Lamb. In it lay the right shank. In it lay plenty, abundance, and satiety. 


Song of Ukulami: 
Tablet I:


For those who listened and in whose mind there is life, into his mind he takes wisdom. 


Of Kumarbi, Father of all the Gods, they shall sing. 
\
Kumarbi took wisdom unto his mind. He raised a bad day as an evil being.
 
Against the Storm-God he planned evil. Against the Storm-God he raised a rebel. Kumarbi took wisdom unto his mind, and like a bead he stuck it on. Like the prize possession of Dagan, a stone of destiny, wisdom was in Kumarbi’s hands. 


Like a bead, he stuck it on.


When Kumarbi had taken wisdom unto his mind, he promptly rose from his chair. He took a staff into his hand. The swift winds, as shoes, he put upon his feet. From his town, Urkis, he set out.
 
To Ikunta-Luli, the Cold Spring, he came. In Ikunta-Luli there was a great rock. Her length was three leagues. Her width was one league and a half league. 


His mind sprang forward upon what she had below, this woman. He slept with the rock. Into her, his manhood flowed. Five times he took her. Then, again, ten times he took her.


Kumarbi, Father the Gods, was seated. Impaluri came to Kumarbi and he saw. Impaluri then went to the Sea, and began to speak:


" What my lord gave me close to the sea, I have given,
“ On his chair, Kumarbi, Father of the Gods, is seated."


When the Sea heard Impaluri's words, the Sea began to speak:


"O Impaluri, my vizier! 
“ The words which to thee I speak, to my words hold thine ear inclined!
“ Go! These strong words, before Kumarbi, speak: 


” Why? Against my house in anger camest thou, so that trembling seized the house.
” Fear seized the servants!
“ For thee, cedar resin has long been broken dishes.
” For thee have long been cooked.
” For thee, by day and night the singers and the lutes hold set.


” Get up and come to my house!” 


Impaluri went before Kumarbi. Kumarbi came out of his house, and into the Sea's house he went. The Sea spoke:


" For Kumarbi a stool to sit, they shall set, 
“ A table before him, they shall set!
” Food to eat and beer to drink, they shall bring him!”


The cooks brought dishes. The cup-bearers brought him sweet wine to drink. 


Once they drank. Twice they drank. Three times they drank. Four times, then five; six times they drank. Seven times they drank. Kumarbi began to speak Mukisanu, his vizier:


" O Mukisanu, my vizier!.
“ The word which I speak to thee, pay heed. To me, thine ear, hold out! 


“ Into thy hand a staff take, upon thy feet, put thee shoes!
“ Now go and be off. 
“ Into the waters go, and these words before the waters confirm.
“ That Kumarbi’s heir will come; the former gods will be restored to heaven " 


When by night, the Sun-God could not see. When the middle night-watch came, time for the stone to give birth came. Mukisanu came. He gathered the stone and secreted her away. 


They brought him to birth, the Son of Kumarbi. Miukisanu, again, carried the rock away back to Ikunta-Luli. Hidden in the sea Kumarbi's son was born.


They brought him to birth, the midwives, the mother goddesses. The fate-goddesses and mother-goddesses lifted the child. They took the child, and they placed him on Kumarbi's knees. Kumarbi began to rejoice over this son. He began to fondle him, and he began to name the dear child. 


Kumarbi began to speak in his own mind: 


" What name shall I put on him, 
“ On the son, whom the fate-goddesses and mother-goddesses gave me? 
“ Out of the body like a blade he leapt. Now, he shall go! 


“ Ullikummi shall be his name!


“ Up to Heaven, to kingship, he shall go, 
“ Kummiya, the dear town, he shall press down!
“ The Storm-God, he shall hit, and like salt, he shall pound him, 
“ Like an ant, with his foot he shall crush him!
“ But Tasmisud, like a reed he shall break off! 
“ All the gods, he shall scatter, like birds, down from Heaven, 
“ And like empty vessels he shall break them!” 


When Kumarbi had ceased the words, again, he began to speak to his own mind: 


" To whom shall I give him, this son?
“ Who will take him and treat him as a gift?
“ Who shall harbour him and to the dark earth, who will carry him?
 “ The Sun-God of Heaven and the Moon-God shall not see him! 


“ He shall not see him, the Storm-God, the brave King of Kummiya, 
“  The Storm-God shall not kill him! 


“ She shall not see him, Istar, the Queen of Nineveh,
 “ The holy woman shall not break him off like a reed”


Kumarbi then began to speak to Impaluri : 


" O’ Impaluri, the words which I speak to thee heed,
“ To my words thine ear, hold inclined!
“ Into thy hand a staff take, upon thy feet as shoes, put the swift winds!
”  To the Irsirra-Gods go, and these strong words before the Irsirras speak: 


” Come! He calls you, Kumarbi, Father of the Gods, to the gods' house!
” But the matter about which he calls you, I know not,
” Now come promptly!””


 " The Irsirras will take him, the child; they will carry him to the dark earth
“ But, the Irsirras must not be seen; so he will not be visible to the Great Gods."
 
When Impaluri heard the words; he took the staff into his hand. Upon his feet he put the shoes. Forth he traveled to the Irsirras. Impaluri came, and began to speak the words: 


" Come! He calls you, Kumarbi, the Gods' Father!
“ But the matter about which he calls you, ye know not.
“ Now hurry, come!" 


When the Irsirras heard the words, they hurried. They hastened, and from their chairs they rose. At once they covered the way. To Kumarbi they came, and he began to speak the Irsirras:


" This child take, and treat him as a gift; to the dark earth carry him, 
“ Hurry, hasten! 


“ As a blade, put him on Ulpelluri's right shoulder!
“ In one day, a yard he shall grow! But in one month a furlong, he shall grow!
“ The stone which is thrown at his head, his eyes shall cover!
" No harm shall befall him!”


When the Irsirras heard these words, they took the child from Kumarbi's knees. The Irsirras lifted the child. Unto their breast, like a garment, they pressed him. Like winds they lifted him, and on Enlil's knees they placed him. 


Enlil’s eyes lifted, and he saw the child. Before the deity it was standing, but its body of stone of kunkunuzzi, it was made. Enlil began to speak to his own mind:


" Who is he, this child, whom the fate-goddesses and mother-goddesses, raised? 
“ Who will any longer endure them, the Great Gods' strong battles?
“ Of no one but Kumarbi is this an evil plan, just as Kumarbi raised the Storm-God, 
“ So against him, this Kunkunuzzi, he raised as a rebel."


When Enlil  had ceased to speak the words, they put the child upon Upelluri's right shoulder as a blade. He grew up, the Kunkunuzzi, and the strong Upelluri raised him. In one day, one yard he grew. In one month, one furlong he grew. The stone which was thrown at his head, his eyes covered.


When the fifteenth day came, the stone had grown high. In the sea, on his knees, he stood like a blade. Out of the water he stood. 


The stone’s height was like a mountain, and the sea up reached to the place of the belt like a garment. Like a wind he was lifted up. Into Heaven, the temples, and the chamber, he reached. 


The Sun-God looked down from heaven, and he saw Ullikummi, and Ullikummi, too, saw the Sun-God of Heaven. The Sun-God began to speak to his own mind :


" What swift god in the sea is standing? 
“ His body is not like that of the standing gods!" 


The Sun-God of Heaven turned his face, and out to the sea he went. When the Sun-God came out to the sea, he held his hand unto his forehead. He burned, and from anger his face became altered.


When the Sun-God of Heaven saw the stone, he crossed the mountains for the second time. Back, he traveled, and to the Storm-God he went. When he saw the Sun-God coming, Tasmisu began to speak:


" Why does he come, the Sun-God of heaven, The Land's King? 
“ The matter about which he comes, that matter surely is grave,
“ It is not to be cast aside! 


“ Strong is the struggle, strong is the battle!
“ Heaven's uproar it is, the land's hunger and death it is!"  


The Storm-God to Tasmisu began to speak:


" For his sitting, a chair they shall set up,
“ For his eating, a table they shall lay!"


While thus, they were speaking, the Sun-God arrived at their house. For his sitting, a chair they set up, but he did not sit down. For his eating, a table they laid, but he did not reach out. A cup they gave him, but his lips, he did not put to it. The Storm-God, began to speak to the Sun-God again:


" Is the chamberlain bad, who set up the chair, so that thou sattest not down? 
“ Is the steward bad, who set the table, so that thou eatest not?
“ Is the cup-bearer bad, who gave thee the cup, so that thou drankest not?"


         
Tablet II:


The Storm-God again began to speak to the Sun-God:
 
" Why eatest thou not ? Is it not pleasant, the drink? " 


The Sun-God spoke to the Storm-God: 


“ God of Storms, I refused your kindness,
" I have not eaten of this meal, I have not drank of this wine,.
“ Until I have spoken to thee, Storm-God, and his noble brother, 
“ Until I have spoken to thee of the Sea, and that which rises from it
“ About that matter, I must speak”


When the Storm-God heard the words of the Sun-God, from anger his face became altered. The Storm-God answered the Sun-God of Heaven:


" On the table, the bread shall become pleasant! 
“ Now eat! 


“ In the cup, the sweet wine shall become pleasant! 
“ Now drink! 


“ Eat and fill thyself, drink and satisfy thyself ! 
“ Then get up, and up to Heaven go!"


When he heard these words, the Sun-God of Heaven rejoiced within his mind. The bread on the table became pleasant, and he ate. In the cup, the sweet wine became pleasant, and he drank. The Sun-God got up, and he went up to Heaven. 


After the departure of the Sun-God of Heaven, the Storm-God  took wisdom unto his mind. The Storm-God and Tasmisu took each other by the hand. Out of the chamber, the  kuntarra-shrines, they went and came out of the temple. 


Istar, his sister, came from Heaven with bravery. She came from the sky looking formidable, and spoke to her own mind: 


" Where do they run, the two brothers?
“ I shall go and see them "


Quickly she took a stand. Ishtar, before her two brothers, stood up and began to speak to the Storm-God:


“ Where runnest thou? To where? What road is it? 
“ Tell it to me, Istar of Heaven, your sister!”


The Storm-God began to speak to her:


“ You wish to know? Then I shall say, what the Sun-God told me,"


Now three, they took one another by the hand, and up to Mount Hazzi they went. The King of Kummiya set his face. 


Yea! His face he set upon the Dreadful Kunkunazzi! 


He set his eye upon the dreadful basalt.


The Dreadful Kunkunazzi he saw, and from his anger, again the Storm-God’s face became altered. 


The Storm-God sat down on the ground. His tears, like streams, flowed forth. The Storm-God, with his eyes tearful, spoke the word: 


" Who will any longer endure it, this one's violence? 
“ And who will any longer fight?
“ Who will any longer endure it, this one's fearfulness?"


Istar replied to the Storm-God:


“ O’ my brother!
“ Neither great nor little, do you know of this mal!
“ Bravery to him has been ten-fold given!
“ The mal of the son, whom the gods beget for themselves, this thou knowest not!


“ If we were graced in Ea’s house,
“ If I were a rich man, then thou wouldst be a rich man.
“ Alas, I shall go and seduce the god!”


They left from the place by the sea. 


They went out from each other, and only Istar returned. From Nineveh she came to the sea. She was ornamented with gold and stone. The balag.di harp, and a galgalturi she took. She burned fragrant cedar.


She set the gold things in motion, and struck the balag.di and the galgalturi. 


She lifted her voice, and took up a song, the Song of Heaven and of the Earth. A Holy song she sang, as Heaven and Earth sang it back.


Sauska kept on singing as she put on a seashell and a pebble, as adornment. Then out of the sea, a great wave rose. The great wave spoke to Istar :


" In front of whom singest thou? In front of whom thy mouth with song fillest thou?
“ The man is deaf and hears not, in his eyes he is blind and sees not.
“ Mercy, he has not!


“ Now! 
“ Go away Istar, find thy brother,
“ As long as the stone has not yet become brave, 
“ As long as his head's skull has not yet become dreadful!
“ So, go away, Sauska, and find your brother, before he becomes really valiant,
“ Before the skull of his head becomes really terrifying!”  


When Istar heard, she quenched the cedar’s burning. The harp and the galgalturi, she threw away. The golden ornaments were discarded. Wailing and tearful, she went.


The Storm-God spoke word to his brother:


“ Fresh fodder they shall mix, and perfumed oil they shall bring
“ Serisu's horns they shall anoint.
“ But Tella's tail, they shall cover with gold.
“ They shall turn the axle, and bring strong stone to the outside.


“ As head-piece strong stones they shall set.
“ The thunder-storms, they shall call forth.
“ The rains and winds which break the rocks for ninety furlongs,
“ And which covers them for eight hundred furlongs,
“ The rains and winds, they shall call.


“ The lightning which strongly flashes, they shall bring out of the sleeping-room.
“ And, they shall bring out the carts.
“ Now arrange them, 


“ Set them! And bring me back word." 


When Tasmisu heard the words he hurried; he hastened. He brought Serisu from the pasture. He brought Tella from Mount Imgarra. In the outer-gate porch, he tied them. 


He brought perfumed oil, and anointed Serisu's horns. Tella's tail, he covered with gold. The axles he turned to the outside, and strong stones were set, as a head-piece.
