The thunder-storms were called forth. The rains and winds which for ninety furlongs break rocks, were called. The rain and winds were called, which cover the stone for eighty iku. The lightning rose from the sleeping room.


It flashed strongly, and terrifically! 
 
The Storm-God covered the distance to the sea. Prepared to fight, at one thousand yards, he took a stand. He took up the battle-gear. He took up the carts from Heaven, and the clouds he brought. The Storm-God, upon Ullikummi the Kunkunazzi, set his face. 


He saw him. 


His height was again tripled; his height turned three times. The Storm-God began to speak to Tasmisu:
 
" The carts! Let them go
“ Summon the rain and winds, summon the flashing lighting! " 


Tablet III:


Tismasu gave order to the heavens. When the gods heard the word, they took their place in the carts. Ashtabi was assigned commander. Upon his cart he sprang, and on the cart he went. 


Astabi yelled, leading the carts he arrayed. He thundered, terrifyingly. And with thunder, Ashtabi let go his attack downwards toward the sea. The gods drew, with a torrent of water, as Astabi took the lead of seventy gods. 


Still he was not able. 


Ashtabi was struck, and the seventy gods fell down into the sea. 


Ulkullimi, the Kunkunazzi, shook his body and heaven too shook. He struck out and heaven was struck. Like an empty garment, he shook out the sky.


The Kunkunazzi grew higher. His height grew one thousand nine hundred leagues and equal width. The Kunkunazzi stood down on earth. Like the wind he was lifted. The Kunkunazzi reached the chamber, and the temple. 


His height was nine thousand leagues. The Kunkunazzi's width was nine thousand furlongs. In Kummiya at the gate, like a shaft, he took his stand. 


The Kunkunazzi rose above Hebat and the temple. 


Hebat had not heard the news of the gods. She did not see the Storm-God and Suwaliyattae, with her eyes . 


Hebat began to speak the words to Takiti:


" I hear not, the Storm-God, nor the weighty word.
“ Of Suwaliyatta and all the gods, news, I hear not.
“ This Kunkunazzi, this Ullikummi of whom they speak,
“ Perhaps he defeated my husband, the weighty Storm-God?


" Hear my words! Take a staff into thy hand, 
“ Upon thy feet as shoes, put on the swift winds,


“ Now go!        
“ He killed him… 
“ Perhaps, the Kunkunuzzi killed my husband, the Storm-God, the weighty king! 
“ Now go, bring me back word!" 


When Takiti heard the words, she hurried; she hastened. She set out drawing forth to the place the Storm-god went. 


A road, there was not.


So, Takiti returned to Hebat, and spoke:


“ My lady, forgive me!”


When Tasmisu heard the Storm-God's word, he promptly rose. Into his hand he took a staff. Upon his feet he put, as shoes, the swift winds. To a high tower, he went up, and took his place opposite Hebat saying: 


" To a humble place go, the Storm-God bade me.
“ Until the years that are decreed for him, 
“ His will shall be fulfilled.”


When Hebat saw Tasmisu, she almost fell from the roof. Had she taken a step, she would have fallen. The palace-women grabbed her and would not let her go. When Tasmisu had ceased to speak the word, he stepped from the tower. 


Down to the Storm-God he went and spoke: 


" Where shall we sit down? 
“ Shall we sit down on Mount Kandurra?
“ If we sit down on Mount Kandurra, another will take seat on Mount Lalapaduwa.
“  Where shall we carry the counsel? 


“ Above in Heaven there will be no king.


" O’ Storm-God, my lord, hear my words 
“ The words which I speak to thee, heed.
“ Hold thine ear inclined to my words!


“ Come! Let us go to Apsuwa, before Ea!
“  For the Tablets of the Former Words, we shall ask.


“ Now, when we arrive, before the gate of Ea's house, 
“ Before Ea's door five times we shall bow, and again, before Ea's inner door;. 
“ But when we arrive before him, fifteen times we shall bow.


“ For Ea, it will perhaps chase his worry away, 
“ Perhaps he will listen and upon us he will have mercy,
“ And the former rule, he will assign us.”


When the Storm-God  heard Tasmisu's words, he hurried; he hastened. From his chair he promptly rose. The Storm-God and Tasmisu took each other by the hand; and, at once they covered the way. 


To Ea's house at Apsuwa, they arrived. They went before Ea's first door, and five times they bowed. Before the inner door; five times they bowed. When they arrived before Ea, fifteen times they bowed. 


Ea stood up, and began to speak:


“ You, Ο’ Tessub, begone before me.
“ Let Tismasu stand up before me, and his words I will heed!”


When Tasmisu heard the words, he ran forth. He kissed him on the knees three times; he kissed him on the ankles four times. Tasmisu struggled to explain to him, while groveling to him: 


“ Death to the basalt on the right shoulder!”


Ea spoke to Tasmisu: 


" Go up on Mount Kandurna, go up on Mount Lalapaduwa, go up on the dark earth 
“ I shall gather the ancient, fatherly, grandfatherly tablets, 
“ I shall bring forth the copper cutting tool,
“ I shall cut off Ullikummi the Basalt, under his feet." 


Tessub could not form the words in his mind. He left the inner chamber and stood, waiting for his brother in the courtyard.


 Ea took wisdom into his mind, and out into the courtyard, he went. All the gods before him bowed as he stood up; all the gods before him watched as he stood up. The Storm-God, Kummiya's brave king, stood before him. 


 Ea saw the Storm-God, and from anger his face became altered.


The Storm-God began to speak:


“ The Kunkunuzzi from the water rises
“ Without your aid our time is ended.”


Ea began to speak his words to the Storm-God, but Sauska fell at his feet stopping the words. She wept to the king, Noble Ea, to forgive Tessub. Tasmisu too bowed at his feet and joined her.


By the hand they took each other. Now four, they crossed the mountains. While covering the way, out of the assembly he came. Enlil began weeping and wailing. 


" Mayest thou live, O’ Ea!
“ Who comes back and forth before the aroma of the gods' propitiation,
“ The boundary! Why does thou transgress it?”


Ea too began to speak Enlil:


" Knowest thou not, O’ Enlil? Has no one brought thee word? 
“ Knowest thou not of him, the rebel whom Kumarbi fashioned against the Storm-God,
“ The Kunkunazzi, who grew in the water? 
“ His height is nine thousand leagues, and Ulkullimmi is lifted like a wind, 
“ Against thee he shall rise, and will take your former kingship.


“ He of that terrible mal!”


Ea and Enlil stood against each other. 


Enlil spoke his words to Ea:


“ For you, I have no words; what am I to say? 
“ Who is king? Who rules the holy temples?”


When Ea ceased to speak and the words between the two ceased, he went to Upelluri. Before him Ea stood and Upelluri lifted his eyes. He saw Ea, and Upelluri began to speak: 


" Mayest thou live, O’ Ea!" 


He stood up as Ea, in turn, began to wish to Upelluri, life :


" May you live, Upelluri, on the dark earth,
“ He upon whom heaven and earth are built!" 


Ea again began to speak to Upelluri: 


" Knowest thou not, O’ Upelluri?  Has no one brought thee a word?
“ Knowest thou not, the swift god, whom Kumarbi fashioned against the gods?
“ Or that Kumarbi plans death against the Storm-God, 
“ And against him fashioned a rebel?


“ The Kunkunazzi,  who in the water grew, knowest thou not of him?
“ He is lifted like a wind! Heaven, the holy temples, and Hebat he covers.
“ Is it because thou, Upelluri, from the dark earth art remote,
“ That this swift god, thou knowest not?"


Upelluri to Ea, again, began to speak: 


" When heaven and earth were upon me built, I knew nothing
“ When they came and heaven and earth, with a cutter, they cut apart, 
“ This, too, I knew not.
“ Now, something makes my right shoulder hurt, but I know not who he is, this god."


When Ea heard the words, he turned Upelluri's right shoulder. There the Kunkunazzi on Upelluri's right shoulder stood, like a blade.  


Ea began to speak to the Former Gods:


" My words hear, O’ Former Gods, who know the ancient words!
“ Again, open them! Open the old, fatherly, and grandfatherly store-houses! 
“ The former fathers' seal they shall bring, and with it, they shall reseal them, 
“ The former cleaver they shall bring out, with which heaven and earth were cut apart. 
“ And as for Ullikummi the Kunkunazzi, under his feet we shall cleave,


“ He, whom Kumarbi, against the gods, raised as a rebel!" 


Tasmisu prostrated himself before Ea, and began to speak:


“ The lines, in his body, the lines have been altered
“ On his head, his hair has been altered.”


 Ea spoke to Tasmisu, saying: 


" Go ahead with my son, before me, do not stand up!
“ My mind within me becomes angry. 


“ The dead!


“ I can see all those dead on the dark earth,
“ With mine eyes I saw; even while the dead are standing up they are dust!"


Ea to Tasmisu again began to speak: 


" First, I struck Ullikummi the Kunkunazzi.
“ Now go ye and fight him again.
“ The Kunkunazzi, as a blade, shall no longer stand." 


Tasmisu began to rejoice. Three times he shouted, and up to Heaven he went. 


The gods heard it. Twice he shouted, and the Storm-God, the brave King of Kummiya, heard it. To the assembly-place they came, and all the gods against Ullikummi the Kunkunazzi, like bulls began to bellow.


The Storm-God leapt upon his cart, and with thunder, he went down to the sea. 


The Storm-God fought the Kunkunazzi; and, the kunkunazzi began to speak words to the Storm-God:


 " What shall I tell thee, O’ Storm-God? Keep fighting?
“ Before my mind I lined up wisdom like a string of beads.
 
" What shall I tell thee? 
“ O’ Storm-God, keep fighting!


“ Kumarbi like a bead thus stuck wisdom unto my mind.
“ Up to Heaven, to kingship, I will go.
“ And Kummiya, the dear town, the temple, and the dwelling I will take.
“ But the gods, like birds, I will scatter them down from heaven!”
 
Again, the Kunkunazzi began to speak:


“ I, the Kunkunazzi, am Ullikummi, for it is the name which Kumarbi put on me!
“ He has decreed it, Kumarbi has said: 


““ Up to Heaven, to kingship, you shall go! 
“” Kummiya, the dear town, you shall press down!
“” The Storm-God, you shall hit, and like salt, you shall pound him! 
“” Like an ant, with your foot, you shall crush him!
“” But Tasmisud, like a reed you shall break off! 
“” All the gods, you shall scatter, like birds, down from Heaven, 
“” And like empty vessels you shall break them!” 


Ea heard these words that were spoken and took wisdom unto his mind. He called upon the Tablets of Former Words and arranged that, which Kumarbi had spoken.


Kumarbi had come into the power of destiny, but it was Ea who fulfills; and it was he that arranged.


Then, again, began to speak the Kunkunazzi to the Storm-God:


“ Behave like a man again, O’ Tessub!


“ Ea, the King of Wisdom, stands on your side,
“ Yet he took away your kingship and let them go into the Apsu and to the mountains. 
“ Let them weep in the land, and the high heavens for you! 


“ O’ Storm God! Ea of Apsuwa!
“ You have come from thee dwellings to me
“ Unto my mind I spoke wisdom, took counsel,
“ And unto his own mind, Kumarbi took wisdom, 
“ And thus he spoke:


““ Up to Heaven, to kingship, you will go and to Kummiya, the dear town!
“” The holy temple, you shall take, but like salt you shall be pounded, 
““ And like an ant, with the foot, you shall crush me!
““ Tasmisud, like a reed, shall break you off!” 
        
And so it was. 


The Storm-god thundered; and like salt, Ullikummi the Kunkunazzi was pounded. Like an ant, with the foot, Ea crushed him. Tasmisud, like a reed, broke him off from the right shoulder of Upelluri. 


The Kunkunazzi was defeated.
The Song of Silver


Tablet I: The Fine Lord


Among Tessub, the Sun-God of the Sky, Sauska, Nineveh's Queen, and all the gods, no one worshipped him; although, his mal was greater than theirs. His word was greater than their words. His wisdom was greater than their wisdom. His battle and his glory were greater than theirs, and their justice was not greater than his justice.


It is Silver, the Fine Lord, whom I sing. Wise men told me the tale of the fatherless boy, for it did not exist before. 


Long ago Silver's mal had disappeared, and they do not know now, his splendour. When heroic men ran to battle, abundance did not exist; grain did not grow. When men were hungry, they ate of grass with their hands. .


Tablet II: The Birth of Silver 


He went, with burning fire; the colour of alabaster, were his eyes. The seed was given forth. Nine months passed. Then, the tenth month arrived.


 Emerging forth, Silver came; and his tears flowed like streams.
Tablet III: Silver and the Orphan Boy


 With power and anger, Silver struck an orphan boy with a stick. 


The orphan boy spoke an evil word. Against Silver he said: 


" O’ Silver, why are you hitting us?
“ Why are you striking us? You are an orphan like us!" 


Now when Silver heard these words, he began to weep. 


Still weeping, Silver went into his house. Silver began to repeat the words to his mother: 


" The boys I struck down in front of the gate are defying me! 
“ I struck a boy with a stick, and he spoke an evil word back to me
“ Ο’ my mother, hear the words which the orphan boy said to me:


““ Why are you hitting us? Why are you striking us?
“” You are an orphan like us!”" 


Tablet IIII: Silver's Quest for Kumarbi  


His mother took the stick away from him. His mother turned around and began to reply to Silver, her son: 


" Ο’ Silver, do not hit me! Do not strike me!
“ The city you inquire about, I will describe it to you.
“ Your father is Kumarbi, the father of the city, Urkes.


“ He travels, and he resides in Urkes. 
“ Judgement of the lawsuits of all the lands he satisfactorily resolves.
“ Your brother is Tessub. He is king in heaven, and he is king in the land.
“ Your sister is Sauska, and she is queen in Nineveh.


“ You must not fear any other god, only one deity must you fear. 
“ Kumarbi stirs up the enemy lands, and the wild animals.
“ From top to bottom he stirs! From bottom to top he stirs!"


Silver listened to his mother's words. He turned his face, and set out for Urkes.


He arrived in Urkes, but he did not find Kumarbi in his house. He had gone to roam the lands. Kumarbi wandered about up in the mountains. 
Tablet V: Silver Threatens the Sun and Moon- 


Kumabri spoke, to them he said: 


" Go down to the dark earth and drive him away with a goad." 


 The gods, they began to go.


Kumarbi closed up his doors; with an evil heart, he judged. He found all the gods guilty. 


A trap he laid. A trick he planned.


Silver shouted.


“ They come to see!”


Silver seized power with his hands. He seized the spear. He dragged the Sun and Moon down from heaven. 


The Sun and the Moon did reverence. They bowed to Silver, and began to speak: 


" O’ Silver, our lord, do not strike us, do not kill us!
“ We are the luminaries of heaven and earth. 
“ We are the torches of what lands you govern.
“ If you strike us, if you kill us, you will proceed to govern the dark lands personally."


His soul within him was filled with love, and he had pity on the gods.
Tablet VI: Tasmisu and Tessub-


The brother, Tasmisu, began to speak to Tessub: 


" Is it not possible for you to thunder? Do you not know how to blast lightning? 


“ On the seat of Kumarbi, Silver has become king.
“ Now he drives all the deities with a goad of pistachio wood." 


Tessub began to speak back to his vizier: 


" Come, let us go and eat our daily bread, for our father, Kumarbi, did not defeat him
“ Shall we now defeat Silver?" 


They took each other by the hand, the two brothers, and to Urkes, they set out. In one stage they made the trip. At the city of Kumarbi, they arrived, and spied on the seat. Sitting there like a shaft, Silver. 


He feared him. 


The Great God feared, the Violent God. 


Tablet VII: The debate between Silver and Copper-
Obverse:


Strong Copper, powerful with huge arms. Does he have any rival? He walks carrying the precious hammer stone and anvil stone. He can create, shape to the form, and thus make it larger.


Striking, like a brother, is his right arm; holding, like a mother, is his left. He has created form, let him show it off. Constantly digging stones, let him accumulate goods. 


After exalted gods conceived him, after suckling the breast, after going towards the Abzu, that spans to the edge of the horizon; abundance brought itself forth for him. 


Mounds and wells were piled and dug. The harrow plough leveled all the mounds. Strong Copper directed the way. The dragon went forth. 


Strong Copper furrowed the fields, for turbulent waters to sustain the ewes. 


Strong Copper guided the mighty waters.


Enlil was brought a quarrel, and became its inquisitor. Silver and Strong Copper parlayed.

Enlil, The Great Mountain, called for his minister Nuska: 


" My minister, Nuska!" 


Nuska answered: 


" Always at your service!" 


Enlil replied:


" Strong Copper takes the throne and serves us,
“ Let him sit in his place." 


He had barely finished speaking thus to him, when Nuska and the minister's retinue went together to him. He brought, and said to Strong Copper:


" Come in!" 


Then Strong Copper clasped against his chest the labours of his huge arms. The abundance of heaven and earth as an offering and a gift. But Silver too held against his chest the goats, bulls and sheep he had slaughtered. 


Not planting trees in the garden, Silver issued out silver, in silver pieces. Strong Copper, after him, gave his famous offerings, very good things from Strong-Copper-On-His-Neck. 


Silver offered an old reed box. He put silver inside it. Silver offered silver, in small silver pieces.


Against Strong Copper, the strong heir of Father Enlil, he hurled vile insults and cast vile curses:


" The porters with their arms tied to their work; the potter, digging the hoe in the ground,
“ For days on end extracting the clay with his hands.
“ The worker from daybreak in a pit, unfit for washing or bathing.
“ The shipbuilder, caulking a boat, heating up fish oil, with garments not easy to clean.
“ The cook, heating up oil, carrying loads of water,
“ Standing by like a witness; the brewer, who does not untie his belt in warm weather,
“ Whose hands do not dry the clay; the maltster, never resting in winter.
“ The trapper, the nest hunter, spreading the nets, working in hollows.
“ The barber, cutting the growth, removing the flourishing roots.
“ The freight wagon oxen shaking their heads.
“ The donkeys submitting their necks to the yoke, 
“ Carrying firewood in its bags, to the oven for warm food.”

Silver spoke:


" You are one who neither builds foundations nor erects reed fences.
“ Get out!
“ You wander about fields, but my assigned task is in houses,
“ My assigned task is in temples and at banquets!"


Silver did not mind the way in which he had spoken. He spoke this way, in the matter of the display of strength.


Strong Copper, the warrior of heaven, kept his body firm. He did not take heed. He did not show hate, but kept quiet. He kept his neck firm, and did not glance down. He showed his rejoicing face to the gods.


Strong Copper turned and addressed the Anuna gods: 


" Ninsumun, the woman of all the destinies, blessed me generously.
“ The speech of wisdom she gave; wisdom of forgetting your insults
“ Pulling it out like a root from the earth.


“ My counsellor stands, stoking the house's fire before Enlil.
“ I will warm water for the Great Mountain.”


Strong Copper answered Silver: 


" Silver, to make lead shine is not an important achievement. 
“ Restitution, according to the tablets, does not do the work of Copper. 


“ A hand without a wrist cannot work. 
“ A weak neck carries nothing, does not add a thing to a load. 
“ A heart is fragile; mankind despairs.
“ No one thinks a boat with bitumen will sink in the river.

“ Filling the oil jar, the trap that ties someone up, the jar all full of lies.
“ For his hand bringing taxes, erasing restitutions by you,
“ People are deceived!


“ An army organised by you goes to the desert.
“ No one should be put in a dwelling bought by you.
“ A field toiled by you does not make grain grow with the labours of the soil.
“ It does not fill the silos with grain, it makes no one rejoice.
“ It does not fill the hairy sacks with grain.
" No king should ride on a barge built by you.
“ No one should carry things from afar on a trip in a chariot of yours.
“ In the palace, no one should place, on a table, your edible offering.
“ No young man of good heart should be pleased by the labours of your soil.
“ No offering of assorted woods or fine oils from you should stand on the streets.
“ You should not work with wood, you should not work with reeds!

" When the heavens were separated from the earth, there was no drinking water, 
“ In order that the people should eat food, my father, Enlil, created me in a single day,
“ And then the Tigris charged like a great wild bull.


" At that time, your feet did not move, and you did not walk around in the midst of Enlil,


“ Not separated from the earth, you got up, and you moved your feet toward his house.
“ They cut you to pieces with the strength provided by me, 
“ And someone opens a temple there, with the abundance I give to the population.


" Silver, consider the palace!
“ The heavens find the time right, according to the turning starry sky, and says: 


“" Come on! You will perform the work of your arms, you will help Enlil."”


“ But you stick out a long tongue like a kid to a goat, so that everybody can see.
“ Indoors, the palace is your station and banquets are your assigned task,
“ So you say!


“ Silver, I will demonstrate to you that the palace is neither your station nor your dwelling.
" Men caulk tiny, very strong boxes for you, as they do a boat.
“ They cover you over with their oldest rags,
“ And someone digs a hole for you in the middle of the cattle-pen.
“ They pour clay on top of you, as on a jar with a sealed mouth,
“ And in the darkest place inside the house,
“ Someone buries you in the most obscure corner of a grave.
" When the time of wet ground has arrived for me,
“ You do not supply the copper hoes that chop weeds,
“ So no one concerns themselves with you when sowing time has arrived.
“ Unlike me,
“ You do not supply the copper adzes that make ploughs,
“ So no one concerns themselves with you when winter time has arrived,
“ Unlike me,
“ You do not supply the copper axes that chop firewood,
“ So no one concerns themselves with you when harvest time has arrived,
“ Unlike me.
“ You do not supply the copper sickles that reap grain,
“ So no one concerns themselves with you for your harvest or winter,
“ You do not supply the copper adzes and chisels which build houses,
“ Not even a female lamb!
“ So no one concerns themselves with you.


" Silver, you are forgotten in the soil inside the house, a scared mouse in a silent house.
“ Silver, the palace is not your station! An obscure place, a grave, such is your station.
“ Silver, banquets are not your assigned task, fasting is your assigned task.
“ Silver! To make lead shine is not an important achievement.
“ The task of making divine statues is not likely to fall within your capabilities.
“ Why do you keep attacking me like a dog?


“ You snake! Get back in the darkest part of the house and lie down in your grave!"


Strong Copper cast his legitimate insults against Silver, and was full of hate against him. Insults of a miserable dog, like water from a brackish well, were cast. 


He exerted his powers against him to harass him. And at this, Silver felt thoroughly harassed; and it did not befit his dignity. 


Silver answered Strong Copper: 


" You do not give blades to the wooden hoe that breaks the ground.
“ The wooden tool mixes the clay; and wedges are not written by you.
“ The wooden shovels pile up the sheave.
“ Match your measuring devices to the measuring stick!


“ You just approach the cargo boat that floats on the canal banks.
“ You just keep knocking on the great door of the house at night.
“ The stonecutter wrestles with stones and with beads,
“ They are too hard and he has to stop because of you.
“ Work away with your tines at the dirt by the oven instead!


" You have accumulated lies about my honourable station.
“ Let me, the mouse, do his work.
“ His assigned task on the ground is noble.
“ Your teeth dig the ground, your tongue moves the dirt.
“ The copper hoe has its digging taken over by the wooden hoe in the harder ground.
“ The copper sickles need to have the hard weeds burned.
“ The copper axes which chop trees,
“ Stripping and pulling out tamarisks and ash shrubs, have their blades dulled.
“ The copper saws have to lie down for a rest beside the mountain trees.
" When you keep hitting the soil, like someone falling from a roof,
“ When they carry you out from the big brambles and winding thorns,
“ Like a dog with a treasure, as if they were catching a thief at midnight,
“ When the great, turbulent waters, regularly, yearly, fill the desert,
“ When they carry the grain from the dry ground to the canal banks,
“ When they carry the sesame from the furrows to the canal banks,
“ When they carry, too, the red onions, white onions, 
“ Edible bulbous leeks and turnips flourishing in the furrows,
“ When they transport the salt and spice seeds lying at the edges of the fields,
“ When they feed the various grains to cattle and sheep,
“ When they bring heaps to the pigs born at the fattener's,
“ When they feed dough to the porcupine's litter,
“ When they crush coarse flour for the huge wild boars,
“ Straig        ht-tailed fish, il-fish, carp, fish-with-bellies,
“ Giraba-fish laying their eggs in large amounts in the shallows,
“ Gurgal-birds, suda-birds, large u-birds from the middle of the sea,
“ Eggs of geese and all kinds of birds, 
“ All the good things which thrive in the desert, you do not bring!”

Silver and Strong Copper having carefully had a debate. Strong Copper had the lead over Silver in Enlil's house.


Reverse:


Enlil joyfully addressed Sumer. In a shower of abundance he raised him to the duties of shepherd. In order to rebuild the city of Enlil, to bring forth the houses of the great gods, to raise the banks of the levees and ditches. Enlil gave strength to the shepherd Ur-Namma in his majestic arms.


On Ur-Namma receiving crown and kingship, after he ate a good feast. Strong Copper helped him mightily. With it, the shepherd Ur-Namma piled abundance in great amounts. With it, he re-built the great temple of Suen in Urim. 


With it, he rebuilt the E-kur, the house of Enlil in Nibru. He made famous the houses of the great gods, and raised high the banks of the levees and ditches.


Under him, his city grew. Under him, wool was sheaved. Under him, oil flowed. Under him, the people ate excellent food. Ur-Namma, king with broad wisdom. Strong Copper, the chosen great god.




Lament for Sumer and Urim: The Lesser Deluge




To overturn the appointed times, to obliterate the divine plans, the storms gathered to strike like a flood. An, Enlil, Enki and Ninhursaja, Mother Ninmah, decided its fate. To overturn the divine powers of Sumer, to lock up the favourable reign in its home. 


The city would be destroyed, to destroy the house.


 The city would be destroyed, to destroy the cattle-pen, to level the sheepfold, so that the cattle should not stand in the pen, that the sheep should not multiply in the fold. So that watercourses should carry brackish water, that weeds should grow in the fertile fields, that mourning plants should grow in the open country.


That the mother should not seek out her child. The father would not say: 


" O’, my dear wife!", 


That the junior wife should take no joy in his embrace. The young child would not grow vigorous on his knee. That the wet-nurse should not sing lullabies. 


To change the location of kingship. To defile the seeking of oracles. To take kingship away from the land, and cast the eye of the storm over all the land. 


To obliterate the divine plans, by the order of An and Enlil!


An frowned upon all the lands. Enlil looked favourably on an enemy land. Nintud scattered the creatures that she had created. Enki had altered the course of the Tigris and Euphrates. Utu had cast his curse on the roads and highways, so as to obliterate the divine powers of Sumer. 


Its preordained plans were changed. The divine powers of the reign of kingship, of Urim, were alienated. The princely son in his house E-kic-nu-jal was humiliated. The unity of the people of Nanna was broken up, to be as numerous as ewes. 


The food offerings of Urim changed. The shrine of magnificent food offerings, no longer would its people dwell in their quarters. They were to be given over, to live in an inimical place. Cimacki and Elam, the enemy, would dwell in their place. 


In his own palace, the shepherd would be captured by the enemy. Ibbi-Suen would be taken to the land Elam in fetters; from Mount Zabu on the edge of the sea to the borders of Ancan. Like a swallow that has flown from its house, he would never return to his city.


On the two banks of the Tigris and of the Euphrates bad weeds grew. No one would set out on the road, no one would seek out the highway. The city and its settled surroundings would be razed to ruin-mounds. 


Numerous black-headed people would be slaughtered. The hoe would not attack the fertile fields, seed would not be planted in the ground. The melody of the cowherds' songs did not resound in the open country. Butter and cheese would not be made in the cattle-pen, dung would not be stacked on the ground. 


The shepherd would not enclose the sacred sheepfold with a fence. The song of the churning would not resound in the sheepfold. The animals of the open country would be decimated, all living things finished off.


The four-legged creatures of Cakkan would lay no more dung on the ground. The marshes should be so dry as to be full of cracks. They had no new seed. Sickly-headed reeds grew in the reed-beds, covered by a stinking morass. 


There would be no new growth in the orchards, as it all collapsed by itself. Urim, like a roped ox, would be subdued, to bow its neck to the ground. The great charging wild bull, confident in its own strength, the primeval city of lordship and kingship, built on sacred ground, its fate could not be changed. 


Who can overturn it? 
It is the command of An and Enlil.
Who can oppose it?


An frightened the very dwellings of Sumer, the people were afraid. Enlil blew an evil storm, and lay silence upon the city. Nintud bolted the door of the storehouses of the land. Enki blocked the water in the Tigris and the Euphrates. Utu took away the pronouncement of equity and justice. Inanna handed over victory in strife and battle to a rebellious land. Ninjirsu poured Sumer away, like milk to the dogs. 


Turmoil descended upon the land, something that no one had ever known. Something unseen, which had no name. Something that could not be fathomed. The lands were confused in their fear. The god of the city turned away, its shepherd vanished.


The people, in their fear, breathed only with difficulty. The storm immobilized them, the storm did not let them return. The time of captivity did not pass. 


What did Enlil, the shepherd of the black-headed people, do? 


Enlil destroyed the loyal households. He decimated the loyal men, and put the evil eye on the sons of the loyal men, their first-borns. He sent down Gutium from the mountains. Their advance was as the flood of Enlil, that cannot be withstood. 


The great wind of the countryside filled the countryside. It advanced before them. The extensive countryside was destroyed, and no one any longer moved about there. The dark time was roasted by hailstones and flames. The bright time was wiped out by a shadow. 


One bloody day, mouths were hushed, heads were crushed. The storm was a harrow, coming from above. The city was struck by a hoe. 


One day, heaven rumbled. The earth trembled, and the storm worked without respite. Heaven was darkened, as if covered by a shadow. The mountains roared. Utu lay down at the horizon. 


Dust passed over the mountains. Nanna lay at the zenith. The people were afraid. 


The city’s people stepped outside. The foreigners in the city even chased away its dead. Large trees were uprooted. The forest growth was ripped out. The orchards were stripped of their fruit, and they were cleaned of their offshoots. The crop drowned while it was still on the stalk, the yield of the grain diminished.


They piled the black-headed up in heaps; they spread them out like sheaves. There were corpses floating in the Euphrates. Brigands roamed the roads. 


The father turned away from his wife without saying " O’, my wife!". The mother turned away from her child without saying " O’, my child!". He who had a productive estate neglected his estate without saying "O my estate!". The rich man took an unfamiliar path away from his possessions. 


In those days the kingship of the land was defiled. The tiara and crown that had been on the king's head were both spoiled. The lands that had followed the same path were split into disunity. The food offerings of Urim; the shrine of magnificent food offerings, were changed for the worse. Nanna traded away his people, who were no longer as numerous as ewes.


The king sat immobilized in the palace, all alone. Ibbi-Suen was sitting in anguish in the palace, all alone. In E-namtila, his place of delight, he wept bitterly. The devastating flood was levelling everything. Like a great storm it roared over the earth. 


Who could escape it? 


The flood destroyed the city, and it destroyed the house, so that traitors would lie on top of loyal men. The blood of traitors flowed upon loyal men.


The storms gathered to strike like a flood.


The house of Kic, Hursaj-kalama, was destroyed. Zababa took an unfamiliar path away from his beloved dwelling. His wife, Mother Bau was lamenting bitterly in her E-Iri-kug. 


"Alas, the destroyed city" she cried bitterly.


"Alas, my destroyed house," she cried bitterly.


Kazallu, the city of teeming multitudes, was cast into confusion. Numucda took an unfamiliar path away from the city, his beloved dwelling. His wife Namrat, the beautiful lady, was lamenting bitterly. 


" Alas, the destroyed city, my destroyed house," she cried bitterly. 


Its river bed was empty. No water flowed. Like a river cursed by Enki, its opening channel was dammed up. On the fields fine grains grew no more. People had nothing to eat. The orchards were scorched like an oven. The open country was scattered. The four-legged wild animals did not run about. The four-legged creatures of Cakkan could find no rest.


Lugal-Marda stepped outside his city. Ninzu-ana took an unfamiliar path away from her beloved dwelling. 


" Alas, the destroyed city, my destroyed house," she cried bitterly. 


Isin, the shrine that was not a quay, was split by onrushing waters. Ninisina, the mother of the land, wept bitter tears. 


" Alas, the destroyed city, my destroyed house," she cried bitterly. 


Enlil smote Dur-an-ki with a mace. He made lamentation in his city, the shrine Nibru. His wife, mother Ninlil, the lady of the Ki-ur shrine, wept bitter tears. 


" Alas, the destroyed city, my destroyed house," she cried bitterly.


Kec, built all alone on the high open country, was haunted. Adab, the settlement which stretched out along the river, was treated as a rebellious land. It was deprived of water. The snake of the mountains made his lair there, so that it became a rebellious land. The Gutians bred there, they issued their seed. 


Nintud wept bitter tears over her creatures. 


"Alas, the destroyed city, my destroyed house," she cried bitterly. 


In Zabalam, the sacred giguna was haunted. Inanna abandoned Unug, and went off to enemy territory. In the E-ana, the enemy set eyes upon the sacred Jipar shrine. The sacred Jipar of en-ship was defiled. Its en-priest was snatched from the Jipar and carried off to enemy territory. 


" Alas, the destroyed city, my destroyed house," she cried bitterly.


A violent storm blew over Umma, the brickwork in the midst of the highlands. Cara took an unfamiliar path away from the E-mah, his beloved dwelling. Ninmul cried bitter tears over her destroyed city. 


" Oh my city, whose charms can no longer satisfy me," she cried bitterly. 


Jirsu, the city of heroes, was afflicted with a lightning storm. Ninjirsu took an unfamiliar path away from the E-ninnu. Mother Bau wept bitter tears in her E-Iri-kug. 


" Alas, the destroyed city, my destroyed house," she cried bitterly.


One day, the word of Enlil was an attacking storm. Who could fathom it? The word of Enlil was destruction on the right, it was lamentation on the left. 


What did Enlil do, in order to decide the fate of mankind? 


Enlil brought down the Elamites, the enemy, from the highlands. Nance, the noble daughter, was settled outside the city. Fire approached Ninmarki in the shrine Gu-aba.


Large boats were carrying off its silver and lapis lazuli. The lady, Sacred Ninmarki, was despondent because of her perished goods. The day was no more, burning like Gerra. The province of Lagac was handed over to Elam. The queen reached the end of her time.


Bau, as if she were human, reached the end of her time: 


" Woe is me! Enlil has handed over the city to the storm.
“ He has handed it over to the storm that destroys cities.
“ He has handed it over to the storm that destroys houses." 


Dumuzid-abzu was full of fear in the House of Kinirca. Kinirca, the city of noble youth, was ordered to be plundered. The city of Nance, Nijin, was delivered to the foreigners. Sirara, her beloved dwelling, was handed over to the evil ones. 


"Alas, the destroyed city, my destroyed house," she cried bitterly. 


Its sacred Jipar of en-ship was defiled. Its en priest was snatched from the Jipar and carried off to enemy territory.


Mighty strength was set against the banks of the Id-nuna-nanna canal. The settlements of the E-danna of Nanna, like substantial cattle-pens, were destroyed. Their refugees, like stampeding goats, were chased by dogs. They destroyed Gaec, like milk poured out to dogs, and shattered its finely fashioned statues. 


" Alas, the destroyed city, my destroyed house," she cried bitterly. 


Its sacred Jipar of en-ship was defiled. Its en-priest was snatched from the Jipar and carried off to enemy territory.


A lament was raised at the dais that stretches out toward heaven. Its heavenly throne was not set up, nor fit to be crowned. It was cut down as if it were a date palm and tied together. Accu, the settlement that stretched out along the river, was deprived of water. 


At the place of Nanna where evil had never walked, the enemy walked! 


How was the house treated thus? 


The E-puhruma was emptied. Ki-abrig, which used to be filled with numerous cows and calves, was destroyed like a mighty cattle-pen. 


Ningubalag took an unfamiliar path away from the Ja-bur. Niniagar wept bitter tears all alone. 


" Alas, the destroyed city, my destroyed house." she cried bitterly. 


Its sacred Jipar of en-ship was defiled. Its en-priest was snatched from the Jipar and carried off to enemy territory.


Ninazu deposited his weapon in a corner in the E-gida, and an evil storm swept over. Ninhursaja at the E-nutura, like a pigeon, she flew from the window, she stood apart in the open country. 


" Alas, the destroyed city, my destroyed house," she cried bitterly. 


In Jicbanda, the house that was filled with lamentation, lamentation reeds grew. Ninjiczida took an unfamiliar path away from Jicbanda. His wife, Azimua, the Queen of the City, wept bitter tears. 


" Alas, the destroyed city, my destroyed house," she cried bitterly.


One day, the storm forced people to live in darkness. In order to destroy Kuara, it forced people to live in darkness. Ninehama, in her fear, wept bitter tears. 


" Alas the destroyed city, my destroyed house," she cried bitterly. 


Asarluhi put his robes on with haste and fled Kuara. Lugalbanda took an unfamiliar path away from his beloved dwelling. Ninsun wept bitter tears:


" Alas the destroyed city, my destroyed house," she cried bitterly.


Eridug, floating on great waters, was deprived of drinking water. Its outer environs turned into haunted plains. The loyal man in a place of treachery was beset by the enemy. To Ka-hejala and Igi-hejala he wept:


" I, a young man whom the storm has not destroyed, 
“ My attractiveness not brought to an end, 
“ Yet we have been struck down like beautiful boxwood trees. 
“ We have been struck down like statues with coloured eyes. 
“ We have been struck down like statues being cast in moulds. 


“ The Gutians, the vandals, are wiping us out. 
“ We turned to father Enki in the Abzu of Eridug.
“ But he has turned away! 


“ What can we say, what more can we add? 
“ What can we say, what more can we add?


" O’, Father Enki, we have been driven out of Eridug. 
“ We who were in charge of the city, during the day, are eclipsed by shadows. 
“ We who were in charge of the city, during the night, are fettered by the storm. 
“ How shall we receive, among our weary ones, him, who was in charge during the day? 
“ How shall we let him, who was in charge by night, 
“ Go astray among our sleepless ones? 


“ Enki, your city has been cursed, it has been given to an enemy land. 
“ Why do they reckon us among those who have been displaced from Eridug? 
“ Why do they destroy us like palm trees, us who were not violent? 
“Why do they break us up, like a new boat that has not been sealed?"


After Enki had cast his eyes on a foreign land, Tidnum had risen up. They called on their cohorts. Enki took an unfamiliar path away from Eridug. Damgalnuna, the mother of the E-mah, wept bitter tears. 


"  Alas the destroyed city, my destroyed house," she cried bitterly. 


Its sacred Jipar of en-ship was defiled. Its en-priest was snatched from the Jipar and carried off to enemy territory.


In Urim no one went to fetch food. No one went to fetch water. Those who went to fetch food, went away from the food and would not return. Those who went to fetch water, went away from the water and would not return. 


To the south, the Elamites stepped in, slaughtering the black-headed peoples. In the uplands, the vandals, the enemy, the Gutians came down as a flood-wave. The Tidnum daily strapped the mace to their loins. 


To the south, the Elamites, like an onrushing wave, were committing slaughter. In the uplands, like chaff blowing in the wind, they scattered over the open country. Urim, like a great charging wild bull, bowed its neck to the ground.


What did Enlil, who decides the fates, then do? 


Again he sent down the Elamites, the enemy, from the mountains. To the foremost house, firmly founded, Kuara, they went. In order to destroy Kisiga, ten men, even five men, ceaselessly bore their strength. Three days and three nights did not pass. The city was levelled. The city was raked by a hoe. Dumuzid left Kisiga like a prisoner of war, his hands were fettered.


The Queen of Heaven took an unfamiliar path away from Kisiga. She rode away from her possessions, and went to the mountains. She loudly sang out a lament over those brightly lit mountains: 


" I am queen, but I shall have to ride away from my possessions,
“ And now I shall be a slave in those parts. 
“ I shall have to ride away from my silver and lapis lazuli,
“ And now I shall be a slave in those parts. 
“ There, slavery overwhelms the people; who can endure it? 


“ There in Elam, slavery overwhelms the people; who can endure it?


“ Alas, the destroyed city, my destroyed house," she cried bitterly. 


My queen, though not the enemy, went to enemy land. Ama-ucumgal-ana was taken captive in Kisiga. Like a haunted city, the priestly rituals were abandoned..


Enlil threw open the door of the grand gate to the wind. In Urim no one went to fetch food, nor drink. Its people rushed around like water being poured from a well. Their strength ebbed away, as they could not even go on their way. 


Enlil afflicted the city with an evil famine. He afflicted the city with that which destroys cities, that which destroys houses. He afflicted the city with that which cannot be withstood with weapons. He afflicted the city with dissatisfaction and treachery. 


In Urim, which was like a solitary reed, there was not even fear. Its people, like fish being grabbed in a pond, sought to escape. Its young and old lay spread about, no one could rise.


At the royal station there was no food on top of the platform. The king, who used to eat marvellous food, grabbed at a mere ration. The day grew dark. The eye of the sun was eclipsing. The people experienced hunger. 


There was no beer in the beer-hall, there was no more malt for it. There was no food in the palace, as it was unsuitable to live in. Grain did not fill the lofty Urim storehouse. The king could not save his own life. 


The grain-piles and granaries of Nanna held no grain. The evening meal in the great dining hall of the gods was defiled. Wine and syrup ceased to flow. The butcher's knife that used to slay oxen and sheep lay hungry in the grass. Its mighty oven no longer cooked oxen and sheep; it no longer emitted the aroma of roasting meat. 


The sounds of the bursaj building, the pure vessel house of Nanna, were stilled. The house which used to bellow like a bull was silenced. Its holy deliveries were no longer fulfilled, its halls were alienated. The mortar, pestle and grinding stone lay idle; no one bent down over them.


The Shining Quay of Nanna was silted up. The sound of water against the boats’ prow ceased. There was no rejoicing. Dust piled up in the Unuribanda of Nanna, the sacred place. The rushes grew.


The mourning reeds grew. Boats and barges ceased docking at the Shining Quay. Nothing moved on the watercourse which was fit for barges. The plans of the festivals, at the place of the divine rituals, were altered. The boat with first-fruit offerings, of the father who begot Nanna, no longer brought first-fruit offerings. Its food offerings could not be taken to Enlil in Nibru. Its watercourse was empty; barges could not travel.


There were no paths on either of its banks, as long grass grew there. The reed fence of the well-stocked cattle-pen of Nanna was split open. The reed huts were overrun, their walls were breached. 


The cows and their young were captured and carried off to enemy territory. The munzer-fed cows took an unfamiliar path in an open country that they did not know. Gayau, who loves cows, dropped his weapon in the dung. Cuni-dug, who stores butter and cheese, did not store butter and cheese. 


Those who were unfamiliar with butter were churning the butter. Those who are unfamiliar with milk were curdling the milk. The sound of the churning vat did not resound in the cattle-pen. Like a mighty fire that once burnt, its smoke was extinguished from the great dining hall of Nanna.


Suen wept to his father Enlil: 


" O’, father who begot me, 
“ Why have you turned away from my city which was built for you? 
“ O’, Enlil, why have you turned away from my Urim which was built for you? 
“ The boat with first-fruit offerings 
“ No longer brings first-fruit offerings to the father who begot him.
“ Your food offerings can no longer be brought to Enlil in Nibru.
“ The en-priests of the countryside and city have been carried off by phantoms.
“ Urim, like a city raked by a hoe, is to be counted as a ruin-mound.
“ The Ki-ur, Enlil's resting-place, has become a haunted shrine.


“ O’, Enlil, gaze upon your city, an empty wasteland.


“ Gaze upon your city Nibru, an empty wasteland.
" The dogs of Urim no longer sniff at the base of the city wall.
“ The man who used to drill large wells scratches the ground in the marketplace.


“ My father who begot me,
“ Enclose in your embrace, my city which is all alone.
“ Enlil, return to your embrace my Urim which is all alone.
“ Enclose in your embrace my E-kic-nu-jal which is all alone.
“ May you bring forth offspring in Urim, may you multiply its people.
“ May you restore the divine powers of Sumer that have been forgotten."


O’, good house, good house! O’, its people, its people!


Enlil then answered his son Suen: 


" There is lamentation in the haunted city, reeds of mourning grow there. 
“ In its midst the people pass their days in sighing
“ My son, the noble son, Suen, why do you concern yourself with crying?


“ Oh Nanna, the noble son, Suen, why do you concern yourself with crying?


“ The judgement uttered by the assembly cannot be reversed.
“ The word of An and Enlil knows no overturning.
“ Urim was indeed given kingship but it was not given an eternal reign.
“ From time immemorial, since the land was founded, until people multiplied,
“ Who has ever seen a reign of kingship that would take precedence for ever?
“ The reign of its kingship had been long indeed but had to exhaust itself.
“ O’, my Nanna, do not exert yourself in vain, abandon your city."


Then, the king, the noble son, became distraught. Lord Acimbabbar, the noble son, grieved. Nanna, who loves his city, left his city. Suen took an unfamiliar path away from his beloved Urim. 


In order to go as an exile from her city to foreign territory, Ningal quickly clothed herself and left the city. The Anuna stepped outside of the city as the storm approached Urim. 


The trees of Urim were sick. Its reeds were sick. Laments sounded all along its city wall, for daily there was slaughter before it. Large axes were sharpened in front of Urim. The spears, the arms of battle, were prepared. The large bows, javelin and shield gathered together to strike. The barbed arrows covered its outer side like a raining cloud. Large stones, one after another, fell with great thuds. Daily the evil wind returned in the city. 


Urim, confident in its own strength, stood ready for the murderers. Its people, oppressed by the enemy, could not withstand their weapons. In the city, those who had not been felled by weapons succumbed to hunger. 


Hunger filled the city like water, and it would not cease. This hunger contorted people's faces. It twisted their muscles. Its people were as if drowning in a pond, they gasped for breath. Its king breathed heavily in his palace, all alone. Its people dropped their weapons to the ground. 


They struck their necks out with their hands and cried. They sought counsel with each other; they searched for clarification: 


" Alas, what can we say about it? 
“ What more can we add to it?


“ How long until we are finished off by this catastrophe?


“ Inside Urim there is death, outside it there is death.
“ Inside it we are to be finished off by famine.
“ Outside it we are to be finished off by Elamite weapons.
“ In Urim the enemy oppresses us, oh, we are finished."


The people took refuge behind the city walls. They were united in fear. The palace was destroyed by onrushing water and defiled. Its door-bolts were torn out. Elam, like a swelling flood wave, left only the ghosts. 


In Urim people were smashed as if they were clay pots. Its refugees were unable to flee, they were trapped inside the walls. Like fish living in a pond, they tried to escape. 


The enemy seized the E-kic-nu-jal of Nanna. They ripped out its heavy pilasters. The statues that were in the treasury were cut down. The great stewardess Niniagar ran away from the storehouse when its throne was cast down before it. She then threw herself down into the dust.


Urim’s mighty cows with shining horns were captured. Their horns were cut off. Its unblemished oxen and grass-fed sheep were slaughtered; they were cut down as date palms and tied together. 


The palm-trees, strong as mighty copper, the heroic strength, were torn out like rushes. They were plucked like rushes. Their trunks were turned sideways. Their tops lay in the dust, and no one raised them. 


The midriffs of their palm fronds were cut off, and their tops were burnt off. The date spadices, that used to fall on the well, were torn out. The fertile reeds, which grew in the sacred giguna, were defiled. The great tribute that they had collected was hauled off to the mountains.


The house’s great door ornament fell down; its parapet was destroyed. The wild animals that were intertwined on its left and right lay before it, like heroes smitten by heroes. Its gaping-mouthed dragons and its awe-inspiring lions were pulled down with ropes like captured wild bulls and carried off to enemy territory. 


The fragrance of the sacred seat of Nanna, formerly like a fragrant cedar grove, was destroyed. Its architrave was stripped of gold and lapis lazuli. The glory of the house, whose glory was once so lovely, was extinguished. Like a storm that fills all the lands, it was built there like twilight in the heavens; its doors adorned with the heavenly stars. 


Its great bronze latches were torn out. Its hinges were smashed. Together, with its door fittings, they wept bitterly like a fugitive. The bolt, the holy lock and the great door were not fastened for it. The noise of the door being fastened had ceased; there was no one to fasten it. The door was cast off and put out in the square.


The food offerings of his royal dining place were altered. In the sacred place the tigi, cem and ala-instruments did not sound. Its mighty tigi, cem, and ala did not perform its sacred song. Verdicts were not given at the Dubla-mah, the place where oaths used to be taken. 


The throne was not set up at its place of judgement, justice was not administered. Alamuc threw down his sceptre, his hands trembling. In the sacred bedchamber of Nanna musicians no longer played the balaj-drum. The sacred box that no one had set eyes upon was seen by the enemy. 


The divine bed was not set up, it was not spread with clean hay. The statues that were in the treasury were cut down. The cook, the dream interpreter, and the seal keeper did not perform the ceremonies properly. They stood by submissively, and were carried off by the foreigners. The holy usga-priests of the sacred lustrations, the linen-clad priests, forsook the divine plans and sacred divine powers. They went off to a foreign city.


In his grief Suen approached his father. He went down on his knee in front of Enlil, the father who begot him: 


" O’, father who begot me, how long will the enemy eye be cast upon my account, 
“ How long? 
“ The lordship and the kingship that you bestowed, Father Enlil, 
“ The one who advises with just words, the wise words of the land, 
“ Great Mountain, your inimical judgement saddens me.


“ Look into your darkened heart, terrifying like waves. O’ Father Enlil,
“ The fate that you have decreed cannot be explained,
“ The destruction of lordship, my ornament."


 Suen went. He put on a garment of mourning.


Enlil then provided a favourable response to his son Suen: 


" My son, the city built for you in joy and prosperity
“ Was given to you as your reign.
“ The destroyed city, the great wall, the walls with broken battlements,
“ All this too is part of that reign.
“ With the dark, black days of the reign that has been your lot.
“ As for dwelling in your home, the E-temen-ni-guru, that was properly built,
“ Indeed, Urim shall be rebuilt in splendour, the people shall bow down to you.
“ There is to be bounty at its base, there is to be grain.
“ There is to be splendour at its top, the sun shall rejoice there.
“ Let an abundance of grain embrace its table.
“ May Urim, the city whose fate was pronounced by An, be restored for you." 


Having pronounced his blessing, Enlil raised his head toward the heavens: 


" May the land, south and highland, be organized for Nanna.
“ May the roads of the mountains be set in order for Suen.
“ Like a cloud hugging the earth, they shall submit to him.
“ By order of An and Enlil it shall be conferred."


Father Nanna stood in his city of Urim with head raised high again. The youth Suen could enter again into the E-kic-nu-jal.  His wife, Ningal, refreshed herself in her sacred living quarters. In Urim she could enter again into her E-kic-nu-jal.


There was lamentation in the haunted city, mourning reeds grew there. In its midst there was lamentation; mourning reeds grew there. Its people spent their days moaning.


Nanna-Suen addressed his city:


“ O’, bitter storm, retreat O’ storm, storm return to your home.
“ O’, storm that destroys cities, retreat O’ storm, storm return to your home.
“ O’, storm that destroys houses, retreat O’ storm, storm return to your home.”
 
Indeed the storm that blew on Sumer, blew also on the foreign lands. It blew on Tidnum, It blew on the foreign lands. It blew on Gutium, the foreign lands. It blew on Ancan, and levelled it like a blowing evil wind. Famine has overwhelmed the evildoer; those people will have to submit.


“ May An not change the divine powers of heaven,
“ The divine plans for treating the people with justice.
“ May An not change the decisions and judgments to lead the people properly.
“ To travel on the roads of the land: may An not change it.
“ May An and Enlil not change it,
“ May An not change it.
“ May Enki and Ninmah not change it,
“ May An not change it. 
“ That the Tigris and Euphrates should again carry water:
“ May An not change it. 
“That there should be rain in the skies
“ And on the ground speckled barley; may An not change it.
“ That there should be watercourses with water and fields with grain:
“ May An not change it.
“ That the marshes should support fish and fowl:
“ May An not change it.
“ That old reeds and fresh reeds should grow in the reed-beds:
“ May An not change it.
“ May An and Enlil not change it.
“ May Enki and Ninmah not change it.
“ That the orchards should bear syrup and grapes,
“ That the high plain should bear the macgurum tree, 
“ That there should be long life in the palace,
“ That the sea should bring forth every abundance:
“ May An not change it. 
“ The land densely populated from south to uplands:
“ May An not change it.
“ May An and Enlil not change it, may An not change it.
“ May Enki and Ninmah not change it, may An not change it.
“ That cities should be rebuilt, that people should be numerous,
“ That in the whole universe the people should be cared for.”


O’, Nanna, your kingship is sweet. Return to your place. May a good abundant reign be long-lasting in Urim. Let its people lie down in safe pastures, let them reproduce. O’, mankind rejoice, princess overcome by lamentation and crying.


O’, Nanna! O’, your city! O’, your house! O’, your people!




Chapter 13
When on High
Tablet I:


When, in the heights, Heaven was not named. The earth beneath did not yet bear a name. The primeval Apsu, who begat them, he was the progenitor. Chaos, Matrix-Tiamut, the mother who bore them all. 


Their waters were mingled together. 


No field was formed. No lea was packed together. No marsh was to be seen. No canebrake matted close. Of the gods, none had been called into being. 


No god had been called forth. Nor none bore a name. No destinies were ordained.


Then, the gods were created. Lahmu and Lahamu, were called into being. They matured while they grew tall. 


Ages increased. Ansar and Kisar were created, surpassing them. Long were the days. Years added to years. 


Then, there came forth, Anu, their son. Anu their firstborn was like unto his forebears. Anu, his son, became Anshar’s equal.


Then, there was Nudimmud, whom his fathers, his begetters, granted abounding in all wisdom. 


Anu, the empty heaven, heir and supplanter; first-born of his father, in his own nature
begot Nudimmud-Ea. With Intellect, and wisdom wider than heaven's horizon, the strongest of all the kindred, he was exceedingly strong. He had no rival. He, far more stalwart than his grandfather Anshar.


Thus were established, and were, the Great Gods!


The divine brethren swarmed about. They confounded Tiamat as their uproar grew ever more loud. The vitals of Tiamut roiled. 


Their romping troubled the heavenly abode. Apsu could not lessen their clamour, as Tiamut was silent before them. Their actions were noisome to her; as  well as offensive, in their behaviour. Still, she was indulgent.


 Apsu, the begetter of the Great Gods, cried unto Mummu, his minister. He said unto him:


" O’ Mummu, thou minister that rejoice my spirit; come, unto Tiamut let us go!”


They went; and before Tiamat, they laid down. They consulted on a plan with regard to the gods, their sons. Apsu opened his mouth to speak, and unto the Glistening One, he addressed the word:


“ Terrible is their way, their clamour too much
“ By day I can not rest, by night I can not sleep.
“ But, I will destroy their way, I will let there be lamentation!
“ I wish to put an end to their behaviour; 
“ Let silence reign, that we may sleep.
“ Let us lie down again in peace."


When Tiamat heard these words, she raged and cried aloud. She bitterly spoke unto Apsu:


" What then shall we do? Shall we put an end to what we created?
“ Their way may be noisome, but let us bear it in good part.”


Mummu answered, and gave counsel to Apsu. Hostile to the gods was the counsel Mummu gave; like a dissenting vizier:


“ Come, their way is strong, but thou shalt destroy it;
“ Then by day shalt thou have rest, by night shalt thou lie down in peace."


Apsu hearkened unto him, and his countenance grew bright. He beamed on account of the planned evil against the gods. He embraced Mummu around the neck, so joyous that he kissed him.


What was plotted between the two was repeated to the gods, their offspring. The gods heard it, and stirred about. They were stunned, stupefied, and collapsing about.


Surpassing in wisdom, Ea was aware of it all, as if privy to their plans himself. He worked it out; he set it up, his master plan. He contrived it to be surpassing, his incorruptible magic spell.


He recited it and brought calm to the mixing waters. He made Apsu fall into deep slumber. He was quickly seized by sleep. 


Mummu the advisor was in daze, however, as if robbed of sleep. He untied his sash and discarded his tiara. Ea took away his glory using the hands that had put it on.


Apsu was afraid. His knees became weak; and they gave way beneath him as he dreamt. Because of the evil which their first-born had planned, the countenance of their faces, they altered. 


Then Ea, who knoweth all that is, went up and he beheld their muttering.


He tied up, and killed Apsu. He laid him to waste. 


Mummu was taken captive, confined, and put under restraint. 


After Ea had captured and vanquished his foes, he set on himself the aureole of the king. In profound quiet, he rested in his chambers.


He called the chambers Apsu, meaning the Recognized Sanctuary. He established therein his living quarters. Ea and Damkina his wife dwelt there in splendour, in the cella of destinies, the abode of designs. 


The most capable, the Sage of the Gods, the lord, was conceived. In the midst of Apsu was Marduk born. Ea, his father begat him. Damkina, his mother, was confined with him.


He suckled at the breasts of goddesses. The nursemaid who raised him endowed him well with glories. His body was magnificent; fiery his glance. He was a hero at birth, he was a mighty one from the beginning.


When Anu, his grandfather, saw him, his heart was filled with joy. He perfected him, so that his divinity was unique. He was much greater than the other gods. He surpassed them in every way. His members were fashioned with cunning beyond comprehension. They were impossible to conceive. Too difficult to visualize.


 Four-fold his vision, four-fold his hearing. When he moved his lips, a fire broke out. Formidable his four-fold perception. His eyes, in like manner, saw in every direction. 


He was tallest of the gods, surpassing in form. 


His limbs, gigantic; by birthright, he was surpassing! 


“ Mar-Utu, Mar-Utu
“ The son, the sun, the Sunlight of the Gods!”, they called to him.


They clad him in the glories of ten-gods. He was of prodigious power. Fifty-glories were heaped upon him. Anu formed and made rise, the four cardinal winds. He put them in his hand:


“ Let my son play!”, he said.


He fashioned dust. He made a storm bear it up.


He caused a wave and it roiled Tiamat. 


Tiamat was roiled, churning day and night. The gods found no rest, and bore the brunt of each wind. They plotted evil in their hearts. They said to Tiamat their mother:


“ When he killed Apsu, your husband, you did nothing to save him but sat by, silent.
“ Now he has made four terrible winds, they are roiling your vitals so we cannot sleep.


“ You had no care for Apsu your husband, for Mummu, who was captured,
“ You remained aloof!
“ Since then you churn back and forth, in a turmoil.
“ As for us, who cannot lie down to rest, you do not love us!
“ Think of our burden, our eyes are pinched, break off this unremitting yoke, let us sleep.


“ Remember Apsu, your husband, in your heart.
“ Remember Mummu who was defeated.
“ Now, you are all alone, and thrash around in desolation.
“ We have lost your love, our eyes ache, and we long for sleep.
“ Declare war, give them what they deserve,
“ Make a battle-plan, and turn them into nothingness.
” Mother! Rise up! Pay them back! Make them empty like the wind!” 


When Tiamat heard these words, they pleased her. Tiamat hearkened unto the word of the Bright-God, and said:


“ I shalt thou entrust, whatever you think best, let us do it forthwith.
“ Let us wage war!


“ We will make monsters,
“ Monsters and gods will march into battle together, against gods!”


The gods were united within her, for the gods did she create. They took up wicked plans against the gods, their ancestors. They banded themselves together and at the side of Tiamat. They advanced, while the old hag, the first mother, mothered a new brood. 


They were furious. Scheming, they devised mischief without resting, night and day. They prepared for battle, fuming and raging. They joined their forces forming a host and made ready for war. Ummu-Hubur, who can form all things, made countless invincible weapons. 


She spawned Mušḫuššu, the monster-serpents; sharp of tooth, and merciless of fang. With poison, instead of blood, she filled their bodies. 


Mušmaḫḫū, fierce dragons, the wyrms, she clothed with terror. With splendour she decked them, and glories. She made them of lofty stature and they bore cruel weapons, without fear of the fight.


She deployed Bašmu, the vipers, and Ušumgallu, the great dragons, and the Monster-Lahamu, hairy hero-men. She made Ugallu, the hurricanes, Uridimmu, the raging-hounds, and Girtablullû, the scorpion-men. She let loose Umū-dabrūtu, the mighty tempests, Kulullû, the fish-men, and Šeg-saĝ, the monster-rams. She deployed Shedu, the winged lion-monsters, Urmahlullu, the lion-men, Gallu, the mighty demons, and Kusarikku, the bull-men. 


All bearing unsparing arms, all were fearless in battle. Whoever beheld them, terror overcame him. Their bodies reared up and none could withstand their attack.


Her commands were mighty, as none could resist them. After this fashioning of the huge-of-stature, she made eleven wise-monsters. Among the gods who were her sons, she exalted Kingu; in their midst she raised him to power. To march before the forces, to lead the host, to give the battle-signal, to advance to the attack, to direct the battle, to control the fight, unto him she entrusted. 


In costly raiment, the lordly garments, she made him sit. She spake, saying:


“ I have uttered thy spell,
“ In the assembly of the gods I have raised thee to power.
“ The dominion over all the gods have I entrusted unto him.
“ Be thou exalted, thou my chosen spouse,
“ May they magnify thy name over all of them, the Anunnaki!


“ Whatever you command shall cause a blaze to die out,
“ Your concentrated venom shall make a mighty one yield."


She gave him the Tablets of Destiny, on his breast she laid them, and said:


“ Thy command shall not be without avail,
“ The word of thy mouth shall be established."


Now Kingu, thus exalted and having received the power that belonged before to Anu; they confirmed the brood of monsters. He decreed the fate among the gods his sons, saying:


" Let the opening of your mouth quench the Fire-God;
“ Let the swinging mace fail of its power
“ Who so is exalted in the battle, let him display his might!"


Tablet II:


Tiamat assembled her creatures, and drew up for battle against the gods, her brood. She made, weighty, her handiwork. She wrought evil against the gods, her children. Thereafter, Tiamat, more than Apsu, had become an evildoer.


To avenge Apsu, Tiamat planned evil; but, she informed Ea that she was ready for battle. Ea harkened to this, and was grievously afflicted. He sat in sorrow. 


He fell silent in his chamber and sat stock still.


The days went by. He had taken thought and his anger had calmed. He went to the place of Ansar, his father; and he stood before the father who begat him. All that Tiamat had plotted he repeated unto him:
 
" Tiamat our mother hath conceived a hatred for us,
“ With all her force she rageth, full of wrath.
“ All the gods have turned to her, with those whom ye created.
“ They go at her side; they are banded together, and at the side of Tiamat they advance.


“ They are furious and devise mischief without resting night and day.
“ They prepare for battle, fuming and raging;
“ They have joined their forces and are making war.


“ Ummu-Hubur, who formed all things, hath made, in addition, invincible weapons.
“ She hath spawned monster-serpents, sharp of tooth, and merciless of fang.
“ With poison, instead of blood, she hath filled their bodies. 
“ Fierce dragons she hath clothed with terror,
“ With splendour she hath decked them; she hath made them of lofty-stature.
“ Whoever beholdeth them is overcome by terror.
“ Their bodies rear up and none can withstand their attack.


“ She hath set up vipers, and dragons, and the monster-lahamu,
“ Hurricanes and raging-hounds, and scorpion-men,
“ Mighty tempests, and fish-men and rams;
“ They bear cruel weapons, without fear of the fight.
“ Her commands are mighty; none can resist them.
“ After this fashioning, of huge-of-stature, hath she made eleven monsters.


“ Among the gods who are her sons, inasmuch as they hath given her support,
“ She hath exalted Kingu in their midst and raised him to power.


“ He marches before the forces, to lead the host.
“ He gives the battle-signal, to advance to the attack.
“ He directs the battle, to control the fight.
“ Unto him hath she entrusted the task; in costly raiment she hath made him sit.


When Ansar heard how Tiamat was mightily in revolt, he bit his lips. His mind was not at peace and he made a bitter lamentation:


“ My son, you yourself were an instigator of battle!
“ Do you bear the consequences of your own handiwork? 


“ In battle, you killed him!


“ You went forth and killed Apsu, but Tiamat hath exalted Kingu.
“ And where is one who can oppose her, Tiamat, whom you have enraged?,
“ Where is one who can face her?”


“ I shall call the gods in deliberation
“ We shall take to the counsel of the gods, Nudimmud.”


The sage-counsellor, wise prince, producer of wisdom, Divine Nudimmud answered Father Anshar gently. With soothing words, calming speech, he addressed him: 


“ My father, inscrutable, ordainer of destinies, who has power to create and destroy.
“ O’ Anshar, ordainer of destinies, who has power to create and destroy,
“ I will declare my thoughts to you.
“ Calm yourself for a moment.
“ Recall in your heart that I made a good plan.


“ Before I undertook to kill Apsu, who had foreseen what is happening now? 
“ Ere, I was the one who moved quickly to snuff out his life.
“ Indeed, it was I who destroyed him.” 


When Anshar heard this, the speech pleased him. He calmed himself and spoke to Ea: 


“ My son, your deeds are worthy of a god; 
“ Such a cruel, irresistible, blow!


“ Go then, to Tiamat, mollify her onslaught.
“ Go and stand before Tiamat, that her anger be pacified,
“ Quickly banished by your magic spell,
“ That her spirit may be appeased, that her heart may be merciful.”


He heard the word of his father, Ansar, and he directed his path to her. Ea went to seek out Tiamat's intentions. He stopped, speechless; then turned back. He came before Anshar the sovereign. 


He entreated him, saying:


“ My father,Tiamat has carried her actions beyond me.
“ I verified her intent, but my magic spell cannot counter it. 


“ Her strength is enormous.
“ She is utterly terrifying, furthermore, she is reinforced with a host.
“ None can go out against her.
“ Her challenge does not lessen in loudness and I felt fear at her clamour.
“ I turned back!


“ My father, do not despair!, Send another to her.
“ A woman's force may be very great, but it cannot match a man’s.
“ Do you be the one to scatter her ranks, to thwart her intentions,
“ Before she lays her hands on us?”


Anshar was shouting in a passion. Forcefully he called for his son. To Anu, his son, he said these words:


“ Cherished son, valiant warrior,
“ Whose strength is enormous, whose onslaught is irresistible!
“ Make haste, and take a stand before Tiamat.
“ Soothe her feelings, let her heart be eased; if she will not listen to what you say,
“ Say something by way of entreaty to her, so that she be pacified!”
 
When he heard what his father, Anshar, said, he set off. Anu went to seek out Tiamat's intentions. 


Drawing close, he beheld the muttering of Tiamat, but he could not withstand her. On his heels, he turned back and came before Anshar, his father who begat him. He entreated him and said:


“ My father, Tiamat has carried her actions beyond me.
“ I verified her intent, but my magic spell cannot counter it. 
“ Her strength is enormous, and she is utterly terrifying.
“ Furthermore, she is reinforced with a host, so that none can go out against her.


“ Her challenge does not lessen in loudness; I felt fear at her clamour! 
“ I turned back!


“ My father, do not despair; send another to her. 
“ A woman's force may be very great, but it cannot match a man’s.
“ Do you be the one to scatter her ranks, to thwart her intentions,
“ Before she lays her hands on us?”


Anshar fell silent, gazing at the ground. Looking toward Ea, he shook his head. The Igigi-gods and Anunna-gods were all assembled. With lips closed tight, they sat in silence. 


No god would go out at the command. None would go against Tiamat as he ordered. So it was that Anshar, Father of the Great Gods, with his heart full of anger, did not summon anyone. 


The mighty firstborn, champion of his father, hastener to battle, the Warrior Marduk, Ea summoned to his secret place. He made the speech to him he had prepared in his mind:


“ O’ Marduk, think carefully, heed your father.
“ You are my son who can relieve his heart!


“ Draw nigh, approach Anshar, and be ready to speak.
“ But wait; for, seeing you, he will be calmed.”


The lord was delighted at his father's words. He drew near and waited before Anshar. When Anshar saw him, his heart was filled with joyful feelings. He kissed his lips, as he banished his gloom:


“ My father, let not your lips be silent but speak.
“ Let me go! Let me accomplish your heart's desire! 
 “ What man is it who has sent forth his forces for battle against you?
“ Why does Tiamat, a woman, come out against you with arms?
“ My father, creator, rejoice and be glad, soon you will trample the neck of Tiamat.”


Anshar made ready to speak, and gave his answer to noble Marduk:


 “Go, Son of the Knower of All There is to Know,
“ Bring Tiamat to rest with your sacral-spell.
“ Make straight, quickly, ride the storm chariot!
“ At the sight of it, being unstoppable, turn her back!" 


The lord rejoiced at the word of his grandfather, and he drew nigh and stood before Ansar. Ansar beheld him and his heart was filled with joy:


" O’ lord of the gods, destiny of the great gods,
“ If I, your avenger, am to conquer Tiamat and give you life,
“ Make an appointed assembly.
“ Convene the assembly, and nominate me for the ultimate destiny.
“ Make my fate preeminent and proclaim it! 
“ In Upsukkinaku seat yourself, joyfully, together,
“ With my word, in place of you, will I decree fate.
“ May whatsoever I do remain unaltered, 
“ May the word of my lips never be changed nor made of no avail."




Tablet III:


Ansar opened his mouth, and unto Gaga, his minister, and spake the word.


" O’ Gaga, thou minister that rejoicest my spirit,
“ Unto Lahmu and Lahamu will I send thee.
“ You know how to find a way; you can make a fine speech.
“ Send over to my presence, the gods, my ancestors.
“ Let them bring all the gods before me; let them converse, at a feast.
“ At a banquet let them sit, let them eat bread, let them mix wine.
“ Let them ordain destiny for Marduk, their champion.
“ Go, Gaga, stand before them, and all that I tell thee, repeat unto them, and say:


“ Ansar, your son, hath sent me, the purpose of his heart he hath made known unto me.
“ The purpose of his heart is unknown!


“ To wit, Tiamat our mother hath conceived a hatred for us.
” With all her force she rageth, full of wrath; all the gods have turned to her.
” Even those, whom ye created, they go at her side.
“ They are banded together, and at the side of Tiamat they advance.
“ They are furious, they devise mischief without resting, night and day.
“ They prepare for battle, fuming and raging,
“ They have joined their forces and are making war.
“ Ummu-Hubur, who formed all things, hath made, in addition, weapons invincible;


” She hath spawned Mušḫuššu!
“ She hath set up Bašmu, and Ušumgallu, and the Monster-Lahamu,
” Ugallu and Uridimmu, and Girtablullû,
” Umū-dabrūtu, and Kulullû and Šeg-saĝ!
“ She hath exalted Kingu; in their midst she hath raised him to power.
