“” Put young and old alike to death!
“” Spare no one; not even the baby sucking milk!
“” You shall plunder the accumulated wealth of Babylon!””


“ The royal troops drew up and invaded the city.
“ The bow twanged, as daggers stabbed and pricked.
“ With flashing shafts and outstretched blades,
“ You homed the weapons of kidinnu-men as abomination to Anu and Dagan.
“ You made their blood course like ditchwater in the city streets,
“ You opened their arteries and let the watercourses tear their blood away.
“ When the great lord Enlil saw that, he cried 


“” Woe!””  and his heart was hardened.


“ An irreversible curse rose to his lips,
“ And he swore that he would not drink from the watercourses.
“ He was revolted by their blood and would not enter Esagila: 


“”” Alas for Babylon, whose crown I fashioned luxuriant as a date palm’s,
“” The wind has scorched it!


“” Woe for Babylon, that I had laden with seed, like an evergreen,
“” Of whose delights I could not have hoped for!
“” Alas for Babylon, that I tended like a thriving orchard,
“” But whose fruit I could not taste!


“” Woe for Babylon, that I suspended like a gemstone on the neck of the Anu,
“” Like a cylinder seal of elmesu-stone!
“” Alas for Babylon, that I clasped in my hand like the Tablet of Destinies,
“” Not handing it over to anyone else!””


“ And this too has Noble Marduk said, from his humble former days, 
“ From time immemorial: 


”” Let one quit the wharf, he shall cross at two cubit’s depth of water on foot!
“” Let one go down sixty fathoms in a well,
“” Not one man shall keep himself alive on the water!,
“” Let them still have to punt the fishing boat 100 leagues out in the open sea!””


“ Henceforth they shall drive out the deep-sea fisherman’s boat 
“ A hundred leagues into the vast expanse of sea-water with a pole.


“ As for Sippar, the Primeval City,
‘ Which the lord of the land did not allow the deluge to overwhelm,
“ Because it was so precious to him,
“ You destroyed her ramparts against the will of Shamash 
“ And you threw down her fortifications.


“ As for Uruk, the dwelling of Anu and Ishtar,
“ The city of courtesans, harlots, and prostitutes for the cult,
“ The city whom Ishtar deprived of husbands and reckoned as her own,
“ There, Sutean nomads, men and women, hurl war cries, and abuses!,
“ They rouse the actors and singers of Eanna, the party-boys and festival goers,
“ Whose manhood Ishtar changed to womanhood, to strike awe into the people.
“ The wielders of daggers and razors, vintner’s shears and flint knives,
“ Who take part in abominable acts for the pleasure of Ishtar,
“ A haughty, insolent, remorseless governor you placed over them.
“ You harassed them and interfered with their rites,
“ Ishtar was angered, and flew into rage over her country.
“ She stirred up the enemy and swept Uruk clean, like granules on the water’s face.


“ The dweller of Parsa had no respite from lamenting,
“ Because of the destroyed E-ugal sanctuary, which had been contaminated.
“ The enemy you roused has no desire to stop!


” Ishtaran has responded thus:


”” You turned the city, Der, into a wasteland.
“” You fractured her populace like reeds.
“” You extinguished their clamour like the dying hiss of foam on the water’s face.
““ And as for me, you did not spare me, but gave me over to the Sutean nomads!


“” For the sake of my city Der, I will judge no disputed truth, 
“” Nor make any ruling for the land.
“” I will give no guidance nor aid in understanding.
“” Men forsook truth and took up violence,
“” They abandoned justice and were plotting wickedness; they took up atrocities. 
“” Against but one country I raised up seven winds
“” He who did not die in battle will die in an epidemic!
“” He who did not die in the epidemic, the enemy will plunder him!
“” He whom the enemy has not plundered, the bandit will murder him!
“” He whom the bandit did not murder, the king’s weapon will vanquish him!
“” He whom the king’s weapon did not vanquish, the prince will slay him!
“” He whom the prince did not slay, Adad will wash him away!
“” He whom the thunderstorm did not wash away, Shamash will parch him!
“” He who has gone out into the world, the wind will sweep him away!
“” He who has gone into his home, the demon, the Croucher will strike him!
“” He who has gone up to a high place, will perish of thirst!
“” He who has gone down to a low place, will perish in the waters!
“” For you encompass the heights and depths alike,
“” You have obliterated high and low place alike.


”” The man in charge of the city says to his mother:


””” If only I had stuck in your womb the day you bore me!
“”” If only our lives had come to an end; if only we had died together.”””


“” For you gave me a city whose walls were to be destroyed!
“” Its people are treated like cattle, like beasts! 
“” Their god turned smiter is the one who hunts them down!
“” It is he whose net is tight-meshed:
“” Those engaged cannot slip through, but die a violent death.
”” Because his net is of such fine mesh, even picked men could not draw
“” But die by the sword.


”” He who begot a son, saying:


“”” This is my son, when I have reared him, he will do good””


”” I will put that son to death; his father must bury him. 
“” Afterwards I will put that father to death, and he will have none to bury him.


“” He who built a house saying:


“”” This is my house, I built it for myself, I shall spend my leisure in it. 
“”” I shall find peace within it,
“”” And on the day fate claims me, I shall fall asleep inside.”””


“” Yet I will put him to death and vandalize his home.
“” Afterwards, it will be wreckage, and I will give it to another.””




Ishum continued:


“ O’ Warrior Erra, you have put the righteous man, the just man, to death.
“ You have put the unrighteous man, the unjust man, to death.
“ He who sinned against you, you put to death.
”  He who did not sin against you, you put to death.
“ The en-priest, assiduous with divine offerings, you put to death.
“ He who made the taklimu-offering promptly,
“ The courtier who served the king, you put to death.
“ The old man on the doorstep, you put to death.
“ The young girls in the bedrooms, you put to death.
“ Even then you found no appeasement whatsoever!
“ Even then you told yourself,


”” They despise me !””


“ This is what you said to yourself, Warrior Erra.


” Now, I shall strike down the mighty, and terrorize the weak.
“ I shall kill the commander, and scatter the troops.
“ I shall wreck the temple’s sacred tower, and the rampart’s battlement,
“ The pride of the city, I shall destroy!


“ I shall tear out the mooring pole, so the ship drifts away, downstream!
“ I shall smash the rudder so she cannot reach the bank!
“ I shall pluck out the mast, and rip out the rigging!


“ I shall make breasts go dry so babies cannot thrive!
“ I shall block up springs so that even little channels can bring no life-sustaining water!
“ I shall make Erkalla shake, so that heaven trembles!
“ I shall make the planets shed their splendour, devour the rays of Shulpae!
“ I shall wrench out the stars from the sky, and throw them away!
“ I shall hack the tree’s roots, so its branches cannot burgeon!
“ I shall wreck the base of the wall, so that the top sways and tumbles!
“ I shall approach the dwelling of the king of the gods, so that counsel shall not prevail,
“ That no direction shall be forthcoming!”


The warrior Erra heard him. The speech that Ishum made pleased him like the finest oil. Thus the warrior Erra spoke:


“ The Sea-lander, spares not the Sea-lander,
“ Subartu spares not the Subartu,
“ Assyrian spares not the Assyrian,
“ Elamite spares not the Elamite, 
“ Nor Kassite spares not the Kassite.


“ Sutean spares not the Sutean, 
“ Gutian spares not the Gutian,
“ Lullubaean spares not the Lullubaean, 
“ Nor country, spare country.
“ Nor city, spare city,
“ Nor house spare house, 
“ Nor man, another man,
“ Nor brother, the brother. 


“ They shall not spare one another,
“ They shall slay each other!
“ Then, afterwards, let the Akkadian arise to slay them all
” Then they shall shepherd the rest.”


The Warrior Erra addressed his words to Ishum, his vanguard:


“ Go, Ishum, the matter you spoke of, do as you wish
” Discretion is yours”


Ishum set out for the Mountain Hehe, the homeland of the Suteans. The Seven, warriors unrivalled, fell in behind him. 


When the warriors reached the mountain, Hehe, he raised his hand, and destroyed the mountain. He reckoned Mountain Hehe as level ground; he cut away the trunks of the cedar forest, the hasuru-trees. The thicket looked as if the deluge had passed over. 
                
He laid waste cities and turned them into wilderness. He obliterated mountains and slew their wildlife. He convulsed the seas and destroyed their produce. He brought devastation upon swamp and thicket, and burnt like the fire of Gerra. He cursed the wildlife and turned them to clay.


After Erra calmed and took up his own abode, all the gods were gazing at his face. All the Igigi-gods and the Anunnaki stood in awe.


Erra made ready to speak, saying to all the gods:


“ Quiet, all of you! Keep quiet, listen to what I have to say.


“ No doubt I intended evil in the bygone lapse.
“ When I am enraged I devastate people.
“ Like a hireling among the flock, I took the lead sheep from the pen.
“ Like one who did not plant an orchard, I am quick to cut it down.
“ Like plunderers from the hill side, I do not distinguish just from unjust.
“ Like a scorcher of the earth, I slay indiscriminately good and evil.


“ One does not snatch a carcass from the jaws of a marauding lion,
“ And when one man is beside himself, another cannot deliver reason.
“ Were it not for Ishum, what might have happened?
“ Where would your provider, your en-priests be?
“ Where, your nindabu-offering? 
“ Where, the fragrance of qutrinnu-offering.”


Ishum made ready to speak. He addressed his words to the Warrior Erra:


“ Warrior, be still, hear what I have to say!,
“ No doubt this is true.


“ What if you were to rest now, and let us serve you!,


“ When you are angry, where is he who can face you?, 
“ Where does he stand on your day of wrath?
“ We all know that no one can stand up to you in your fury!”


When Erra heard this, his face beamed, like radiant daylight. His features glowed. He entered E-meslam and took up his abode. 


He called Ishum to tell him the word, to give instructions concerning the scattered peoples of Akkad:


“ Let the people of the country, who had dwindled, become numerous again,
“ Let short and tall alike traverse its paths,
“ Let weakest Akkadians fell mighty Suteans; let one drive off seven, like flocks of sheep,
“ You shall make their cities into ruins and their highlands into wilderness,
“ You shall take massive spoils, and bring it to Shuanna.
“ You shall reconcile the angry gods with their own abodes,
“ You shall bring them back to their dwellings.
“ You shall make gods of livestock, and grain descend once more to the land.
“ Let Shakkan and Nisaba go down into the country!


“ You shall make mountain deliver its yield, sea its produce.
“ You shall make the ruined fields deliver produce.
“ Let the governors of all cities haul their massive tribute into Shuanna.
“ Let the ruined temples lift their heads like the rays of the sun.
“ Let the Tigris and Euphrates bring the waters of abundance.
“ Let the governors of all cities make the provider for Esagila and Babylon their lord.”
 
For countless years, praises of the Great Lord Nergal and his Noble Warrior Ishum would be sung.


Erra became angry and set his might to overwhelming nations and destroying their peoples, but Ishum, his counsellor most wise, came to placate him so that remnants still remained.


Chapter 14 - Kinâḫna-


The Song of Lamma-
Tablet I:


While Sauska was speaking to her brother Tessub, the arrow of Lamma sped and it pierced Sauska in her breast. 


A second arrow sped past. 


They hastened; Tessub and Sauska, moved to the chariot, to ride. But, Lamma’s arrow pierced the shaft so that the chariot could no longer set out. 


They could no longer set out.


Lamma forced Tessub to the ground. He took a stone and hit it behind Tessub. The stone went up after him. It struck the sky and shook out the sky like a garment, so that Tessub fell down from there. 


Lamma laughed, and took the reins and the whip out of Tessub's hand. 


Tessub turned back and began to speak to Lamma: 


" You have taken my reins and whip from my hand, and into your own hand, taken them. 
“ Those reins are sacred! 
“ Others will summon you to the kallistarwa-house,
“ The reins bind to you.


“ Let a woman not eat of the sheep they sacrifice to the reins!”


Ea thwarted Lamma’s hand. His prize was taken, and he was constrained by his word to El. Tessub would not be killed. 


Lamma forced the Storm-God from his throne and Ea declared him victor that day.
Tablet II:


Now when Lamma heard Ea's words, he began to rejoice within himself. He slaughtered. He ate and drank, and went up to heaven, to kingship.


For years Lamma was king in heaven. In those years wolves were peaceful.The lands flourished. The mountains flowed with wine. 


The valleys and plains flowed with grain poured out. Man was well off, and he was fully blessed in what he desired. There he fatted. 


No one began to pray to Lamma.


Tablet III:


The wife of the deity, she lifted up her eyes. She thirsted and she hungered. She saw Lamma coming toward her three danna’s away. The Goddess Kubaba began to say to Lamma: 


" First, I have seen the Great Gods, the elders, your forefathers. 
“ Go to meet them and bow to them." 


Lamma began to reply to the goddess Kubaba: 


" The Primeval Gods are great; they have arisen, but I do not fear them at all. 
“ Do I not put bread into their mouths? 
“ The paths which the winds are to go and come 
“ On those, I, Lamma, King of Heaven, allot to the Gods!" 


The tempestuous winds brought Lamma's evil words to Ea while he was on his way. His mind became angry. 


Ea began to speak to Kumarbi:


" Come, let us go back. This Lamma whom we made King in Heaven,
“ Just as he himself is complacent, so he has made the countries complacent,
“ And no one any longer gives bread or drink offerings to the gods."


Ea and Kumarbi turned their faces. Ea went to Apzuwa, but Kumarbi fled. He went away to Tuttul. Ea made a messenger stand up in front of him; undertaken to dispatch him to Lamma,  he said:


" Go, speak these words to Lamma: 


“ Ever since we made you King in Heaven, you have not done anything.
“ You have never convoked an assembly, may you be humbled.”


The messenger departed and recounted Ea's words to Lamma, just so. 


When Lamma heard Ea's words, he began to rejoice within himself. He laughed heartily at Ea’s words. Ea began to say to Izzummi, his vizier: 


" Go down to the Dark Earth; tell the words which I am speaking to you to Nara-Napsara, 
“ Tell my brother, saying: 


“ Take my speech and hearken to my words. 
“ Lamma has made me angry, so I have deposed him from the kingship in heaven.
“ That Lamma whom we made king in heaven, just as he himself is complacent,
” So he has made the countries complacent,
” No one any longer gives bread or drink offerings to the gods.
” Now, Nara, my brother, hear me; mobilize all the animals of the earth!
” Mt. Nasalma, go up, and unto his head, deliver judgement!“


Tablet IIII:


As Tessub and Ninurta, his vizier, battled, they treated Lamma the same way he had Ashtabi. They spread him over the mountain. They trampled him. 


They beat him! 


They whipped Lamma! 


They cut up flesh from his back. 


They cut up 700 of his ikdu. 


Lamma spoke back to Tessub:


" Tessub, my Lord!
“ Long ago you reigned, but to me, kingship now falls!" 


Tessub spoke back to Lamma: 


" Let them proceed to take it from you.
“ Let them hand it to me, let them take the cup from you quickly,
“ For I’ve only gifted it to you." 


The Disappearance of the Sun God-


Tablet I:


The Sea-God spoke:


“ Hear my plan! When the Storm-God goes to the Noble Sun-God, his son.
“ When he next sets, then let your favour show itself with me. 
" If I seize the Sun-God and hide him, what can the Storm-God do? 
“ He will be helpless against you." 


When they began to praise themselves, the Daughter of the Sea-God called from the sky. The Sea-God heard her and placed a kukubu-vessel on his left side saying: 


" Now, whenever the Sun-God falls, if he falls in water, flame, tree, or bush,
“ He will be caught in a snare." 


The Sea-God said to the Sun-God: 


" This is what I placed forth for you."


 The Sun-God went to the Sea-God, and into his chamber. He covered the kukubu-vessels with wax. Then the Sea-God stopped them up on top with copper, and said: 


" Keep on praising yourselves. “


In the land, conditions were bad, and it was dark. No one could withstand the Sea. The Storm-God called Telipinu, his favourite and firstborn son: 


" Come now, Telipinu! You, yourself, go forth to the sea.
“ Bring the Sun-God of the Sky back from the sea."


Telipinu went to the sea, and the Sea became afraid of him. So much so, that he gave him his daughter. He also gave the Sun-God to him.


Telipinu brought back from the sea, the Sun-God and the Daughter of the Sea, and the Storm-God kept them with himself. 


The Sea sent a messenger to the Storm-God: 


" Telipinu, your son, took my daughter as his wife.
“ He took her away, so what bride-price will you give to me?" 


The Storm-God said to Hannahanna: 


" A river came from the sea, he kept demanding the bride-price.
“ Shall I give it to him, or shall I not give it to him?" 


Hannahanna said to the Storm God: 


" Give it to him. Go! 
“ Telipinu took the Sea's daughter hither for brideship.
“ They have already taken up dwelling in the heavens. " 


So the Storm-God gave to him, a thousand of each. He gave one thousand cattle and one thousand sheep. 


The Sea-God accepted them. 


The Storm-God summoned his brothers and reported to the gods that the danger had passed, the Sun-God had been returned. 


The gods were then sent back to their homes. 


Tablet II: 


The Sea-God heard the words of his daughter, this lovely woman asked the Storm-God: 


" To restore the land, what miracle will they make? How valiant are your sons?”  


The Storm-God said to the wife of Telipinu:


" Tell me! Indeed, great and valiant are my sons. 
“ But If a man has been killed, can they restore him to life? 
“ If an ox or sheep has been killed, can they restore it to life?:


“” What miracle can your sons hope to do?””


“ Frost has paralyzed the entire land. He has dried up the waters. Frost is great!" 


Then the Storm-God said to his brother, the Wind: 


" The waters of the mountains, the gardens, the meadows.
“ Let your refreshing go through the lands, but let him, Frost, not paralyze them." 


" He paralyzed the herbs, the lands, the cattle, the sheep, the dogs, and the pigs.
“ But he won't paralyze the crops which are sons of the heart.
“ If he tries to paralyze them, the fat will hold them inside,
“ So that he will not paralyze them when he paralyzes each and every thing." 


The Wind proceeded to say to the Storm-God: 


" Why has this happened?" 


Tablet III:


Frost said to his father and mother: 


" You eat and drink this, but you have taken account of nothing.
“ The shepherd and cowherd have died!"


He still paralyzed the land, but the Storm-God didn't even know. His plans were for naught.


The Storm-God sent for the Sun-God, saying:


" Go bring the Sun-God." 


They went. They searched for the Sun-God, but they did not find him. And the Storm-God said: 


" Why have you not found him? 
“ My own body parts have become warm!
“ Where could he have gotten lost?" 


So, he sent the War-God, saying:


" Go bring the Sun-God." 


But Frost seized the War-God. 


Then, the Storm-God said: 


“ Go call the Tutelary Deity, will Frost paralyze him too? 
“ Is he not a child of the steppe?" 


But Frost seized him too. 


Then the Storm God said: 


" Go call Telipinu. That son of mine is mighty: 
“ He breaks up the ground, plows, irrigates, and seeds grain." 


But Frost held him too.


The Storm-God said:


" Go call Gulsa and Hannahanna, if those have died, then these too may have died.
“ Did Frost come to their gate too?" 


Frost said to the Storm-God: 


" Because you kill and cast away, all have died. You no longer hold this same cup." 


Hasamili's full brothers are Frost's half brothers. So Frost did not seize them. Therefore,  the Storm-God called them. The Storm-God spoke to Frost: 


" My hand is stuck to the cup; my feet too, they have caused me to get stuck. 
“ Even if you seize these feet and hands, do not seize my eyes too."


Frost spoke to the Storm-God: 


" You will look at your children as I seize them.
“ I will go up to the sky. Then, I will take as trophy your eye's, hands, and feet.“
Tablet IIII: 


Frost turned. He shot the Moon-God. Down; he threw him into the city gate. 


The old men and women died in panic. The Storm-God thundered frightfully, paralyzed by the frost. His eyes, hands, and feet seized.


The Moon-God fell from the sky and fell upon the gate complex. No one saw him. The Storm-God sent rain after him.


 He sent rain after him! Fear seized him! Anxiety seized him! 


The god, Hapantali, went there. He stood beside him and uttered over him the words of a spell. The goddess Kamrusepa looked down from the sky and said: 


" What in the world has happened here? 
“ The Moon-God fell from the sky and fell upon the gate complex.
“ The Storm-God saw him and sent rains after him; 
“ He sent rains and fear seized him, anxiety seized him. 
“ Hapantali went there. 
“ He stood beside him and uttered over him the words of a spell. 
“ What will you proceed to do? 


“ I, Kamrusepa, will send after him. 
“ So I will go to the mount and release heat from the rock. 
“ Let him bark, then let him go, and let the fears and anxieties remain inside the water” 


But the sky bore open and Frost made it. 


Then, he became frightened. 


The Storm-God became frightened, as Frost bore open the sky and made it to Heaven. 


The Storm-God became frightened.
Tablet V:  


When the Storm-God thunders frightfully, the man of the Storm-God takes the following: two bulls, two sheep, one copper hammer, one copper knife, one copper axe, red, white, and black wool, of each a loop. 


When they have assembled all this, they feed the man of the Storm-God. The man of the Storm-God takes fifty thick breads, and a selection of pegs, drinking pegs of silver and bronze, and a peg of cornel wood, and he fills them. 


Labarna, the king, with his mind, names them. Labarna the king names the cups .


Next he drinks two oxen, a wagon, and a spear, the weapon of the Storm-God. Three times, he breaks three thick loaves. 


Next, while standing, he drinks the thunder and lightning, the clouds, and the rains of the Storm-God. Three times, he breaks three thick loaves.


Next, he drinks the fears and anxieties of the Storm-God. Nine times, he breaks nine thick loaves. 


All the while the man of the Storm-God is drinking this cup no one is permitted to make a sound. It is forbidden! 


Then, he places them, the cups, in the gate separately. While standing yet still, he drinks the divine heptad of the Storm-God. One time, he breaks eleven thick loaves. 


Then the man of the Storm-God goes back home to his house and makes a libation before the Storm-God of the Sky. 


Scribe note: 


I lost none of the Gods' words. But whenever Telipinu is difficult toward anyone; I speak the gods' words and invoke him. And the Sun-God says: 


" Let the gods' words go, and my allocation is just" 


Hannahanna says: 


" And if you, Ο’ Sun-God, give good to someone, may he give you nine; 
“ And may the poor man give you one sheep."


Invocation of the Sun-God and Telipinu.


Before the flood swept over-


The flood climbed up the earth. In the midst of the earth the flood rose. They covered the mountains. They were on the terraces.


The flood threatened the sun. The flood threatened the moons. The flood reached up to the stars of heavens. The gods held council. The Weather-God could not hold back the fury of the Sea. 


Kumarbi spoke in the assembly: 


“ We Great Gods! 


“ What god is there, who we recognised as king?
“ Where has he fled? 
“ We should recognise the Sea! 


“ Bear a tribute to the Sea!
“ Put copper, lapis lazuli, parašḫaš, silver, and gold in the water!


“ Lest there be another Great Flood!”


Astarte and the Tribute of the Sea-


Ptah rose from his throne, Dominion, Prosperity, Health! 


He carried tribute from the tribunal; then, Renenut carried this tribute to the sea. It was due to him as ruler of life, prosperity, and health. 


One of the gods said: 


" He rules over the sky.
“ Now behold, tribute must be brought to him,
“ Deliver his tribute or he will carry us off as booty.
“ Give him our own for now." 


Renenut carried this tribute of silver, gold, and lapis lazuli. She carried the boxes to the Sea.


The Ennead of Gods, argued over the Sea’s demands. 


He would take all from them. 


As an intermediary, Astarte was chosen for that purpose. Ptah and Renenut took a message to Astarte. 


Renenut said to one of the birds:


" Hear what I shall say; thou shouldst not go away with another . 
“ Come, that thou mayest go to Astarte!


“ Go and fly to her house, 
“ Speak under the window of the room where she is sleeping.
“ Say to her : 


“ If thou art awake, hear my voice; if thou art asleep, I shall waken thee.
” The Ennead must send tribute to the Sea, as ruler over the earth
” And, as ruler over the sky. 


” Pray, come thou to them in this hour!
” That thou go thyself, carrying the tribute of the Sea.”


Then Astarte wept,


“ Am I now to be thou slave?”


Yet, the goddess went. So, she bore the tribute of the Sea. 


She reached the Sea, singing and laughing at him. The Sea saw Astarte sitting on the shore. 


Then, he said to her: 


" Whence comest thou, thou daughter of Ptah, thou furious and tempestuous goddess?


“ Are thy sandals, which are on thy feet, broken?
“ Are thy clothes, which are upon thee, torn away,
“ By the going and coming which thou hast made in heaven and earth?"


Astarte offered herself to him. 


The Sea sent a message to the gods.   


" As tribute of the Ennead, if they give to me thy daughter,
“ Then, what should I do against them myself?" 


Astarte heard what the Sea said to her. She lifted herself up to go before the Ennead, to the place where they were gathered. 


The Great Ones saw her. They stood up before her. The Lesser Ones saw her. They lay down upon their bellies. A throne was given to her, and she sat down. There was then, presented to her the sceptre of the Sea.


Astarte assured that the gods would not be annihilated, but the Sea still hungered for tribute. The messenger of Ptah went to tell these words to Ptah and to Nut. Then Nut untied the beads which were at her throat. 


Behold, she put them into the scales. Ptah planned on how to best deal with the Sea, he bade Nut from giving the lapis lazuli.


She said to him:


“ If I do not, it means arguing with the Ennead.,
“ Then he will send and demand more; 
“ How long until he demands of us the seal of Geb 
“ So that he may fill the scales with it.


“ Then, he will send the flood
“ And he will cover the ground and the mountains, and then heaven!”


Ptah replied:


" I will send for Seth to come to fight with him, because seating himself calmly.
“ He, the Sea himself, will not come to fight with us." 


Chapter 15
The Debate Between Bird and Fish: 


In those ancient days, when the good destinies had been decreed, after An and Enlil had set up the divine rules of heaven and earth, then, the third of them, Lord of the Abzu, the Lord of Broad-Wisdom, Enki, the Master of Destinies, gathered together the people. 


He founded dwelling places. 


He took, in his hand, waters to encourage and create good seed. He laid out, side by side, the Tigris and the Euphrates; and caused them to bring water from the mountains. He scoured out the smaller streams, and positioned the other watercourses. 


The lord, Enki, made spacious sheepfolds and cattle-pens, and provided shepherds and herdsmen. He founded cities and settlements throughout the earth, and made the black-headed multiply. 


He provided them with a king as shepherd, elevating him to sovereignty over them. The king rose as the daylight over the foreign countries. Enki knit together the marshlands, making young and old reeds grow there. 


He made birds and fish teem in the pools and lagoons. He gave the marshlands to all kinds of living creatures, as their sustenance. He placed them in charge of this abundance of the gods. 


When Nudimmud, august prince, the Lord of Broad-Wisdom, had fashioned them, he filled the reed-beds and marshes with Fish and Bird. He indicated to them their positions, and instructed them in their divine rules.


Fish laid its eggs in the lagoons. Bird built its nest in a gap in the reed-beds. The bird frightened the fish of the lagoons in its dwelling. 


Fish took up a stand and cried out. Grandiosely, it initiated hostilities. It roused the street by quarrelling in an overbearing manner. 


Fish addressed Bird murderously:


" Foul Bird! Croaking, endlessly; sounding noise in the marshes, tirelessly squawking!
“ Forever gobbling away greedily, while your heart is dripping with evil!


“ Standing on the plain, you can keep pecking away until they chase you off.
“ The farmer's sons lay lines and nets for you in the furrows.
“ The gardener sets up nets against you in gardens and orchards.
“ He cannot rest his arm from firing his sling; he cannot sit down because of you.


“ You cause damage in the vegetable plots; you are a nuisance.
“ In the damp parts of fields, there are your unpleasant footprints.
“ Bird! You are shameless.


“ You fill the courtyard with your droppings.
“ The courtyard sweeper-boy who cleans the house chases after you with ropes.
“ By your noise the house and palace are disturbed and your din drives people away.


" They bring you into the fattening shed; they let you moo like cattle, bleat like sheep.
“ They pour out cool water in jugs for you, then they drag you away for the daily sacrifice. 
“ The fowler brings you with bound wings; the fisherman brings you into the palace.
“ They tie up your wings and beak. 
“ Your squawking is to no profit, what are you flapping about?
“ With your ugly voice you frighten the night; no one can sleep soundly!


“ Bird! Get out of the marshes! Get this noise of yours off my back!
“ Go out of here into a hole on the rubbish heap, that suits you!"


Thus, Fish insulted Bird on that day. 


Bird, with multicoloured plumage and multicoloured face, was convinced of its own beauty. It did not take to heart the insults Fish had cast at it. As if it was a nursemaid singing a lullaby, it paid no attention to the speech, despite the ugly words being uttered. 


Then, Bird answered Fish; it then insulted Fish. 


Bird raised its head and said to Fish:


" How has your heart become so arrogant, while you yourself are so lowly?
“ Your mouth is flabby, although your mouth goes all the way round.


“ You cannot see behind you.
“ You are bereft of hips, and also of arms, hands and feet.
“ Just try bending your neck to your feet! 


“ Your smell is awful;
“ You make people throw up, and they bare their teeth at you!
“ No trough would hold the kind of prepared food you eat.
“. He who has carried you dares not let his hand touch his skin.


“ In the great marshes and the wide lagoons, I am your persecuting demon.
“ You cannot eat the sweet plants there, as my voice harasses you.
“ You cannot travel with confidence in the river, as my storm-cloud covers you,
“ As you slip through the reed-beds you are always beneath my eyes.


“ Some of your little ones are destined to be my daily offering;
“ You give them to me, to allay my hunger.
“ Some of your big ones are, just as certainly, destined for my banqueting hall,
“ And you just sit there in the mud!”


" But I am the beautiful and clever Bird, fine artistry went into my adornment,
“ But no skill has been expended on your holy shaping!


“ Strutting about in the royal palace is my glory;
“ My warbling is considered a decoration in the courtyard.
“ The sound I produce, in all its sweetness,
“ Is a delight for the person of Culgi, son of Enlil. 


“ Fruits and produce of gardens and orchards,
“ These are the enormous daily offerings due to me. 
“ Groats, flour malt, hulled barley and emmer-grain are sweet things to my mouth.
" How do you not recognise my superiority from this? 


“ Bow your neck to the ground!"


Thus, Bird insulted Fish on that occasion. 


Fish became angry. Trusting in its heroic strength and solidness, Fish swept across the bottom like a heavy rain cloud. It took up the quarrel. 


It did not take to heart the insults that Bird had cast at it; but, it could not bring itself to submit, and spoke unrestrainedly. 


Again Fish replied to Bird:


" Chopped-off beak and legs, deformed feet, cleft mouth, thin tongue!
“ You clatter away in your ignorance, never with any reflection!


“ Gluttonous, malformed, and filling the courtyard with droppings,
“ The little sweeper-boy sets nets in the house and chases you with ropes.
“ The baker, the brewer, the porter, all those who live in the house are annoyed with you.


“ Bird, you have not examined the question of my greatness;
“ You have not taken due account of my nature. 
“ You could not understand my weakness, nor my strength; 
“ Yet, you spoke inflammatory words.


“ Once you have really looked into my achievements, you will be greatly humbled.
“ Your speech contains grave errors; you have not given it due consideration.
" I am Fish.


“ I am responsibly charged with providing abundance for the pure shrines;
“ For the great offerings at the lustrous E-kur.
“ On the august platform of the great offerings of the gods,
“ I stand proudly, with head raised high!


“ Just like Acnan I am here to satisfy the hunger of the land.
“ I am her helper!
“ Therefore people pay attention to me, and they keep their eyes upon me.
“ As at the harvest festival, they rejoice over me and take care of me.
“ Bird, whatever great deeds you may have achieved,
“ I will teach you their pretentiousness. 


“ I shall hand back to you in your turn, your haughtiness and mendacious speech."


Thereupon, Fish conceived a plot against Bird. Silently, furtively, it slithered alongside. When Bird rose up from her nest to fetch food for her young, Fish searched for the most discreet of silent places. 


It turned her well-built nest of brushwood into a haunted house!


It destroyed her well-built house. Tore down her storeroom. It smashed the eggs she had laid and threw them into the sea. Thus Fish struck at Bird, and then fled into the waters. 


Then, Bird came, lion-faced and with an eagle's talons, flapping its wings towards its nest. It stopped mid-flight. Like a hurricane whirling in the midst of heaven, it circled in the sky. 


Bird, looking about for its nest, spread its limbs wide. It trampled over the broad plain after its well-built nest of brushwood. Its voice shrieked into the interior of heaven like the Mistress’.


Bird sought for Fish. Scanning the marshes, Bird peered into the deep water for Fish, watching closely. Extending its claws, it justly snatched from the water Fish's tiny fish-spawn, gathering them all together and piling them up in a heap. 


Thus, Bird took its revenge and struck its heart.


Bird replied to Fish:


" You utter fool! Dumb, muddle-headed Fish, you are out of your good sense!
“ The mouths of those who circle the quay never get enough to eat,
“ And their hunger lasts all day! 


“ Swine! Rascal! 
“ Gorging yourself upon your own excrement,
“ You freak! You are like a watchman living on the walls!
“ Curse you!


“ Fish, you kindled fire against me; you planted henbane.
“ In your stupidity you caused devastation; you have spattered your hands with blood.
“ Your arrogant heart will destroy itself by its own deeds!


“ But, I am Bird, flying in the heavens and walking on the earth.
“ Wherever I travel to I am, therefore, the joy of its heart, named greatly.


“ O’ Fish, I am bestowed by the great princes.
“ I am of first-class seed, and my young are first-born young,
“ I am the one who walks with uplifted head to the lustrous E-kur.
“ From now, until distant days, the numerous people say:


“” How can you not recognise my pre-eminence?””


” Bow your neck to the ground."


Again Bird had hurled insults at Fish. Then Fish shouted at Bird, eyeing it angrily: 


" Do not puff yourself up from your lying mouth! Our judge shall take this up.
“ Let us take our case to Enki, our judge and adjudicator."


So, with the two of them jostling and continuing the evil quarrel, in order to establish the one over the other in their grandness and pre-eminence, the litigation was registered within Eridug. They put forward their argumentation. 


There they stood in dispute, yelling and thrashing about, amid roaring like that of a bull. They crept forward like bitter men, and requested a verdict from King Culgi, son of Enlil. 


Bird spoke:


" You Culgi, lord of true speech, pay attention to my words!
“ I had put home and laid eggs there.
“ I had bestowed my young, safely, and had given as their sustenance.


“ After I had started quarrel, Fish, he destroyed my house! 
“ He turned my nest of brushwood into a haunted house.
“ He destroyed my house, and tore down my storeroom.
“ He smashed my eggs and threw them into the sea. 
“ Now, examine what I have said, return a verdict in my favour."


She prostrated herself to the ground.


Culgi announced the word. Prince of august, spoke from the heart: 


" Your words are sterling words, such as delight the heart." 


" For how long are you going to persist in quarrelling?" 


Like they both came out supreme, like butting bulls, they jostled each other.


Fish spoke:


" Lord, let it be favourable to me!" 


Culgi answered:


" I shall instruct you in the divine rules and just ordinances of our dwelling-place.
“ Like Enki, King of the Abzu, I am successful in finding solutions, 
“ And am wise in words."


He answered Bird and Fish:


" To strut about in the E-kur is a glory for Bird, as its singing is sweet.
“ At Enlil's holy table, Bird receives precedence over you there!


“ It shall utter its cries in the temple of the great gods.
“ The Anuna-gods rejoice at its voice
“ It is suitable for banquets in the great dining hall of the gods.
“ It provides good cheer in the king's palace.
“ With head high, at the table of Culgi, son of Enlil, the king granted long life.”

In the Abzu of Eridug, judgement was given in favour of Bird.


Because Bird was victorious over Fish in the dispute between Fish and Bird,


Father Enki be praised!


The Mighty Puissant, Aliyan Ba’al
Yahm Saga
Ba’al Tablet I: Foundations


El met with Yamm, himself. The word he carried was too important for a messenger to bear. 


He spoke to Yamm:


“ One night and a day, two days will pass, he will arrive with his game, 
“ Hadd!
“ You will meet him on Saphon.”


Yamm replied:


“ Truly, I know! 


“ Will you bind him?
“ I am constrained in my attack and he will attack me in my lions,
“ He will take blood from my back.”


El assured Yamm:


“ You shall attack him in his lions, 
“ One night and a day, two days will pass.
“ He will arrive with a life.


“ You will meet Hadd; he will bring your food on Saphon.
“ You shall send him running as a doe!”


The gods were summoned to assembly. Even the distant ones. El sat in his marzeah, the shame of the eternal one. The shameful conduct of the usurper, he was drunken in his house. 


The gods came to the House of the Lord.


“ Who surely travels through the land? Who goes in the dust? 


“ There is destruction, a mess of mud on the ground!”
 
He drank overflowing curdled milk. He gave them to drink overflowing curdled milk. He took a cup in his hand, a flagon for each hand. 


El the Benign spoke:


“ My son by the name of Yawu, O’ Goddess Elat, Lady Athiratu,
“ Yahm shall be his name!”


He declared Yahm’s name; Elat, Lady Athiratu answered: 


“ For our sustenance you are the one who has been proclaimed.”


The bull, El, answered:


“ I, Beneficent El, have taken you upon thy hands, I pronounce your name.


“ Yahm is your name, Beloved of El. Yahm is your name!
“ My silver house, which in the hands of Mightiest Ba’al, must be taken.


“ Thus, because he reviles me, drive him from his royal throne.
“ Drive him from his resting place, his throne of dominion!


“ But, if you do not drive him from his throne, he will beat you like flour!”


A feast! 


El sacrificed! 


He proclaimed the name of Yahm! 


He slaughtered large stock as well as small. He felled bulls and fatling rams, yea’, old calves, sheep by the flock, and kids.


Ba’al Tablet II: Lord of Memphis


They set out for the great and wide Memphis, from Kaphtor, the throne where he sat, to Memphis, the land of his inheritance. 


From a thousand acres, ten thousand hectares, at the feet of Kothar they bowed down and fell. They prostrated themselves and did him honour. They spoke to Kothar wa-Hasis, and recited to the skilled artisan:


“ Decree of Bull El, your father, word of the Beneficent One, your scion:


“ Kothar! Prepare to pour your mold, prepare in the mountain! 
“ Hasten! 
“ Hurry!
“ Rush!
“ To me, let your feet run! To me let your legs race! 
“ To the Mount Khurshan-zurkas.
“ For a message I have, and I will tell you a word.


“ I will recite to you, the word of trees and the whisper of stones,
“ The converse of heaven to hell, of deeps to stars,
“ The word people do not know, earth’s masses do not understand.
“ Come and I will reveal it!”


Kothar wa-Hasis answered:


“ Go!


“ Go divine servants, you delay but I depart.
“ Kaphtor is very far; Memphis is very far, O’ gods.
“ Two lengths beneath earth’s spring; three expanses of caves.”


Then they surely went toward Inbb, Anat's abode. 


Across one thousand courts, ten thousand houses, at the feet of Anat they bent over and fell. They prostrated themselves and honoured her. They lifted their voice and declared:


" Message of Bull El, your father, word of the Beneficent One, your scion:


“ Remove war from the earth, set love in the dust,
“ Pour peace amidst the earth, tranquility amidst the fields.
“ Hurry! 
“ Hasten!
“ Rush! 
“ To me let your feet run, to me let your legs hasten  .


“ To the Mountain Khurshan-zurkas.
“ For a message I have and I will tell you a word.


“ I will recite to you the word of trees and the whisper of stones,
“ The converse of heaven to hell, of deeps to stars,
“ The word people do not know; earth’s masses do not understand.
“ Come and I will reveal it!”


There he went off on his way, to El of the Sources of the Floods. In the midst of the headwaters, of the two oceans. He penetrated El’s fields, and entered the pavilion of the king, Father of Shunem. 


At El’s feet he bowed, and fell down. He prostrated himself, doing him homage:


” O’ Kothar wa-Khasis! 


“ Quickly build the house of Yahm,
“ Erect the palace of Judge Nahar! 
“ Build the house of Prince Yahm!
“ Erect the Palace of Judge Nahar in the midst of Mount Hazzi.
“ Quickly, this house you shalt build; quickly, erect this palace!”


Shapash, the torch of heaven, overheard the command of El. She delivered word of El’s plan to Ashtar.


Quoth the Gods’ Torch Shapash:


“ Hearken I pray thee! 
“ Thy father, Bull El, favors Prince Yahm.
“ Indeed, he has set the table of heaven for him! 


“ Should thy father, Bull El, hear thee,
“ He will pull out the pillars of thy dwelling, and overturn the throne of thy kingship,
“ Ye’, break the scepter of thy dominion!


“ Retire from the presence of Yahm”


Quoth Athtart, Warrior of the Heavens:


“ Oh, my father, Bull El! 


“ Against me, the Father has set himself.
“ I have no house like the gods nor court like the holy ones.
“ Like a lion, I will descend with my desire


“ Alone I shall go down into the grave of us both.
“ The skillful ones, the Kotharim will wash me, to dwell in the house.
“ Because the table is to be set, with the will of the Bull El, his father,
“ Before Prince Yahm, before Judge Nahar!


“ Am I indeed king or am I not king?"


El, at hearing the word of Ashtar, rebuked him:


" You, you have risen up against me, because of your overweening!”


Mightiest Ba’al responded: 


" From your throne of kingship you will be driven,
“ From the seat of your dominion, expelled!
“ On your head be ayamari, O’ Yahm,
“ Between your shoulders yagarish, Judge Nahar.
“ May Horon split open, O’ Yahm, may Horon split open your head, 
“ May Horon smash your good place, Athtart, upon thy skull!
“ Broken thy staff will be, and down you will fall at the peak of your years.


“ In the limit of your years, may you be humbled like the Father of Shunem,
“ May thy twain wives also!"


Ba’al Tablet III: Judge Nahar


Yahm sent for two messengers. Judge Nahar called for envoys. He raised his voice and cried:


“ They rejoice exceedingly; as the jubilant rejoice at the peak of their triumph.
“ Let us shatter their rejoicing heart, let their nose be broken!


“ Depart ye, lads, don't tarry! 


“ There now, be off on your way towards the assembly of the convocation,
“ In the midst of the Mount of Lala.


“ At the feet of El, do not fall down; do not prostrate to the assembled body.
“ Proudly standing, say ye’ your speech, and say unto the bull, my father, El,
“ Declare unto the assembly of the convocation:


“ Message of Yahm, your lord, of your master, Judge Nahar.
“ Surrender the god with a following, him whom the multitudes worship.
“ Give Ba’al to me to lord over, Dagon’s son, whose spoil I’ll possess.
“ Hand over Ba’al and his henchmen, that I may humble him!”


The lads departed. They delayed not. They were off on their way to the midst of the Mount of Lala, toward the assembly of the convocation.


The gods were sitting down to eat. The Holy Ones dined. 


Behold! 


Ba’al stood beside El to cut up meat. As soon as the gods caught sight of them, when they saw the messengers of Yahm, the gods lowered their heads. When they saw the envoys of Judge Nahar, they bowed their heads onto their knees, though they occupied their thrones of princeship.


Ba’al angered; he rebuked them:


“ Why, O’ gods, have ye’ dropt your heads down upon your knees,
“ And onto your thrones of princeship?


“ I see the gods are cowed with terror of the messengers of Yahm,
“ The envoys of Judge Nahar!


“ Lift up, O’ gods, your heads from the top of your knees,
“ From your thrones of princeship.
“ For I will answer the messengers of Yahm, the envoys of Judge Nahar."


The gods raised up their heads from upon their knees, from upon their thrones of princeship. Thereupon, came the messengers of Yahm, the envoys of Judge Nahar. 


At El’s feet they did not fall down. They did not prostrate themselves to the assembly of the convocation. 


Proudly standing, they said their speech. 


Fire! 


Burning fire! 


Two fires doth flashed! 


A whetted sword was their eyes. They said unto the bull, Father El:


" Give up, O’ gods, him whom you are hiding, surrender the god with a following,
“ Him whom the multitudes worship!


“ Give Ba’al to me to lord over; Dagon’s son whose spoil I’ll possess.
“ Hand over Ba’al and his henchmen that I may humble him,
” That I may seize his inheritance.”


Quoth the bull, his Father El:


“ Thy slave is Ba’al, O’ Yahm, thy slave is Ba’al forever.
“ Judge Nahar, Dagon’s son is thy captive.


“ He must bring tribute to you, like the gods, 
“ Even he must bring, like the sons of the Holy One, tribute to thee.
“ He shall be brought as thy tribute, for the gods bring thy gift.
“ The Holy Ones are thy tributaries!”


Prince Ba’al became a companion to anger! 


Seizing a cudgel in his left hand, a bludgeon in his right hand, he reached to strike the lads. 


His right hand, Anat seized. Ashtoreth seized his left hand saying:


“ How canst thou strike the messengers of Yahm, the envoys of Judge Nahar?


“ A messenger holds the staff of royalty, and you would slay him?
“ A messenger bears upon his shoulders, words of his lord.”


Then indeed, a companion to anger, Prince Ba’al was:


" I, myself, say to Yahm your master, your lord, Judge Nahar:


” Hear the word of the Avenger, the Annihilator, the Accomplisher Hadad!
” You will do homage, before me you shall bow down.


” I will go after Yahm, I will drive him from his throne
”  And Yahm will in be the sieve of destruction. 


” Yahm will be in the breast of death, in Judge Nahar will be gnawers. 
” There, attackers, the worms, lowly maggots, move about. 
” There, your sword, I will destroy! 
“ The house I will burn! 


” To the earth will fall the strength of us two, the nobles; 
” And into the dust the might of us two, the mighty!"


Quickly after the words left his lips, Ba’al set forth. 


She raised her voice at the word, but Lady Athiratu remained in waiting:


“ May he sink beneath the foot of Judge Nahar!
“ May he be crushed beneath the throne of Prince Yahm!”


Ba’al Tablet IIII: Yagrush & Ayamur


Quoth Kothar wa-Khasis, from beneath the throne of Yahm:


“ Did I not tell thee, O’ Prince Ba’al, did not I declare O’ Rider of Clouds?


“ Now, thine enemy, O’ Baal!, now thine enemy, wilt thou smite!
“ Now, wilt thou cut off thine adversary! 


“ Thoult take thine eternal kingdom,
“ Thine everlasting dominion!”


Kothar brought down two clubs. 


He gave them names:


“ Thou, thy name is Yagrush. Yagrush, chase Yahm! 
“ Chase Yahm from his throne, Nahar from his seat of dominion!


“ Do thou, swoop from the hand of Ba’al, like an eagle between his fingers;


“ Strike the back of Prince Yahm, from between his fingers;
“ Strike the back of Prince Yahm, between the arms of Judge Nahar!”


The club swooped from the hand of Ba’al, like an eagle soaring from between his fingers. It struck the back of Prince Yahm. It struck between the arms of Judge Nahar. 


Yahm was firm. He was not bowed.


His joints bent not, nor his frame broke. 


Kothar brought down two more weapons, clubs. 


He gave them names:


“ Thou, thy name is Ayamur. Ayamur, drive Yahm! 
“ Drive Yahm from his throne, Nahar from his seat of dominion! 


“ Do thou, swoop from the hand of Ba’al, like an eagle between his fingers,


“ Strike the pate of Prince Yahm, strike between the eyes of Judge Nahar,
“ Yahm shall collapse and fall to the earth!”


The club swooped from the hand of Ba’al, like an eagle from between his fingers. It struck the pate of Prince Yahm, it struck there, between the eyes of Judge Nahar. 


Yahm collapsed! He fell to the ground.


His joints bent. His frame broke 


Thereupon, Ba’al dragged him out. Ba’al rent Yahm; he cut up Judge Nahar. He smashed Yahm. 


He annihilated Judge Nahar.


By name Ashtoreth rebuked him:


“ For shame, O’ Puissant Ba’al, for shame O’ Rider of the Clouds!


“ Our prisoner is Yahm. Shame, for our captive is Judge Nahar!”


As the word left her mouth Puissant Ba’al was indeed ashamed. 


He answered:


" Yahm is surely dead;


And she replied:


“ Indeed! Ba’al reigns! Ba’al shall be king!”


Then up spoke Yahm: 


" Lo, I am as good as dead! 


“ Surely, Ba’al now will reign! 
“ Surely he is king!


Ba’al indeed rules, indeed he shall be king.




Ba’al Tablet V: Maiden Anath


Radamin served Mightiest Ba’al. He waited upon him. He honoured the prince, Lord of the Earth. 


He rose, set the tables, prepared food and gave it to him to eat. He divided a breast before him, with a sharpened blade and cut up a fatling. 


Radamin got up, and made ready a feast. He gave him drink.


He placed a cup into his hand, a flagon into both his hands. It was a large beaker, great to see, a jar of men of heaven, cup of the sky folk, a holy cup, such which women should never behold. It was a goblet, such as should none but Athirat set her eye on. 


A thousand pitchers, he took, of new autumn wine. Ten thousand, he mixed in the mixing bowl. It held barrels full, enough wine to be mixed in ten thousand portions. 


While he was mixing it, one rose. 


One chanted and sang. With cymbals in the hands of the minstrel, the youngster with a sweet voice sang.


Ba’al, in the heights of Tsaphon, caught sight of his daughters. He set his eye on Pidraya, daughter of Ar. He set his eye on Talaya, daughter of Rabb. 


Pidar knew that his gaze was upon them.


There she came, bathed in the scent of henna of seven maids. She smelled of coriander ambergris. She locked the gates of the house and met the picked fighters. She met the messengers at the base of the mountain. Anat gave battle in the valley.


Anath did battle on the plain, fighting between two towns, smiting the west-land’s peoples, smashing the folk of the sunrise. Under her, heads, like sheaves, rolled. Over her, hands, like locusts, like a grasshopper, a mass of heroes’ hands fly. 


She bound the heads to her back, and fastened the hands in her girdle. She plunged knee-deep in the warriors’ blood. Hip-deep in the gore of heroes, with darts, she drove out the enemies.


With the strings of her bow, she chased off her foes.


Then, Anath went to her house. The goddess proceeded to her palace. Not sated with her fighting between the two towns, she pictured the chairs as heroes. Pretending the tables were warriors, and that the footstools were troops, much battle she didst and beheld.


She fought. 


She contemplated. 


Anath’s liver swelled with laughter. Her heart filled up with joy. Anath triumphed. For she plunged knee-deep in knight’s blood, hip-deep in the gore of heroes. 


Then, sated with battling in the house, fighting between the two tables and the deeps of the knight’s blood, she poured the fatness of dew in a bowl.


Maiden Anath washed her hands, Yamamat Liimmim washed her fingers. She washed her hands of knight’s blood, her fingers of the gore of heroes. She put back chairs with chairs, tables to tables. Footstools turned back into footstools.


She drew some water and bathed with sky dew, fatness of earth, and spray of the rider of the clouds. It was dew that the heaven sheds, spray that is shed by the stars, and ambergris, whose home’s in the sea.


She put corals on her breast, and sang of her love. She sang for a feeling of tenderness of Mightiest Ba’al, affection of Padriya, daughter of Ar, love of Talliya, daughter of Rabb, and friendship of Arsiya, daughter of Ya’abdar.


Ba’al made call to his warriors, his messengers, his envoys;


“ So then, O’ lads, enter ye;


“ At Anath’s feet bow and fall down, prostrate, do her honor.
“ Say unto Maiden Anath, declare unto Yamamat Liimmim,
“ Message of Puissant Ba’al, word of the powerful hero:


“ Take war away from the earth, banish all strife from the soil,
” Pour peace into earth’s very bowels, much amity into earth’s bosom.
” Hasten! 
“ Hurry! 
“ Rush!


“ To me thy feet shall trot, to me thy legs shall sprint,
“ For, I've a word I fain would tell thee, a speech I would utter to thee.
“ Speech of tree and whisper of stone,
” Speech of the deeps with the stars,
“ Ye’, a thunderbolt unknown to heaven
“ A word not known to men, nor sensed by the masses in the earth.


“ Come, pray, and I will reveal it in the midst of my godly Mount Zaphon,
“ In the sanctuary, mount of my portion, in the pleasance of the hill I possess.”


No sooner than she espied the gods, Gapn and Ugar, that Anath’s feet stumbled. Her loins broke behind her. Her face broke out in sweats above her. The joints of her loins were bent. Those of her back were weakened. 


She raised up her voice and cried:


“ Why come Gapn and Ugar? What enemy has risen against Ba’al?
“ What foe against the Rider of the Clouds?
“ Did I not crush El’s beloved Yahm?
“ Did I not destroy El’s flood Rabbim?
“ Pray, did I not muzzle the dragon?
“ I crushed the crooked serpent, Lotan; Shaylat, the seven-headed,
“ I crushed El’s beloved Ar.
“ Did I not silence the divine calf of El, Atik the Rebellious One.
“ I crushed the godly bitch Hashat, Ishat the Fiery.
“ I destroyed the house of El-Dhubub, who fought thee and seized the gold,
“ Who drove Ba’al from the heights of Zaphon, sans frontlet.
“ His ear pierced through, I chased him from his throne of kingship
“ I chased him from the dias, the seat of his dominion.


“ Now, what enemy has risen against Ba’al?
“ What foe is there against the Rider of Clouds?”


The lads, twain, made answer:


“ No enemy’s risen against Ba’al, no foe against the Rider of Clouds!
“ Message of Puissant Ba’al, word of the powerful hero:


““ Take war away from the earth, banish all strife from the soil,
“” Pour peace into earth’s very bowels, much amity into earth’s bosom.


“” Hasten! 
“” Hurry! 
“” Rush! 


“” To me thy feet shall trot, to me thy legs shall sprint,
“” For I've a word I fain would tell thee, a speech I would utter to thee.
“” Speech of tree and whisper of stone,
“” Speech of the deeps with the stars,
“” Ye’ , a thunderbolt unknown to heaven,
“” A word not know to men nor sensed by the masses in earth.


“” Come, pray, and I will reveal it in the midst of my godly Mount Zaphon,
“” In the sanctuary, mount of my portion, in the pleasance of the hill I possess.””


 The Maiden Anath answered. Yabamat Liimmim replied:


“ I’ll take war away from the earth, banish all strife from soil
“ Pour peace into earth’s very bowels, much amity into earth’s bosom.


“ Let Ba’al rule! Let him reign!


“ Yet another word I will say:


“ Go! 
“ Go divine attendants, ye are slow and I am swift.
“ From my mount to the godhead afar; Enibaba to the distant divinity,
“ It is two mathpads under the earth’s furrows, three underneath the hollows.”


There she was off on her way to Ba’al of the summit of Zaphon. From a thousand fields, ten thousand acres, Ba’al saw his sister’s approach, the advance of his own father’s daughter. He dismissed his wives from her presence. He placed an ox before her. A fatted one in front of her.


She drew some water and bathed with sky dew, fatness of earth, and spray of the rider of the clouds, dew that the heaven sheds, and spray that is shed by the stars. She rubbed herself in with ambergris, whose source was a sperm-whale, one thousand tracts away, in the sea.


Ba’al Tablet VI: Father Bull & Lady Sea 


The Maiden Anath replied:


“ Our king is Mightiest Ba’al!
“ Our ruler second to none; all of us will bring him a chalice,
” All of us will bring him a cup!


“ In lament, indeed, he cries to Bull El, his father, 
“ The king who created him, established him!
“ He cries to Athirat and her children, the goddess and the band of her brood: 


““ For Ba’al has no house like the gods’, no court like Athirat’s children’s. 
““ The dwelling of El is the shelter of his son, the dwelling of Lady Athirat of the Sea, 
““ The dwelling of Pidray, Daughter of Light, the shelter of Tallay, Daughter of Showers, 
““ The dwelling of Arsay, Daughter of the Wide World,
““ The dwelling of the Noble Brides.


“ But one more thing; something more I would tell ye,
“ Make ready:


“ I beseech ye, just try doing homage to Lady Athirat of the Sea
“ Obeisance to the creatress, Progenitress of the Gods,
“ And to Hayani, the Clever One, who does go up to the bellows.


“ In the hands of Khasis are the tongs; he smelts silver, he hammers out gold,
“ He casts silver by the thousand fold;.
“ Gold he hammers out into ten thousands of pieces.


“ He casts a canopy and a couch, a divine dias fit for a god,
“ Worth twice ten thousand pieces!
“ A divine dias coated with silver, overlaid with a film of gold.
“ A divine throne fit for a god, with a cushion at its back.
“ A divine footstool fit for a god, covered over with a mat.
“ Divine sandals, possessors of thongs.
“ A divine table fit for a god, whose surface he does fill
“ With rhytons in the shapes of creeping species,
“ All manner of game from the depths to the foundations of the earth,
“ A divine bowl fit for a god, whose handle is shaped like small herd animals of Amurru,
“ A stelae whose appearance is as in Yam'am.
“ Where wild-oxen are by the ten thousands, there by myriads."


Quoth the Maiden Anath:


“ He’ll heed me for his own good, for I’ll fell him like a lamb to the ground.
“ I’ll make his gray hair flow with blood, the gray hair of his beard with gore,
“ Unless he give a house unto Ba’al like the gods, and a court like Asherah’s children’s.”


She stamped her foot, and the earth trembled! 


There she was, off on her way, towards El of the Sources of the Floods. In the midst of the headwaters of the two rivers, she penetrated El’s field and entered the pavilion of the king, Father Shunem.


Her voice, Bull El, her father heard. He replied from within the seven chambers, from inside the eight enclosures. 


The heavens were wearied by the hand of Divine Mot. 


Even the God’s Torch Shapash, who wings the expanse of heaven, was in Divine Mot’s hand. The Torch of the Gods, who did glow hot, was obscured by dust. 


Quoth the Maiden Anath:


“ In the building of your mansion, O’ El, rejoice not in the heights of your palace.
“ Lest I seize them by my right hand, with the might of my strong arm,
“ My long hand will smash thy skull, I’ll make thy gray hair flow with blood,
“ And fill the gray hair of thy beard with gore!”


El replied in the seven chambers, inside the eight enclosures. He answered from the closed room:


“ I ween, daughter of mine, was not thou gentle and contumely among goddesses? 
“ What wouldst thou, O’ Maiden Anath?”


The Maiden Anath replied:


“ Thy decree, O’ El, is wise, wisdom with ever-life thy portion,
“ Thy decree a fortunate life!


“ Our king is Mightiest Ba’al!
“ Our ruler second to none, all of us will bring him a chalice,
” All of us will bring him a cup!
“ In lament, indeed, he cries to Bull El, his father.
“ El, the king who created him, established him; 
“ He cries to Athirat and her children, the goddess and the band of her brood: 


““ For Ba’al has no house like the gods’, no court like Athirat’s children’s. 
““ The dwelling of El is the shelter of his son, the dwelling of Lady Athirat of the Sea, 
““ The dwelling of Pidray, Daughter of Light, the shelter of Tallay, Daughter of  Showers, 
““ The dwelling of Arsay, Daughter of the Wide World,
“” The dwelling of the Noble Brides.


On a stone, she prepared it. She grasped her spindle in her hand, held high in her right hand. She tore off its skin, the covering of the flesh. She flung its vestment into the sea; its two skins into the rivers.


She placed a khupatar-pot on the fire. A khubrush-vessel upon the coals. She exalted Bull El Benign. She did obeisance to the Creator of Creatures. 


When she lifted her eyes, she looked. Athirat indeed saw Ba’al’s advance. She saw the advance of Adolescent Anat. The approach of Yabamat the In-Law, Li`imim of the Peoples.. 


At her feet, came a shake. Around her hips was cracking. Above, her face sweats. The joints of her loins convulsed. Weak were the bones of her back. She raised her voice and declared: 


“ Why has Mightiest Ba’al come? Why has Adolescent Anat come?
“ Are my smiters, to be the smiters of me? 
“ Have my children slain each other, have they finished each other off? 
“ Or perhaps they’ve raised a rebellion?


“ The company of my kinfolk, are they my murderers,
“ Or the destroyers of the band of my brood?” 


The gleam of silver, Athirat eyed, gleam of silver, glint of gold. Lady Athirat of the Sea rejoiced. 


Aloud to her attendant, she declared: 


“ See the skilled work of the crafter of the deeps!
“ Now, attend thou, deft one,
“ Ye’, give heed!
“ O’ thou Fisherman of Lady Athirat of the Sea.
“ Take a net in hand, a great one in your hands! 
“ Cast into the Beloved of El, Yahm the Deep, 
“ River of El, the God Benign.“


She ordered the deft one to gather up fish from the sea. A feast for her support of Puissant Ba’al. He was also to deliver the words of his mistress. Yahm was to be confined to his domain, under her command, for Mighty Ba’al was now to become king of the gods, El’s great heir.


Ba’al Tablet VII: Ill Feasts


Then came Puissant Ba’al. The Rider of the Clouds advanced. Down he sat and eyed the table. Lo’, he took his stand and cried defiance. He stood erect and spit in the midst of the assembly of the divine beings:


“ Abomination has been placed upon my table, filth in the cup I drink.
“ For two kinds of banquets Ba’al hates, three, the Rider of Clouds:


“ A banquet of shamefulness, a banquet of baseness,
“ And a banquet of handmaids lewdness.


“ Yet herein is flagrant shamefulness,
“ And yet, herein is the handmaids’ lewdness.”


After this, Puissant Ba’al went. Also, the Maiden Anath went. 


As they did homage to Lady Asherah of the Sea, obeisance to the Progenitress of the Gods. 


Quoth Lady Asherah of the Sea:


“ Why do ye homage to Lady Asherah of the Sea
“ Obeisance to the Progenitress of the Gods?
“ Have ye done homage to the Bull El Benign, obeisance to the Creator of Creatures?”


 The Maiden Anath answered:


“ We do homage to thee, Lady Asherah of the Sea,
“ Obeisance to the Progenitress of the Gods.


“ We are bringing you gifts, Lady Athirat of the Sea.
“ We are honouring you, Creatress of the Gods,
“ Then we will bring gifts to him.”


“ Hearken to Mightiest Baal.”


 Lady Athirat of the Sea answered:


“ Hearken, Adolescent Anat.” 


The gods ate and drank. A suckling of breast was provided. With a sharpened knife, a cut of fatling. They drank wine from goblets. From cups of gold, the blood of trees. 


Loudly unto her lad, Asherah doth cried:


“ Look thou, Qadesh wa-Amrur, Fisherman of Lady Asherah of the Sea!
“ Saddle a donkey, harness a jackass. 
“ Attach trappings of silver, a housing of gold, put on the trappings of thy she-asses.”


Qadesh wa-Amrur obeyed. He saddled a donkey, harnessed a jackass. He attached a harness of silver, a housing of gold. He put on the trappings of his she-asses. Qadesh wa-Armur embraced her, and placed Asherah on the donkey’s back, on the beautiful back of the jackass. 


Qadesh lead. Armur was like a star in front, torch in hand. He blazed a trail.


The Maiden Anath followed, but Ba’al departed for Zaphon’s Summit. 


Indeed, straightaway Asherah turned her face towards El of the Sources of the Two Floods. In the midst of the headwaters of the two oceans, she penetrated El’s field and entered. In the pavilion of the king, Father Shunem, at El’s feet she bowed and fell down. She prostrated herself and did him reverence.


As soon as El espied her, he parted his jaws and laughed. His feet upon the footstool, he put, and twiddled his fingers. 


He lifted his voice and cried:


“ Why has come, Lady Asherah of the Sea, why hither, the Progenitress of the Gods?
“ Art thou become hungry and faint, having journeyed so far?
“ Or art thou become thirsty and parched, having traveled through the night?


“ Eat, pray ye’, drink! 


“ Eat thou bread from the tables, drink thou wine from the flagon,
“ From the golden goblets, the blood of vines.


“ See, does El the King’s love stir thee? Does the Bulls' affection arouse thee?”


Quoth Lady Asherah of the Sea:


“ Thy decree, O’ El, is wise, wisdom with ever-life thy portion,
“ Thy decree a fortunate life!


“  Our king is Mightiest Baal, our ruler second to none.
“ All of us will bring him a chalice, all of us will bring him a cup. 


“ But alas! In lament, indeed, he cries to Bull El, his father.
“ El, the king who created him, and established him.
“ He cries unto Asherah and her children,
“ Elat and the band of her kindred:


““ Look, no house has Ba’al like the gods, nor court like the children of Asherah
““ The abode of El is the shelter of his son, the abode of Lady Asherah of the Sea,
“” Is the abode of perfect brides,
“” Tis the dwelling of Padriya Daughter of Ar,
““ The shelter Talliya the Daughter of Rabb,
“” And the abode of Arsiya the Daughter of Ya’abdar.”


Quoth the Kindly One, El Benign:


“ Am I a slave, an attendant, a lackey, a henchman of Asherah?
“ Am I a slave, to handle a trowel and blocks?
“ Or is Asherah, a handmaid, a servant, a slave-girl, to make bricks?


“ Let a house be built for Ba’al like the gods and a court like the children of Asherah’s!”


Quoth Lady Asherah of the Sea:


“ Thou art great indeed, O’ El, and wise. Thy beard’s gray hair instructs thee.
“ Warmth moves to thy breast.


“ Now, too, the seasons of his rains, will Ba’al observe
“ In the season for his barque to appear with the tempest;
“ And, he will peal his thunder in the clouds
“ Flashing his lightning; darting his gleam to the earth.


“ The house of cedar, let him burn it; perfect it!
“ Yea the house of brick, remove it, let it be carried off!
“ Be it told to Puissant Ba’al:


“ Sumon weeds into thy house, herbs into the midst of thy palace.


“ The mountains shall bring thee much silver, the hills a treasure of gold.
“ They’ll bring thee god's grandeur aplenty, so build thou a silver and gold house,
“ A mansion of most pure lapis lazuli!”


The Maiden Anath rejoiced!


She stamped with her foot, so that the earth quaked. 


There she was, off on her way, unto Ba’al, upon Zaphon’s summit, over a thousand fields, ten thousand acres, laughing. The Maiden Anath lifted her voice and cried:


“ Receive, Ba’al, the glad tidings I bring thee!
“ They will build thee a house like thy brethren’s, and a court like unto thy kindred’s.


“ Summon weeds into thy house, herbs in the midst of thy palace.
“ The mountains shall bring thee much silver, the hills a treasure of gold.
“ They’ll bring thee god's grandeur aplenty; so build thou a silver and gold house
“ A mansion of most pure lapis lazuli!”


Puissant Ba’al rejoiced! 


He summoned weeds into his house. He summoned herbs into the midst of his palace. The mountains did bring him much silver. The hills brought a treasure of gold. The camels brought him god’s grandeur aplenty. The tall trees yielded logs.


He sent messengers unto Kothar wa-Khasis:


“ The downpour is the binding upon your two heads,
“ The lightning between your two eyes. 
“ Travel over a thousand fields in the sea, a myriad traversal on the rivers.
“ Cross over the mountain gabal, cross over the summit, Qa’al. 
“ Cross over the coasts of the heavenly height, Ihat-nop-shamem. 
“ Proceed to the Fisher of Athirat, go to Qadesh-Amrar. 


“ Then, you shall head for Great and Wide Memphis, to the midst of Hikpat-El,
“ For Kaphtor, the throne where he sits, Memphis, the land of his heritage. 
“ From across a thousand acres, a myriad hectares,
“ At the feet of Koth bow down and fall,
“ May you prostrate yourself and honor him. 
“ Say to Kothar wa-Hasis, recite to Hayyin the Skilled Craftsman: 


“ Decree of Mightiest Ba’al, word of the Mightiest of Warriors: 


““ Hayani the Clever One, go up to the bellows, in the hands of Khasis, take the tongs.
““ Smelt silver, hammer out gold; cast silver by the thousand fold, 
“” Gold be hammered out into ten thousands of pieces.
““ Cast a canopy and a couch, a divine dias fit for a god. 
““ A divine dias coated with silver, overlaid with a film of gold.
“” A divine throne fit for a god, with a cushion at its back.
“” A divine footstool fit for a God, covered over with a mat.
““ Divine sandals, which possess thongs. 
“” A divine table fit for a god,
““ Whose surface shall be filled with rhytons in the shapes of creeping species,
““ All manner of game from the depths to the foundations of the earth,
““ A divine bowl fit for a god whose handle is shaped like small herd animals of Amurru,
“” A  stelae whose appearance is as in Yam'am, where wild-oxen are by the myriads."


Ba’al Tablet VIII: House of Baal


Kothar wa-Khasis arrived. 


Before him an ox was set, a fatted one at his disposal. A throne was placed and he was seated to the right of Puissant Ba’al. 


The gods ate and drank. Then, answered Puissant Ba’al, responded, the Rider of the Clouds:


“ Quickly, a house, O’ Kothar, hurry! 
“ Raise up a palace, hasten! 
“ The house thou shalt build!


“ Quickly shalt thou raise up the mansion, in the midst of the fatness of Zaphon.
“ A thousand fields the house shall cover, a myriad of acres, the palace!”


Quoth Kothar wa-Khasis:


“ Hearken, O Puissant Ba’al; give heed, O’ Rider of the Clouds!
“ A window I’ll make in the house, a casement within the palace.”


He, Puissant Ba’al, replied:


“ Make not a window in the house, a casement, not, within the palace.”


Quoth Kothar wa-Khasis:


“ Thou’lt heed my words, O’ Ba’al!”


Again spake Kothar wa-Khasis:


“ Hark! Pray, Puissant Ba’al! A window I’ll make in the house,
“ A casement within the palace!”


But Puissant Ba’al replied:


“ Make not a window in the house, a casement, not, within the palace.”
“ Let not Padriya daughter of Ar be seen, or Talliya Daughter of Rabb be espied
“ By the Beloved of ‘El, Yahm! 


“ He cried defiance against me! And spat on me!”


Quoth Kothar wa-Khasis:


“ Thou’lt heed my words, O’ Ba’al!”


His house was quickly built. For Hadd his palace was raised. They brought, from Lebanon, its trees for logs and timber. From Sirion, its precious cedars. 


Fire was set to the mansion!


Flame to the palace!


Lo, a day and a second; behold, fire fed on the house. 


Flame devoured the palace! 


A third, and a fourth day, fire consumed the house. 


Flame upon the palace! 


A fifth, then a sixth day, fire ate the house.


Flame upon the palace!


There on the seventh day, the fire died down in the house. 


The flame departed the palace. 


The silver turned into blocks. The gold turned into bricks. 


Puissant Ba’al exulted:


“ My mansion have I builded of silver, my palace, indeed of gold!”


For his house Ba’al made preparations, Hadd made for his palace. 


He slaughtered both neat and small cattle. He felled bulls together with fatlings, rams and one year old calves, lambs and the kids.


He summoned his brethren to his house, his kindred within his palace. He summoned Asherah’s seventy children. 


He sated the he-lamb gods with he-lamb and wine. He sated the cow-goddesses with cows and wine. He sated the throne-gods, with footstools and wine. He sated the chair-goddesses with thrones and wine. He sated the gods with jars of wine. He sated the goddesses with pitchers of wine.


So, the gods ate and drank. They sated themselves with fatness of the teat, with tender fatling carved by a bounteous knife, while drinking the wine from flagons. They drank from gold cups, the blood of vines.


Gapn and Ugar returned from Memphis amidst the clamour of the celebration. 


A proclamation for Hadad:


“ The dais of lapis lazuli, O’ Mightiest Ba’al, Smiter of the Darling, Beloved of ‘El,
“ Smasher of Yahm, the foot on the top of of his skull.” 


The gods retired to the mountain.


Ba’al marched, city to city. Sixty-six towns he took. Seventy-seven hamlets. Eighty towns Ba’al held. Ninety towns, Ba’al pounded and sacked. 


He returned to dwell in his house. In the midst of the house, quoth Puissant Ba’al:


“ I will make one!


“ Kothar, this day, this very hour
“ A casement shall be opened in the house, a window within the palace.
“ Ye’, I’ll open a rift in the clouds at thy word Kothar wa- Khasis!”


Kothar wa- Khasis laughed. 


He raised up his voice and cried:


“ Said I not to thee, Pussiant Ba’al, thou’lt heed my words?”


He opened a casement in the house, a window within the palace. Ba’al opened rifts in the clouds. He gave forth his holy voice and discharged the utterance of his lips. 


His holy voice convulsed the earth. The mountains quaked. The people afar were dismayed. 


East and west quivered. 


Earth's high places reeled. Ba’al’s enemies took to the woods, Hadd’s foes ran to the hollow side of the mountain. 


Then, quoth Puissant Ba’al:


“ Ba’al’s enemies, why do you quake?
“ Are you dismayed by the weapons of the valiant one?”


The eyes of Ba’al sought out, for his hand, the yew-club of cedar. It swung into his right hand. 


Ba’al dwelled in his house:


“ No king, nor commoner, on earth shall install himself on this throne!


“ Tribute I’ll send not to Divine Mot, I will not dispatch a courier to El’s Darling Ghazir!
“ But a herald shall be sent.


“ Mot may call out in his soul, the beloved surely thinks in his heart:


“” I alone will have sway o’er the gods, 
“” So that gods and men may feed, he who satisfies multitudes of the earth.””


Then surely Ba’al called to his two pages:


"Attend to me! Gapn and Ugar!”




Mavt Saga
Ba’al Tablet IX: Children of Asherah


Amid the darkness, where daylight is obscured, amid the tall shaggy ostriches, was his portion. The sun-scorched places were his provinces, where rain clouds were withheld. 


To the sun-scorched places they were sent, the sons of Ghulumat, Amamis twain. The sons of Zulumat, the stately winged ones twain, spread their wings. Flocks were circling round the clouds. Birds were circling round in the heavens


Where the snow and the lightning were bound, aloud unto his lads, Ba’al cried:


“ Look ye’, Gapn and Ugar sons of Ghulumat, Amamis twain!


“ Sons of Zulumat, there now, be off on your way, 
“ Unto the Mount of Targhuzizz.
“ Unto the Mount of Sharrumagi,
